He phrases this carefully, not wanting Marcus to feel used, which means Marcus feels the care and reads it correctly.
- Marcus says he'd be glad to help. He means it and is also aware that saying anything else would look like avoidance.
- He walks to the harbor office after his afternoon class. He has been in it once before, for a vessel documentation check when he was planning a classroom exercise on maritime regulation that never materialized. He knows where it is.
- Eira is at her desk. She looks up when he comes in. She is not unfriendly, but she is not expecting him and the harbor office is her professional space; she does not yield ground in it.
- He explains what Fenn asked him to do. He says he's been told that a fisherman heard the man speak something that might be German. He offers: if any written item should surface — a document, a phrase — he's willing to assist with identification.
- Eira says: "The Coast Guard has what I know." She says it without inflection, which is the way she says most things, but it contains a great deal.
- He says: "Of course. I won't take up your time." He starts to leave. Then stops. He says, more carefully: "If it would be useful — not to the Coast Guard necessarily, but to you — I know something about identification documents from that period. Varieties of German. I'm at the school."
- Eira looks at him for a moment. She says: "I'll note it."
- He walks back. It is nearly dark; the harbor is a sound more than a sight. He thinks about the phrase *a man without papers* — which in German would be *ein Mensch ohne Papiere*, and which in German carries a weight that the English phrase only approximates. To be without papers in Europe in the last decade was to be without proof of your right to exist. He wonders whether the man knew what he was without.
- He tells Henry about the visit that evening, briefly. Henry says: "She'll use you when she needs you. Not before."

---

## Outcome / Turn
Marcus has offered his expertise; Eira has filed it. Nothing has advanced except the existence of a connection between them that can be activated later. The rumor about the German-speaking man is now in Marcus's head as an open question.

## New Question or Pressure Raised
Is the body connected to German identity in some way that will touch Marcus's specific expertise? If so, when?

---

## Continuity Notes
- Ed Larrabee: local fisherman, heard the man say something "European, maybe German" on the dock in the afternoon of October 9, before the storm. This is the basis of the rumor that comes to Marcus. Not a major character.
- Aldous Fenn, principal of Coldwater Reach High School. He means well; his request is reasonable; Marcus reads the subtext accurately.
- Marcus's offer to Eira: identification documents, varieties of German, general linguistic expertise. This is foreshadowing for Chapter 19 (when Marcus independently finds August Schell's name) and Chapter 29 (when Eira finally closes the file).
- Eira's response — "The Coast Guard has what I know" — is precise and true and also a way of declining to open any of the doors she has not yet decided to open.
- Henry's assessment: "She'll use you when she needs you. Not before." Henry reads social dynamics accurately.
- The chapter should not feel like a plot-delivery mechanism. Marcus's walk to the harbor office, his observation of the town at near-dark, his thinking about *ein Mensch ohne Papiere* — these carry as much weight as the exchange with Eira.
- Marcus is wearing the older suit (Tuesday). This detail can anchor the chapter's opening without comment.

# Chapter 07 — The Log

**One-line summary**: Alone in the harbor office on a December evening, Eira finds an irregularity in her October departure log that points to Birch Macomber.

**POV**: Eira Bowman  
**Tense**: Third-person limited, past  
**Setting**: Harbor office; Eira's house (briefly, at the end)  
**Time**: December 1943 — an evening, possibly a Thursday, the town at the Congregational Christmas program

---

## Goal
Eira wants to understand what she is actually carrying — to make the thing she knows into something with a shape she can work with or set down. She goes back to the logs.

## Obstacle
The irregularity she finds is in her own handwriting, which means she cannot extricate herself from it. She made the entry or she didn't. She does not remember which.

---

## Action Beats

- Chapter opens: the harbor office at night, December, everyone else in town at the Congregational Christmas program (she has not gone; she never goes; Owen used to go for both of them). The coal stove in the corner. The tide chart pinned above the chart cabinet. The sound of the harbor is specific in winter — less traffic, the ice forming at the inner anchorage's north end, the particular knock of the mooring lines against the pilings.
- She pulls the October log books. This is a professional act, the kind she performs regularly, but tonight it is not routine. She is looking for something.
- She lists, methodically, every vessel in harbor October 7–12: lobster boats *Thistle* and *Doris V.*, both still at mooring; the Lighthouse Service ice barge *Ploughman*, anchor; coaster *Macomber*, Birch Macomber, Machias, arrived October 8.
- The *Macomber*'s departure entry: October 11, 0430. In her handwriting.
- She goes to the memory. October 11 was the storm's second day. She came in at 0700; the wind was still running at 40 knots from the northeast; the harbor was closed to outbound traffic by standing regulation. She would not have passed a departing vessel — she would have been at the dock to stop it. She does not remember the *Macomber* being there to stop, which means it had already gone before she came in. But the log says 0430.
- Possibility one: she was there at 0430 and doesn't remember. It was the second day of the blow; she had been up most of the previous night. Exhaustion does things.
- Possibility two: she logged it from a note — the harbor has no formal overnight watchman, but the pier shed has a message board. Someone could have left a note, and she could have transferred it to the log without thinking.
- Possibility three: she invented the time as a placeholder, intending to confirm later, and then didn't, because the body arrived the next morning and everything else became secondary.
- She sits with all three possibilities. She does not decide between them. She closes the log.
- What she knows for certain: a coaster does not put out at 0430 on the second day of a nor'easter unless there is a reason. Macomber is a cautious skipper. If he put out then, something made him.
- She adds a note to her personal log (not the official record): *Macomber departure — October 11, 0430. Machias. Reason for departure in bad conditions: unknown. Inquiry?*
- She goes home. Owen's oilskin coat on the hook. She makes tea; she does not drink it. She sits at the kitchen table with her hands around the cup until the tea goes cold, then goes to bed.

---

## Outcome / Turn
The irregularity in the departure log is now a defined question: why did Macomber put out in the storm's second day, and who (if anyone) put that time in the log? This is not yet evidence of anything. It is a shape — a thing that wants explaining.

## New Question or Pressure Raised
Where did Macomber go, and what was he doing in Coldwater Reach in the first place? The body came in on October 13 — two days after the *Macomber* departed.

---

## Continuity Notes
- The October log entries must be consistent with Chapter 04 (where Eira gives the same information to Lieutenant Pruitt). Same vessels, same dates.
- The Macomber departure entry: October 11, 0430. This is the irregularity. The Drafter must not resolve whether Eira made it legitimately or not — it should remain genuinely uncertain. The important thing is that she cannot explain it.
- Eira's personal log is separate from the official harbor log. The personal log is where she notes things she is not ready to make official.
- Birch Macomber: skipper, 50s, spare, cautious with money — reliable, not a risk-taker. The fact that he put out in bad conditions is what makes the departure anomalous for this particular man.
- The Congregational Christmas program: the town is occupied. Eira is alone in the office by choice. The detail is period-accurate and establishes her relation to the town's social fabric — she participates in the work of the community but not in its social rituals.
- Owen's oilskin coat and the tea going cold: both are Eira processing without processing. She does not editorialized about her grief or her worry; she makes tea and lets it go cold. That is the register.
- Drafter note: the harbor office interior should be established more fully here than in earlier chapters. The coal stove, the tide chart, the chart cabinet, the pending drawer, the two cups on the hook — render them as a working environment, not as atmosphere.

# Chapter 08 — Vater

**One-line summary**: At the December dinner table, Albrecht acknowledges — in German, without explanation — that Karl comes from near his own hometown; Hannah is the only one listening.

**POV**: Hannah Pfeiffer  
**Tense**: Third-person limited, past  
**Setting**: Pfeiffer farmhouse kitchen; the barn (briefly); Hannah's room  
**Time**: December 1943 — a weekday evening

---

## Goal
Hannah wants to understand what her father makes of Karl's presence on the farm — whether he recognizes the proximity (Straubing to Regensburg, 50 km) and what it means to him.

## Obstacle
Her father's silence is not a wall but a habit; it lets very little through, and what does come through arrives in a language she understands imperfectly and cannot respond to fully. Her mother's silence is something else — climate, not communication.

---

## Action Beats

- The chapter begins in the barn at evening, the last feeding of the day. The cows are settled. Hannah can hear, through the barn wall, the POWs in the icehouse — the sound of a door, someone speaking low. She cannot make out the words. She finishes her work.
- At the house: her mother has made soup from the root cellar's produce — potato and turnip, very little else. The kitchen smells of this and of the woodstove. Her father is already at the table.
- Dinner: the three of them without conversation. This is the norm. Hannah eats; her mother eats; her father eats and then, without looking up, begins to talk — in German, as he always does now at home — about the north pasture fence, which the boys have been repairing. He says Karl works quickly with the post driver. He says this with what is, for Albrecht, something close to approval.
- Hannah asks, in German — she has to gather herself to do it, because speaking German with her father feels like entering a room she's only supposed to observe: *Hat einer von ihnen etwas gesagt über Straubing?* (Has any of them said anything about Straubing?)
- Her father pauses. He puts down his spoon. He says: *Regensburg ist nicht weit.* (Regensburg is not far.) He says it the way he says things that have been sitting in him for a while: flat, without inflection, as if stating a weather fact.
- Then he picks up his spoon and finishes his soup. He folds his napkin. He goes to the radio chair.
- Hannah's mother has not looked up from her bowl. After a moment she rises and takes the dishes to the sink. She hums something — not a song Hannah recognizes, something old, Bavarian maybe. She stops after a few bars.
- Hannah washes the dishes. The first hard snow fell this morning; she can see it on the barn roof through the window. Blue light on white.
- She thinks about what her father said: *nicht weit.* Not far. Not: *I know where that is.* Not: *he is nearly my neighbor.* Just the statement of proximity — the distance reduced to a geographic fact, which is the only safe form it can take.
- She goes upstairs. Rolf's coat on the hook. She sits on the bed. She thinks: her father knows. He has always known. The knowing is not what he's protecting; the question is what the knowing costs him, and whether Karl's presence makes the cost higher or lower.
- She thinks about the word *Heimweh* — she has heard her father use it, not about Germany specifically but in the abstract, the way you use a word that doesn't need a referent because the feeling is already understood. Homesickness. Except it isn't sickness exactly — *Weh* is pain, ache. Home-ache. Different thing.

---

## Outcome / Turn
Albrecht has, in his minimal way, acknowledged Karl's proximity to his hometown. He won't discuss it, but he has registered it. Something between them — father and daughter, father and the young prisoner he has not yet looked at directly — has shifted.

## New Question or Pressure Raised
What does Karl represent to Albrecht — threat, proof, memory, or something he has not named? And what does Hannah do with the knowing that her father knows?

---

## Continuity Notes
- Albrecht Pfeiffer: from Straubing; Karl from Regensburg, approximately 50 km away. Canon facts #10, #12.
- Albrecht speaks only German at home at this point. Canon fact (from characters.md). His English is correct but slightly formal when used; it is simply not used at home.
- Magda: her near-silence is a climate, not a subject. She hums something Bavarian — this is not a scene about her; it is a fact registered by Hannah. Do not editorialize.
- The word *Heimweh*: Hannah's interior, not spoken. Not translated in narration; context carries it.
- The word *Vater* in the chapter title is Hannah's German-interior name for her father. In narration she thinks of him as *Vater* when she is operating in the German mode of her consciousness.
- The first snow: period-accurate for downeast Maine, December. The blue light on white through the window is an image, not a metaphor; let it be a thing Hannah sees.
- Rolf's coat: present in Hannah's room throughout the novel as a constant. It is not symbolic; it is her brother's coat that she wears.
- This chapter should be quiet, even spare by Hannah's chapter standards. The density of the interior should be in what she does not say to her father rather than in reflection after the fact.

text: Setup done. Now the Rising Action chapters 9–15.

input.content:
# Chapter 09 — The Board

**One-line summary**: Marcus learns the school board discussed his position at the December meeting; Henry advises him to do nothing and wait for them to act first.

**POV**: Marcus Klein  
**Tense**: Third-person limited, past  
**Setting**: Coldwater Reach High School; Henry and Lillian's house  
**Time**: January 1944

---

## Goal
Marcus needs to understand the threat's shape — what the board said, what it might do — so he can decide how to respond. The goal is situational clarity, not action.

## Obstacle
He doesn't know what was said. The board isn't required to tell him. The person who tells him (Edna Locke) knows only what she observed and is already taking a risk in telling him this much. Marcus cannot ask directly without appearing either anxious or aggressive.

---

## Action Beats

- January faculty meeting: distribution of the spring schedule. Marcus's name is on it as usual — German I, German II, Latin I, Latin III. He files this without visible relief.
- After the meeting, the other teachers file out. Edna Locke — history teacher, been at the school since 1919, a woman who treats him with the steady consideration of someone who has had occasion to think about fairness — stays at the table after the others have gone. She waits until the last footstep has retreated down the corridor.
- She tells him: at the December board meeting there was "some talk." Concern from some parents. After the holidays there may be a formal item on the agenda — a review of his appointment. She doesn't know more than this; she was told by the board secretary, who thought she should know.
- Marcus thanks her. He says he appreciates it. He means both sentences. She nods and goes. The exchange takes under three minutes; she has a class in four.
- He goes back to his classroom and teaches the afternoon Latin section. His voice is as it always is — precise, measured, not performing calm but genuinely deploying the part of himself that finds Latin grammar interesting. *The ablative absolute,* he tells them. *A construction that carries an entire subordinate action in two words. Economy as a Latin virtue.*
- That evening: chess with Henry. Marcus tells him what Edna Locke said. Henry listens without interrupting; he is studying the board. He moves his rook; then: "You want to know what I'd do?"
- Marcus says yes.
- Henry: "Nothing. Do your job. Make them act first. If you make a move before they do — write a letter to someone, complain, ask a question at the board meeting — you've given them a reason to move faster. Let it sit."
- Marcus: "And if they do act?"
- Henry: "Then you have a case. Right now you have a rumor."
- Marcus moves a bishop. He thinks about the logic of this — it is not cowardice; it is the practice of making the other side expose itself. He has seen it applied to worse situations.
- He writes a letter to the Emergency Committee at Cambridge before bed. Not a request for relocation. An informational note: the board has raised questions about his appointment; he does not believe action is imminent; he is continuing to teach. He signs it and puts it in his coat pocket to post in the morning.
- He thinks, before he sleeps, about the word *Duldung* — German, meaning toleration, being put up with. It is not the same as acceptance. He has been the object of *Duldung* in several countries. He knows what it feels like. He knows also that it is not nothing; it is a space you can work in.

---

## Outcome / Turn
Marcus knows the shape of the threat. He is not going to make a move before the board does. He has Henry's counsel as both strategy and companionship. The letter to Cambridge is an insurance filing, not a withdrawal.

## New Question or Pressure Raised
When will the board's formal action come, and what form will it take? Does Marcus have allies — besides Edna Locke — he doesn't know about?

---

## Continuity Notes
- Edna Locke: history teacher, Coldwater Reach High School, been there since 1919. She is Marcus's one overt ally on the faculty. Her appearance here is her first; she returns at Chapter 24 (the hearing).
- Henry's advice — "make them act first" — is the wisdom of someone who has navigated discrimination with precision for a long time. It should read as counsel from experience, not as a lesson Marcus is receiving. Henry has been through this. He doesn't explain that.
- The Emergency Committee at Cambridge: Marcus notified but not alarmed. The letter is filed in continuity — it becomes relevant if the board hearing in Chapter 24 goes badly.
- The word *Duldung*: Marcus's interior. Not translated; context carries it as "the space of being put up with."
- Marcus's classes: German I, German II, Latin I, Latin III. The spring schedule lists them; the Drafter can reference these as needed.
- The ablative absolute: a real Latin construction. Marcus genuinely finds this interesting; the parallel to his own life (carrying subordinate contexts in compressed forms) should be implicit only, never stated.
- Chess: Thursday evenings. Established here. Henry wins more than Marcus.

# Chapter 10 — February Ice

**One-line summary**: While coordinating the freeing of a coaster from the inner-anchorage ice, Eira learns that Birch Macomber took Portland contract work last fall.

**POV**: Eira Bowman  
**Tense**: Third-person limited, past  
**Setting**: Coldwater Reach harbor; the inner anchorage; the dock  
**Time**: February 1944

---

## Goal
Eira's primary goal is professional: free the coaster *Wren* from where it has grounded on the ice-ledge and get it to safe water. The secondary goal — information about Macomber — is opportunistic; she doesn't set out to get it.

## Obstacle
Coordinating the buoy tender takes time; the tide window is narrow; the skipper of the *Wren* is not in distress but is not quite cooperative either. The information about Macomber comes through conversation, not interrogation, which means she can ask only so much before the question becomes visible.

---

## Action Beats

- February: the inner anchorage has gone hard at the north end — ice from the shore out about forty yards, thin at the edge. A coaster from Rockland, the *Wren*, came in on a falling tide and found itself with its keel on the ice-ledge at the mooring field's edge. Not in danger, but stuck.
- Eira is down at the dock at first light to assess. The *Wren*'s skipper is Estelle Hartley — one of three or four women skippers operating on the Maine coast, a fact Eira notes without dwelling on it the way other people would. Hartley is about forty, has sailed this coast since 1927, and is annoyed but not panicked.
- They discuss options: try to rock the *Wren* off with the engine, wait for the tide to lift it, or call the buoy tender for a tow. Eira calls the buoy tender — the tide won't lift her enough for another four hours and the ice is still building from the north in the overnight cold.
- While they wait: Eira and Hartley stand on the dock. The cold is specific — the cold that comes off ice is different from the cold that comes off open water; it is drier, stiffer. They drink coffee from Eira's thermos.
- Hartley knows the Maine coast trade thoroughly. Eira asks, in the way of professional conversation: "You see Birch Macomber down your way?" Hartley: "Portland, mostly, these days. He's been running something regular down that way since fall." Eira: "October-ish?" Hartley: "Could be. Some kind of contract, he said. Paid well, which is how I know it, because Birch doesn't usually turn down a run for the weather and he turned one down from me in September."
- Eira: "What kind of contract?" Hartley shrugs: "He didn't say. Something out of Portland for the Navy district, maybe. He had papers."
- The buoy tender arrives; the tow goes straightforwardly. The *Wren* comes off the ledge, shifts into open water, and Hartley's crew checks the keel — no damage.
- Eira thanks Hartley; Hartley thanks her for the tow call. The *Wren* heads south. Eira walks back to the harbor office.
- That evening she writes in her personal log: *Macomber — Portland, Navy district contract — fall 1943. Had papers. Turned down a Rockland run in September. Inquiry: what was the contract?*

---

## Outcome / Turn
Eira has a new lead: Macomber was running a Navy district contract out of Portland, which connects his presence in Coldwater Reach in early October and his irregular departure to a purpose of some kind. "Had papers" is notable — it means his movement was officially sanctioned, which means records exist somewhere.

## New Question or Pressure Raised
What was the Portland Navy district contract, and does it explain why August Schell (not yet known to Eira by name) received a postcard from Portland with a date and time on it? Is there a connection between Macomber's contract work and the body?

---

## Continuity Notes
- Estelle Hartley, skipper of the coaster *Wren*, Rockland. A woman skipper; Eira notes her as a professional, not as a rarity. This is consistent with Eira's character — she doesn't perform solidarity or special kinship; she works with competent people.
- The inner anchorage freezing in the inner section: consistent with the world.md description of the harbor. The outer harbor stays open; the inner anchorage freezes by January in hard winters.
- Birch Macomber: Portland-based by winter 1944; Navy district contract beginning fall 1943. Hartley doesn't know the specifics.
- "Had papers": meaning some kind of official Navy district authorization, possibly a requisition or contract documentation. Macomber is not operating outside the law; his contract work is legitimate. This is important: the body's connection to Macomber, when it comes, will not be criminal. It will be mundane and sad.
- The buoy tender: Lighthouse Service, puts in twice monthly at Coldwater Reach normally. Eira calls for an emergency tow, which is within her authority as harbormaster.
- Eira and Hartley's conversation: it should read as two working women having a working conversation, not as an interview. The Macomber information is obtained without Hartley realizing it's being sought.

# Chapter 11 — The Word for Harbor

**One-line summary**: Hannah leaves word-cards at the fence; Karl tells her he has never seen the sea; she plants the idea without planning to.

**POV**: Hannah Pfeiffer  
**Tense**: Third-person limited, past  
**Setting**: The Pfeiffer farm: barn, fence line between the barn and the icehouse  
**Time**: February 1944

---

## Goal
Hannah wants to communicate with Karl in a way that neither Lonzo's presence nor the limits of their shared language can entirely prevent. She has found a method: word-cards.

## Obstacle
Every exchange has to happen within sight of Lonzo Reed, which means it must be deniable — a farm task, a handed object, a moment's pause that looks like something else. Karl's English is growing but slowly; their common ground is German, and speaking German with him in front of Lonzo feels like a thing she cannot fully explain.

---

## Action Beats

- The word-cards began three weeks ago, in January. Hannah cuts small rectangles from the cedar planking of old butter boxes — the kind her father saves for kindling. She writes in pencil, one word per card, English on one side and German on the other. She started with objects: *barn / Scheune. gate / Tor. bucket / Eimer. rope / Seil.* She leaves them on the fence post nearest the icehouse, under a stone.
- Karl picks them up. He returns them — she finds them back on the post by the next afternoon, the English side worn from handling. He has been memorizing.
- February: she makes a new card. She writes *harbor / Hafen* and stands for a moment looking at it before she puts it under the stone. She is thinking about the man who drowned and the harbor where the body came in — not that she knows anything specific, only what the town talk has reached her: a man, unidentified, the breakwater.
- That afternoon Karl comes to the fence with the feed bucket — he has adopted this routine; she has let him. He pulls the card from his pocket: *harbor / Hafen.* He says the English word carefully, testing it. Then says in German: *Was ist das, ein Hafen, hier?* (What is that, a harbor, here?)
- She says, in German: *Ja. Drei Meilen.* Three miles. She points roughly south toward the coast.
- He says, in German, with the particular careful formality he uses when saying something important: *Ich habe das Meer nie gesehen.* (I have never seen the sea.)
- She looks at him. She believes him. He is from Regensburg, ninety kilometers inland, up the Danube from Munich. Tunisia was his only coast.
- She does not say: I could show you. She does not say it because she doesn't know yet whether she means it. She takes the bucket and goes to the barn.
- For the rest of the afternoon she thinks about what it means that a person can live nineteen years and never see the sea. Rolf grew up here and was eighteen when he left for the Pacific coast, then North Africa. She walked to the harbor by herself at nine years old, without asking permission, and sat on the breakwater until the tide came in and soaked her shoes. She thinks about what the harbor looks like in February — the ice at the north end, the lobster boats, the smell of diesel and brine and the cold that comes off the water. She has never thought of it as something you could be denied.
- At dinner she asks her father, in German: *Hast du das Meer gesehen, bevor du nach Amerika kamst?* (Did you see the sea before you came to America?) Her father looks at her. He says: *Erst in Hamburg.* (Not until Hamburg.) She nods. She does not ask more.

---

## Outcome / Turn
Karl has said he has never seen the sea; Hannah has registered this as a fact she is responsible for knowing. She has not yet decided what to do with it, but it has been filed.

## New Question or Pressure Raised
Will Hannah take Karl to see the harbor? What would it cost, and what would it mean, and who would know?

---

## Continuity Notes
- Word-cards: butter box cedar planking, pencil, English one side / German the other. These are the object that mediates Hannah and Karl's exchange through winter. The Drafter should use them as a recurring physical detail.
- Karl's English as of February 1944: growing from zero; he knows some words from the other POWs, from the farm (tools, animal names, commands), and now from the cards. He is not yet conversational.
- Karl from Regensburg: ninety kilometers inland, up the Danube. Bayarian, not coastal. He was captured in Tunisia (May 1943) but Tunisia is not the same as seeing the sea — a detail for the Drafter to handle as they see fit.
- *Ich habe das Meer nie gesehen*: this line should land with weight; it is one of the novel's hinge lines. It plants the possibility that Hannah acts on in Chapter 23.
- Albrecht's *Erst in Hamburg* — he first saw the sea when he emigrated, in 1922, boarding the ship. This is consistent with Straubing being an inland Bavarian town. It also makes Hannah and her father, briefly, of the same mind about something, which is rare.
- The body on the breakwater is present in this chapter only as town talk filtered to Hannah — she does not know details, only that there was "a man on the breakwater." This is sufficient; do not over-explain.
- Lonzo Reed's presence: he is always there but his attention varies. He does not hear the German exchange in this chapter or, if he does, he does not respond to it.

# Chapter 12 — Two Suits

**One-line summary**: Marcus decides to stay in Coldwater Reach; he answers Cambridge's offer with no; Ada Gile mentions a Coast Guard inquiry about a piece of mail that did not belong to anyone in town.

**POV**: Marcus Klein  
**Tense**: Third-person limited, past  
**Setting**: Henry and Lillian's house; the post office; the street between them  
**Time**: March 1944 — a Saturday

---

## Goal
Marcus needs to answer the Cambridge letter — a Boston university posting has been offered — and in doing so must commit to what he is doing in Coldwater Reach.

## Obstacle
He is not sure yet whether choosing to stay is humility, fear, or wisdom. He has been carrying the letter for three weeks without knowing which.

---

## Action Beats

- Saturday: Marcus puts on the older suit (Thursday suit, but it is Saturday — he uses the schedule loosely on weekends). He has been carrying the Cambridge letter in his coat pocket for three weeks. The letter offers a position at a Boston-area university: visiting instructor in German philology. It would be a step toward his actual professional life. He knows this.
- He walks to the post office for stamps. This is what he tells himself he is going out to do. He is also, he knows, testing whether leaving the house on a particular errand changes his thinking.
- Ada Gile is at the counter. She knows who he is — she knows who everyone is; it is her professional requirement. She is not unfriendly to Marcus; she treats him with the same direct efficiency she applies to everyone, which is its own kind of respect.
- He buys stamps. He lingers slightly — not enough to be obvious, he thinks, though Ada misses nothing. She is sorting a bundle of V-Mail for delivery.
- He asks — carefully, the way he asks most things, framing the question so the answer doesn't have to be personal: "Has there been any further inquiry about the business last fall? The man on the breakwater."
- Ada says: "The Coast Guard came to me in November. There was a piece of mail that came through in the first week of October — addressed to general delivery, no full name. I held it the usual time and then it was collected by the Coast Guard when they asked. They seemed to know what they were looking for."
- He says: "What was the name on it?" She says: "A. Schell." She says it neutrally, as a fact that belongs to the record, not to a conversation. "No one of that name in Coldwater."
- He says thank you. He goes out. He walks home.
- At the kitchen table: Lillian brings coffee without being asked. He takes the Cambridge letter from his coat pocket and puts it on the table. He reads it again. Boston. Philology. Colleagues. A life he can recognize from the inside.
- He thinks about the word *heimisch* — not for the first time. He cannot say he is *heimisch* in Coldwater Reach. He also cannot say he was *heimisch* in Cambridge, or in London, or in Paris, or in the London boarding house he lived in for eleven months in 1939–1940. He does not know that the condition can be recovered.
- He thinks about Henry and Lillian. He thinks about Edna Locke in the corridor after the faculty meeting, choosing to tell him a thing that cost her something. He thinks about Hannah Pfeiffer looking out the window in German class, understanding more than she answers.
- He takes out a sheet of paper and writes to the Emergency Committee. One page. *I am grateful for the offer. I intend to remain in Coldwater Reach for the present year. I will write again in the autumn.* He does not explain further. He seals it and puts it with the stamps.
- He drinks his coffee. He thinks: *A. Schell.*

---

## Outcome / Turn
Marcus has committed to staying — provisionally, for the year. This is the first genuine act of investment in Coldwater Reach. It is not triumphant; it is a decision made by process of elimination and something he cannot yet name.

## New Question or Pressure Raised
Who is "A. Schell"? Marcus has the name now from Ada; the question is open.

---

## Continuity Notes
- The Cambridge letter: a Boston-area university posting, visiting instructor in German philology. This is a real alternative to Coldwater Reach; it should not read as obviously inferior. Marcus's choice to stay is not sentimentality.
- Marcus's answer to Cambridge: staying "for the present year." He writes "I will write again in the autumn" — which means by Chapter 24 (the hearing, November 1944) he will have had to follow up. Whether he did or not is a detail the Drafter can handle; the Emergency Committee does not reappear as an active plot element.
- Ada Gile: the postcard addressed to "A. Schell," general delivery, Coldwater Reach. Postmark Portland, early October 1943. Coast Guard collected it in November 1943. She says "a piece of mail" — she has not said it was a postcard, but Eira will learn that detail directly in Chapter 13. Marcus only knows "A. Schell" and the general delivery address.
- Marcus now has the name "A. Schell" — this connects to what he finds in Bangor in August 1944 (Chapter 19). The Drafter should ensure that Marcus doesn't rush to use this; it sits in him, unresolved, until the Bangor trip.
- The word *heimisch*: Marcus thinks it, does not speak it. This is a repeat from Chapter 03 (where it appears in his narration); the repetition is intentional — it is the word his displacement always returns to.
- The two-suit detail: Saturday, he uses the older suit, which is technically the Tuesday/Thursday suit. He is using the schedule loosely on weekends. The Drafter can note this as a small act of loosening in a man who holds himself carefully.

# Chapter 13 — Ada Gile's Window

**One-line summary**: Eira goes to the post office with a purpose and learns from Ada that the postcard addressed to "A. Schell" contained a date and a time — nothing more.

**POV**: Eira Bowman  
**Tense**: Third-person limited, past  
**Setting**: The Coldwater Reach post office (Reach Road); the harbor office  
**Time**: March 1944

---

## Goal
Eira wants to know what Ada Gile knows about the body inquiry — specifically whether anything came through the post that might connect to the man on the breakwater.

## Obstacle
Ada does not gossip. She informs, on her own terms, when the question is asked correctly. Eira has to ask without appearing to investigate, because investigating means explaining that her official report is incomplete.

---

## Action Beats

- A March morning, the harbor quiet, an east wind coming off the water that carries sleet. Eira makes the walk to the post office herself — she usually sends one of the dock hands, but today she goes.
- The post office: the counter, the pigeonhole grid behind it, Ada Gile's reading glasses on her nose, the smell of paper and ink that Eira has associated with this room since childhood. Ada knows the harbor business through the mail it receives; she and Eira have had a professional relationship for eight years.
- Ada hands Eira the harbor office mail bundle: three Bureau of Navigation circulars, a quarterly report from the Lighthouse Service, a V-Mail from the buoy tender's schedule coordinator.
- Eira lingers. She says: "Ada. That business last fall — the body. Did the Coast Guard speak with you?"
- Ada: "They did."
- Eira: "About mail."
- Ada, not a question: "Yes."
- Eira: "Was there something?"
- Ada: "A postcard. Came in the first week of October. General delivery, the name 'A. Schell.' No last name used as a first name or any other way I could place it. Coldwater has no one by that name."
- Eira, carefully: "What did it say?"
- Ada considers this question — considers whether it is within her professional scope to answer it, and decides it is: "A date and a time. Nothing else. I assumed it was a meeting of some kind."
- Eira: "The Coast Guard took it?"
- Ada: "In November. Along with a statement from me about when it arrived."
- Eira: "Thank you."
- She walks back to the harbor office. Sleet has turned to rain by the time she arrives.
- At her desk: she opens her personal log to the Macomber section and writes: *A. Schell — general delivery — postcard — Portland postmark — early October. Contents: date and time. No other text. Coast Guard has it.*
- She sits back. She looks at what she has. A postcard sent from Portland to a man in Coldwater Reach who had no established presence in the town — no boardinghouse, no known address — with a meeting time on it. The *Macomber* coaster, running a Navy district contract out of Portland, in Coldwater Reach the night of October 9. A man at the end of the pier with a canvas bag. The body three days later.
- She does not write the connection down. It is a set of coincidences, not evidence. But it has a shape now, and the shape is specific.
- She makes coffee. She does not drink it.

---

## Outcome / Turn
Eira now has enough to form a theory: August Schell (not yet known by name) received a postcard arranging a meeting in Coldwater Reach in early October. The meeting may have been connected to Macomber's presence. He came, the meeting happened or didn't, and then he was dead. The theory explains the shape without resolving it.

## New Question or Pressure Raised
What was the meeting? Who sent the postcard from Portland? Is there a way to find out without making her own omission visible?

---

## Continuity Notes
- The postcard: addressed to "A. Schell," general delivery, Coldwater Reach. Portland postmark, first week of October 1943. Contents: a date and a time. No other text. This is the same information Ada gives Marcus in Chapter 12, but Eira receives additional detail (it was a postcard, not just "a piece of mail"; she learns the contents).
- This is a Drafter continuity check: Chapter 12 (Marcus, March) and Chapter 13 (Eira, March) happen in roughly the same week. Marcus visited Ada first. Ada gives Eira the same information and a bit more. Ada does not remark on Marcus having asked — it is not her habit to report one inquiry to another asker.
- The theory Eira forms: coherent but not provable. The narration should render it as a professional assessment — the way she'd assess tide height from visible data — not as an emotional revelation.
- "She does not drink the coffee": this is established as a signal in Eira's chapters — when she makes coffee and doesn't drink it, she is preoccupied. Cf. Chapter 07.
- Ada Gile's manner: "not a gossip; an informer." She answers questions asked correctly; she does not offer. She is Coldwater Reach's institutional memory in a way that parallels Eira's relation to the harbor.
- The harbor office's running logs (official vs. personal): the personal log is where Eira does her actual thinking. The official log is the record. The distinction is important throughout.

# Chapter 14 — Mud Season

**One-line summary**: Hannah turns 18; V-Mail arrives from Rolf in Italy; Marcus's lesson on the word *mündig* plants a seed about what it means to speak for yourself.

**POV**: Hannah Pfeiffer  
**Tense**: Third-person limited, past  
**Setting**: Pfeiffer farmhouse; Coldwater Reach High School; the road between them  
**Time**: April 1944 — Hannah's birthday, April 14

---

## Goal
Hannah wants — though she couldn't name it exactly — to feel what being eighteen means, whether it is different from seventeen.

## Obstacle
The farm demands don't change because the calendar does. Her parents' silences don't lift for birthdays. The mud of April makes everything harder. What she hoped might feel like arrival feels more like continuation.

---

## Action Beats

- April 14. The farm in mud season — the thaw has turned every path into a sucking ankle-deep problem; the cows are restless to get to pasture but the ground won't hold them yet; the barn smells of winter that has gone on too long. Hannah is awake at 5 as always.
- Breakfast: her mother sets a plate with a piece of cake on it — cardboard-pale, no icing because there's no sugar, made with the last of the powdered milk and a tablespoon of something sweet enough to count. It is a cake in the way that the farm in mud season is a farm: basically itself, without any of its usual ornament. Her father looks at it. He says: *Herzlichen Glückwunsch, Hannchen.* He calls her Hannchen — the diminutive he has used since she was small, which she is too old for and he is still using.
- She says: *Danke, Vater.*
- At school: German class before noon. Marcus Klein is teaching. Today the lesson is on legal and civic vocabulary — words of adulthood, of civic participation. He writes *mündig* on the board and says, in the way he says things he finds genuinely interesting: "This word. It is the state of being of age, of having majority. But look at its root: *Mund.* Mouth. To be *mündig* is literally to have your own mouth — to speak for yourself, without a guardian speaking in your place."
- Hannah writes this in the margin of her notebook: *mündig — having your own mouth.*
- After school she stops at the fence. Karl is post-driving. She says, in English: "Today I am eighteen." She has not planned to say this; it arrives.
- He looks up. He takes in the sentence in his slowly-developing English. He says, in English, correctly: "I see." Then, in German: *Glückwunsch.* He says it without ceremony, the way you say congratulations to someone you mean it for.
- She walks home.
- The V-Mail from Rolf is on the table when she arrives — Ada Gile delivered it while Hannah was at school. The letter is short: he is well; Italy is difficult to describe; he is sorry to miss her birthday; the potatoes should have come up already if the weather is right; give his love to Mutti and Vati. He writes her name in his labored hand — he was never as good at the written word as she is — and at the end he writes *Alles Gute, dein Rolf.* All the best, your Rolf.
- Her mother takes the letter from the table and reads it without expression, then holds it in her lap for a long moment, then folds it and puts it back on the table.
- Her father takes it next. He holds it for a long time. He does not unfold it again.
- That evening Hannah writes back. She describes the mud, the cake, the potatoes (not up yet, but maybe next week). She does not describe the word *mündig*. She does not describe what Karl said.
- She puts on Rolf's coat and steps outside. April cold, but a different cold from February — the air has something else in it, the beginning of something. She stands in the yard and thinks about *mündig*. Having your own mouth. She has been practicing, she thinks, for eighteen years.

---

## Outcome / Turn
Hannah has turned 18. The birthday is quiet and undramatic. But *mündig* has been planted — the idea that adulthood is about speaking for yourself. What she will speak for, and to whom, is the rest of her arc.

## New Question or Pressure Raised
Rolf is in Italy, alive. When will that change? What does Hannah do with the voice she is now, technically, old enough to use?

---

## Continuity Notes
- Hannah's birthday: April 14, 1944. Canon fact #8. She turns 18.
- Rolf Pfeiffer: alive, in Italy, as of April 1944. V-Mail from Italy, censored. He is a warm presence through the letters, not a narrative actor.
- *Mündig*: from *Mund* (mouth). Means "of legal age," "having majority." Marcus teaches this as part of civic vocabulary; the etymological point is genuine and Marcus's engagement with it is real, not pedagogical performance. The word lands in Hannah because of when she hears it.
- *Herzlichen Glückwunsch, Hannchen*: Albrecht uses the diminutive. Hannah is too old for it; he uses it anyway. This is the tenderness he doesn't know how to express otherwise.
- Karl's response — "I see" in English, then *Glückwunsch* in German — is a careful small acknowledgment. He is not performing emotion; he is being accurate.
- Magda's response to Rolf's letter: she holds it without speaking. Her grief for what was lost (her brother, Germany, the farm she knew) is registered as posture and action — the holding — not as interior description.
- The V-Mail system: letters are photographed, shipped on film, printed at destination. By the time Rolf's letter arrives it is ten days to two weeks old. Hannah knows this and compensates in her replies.
- The potatoes as a recurring Rolf/Hannah touchstone: practical, specific, farm-grounded. Rolf is not romantic about his absence; he asks about potatoes.

# Chapter 15 — The Sixth of June

**One-line summary**: D-Day. Marcus holds his private knowledge of France while managing a classroom; that evening he reads the first letter the Emergency Committee sent him and puts it away again.

**POV**: Marcus Klein  
**Tense**: Third-person limited, past  
**Setting**: Coldwater Reach High School; Henry and Lillian's house  
**Time**: June 6, 1944

---

## Goal
Marcus has no single goal today; the day imposes itself on every ordinary thing he would otherwise be doing. What he is trying to manage is the gap between what the day means publicly and what it means in his own specific experience of France.

## Obstacle
He cannot explain what France is to him without explaining more than he is prepared to explain. His knowledge of Paris — of specific streets, a specific apartment, the specific population of a neighborhood — is not something he can share in this room, in this school, in this war. The obstacle is the gap between his experience and the language available to him here.

---

## Action Beats

- The news comes over the school's BC-1206 receiver at 7:02 a.m.: Allied forces have landed on the coast of Normandy. The announcement is precise and controlled in the OWI voice — no triumphalism, not yet, but the barely-contained weight of the fact.
- Principal Fenn makes an announcement at the morning assembly. He calls it the beginning of the end, and the students receive this with a particular kind of held-breath hope that is also anxiety, because for most of them "the end" means brothers and cousins coming home or not coming home. Hannah Pfeiffer, in the third row, looks at her hands.
- Marcus teaches the morning class. German II: today they were meant to cover indirect discourse. He writes it on the board anyway — you keep the classroom running because that is what the classroom is for, and because the alternative is a room full of seventeen-year-olds with no structure and too much fear. Hannah answers a question with accuracy and a flatness that tells him she is somewhere else in her head.
- During the free period, the teacher's lounge. The other teachers are around the radio — the principal has rolled it in from his office. The news is repeating. The map that Edna Locke has been keeping on the history-room wall — with pins for the various theaters — doesn't have Normandy marked yet. She is in there marking it.
- Marcus goes to his classroom. He stands at the window. Paris. The apartment on the Rue du Temple that he shared with two other scholars for eight months, 1939–1940. The neighborhood: the old Jewish quarter, the Marais. He knows what happened to those streets, what the Occupation meant, the way a person knows a thing that was reported to him secondhand by someone who escaped later. He does not know what happened to the specific people. He will not know for some time.
- He thinks: the Allies are on the coast. Four years and eight months since he left Paris. The city has been occupied for four years. Today it begins to un-be occupied, or the beginning of that begins. This is good. It is the right thing. He is glad and he cannot be glad completely because being glad completely would require believing the damage is undone, which it is not.
- After school: Henry has the radio on. Lillian is in the garden, not because she doesn't want to listen but because the garden is the garden and it wants doing. They sit at the kitchen table, Marcus and Henry, and listen to the report. Henry says: "Long way to go still." Marcus says: "Yes." They listen.
- That evening, in his room, Marcus opens the drawer in the bedside table where he keeps the letters he has carried longest. He takes out the Emergency Committee's first letter, autumn 1940, which told him there was a place for him in Cambridge, Massachusetts, and that a position would be held. He reads it. He puts it back. He goes to sleep.

---

## Outcome / Turn
D-Day has happened; the world has turned a corner. Marcus registers it with full attention and cannot access the full register of feeling it deserves. What he can access — the letter, the specific streets, the years — he keeps to himself. He goes to sleep.

## New Question or Pressure Raised
What will France's liberation bring — news he has been waiting for, or news he has been afraid to receive? His people in Paris (the scholars, the neighbors) remain unknown until after the war.

---

## Continuity Notes
- D-Day: June 6, 1944. The Allied landings at Normandy. Historical date.
- Marcus in Paris: approximately 1939–1940, prior to the fall of France (June 1940). He lived in the Marais, the old Jewish quarter. He left Paris before the fall, heading to London (per his background). This is consistent with canon: Paris → London → Cambridge, Massachusetts, then Quebec internment. The Rue du Temple address is invented but geographically accurate for the Marais.
- The Occupation of Paris: June 1940 – August 1944. Marcus does not know what happened to specific people; he has received secondhand reports. This is not discussed directly in the chapter; it is the substrate.
- Hannah Pfeiffer in the assembly: she is looking at her hands because she is thinking about Rolf, in Italy. Italy is a separate theater; the Normandy landing does not directly affect Rolf's situation, but the news of Allied action everywhere is reason to think about him.
- The BC-1206 radio receiver: period-accurate. Schools had radio equipment; the principal could roll in a receiver for a major announcement.
- Edna Locke marking the map: this is her chapter, the history classroom, her professional domain. Small detail that places her.
- The Emergency Committee's first letter, autumn 1940: Marcus has carried this letter since Cambridge. It is not described in detail; its existence is established here for later use.
- Henry and Lillian: they listen together without dramatizing. Lillian is in the garden. The domestic order is not a response to D-Day; it is what they are doing, and it continues.

text: Rising action done. Now the Complication chapters 16–23.

input.content:
# Chapter 16 — The Inspector

**One-line summary**: A naval district inspector mentions a Swedish vessel's missing crew member named August Schell; Eira realizes she already had the name.

**POV**: Eira Bowman  
**Tense**: Third-person limited, past  
**Setting**: Harbor office; the dock  
**Time**: July 1944

---

## Goal
Eira's goal is professional: pass the routine naval district inspection. The secondary development — the inspector's mention of August Schell — is not something she pursued.

## Obstacle
The information arrives at her and she has to decide, in the moment, what to do with the fact that she already knew the name from Ada Gile. Saying so would mean explaining how she came to have it.

---

## Action Beats

- A Tuesday in July, the summer heat soft and bright on the water. The buoy tender's monthly call brings Commander Whitmore, naval district inspector, for a routine review. Eira has been through two of these; she knows the form. She has the logs out, the vessel documentation, the communication records, the safety equipment inventory.
- Whitmore is a professional — not easy to read but not antagonistic. He goes through the records methodically. The harbor office is in order; it is always in order. He notes this.
- After the formal inspection, they have coffee at Eira's desk. He says: "I have a civilian matter to mention, while I'm here." He opens a secondary folder. The October 1943 body incident — her report is in the folder; she recognizes the form number. The naval district has been cross-referencing coastal incidents from that autumn. A Swedish merchant vessel, the SS *Ångermanälven*, filed a missing crew member report with the Seamen's Bureau in November 1943: August Schell, German-born naturalized Swedish national, age approximately 40. He went missing somewhere in the run between Portland and Nova Scotia in the second week of October 1943.
- Whitmore: "The timeline matches your incident. We can't confirm without documentation — your body had no papers — but the district thinks it's likely the same man. I'm notifying coastal officers in the zone as a matter of record."
- Eira: "August Schell."
- Whitmore: "Yes. You've heard the name?"
- Eira looks at him for a moment. She says: "I may have come across it in the course of the ordinary mail." This is technically true: Ada Gile mentioned a piece of mail addressed to "A. Schell." It is not a lie. It is the minimum possible truth.
- Whitmore: "If you have anything on record that references the name, pass it to the district office. This is not an active investigation — we're calling it likely the same man and leaving it in the open files. German-born but Swedish national, no indication of anything other than a maritime fatality. No security implication."
- He finishes his coffee. The formal visit ends. He goes back out to the tender.
- Eira sits at her desk for a long time.
- She opens her personal log. She writes: *August Schell. German-born. Swedish national. Crew — SS Ångermanälven. Missing Portland to Nova Scotia, second week of October 1943. Naval district: likely our body. No security concern. Case remaining open.*
- She looks at what she has built, entry by entry, over eight months. The postcard to general delivery. The date and time. Macomber's departure in the second day of the blow. The man at the end of the pier with the canvas bag.
- August Schell. He was on the Ångermanälven and he was German-born, which means he had reasons to have left Germany that she doesn't know and that she probably will never know. He received a postcard from Portland arranging a meeting. The meeting was at Coldwater Reach, the night of October 9. He came. She was there. She walked past him at the end of the pier.
- She does not know what happened after. She knows he was in the water three days later.
- She closes the log. She goes and checks the mooring on the lobster boat at the south dock that has been pulling on its line in the tidal current all week.

---

## Outcome / Turn
The body has a probable name: August Schell. German-born, Swedish national, merchant sailor. Not a spy. Not an agent. A man from Germany who got on a neutral Swedish ship and sailed the North Atlantic in wartime and drowned, apparently, in a Maine harbor. Eira has the name from two sources now. She tells Whitmore the minimum.

## New Question or Pressure Raised
Who sent the postcard from Portland? What was the meeting for? Is Macomber's connection to Schell something she will ever be able to follow? And does she need to?

---

## Continuity Notes
- Commander Whitmore: naval district inspector, not previously named. He appears in Chapter 29 (final contact by telephone to officially close the case). He is not a villain; he is bureaucratic machinery doing its job.
- The SS *Ångermanälven*: Swedish merchant vessel. "Ångermanälven" is the name of a river in northern Sweden — appropriately nautical for a Swedish ship. The vessel ran the Portland-to-Nova Scotia route, which is historically plausible for wartime neutral merchant shipping.
- August Schell: German-born, naturalized Swedish national. Age approximately 40. Missing second week of October 1943. This is consistent with the body arriving October 13 (after the storm).
- "German-born but Swedish national, no indication of anything other than a maritime fatality. No security implication." — This is Whitmore's assessment and the naval district's conclusion. It is the novel's anti-spy-thriller statement made through bureaucratic voice. A German-born Swedish national who drowned in a Maine harbor was not an agent.
- Eira's minimal disclosure: "I may have come across it in the course of the ordinary mail." She is not lying. She is not telling the full story. This is the pattern of her whole arc.
- The "meeting" theory: Eira now has a coherent picture: Schell received a postcard arranging a meeting in Coldwater, came for the meeting, and drowned — before, during, or after. The meeting's purpose remains unknown. Macomber's Portland contract work is the connecting thread she cannot pull.
- Eira checking the lobster boat mooring at the end: a return to work. This is how she closes things — by going back to the dock.

# Chapter 17 — Off Duty

**One-line summary**: Karl earns limited off-duty movement; he and Hannah walk the fence line together and exchange real conversation for the first time.

**POV**: Hannah Pfeiffer  
**Tense**: Third-person limited, past  
**Setting**: Pfeiffer farm: the fence line, the pasture perimeter  
**Time**: July 1944 — a Sunday afternoon

---

## Goal
Hannah wants to understand Karl as something more than a figure at the fence — she wants the conversation that the word-cards have been building toward.

## Obstacle
The fence is still there — literally and in every other sense. Off-duty movement means he can walk a perimeter around the farm; it doesn't mean the ordinary rules have suspended. Lonzo Reed watches from his chair.

---

## Action Beats

- By the summer of 1944, Karl has been assessed for limited off-duty movement under the Geneva Convention's provisions for POW welfare — a bureaucratic process that takes months and produces a form, which Lonzo Reed countersigns with his own name as supervising guard. The practical result: on Sunday afternoons Karl may walk the perimeter of the farm property, within sight of the icehouse or the barn at all times.
- Hannah has known this for two weeks. She has thought about it for two weeks.
- Sunday: the farm is slower. The other POWs play cards inside the icehouse or sit in the July sun on the south side. Karl walks. He goes along the fence line that borders the upper pasture.
- Hannah is hanging laundry on the line near the barn — a real task, she is not manufacturing an occasion. Karl comes by.
- She says: "You want more words?" She holds up an imaginary card. He half-smiles — the most she has seen from him — and says, in careful English: "Maybe." He has a vocabulary now: tools, weather, farm animals, some numbers, a handful of complete sentences. He is not conversational but he is functional.
- They walk, both of them, along the fence line — she on the inside (the farm side), he on the perimeter. The fence between them is agricultural wire and wooden posts; it is about four feet high. The situation is absurd in a way that neither of them names.
- He asks about the sea again — not the way he asked before, but as a continuation of the existing conversation. He says: "You say three miles. You go there?"
- She says: "Yes. Since I was small."
- He says: "What does it look like."
- She thinks about how to describe it in terms that will make sense to someone who has only read about the sea. She says: "Very wide. The color changes. In the summer it is sometimes green, sometimes grey. In the storm it is white at the top of the water." She runs out of simple words. She says, in German: *Es riecht nach Salz.* (It smells of salt.)
- He nods. He says: "In Regensburg, the river smells of mud and of the ships." Then: "Here it is different."
- She says: "Yes." Then: "Your family lives by the river?"
- He says — slowly, choosing the English — "My father. His shop is near the river road. He is a butcher." He pauses. "Was. I think was. I do not know now."
- She understands: the war, the Allied bombing, the uncertainty of letters from a country being dismantled.
- She says: "My brother is in Italy. I don't always know what is true."
- He says: "Yes." *Ich auch.* He has used this phrase with her before; it is still exact.
- Lonzo Reed is visible from the fence line, reading something in his chair. He doesn't look up.
- They walk the rest of the perimeter slowly, without more words. At the far corner, their paths separate naturally — she back to the laundry, he back toward the icehouse. She notices, walking back, that the July light on the pasture has the particular quality of late afternoon, very golden, the kind that makes everything look like it's been placed intentionally. She doesn't think that the light means anything. She thinks that it looks like that.

---

## Outcome / Turn
Karl and Hannah have had a real conversation — incomplete, cross-lingual, conducted along a fence, but real. The connection is no longer mediated entirely by word-cards. Karl's uncertainty about his father, Hannah's about Rolf — they are held in the same hand for a moment.

## New Question or Pressure Raised
Karl has never seen the sea; he said so again. Hannah knows where the harbor is. The question is still not asked and not answered, but it is closer.

---

## Continuity Notes
- Limited off-duty movement: Geneva Convention provisions for POW welfare. Bureaucratically slow; Karl is approved by summer 1944. Lonzo Reed countersigns as supervising guard. This is historically accurate to how the POW program operated.
- Karl's English as of July 1944: functional (tools, weather, animals, numbers, some sentences) but not conversational. Nine months since arrival.
- Karl's father: a butcher in Regensburg, near the river road. This detail recurs in Chapter 28 (Karl's interior chapter). By winter 1944, the father is dead — Allied bombing. The Drafter should not foreshadow this explicitly; it should simply be present as uncertainty: "I do not know now."
- The fence: four-foot agricultural wire and wooden posts. It is always there; the absurdity of conducting friendship through a fence should be present in the narration without being remarked on editorially.
- *Es riecht nach Salz*: Hannah's shift to German when her English runs out is natural to her character. Karl understands.
- *Ich auch*: Karl uses this phrase again. It is his verbal tic in this relationship — the simplest claim of commonality. By this point it carries history.
- Rolf in Italy: still alive as of July 1944. Hannah receives V-Mail irregularly; she speaks about him with honest uncertainty.
- Lonzo not watching: this chapter begins the pattern of Lonzo's deliberate inattention. He is visible; he simply chooses not to see.

# Chapter 18 — What Henry Notices

**One-line summary**: Henry moves through the school after hours, sees the petition in the teacher's lounge waste basket, and tells Marcus enough without telling him everything.

**POV**: Henry Crow  
**Tense**: Third-person limited, past  
**Setting**: Coldwater Reach High School (after hours, evening); Henry and Lillian's house (kitchen)  
**Time**: August 1944

*Exceptional chapter — target 1,200–1,800 words*

---

## Goal
Henry is doing his job: cleaning the building. What he finds is not something he went looking for. His goal, having found it, is to give Marcus what he needs to know without compromising how he came to know it.

## Obstacle
If Henry says too much he implicates himself — his access to rooms and papers is custodial, not investigative, and he has navigated that boundary carefully for nineteen years. If he says too little, Marcus walks into the hearing without knowing the petition exists.

---

## Action Beats

- 6 p.m. The school is empty — or as empty as a building gets; it never fully goes quiet, it just lowers to a murmur of pipes and settling. Henry works the building from the top floor down, the way he always has: it takes him ninety minutes on a weeknight, less if the teachers picked up after themselves (they didn't).
- He goes through the rooms with the particular unseeing efficiency of a man who has worked in this building for nineteen years and knows every room's habits. The music room's piano still open. The history room's map of Europe with a fresh pin in Normandy, and one in Florence (Edna Locke added it last week when the news came). The principal's office dark, door locked, the letter on the desk visible through the glass panel — school board stationery, a name at the top that he recognizes as a board member, first paragraph visible: *the matter of Dr. Klein's continued appointment.*
- He doesn't linger. He goes to the teacher's lounge. Three wastebaskets. The first two: corrected papers, a sandwich wrapper, the usual. The third — the one near the table where the coffee urn sits, where people stand and talk — has a sheet of paper that has been crumpled and then partially smoothed, as if someone thought better of discarding it and then decided to discard it anyway.
- He doesn't open it all the way. He sees: a header (*Petition to the Coldwater Reach School Committee*), and below it three names that he recognizes — parents of students, one of them on the school committee itself. He smooths it enough to read the first sentence: *We, the undersigned, respectfully request a formal review of the employment of Dr. Marcus Klein on the grounds of his foreign national status and the uncertain loyalties attendant thereto.* He puts it back in the basket, crumpled as he found it.
- He finishes the building. He goes home.
- At the kitchen table: Lillian has made supper; Marcus is at the table with his grade book, annotating in Kurrent. Henry sits down. Lillian brings the food without ceremony.
- After supper, when Lillian is in the other room, Henry says: "You have at least one friend on the board. Edna Locke." Marcus looks up. Henry: "And you have organized opposition. Not many people. But they have a draft petition."
- Marcus: "How organized?"
- Henry: "Three names. One of them is on the committee."
- Marcus is quiet for a moment. He says: "Do you want to tell me how you know?"
- Henry: "Not particularly."
- Marcus nods. He closes the grade book. He says: "Thank you, Henry."
- Henry moves a piece of bread around his plate. He says: "You already knew something was coming. Now you know what it looks like."
- Marcus: "Yes."
- Henry: "That's all I've got."
- They do the dishes together without talking.

---

## Outcome / Turn
Marcus knows the petition exists and has at least three names behind it, one of them on the committee. He now knows the shape of the opposition and can brace for it. Henry has done the minimum necessary and not a word more.

## New Question or Pressure Raised
Who are the three names? Will the petition make it to a formal school board vote? What will Marcus do differently now that he knows?

---

## Continuity Notes
- This is Henry Crow's only POV chapter. Canon fact #22: exceptional POV chapters are 1–2, for Henry or Lonzo or Karl. Henry's chapter comes at the story's midpoint (chapter 18 of 30).
- Henry has been at the school for nineteen years (since 1924–1925). He knows every room and habit of the building. This is stated in characters.md.
- Henry's deliberate non-explanation: he doesn't tell Marcus how he found out because doing so would acknowledge his access to rooms and papers in a way that could be used against him. He has navigated this for nineteen years.
- The petition: not fully read; Henry sees the header, three names, and the first sentence. The three names are: unnamed parents of students, plus one school board member. The Drafter may name them if needed for later chapters, but they are background characters.
- The principal's letter through the glass: the content is *the matter of Dr. Klein's continued appointment* — this is setting up the formal hearing in Chapter 24 (November 1944), about three months away.
- Henry and Marcus's friendship: their dialogue here is compressed and efficient — two men who have learned to communicate at the minimum necessary level because both have paid the price of over-explaining. The intimacy is in the efficiency.
- Voice note: Henry's narration is "measured, dry, not given to complaint." He notices racial dynamics with accuracy and without melodrama. His chapter should not explain itself for outside audiences. The building he works in, the people he observes, the things he sees — these are rendered as they are, without the layer of commentary that would signal he is performing his experience for a reader who needs it explained.
- Lillian: present but not speaking in the evening scene. She is in the other room. Her presence is the thermostat of the household — not participating in the specific conversation but providing the condition under which it's possible.

# Chapter 19 — August

**One-line summary**: Marcus takes the bus to Bangor, finds August Schell's name in shipping notices at the public library, and rides home holding the knowledge alone.

**POV**: Marcus Klein  
**Tense**: Third-person limited, past  
**Setting**: The bus Machias–Bangor; Bangor public library; the bus home  
**Time**: August 1944 — a weekday during the summer recess

---

## Goal
Marcus wants to find whatever record exists of the shipping inquiry around the October 1943 body — specifically to see if "A. Schell" from the postcard appears in the maritime record.

## Obstacle
Library records are incomplete; he's not sure what he's looking for precisely or which archive to approach. He is also not sure, the whole bus ride up, why he is doing this — what use the knowledge will be to him, what right he has to pursue it.

---

## Action Beats

- Marcus boards the Machias bus at 7:15 a.m., arrives Bangor at noon. He has his lunch in a brown paper bag from Lillian — a routine she established without being asked, for his long-day trips. The bus is half-empty: a few farming families, a soldier on leave, an old man who sleeps the whole way.
- He thinks, on the bus, about what he is doing. Ada Gile gave him the name "A. Schell" in March. He has sat with it for five months. He doesn't know why this autumn he decided to go find it in a library rather than continue sitting with it. He considers several explanations and finds none of them quite adequate.
- The Bangor public library: a good building, well-stocked. He knows how to work a library; he has worked in far better ones. He goes to the periodicals room and asks for the Bangor Daily News on microfilm, October through November 1943.
- He searches the shipping notices — daily columns of maritime arrivals and departures, vessel names, ports of origin. Portland arrivals, October. He finds the SS *Ångermanälven* in the Portland shipping column: arrived October 7, Portland; departed October 12, Portland, bound Halifax. Swedish merchant vessel.
- He searches the November issues for the missing crew report. He finds it in the seamen's bureau notices, mid-November 1943: *SS Ångermanälven reports crew member missing — August Schell, German national, Swedish citizen, age approximately 40, missing from vessel during passage October 10–12, 1943.*
- He sits with this for a moment.
- He takes a note in his Kurrent script — not a summary, just the key facts: name, vessel, dates, "German national, Swedish citizen."
- He returns the microfilm. He goes to the reading room for an hour and reads a journal article about medieval Bavarian dialects that he has been meaning to read since January. It is exactly as interesting as he expected.
- The bus home: he has the Penobscot River at the window for twenty minutes south of Bangor, and the August light on it is the kind of light that makes you think a country is beautiful, which it is, alongside everything else it is.
- He thinks about August Schell. German national, Swedish citizen. Age approximately 40 in 1943 — which means born approximately 1903, which means he would have been thirty-five when the Anschluss happened, thirty-seven when France fell, thirty-nine when Marcus arrived in Coldwater Reach. The arithmetic of a life displaced.
- A German-born man who became a Swedish citizen and sailed neutral merchant vessels through wartime seas. A postcard in his name, sent from Portland to a small Maine fishing town, with a date and a time. He came for the meeting. He drowned in a storm. Nobody knows what happened on the pier.
- Marcus thinks: he was not a spy. He was not an agent. He was a man who got out of Germany before the war made getting out impossible, and made himself useful as a sailor on a neutral ship, and came to a meeting in Coldwater Reach on the night of October 9, 1943, and died.
- Marcus knows this with the certainty of someone who has thought about it very carefully, not with the certainty of someone who has evidence. He does not confuse the two. He tells no one what he found.

---

## Outcome / Turn
Marcus now knows August Schell's full probable identity: German national, Swedish citizen, crew of the SS *Ångermanälven*. He has arrived at this independently from Eira, who received the same information from Commander Whitmore in July. Both characters now hold the probable name; neither knows the other holds it.

## New Question or Pressure Raised
Why does Marcus say nothing? What would it mean to tell someone — Eira? Henry? The Coast Guard? The chapter does not answer this; Marcus himself cannot fully answer it.

---

## Continuity Notes
- Bangor public library: a real institution. Period-accurate; good periodicals collection.
- The *Bangor Daily News* shipping notices: historically accurate format for Maine coastal papers. Arrival and departure listings appeared regularly in maritime columns.
- The SS *Ångermanälven*: Portland, October 7–12. The missing crew report filed mid-November. This is consistent with the naval inspector's account in Chapter 16.
- August Schell: German national, Swedish citizen. Born approximately 1903. This timeline is consistent with Marcus's arithmetic in the chapter.
- Marcus's Kurrent note: he writes in his natural script when not performing for others. The note is taken in shorthand; he does not transcribe it into anything that could be found.
- Marcus's reasoning about Schell — "not a spy, not an agent, a man who got out of Germany" — is not a conclusion from evidence but a reading of the arithmetic of displacement. He is projecting his own experience partially, and he knows this; the narration should acknowledge the projection without endorsing it as fact.
- "He tells no one what he found": this is the key turn. The question is not why he found it (curiosity, the pull of a name, the professional instinct of a philologist to track a word until it has a referent) but why he keeps it. The chapter should resist a clean answer.
- The medieval Bavarian dialects article: not symbolic. Marcus reads it because he wants to and it is good. This is his version of Eira going to check the mooring after hard news.

# Chapter 20 — Addendum

**One-line summary**: Eira writes a complete account of what she saw on October 9, 1943 — and files it, unforwarded, in Owen's chart drawer.

**POV**: Eira Bowman  
**Tense**: Third-person limited, past  
**Setting**: Harbor office; briefly, the anchorage  
**Time**: September 1944

---

## Goal
Eira wants to make the record honest — not for external purposes but for her own. She cannot continue to work from a log with a known omission in it.

## Obstacle
Making the record honest means making her own failure visible, even if only to herself. The act of writing it down is not costless.

---

## Action Beats

- A slow September morning. The harbor quiet — the summer traffic has thinned; the lobster boats are still going out but the summer coasters have moved to shorter runs. Leaves are beginning on the ridge above town. Eira is at her desk by seven.
- She takes out a sheet of official harbor office stationery. She puts the date at the top — not October 9, 1943, but today's date, September 1944 — and writes: *Addendum to Incident Report, October 13, 1943 (Unidentified Decedent, Breakwater).*
- She writes what she saw on the night of October 9, in the order she saw it:
  - She was on the dock at approximately 0130 hours, checking the *Macomber*'s mooring bridle, which she believed to be at risk of chafing on the bollard in the first gusts of the incoming blow.
  - At the end of the dock, on the public portion of the pier (not the private vessel berths), she observed a man she did not recognize. He was standing still, facing the anchorage. He carried a canvas bag, over-the-shoulder style. He appeared to be in good physical condition, dressed in maritime working clothes.
  - She did not approach the man. She heard a sound from the mooring — she believed it was the bridle under strain — and turned to attend to it.
  - When she completed her inspection of the mooring (approximately five minutes), she looked back to the end of the pier. The man was not visible. She assumed he had taken shelter in the pier shed, which was unlocked.
  - She returned to the harbor office. She did not enter the observation in the vessel traffic log. She did not approach the pier shed to confirm the man's presence.
  - On October 13, 1943, a body was recovered from the far end of the breakwater. She has reason to believe it was the same man.
- She reads the addendum back. It is accurate. She is writing in the same register she uses for all official harbor records: precise, without modifier, no editorializing. *She did not approach the man.* There it is.
- She looks at what she has written for a long time.
- She does not attach it to the official incident report file. She does not address it to the Coast Guard. She folds it in three, the way she folds documents for the pending drawer.
- She opens the chart cabinet. The pending drawer — Owen's drawer — still has his handwriting on the two files at the back: *Lighthouse Service correspondence, 1934* and *Tidal survey, pending, 1935.* She puts the folded addendum in front of them. She closes the drawer.
- She goes outside. The harbor is flat in the September morning. She walks to the end of the dock and looks at the anchorage. Two lobster boats at mooring; the barge at anchor. The buoy tender comes on Friday.
- She goes back inside and makes coffee. This time she drinks it.

---

## Outcome / Turn
