input.content:
# Monday, Wednesday, Friday

The harbor reached him before the harbor was visible.

He had learned this about the walk from his first week in Coldwater Reach: the road from the Crow house to the school ran along the inland side of the ridge, and then at a point roughly eight minutes from the house it crested, and for perhaps half a minute the smell came — brine and diesel exhaust and the cold particular to open water in October — before the road turned back down toward the north end of town and the harbor fell away, replaced by the back of the Congregational church and the bare maples behind it. He had been making this walk for nineteen months. He knew it in the way you know a repeated thing: without novelty, and without quite losing the fact of it.

He had left the house at twenty past seven, in the good suit. Monday, Wednesday, and Friday were the good suit; Tuesday and Thursday were the other one. This was not a rule he had stated; it had arrived as a consequence of his schedule and remained. The good suit was a medium gray worsted, bought in London in the autumn of 1938 — Cheapside, a tailor who measured him in silence and made no remark about his accent — and it was still good, with qualification. The elbows were going. He had been intending to address this for three months.

The Congregational church sat at the corner where the ridge road met the main street. It was never unlocked at the hour he passed it. He had been inside it twice in nineteen months, both times for community gatherings that his position on the faculty made appropriate to attend; on every other occasion he knew it only by its bell, which rang the quarter-hours and which had entered his internal chronometry without his permission. On the stretch of road between the ridge and the school he knew what time it was by the bell rather than the watch in his coat pocket, and this had been true for some time.

The high school occupied what had been a wool processing mill at the north end of the village — three stories of timber-frame and brick, the brick the dark orange-red of nineteenth-century New England industry, the smell inside a compound of chalk dust and old wood and the heat from the basement plant that Henry Crow had been managing for nineteen years. Henry's connection to the building and Marcus's were of entirely different kinds, and they arrived at different hours and by different doors; but they lived in the same house, and the school was the shared fact of their days in the way that the harbor and the farm were shared facts for others in the town.

He arrived before his students and wrote the paradigm on the board in chalk. *Ich habe. Du hast. Er hat.* He had trained himself to print for the blackboard, because his natural hand was the old Viennese Kurrent — letters joined in a form that a stranger would read as encryption — and there was no pedagogical advantage in writing what the students could not read. In his grade book, in the margins of examinations, he still wrote Kurrent: faster, more exact, and his, in the way that certain things remain yours even when you have given most of the rest away.

---

The German class met on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays. There were six students. He had expected this number; in a coastal town in wartime Maine, German was not a subject that recommended itself to practical parents. The six who were there were there for their own reasons, and he had learned to stop inquiring too directly into what those reasons were.

He had formed his opinion of each in the first weeks. There was Curtis Aiken, who had decided that German grammar was a solvable problem and was solving it with the single-mindedness of a man winning an argument with a machine. There were two girls in the front row, attending with consistent thoroughness, whose note-taking was immaculate; Marcus suspected that what interested them was the act of taking notes rather than the subject of the notes, but he did not consider this a defect. There was a boy who came irregularly and translated everything phonetically, with a sincerity that was touching and a method that was wrong. There was a girl in the back row whose last name Marcus had mispronounced twice — she had corrected him the second time with a precision he found professionally admirable — and had not mispronounced since.

And there was Hannah Pfeiffer, who sat in the second row on the left and already knew more German than any of them.

He had established this in the first week. She was not concealing it from him — she was deciding, with each exercise, how much to show, and that was her right. She knew the declensions. She knew the conjugations. When he asked for a translation and the room went quiet with effort, her pencil moved and stopped, and after the pause that indicated she had made a calculation, she gave the answer. Correctly, each time. He noted this in the margin of his grade book in Kurrent — *Sie weiß mehr, als sie zeigt*: she knows more than she shows — and said nothing to her about it directly. She would give him more when she decided to. Students usually did.

On a Friday in the third week of October he picked up the chalk to write the assigned passage from Goethe and wrote instead, without quite intending to:

*Wer, wenn ich schriee, hörte mich denn aus der Engel Ordnungen?*

The opening line of Rilke's first elegy — not on the curriculum, not what he had planned; it had come out of the chalk before the intention had caught up with the hand. He stood back and looked at it. Hannah Pfeiffer looked at the board for a longer time than the others did. He erased it before they had finished copying it and wrote the Goethe.

---

In the teacher's lounge at noon, the body came up.

The lounge was a small room adjacent to the principal's office. Aldous Fenn, the principal — fifty-five years old, twenty-two years in this particular post, a man of brisk administrative fluency who had reduced his job to something manageable without reducing it further — was refilling his coffee cup when he said it: "That body on the breakwater. Coast Guard still hasn't put a name to him." He replaced the pot and turned to the table. "No papers. No identification. Some kind of drifter, most likely."

There were four of them in the room: Fenn, Marcus, Thibodeau the mathematics teacher, and Calloway the home economics teacher, who had been at the school a year longer than Fenn and who Marcus understood to be its institutional memory. The body had been found two weeks before. Marcus had heard of it already, in the manner one heard of things in a town of nine hundred.

Thibodeau said: "Coast Guard say anything further?"

Fenn said: "Just that they were looking into it."

There was a pause. During the pause, Thibodeau and Calloway each looked at Marcus for a fraction of a second, and then looked at the table.

Marcus drank his coffee.

The pause lasted four seconds. Then Fenn said something about the coal delivery and the conversation reorganized itself around that.

He had been calculating this particular reflex — the half-second redirection of gaze — since at least 1936. He had been calculating it long enough that he no longer experienced it as injury. What it was, precisely, was a category error: when a German-seeming thing appeared in proximity to a German-seeming subject, the category operated. That he was Austrian rather than German, and that his relation to Germany's current behavior was rather different from what the category implied, was not information the reflex could accommodate. Reflexes were by definition prior to information.

He noted it, as he noted all such things, with accuracy rather than feeling. He was not offended. He had expended a certain portion of himself on being offended, between 1936 and 1940, and found the expenditure disproportionate to the result.

Walking back to his classroom, he thought: the reflex was a fact. Like the harbor smell at the ridge. You could know where the water was without seeing it.

---

The evenings at the Crow house ran on Lillian's schedule, which was a reasonable schedule. Dinner at six. The radio in the front room after, or reading, or chess with Henry on Thursday evenings if neither of them was needed elsewhere. He had adopted these rhythms by attrition — the steady pressure of nineteen months in the same house — and had stopped noticing that he was following one. Lillian had said once that he was the easiest person she had ever fed. He was not certain whether this was a compliment; it was, at minimum, accurate.

That week — the last of October, the body still recent enough to surface in conversation — he told Henry about the teacher's lounge.

Lillian had made salt fish with potatoes and there was bread, and they ate with the attention that a good plain meal rewards. After a while Henry said, without particular weight: "They find out who he was?" He meant the body.

"Not yet," Marcus said. "The Coast Guard is handling it."

Henry considered the bread. "They will or they won't," he said. "Doesn't change anything for us."

Marcus thought about this. It was a sentence that could have been resignation and was not; what it was instead was more precise — a declarative that came from a position Marcus recognized: the position of a man who has lived long enough inside systems that do not recalibrate for his presence to be surprised any longer by that fact. *They will or they won't.* An accurate forecast of probability. *Doesn't change anything for us* — and by *us*, the specificity was deliberate: this table, these two men, the house around them. Not offered with emphasis. Offered as a fact.

He thought about the word *Duldung*. Toleration: being-permitted-to-remain. One was *geduldet* by a town, by a board, by a room that looked at you for half a second. The grammar was passive; the condition was not chosen. In most of Coldwater Reach he was *geduldet*. In this kitchen he was not any particular category. He was at the table.

"No," he said. "It doesn't."

---

The civil defense warden meeting was on a Thursday evening in the first week of November, in the school basement. The basement had been the mill's boiler pit; Henry had made it habitable over nineteen years of custodial attention without overcoming its essential character as an underground room. The ceiling was low. The walls were old stone. Voices arrived already absorbed, as if the room had made its own prior arrangements with sound.

Two long tables had been pushed together at the center. A ring of men sat around them.

The civil defense warden was a man named Haverstock, who had owned the hardware store before the county emergency acquisition program changed the nature of what he did, and who had found in his wartime administrative role a purpose commensurate with his organizational temperament. He called the meeting to order at half-past seven. Around the table: Haverstock; two selectmen Marcus knew by sight; the fire chief, a quiet man named Gould, who attended these meetings with the patience of someone for whom the agenda held no surprises; two Coast Guardsmen in uniform; and six or seven others — the practical men of a small town who accumulate roles the way any surface accumulates what settles on it, simply by being present long enough.

He was there as the faculty representative for the building. It was his Tuesday fire watch rotation that would be confirmed tonight; he already knew this.

Across the table from him: Eira Bowman.

He knew who she was. He had seen her at the harbor — the figure in the oilskin coat at the pier shed door, the person who walked the breakwater at low water. He had known her name since his second month in Coldwater Reach and had encountered it since in the context of harbor business. She was the only woman at the table. She was not performing this fact; she had a folder of papers in front of her and she was listening to Haverstock with the quality of attention that assesses what is being said against what is already known, registering the difference without announcing it.

The meeting was procedural. Blackout compliance — the winter schedule, adjusted for the earlier nightfall. Fire watch rotation for the coming month. His Tuesday assignment was confirmed; he wrote it in his notebook. Forty-five minutes of the careful management of theoretical emergencies, conducted by men who understood that the theoretical and the practical were not entirely separated by geography. Then Haverstock said that was all, and the men pushed back from the table.

---

He and Eira Bowman reached the corridor door at the same moment.

He held it.

She stepped through without pausing and he followed and they were in the school hallway — chalk dust, old wood, residual warmth from the day's classes. She was moving toward the main entrance at the same pace he was, the pace of a person returning to something, direct, without social adjustment. He did not attempt conversation.

At the main door she stopped and looked at the sky. Clear, and cold; there would be frost before morning. He stopped beside her.

"You're the German teacher," she said. Not a question.

"Austrian, in fact," he said.

She turned slightly and looked at him — not with surprise, with the expression of a person indexing a correction — and then looked at the road. "Same road home," she said. Meaning the direction.

He fell in beside her. They walked without speaking. The silence had the quality of two people who had established, without discussion, that talk requires a reason. Fifty yards along, her road diverged from his and she turned left without a word — not abruptly, not with any social announcement; simply turned, because the road went that way, and she went with it.

He walked on alone.

---

At the desk under the north window of his room, he opened the grade book.

He kept things in order — the papers stacked on the right side of the desk, the pen in its place, the books on the shelf Henry had brought up from the basement storeroom. A man who has moved eight times in five years learns to keep things in order, because the order will need to survive the next move, and the one after.

He opened to the German class. His annotations in the margins were in Kurrent — *GU* for *gut*, *S.* for *Schwäche*, weakness, and occasional longer notes where the nature of the weakness rewarded recording. Beside Hannah Pfeiffer's name, in the margin of her last examination, he had written: *Sie weiß mehr, als sie zeigt.*

He had meant it as an observation of her method, which was a teacher's legitimate concern. Looking at it now in the script that no one else in Coldwater Reach could read, he thought that it might also be said of several people in this town — the harbormaster who had turned left without farewell; the men who had looked at him for a fraction of a second and looked at the table; the town itself, which had permitted him to be present for nineteen months without quite closing around him.

He thought about the word *unheimlich*.

It had no precise English equivalent — he had looked, professionally, and found only approximations. *Uncanny* came closest but carried a note of the supernatural that the German did not require. The literal structure of the word was plainer: *un-heimlich*, the negation of *heimlich*, which was itself double. *Heimlich* meant familiar, intimate, belonging to the house — and also, by extension, hidden, secret, kept from outside view. To be *heimlich* in a place was to be absorbed by it, to have grown so native to it that the place no longer showed you from the outside. *Unheimlich* was the reversal: the sensation of being in a place where you were exposed precisely because you had not grown familiar enough to disappear into it.

He had not been *heimisch* anywhere since 1937. He was not, in noting this, doing anything he would have called self-pity; he was being accurate, which was a different exercise, and one he had considerably more practice with.

Outside the north window, the night was clearing toward frost. The harbor smell came over the ridge in the wind — faint, cold, exact.

He put the cap on his pen and left the grade book open on the desk.

The chapter establishes Marcus's routine in Coldwater Reach: the daily walk from the Crow house to Coldwater Reach High School (good suit Monday/Wednesday/Friday; older suit Tuesday/Thursday), the German class of six students (including Hannah Pfeiffer, who knows more than she reveals), the teacher's lounge at noon where Principal Aldous Fenn mentions the unidentified body on the breakwater and Thibodeau and Calloway briefly direct their gaze at Marcus before looking back at the table — a reflex Marcus has been calculating since 1936. Marcus thinks about Duldung — being-permitted-to-remain — and the distinction between being tolerated by a town and being at the table in this kitchen. The civil defense warden meeting in the school basement, first week of November: Marcus as building representative, Haverstock running the meeting, Marcus and Eira Bowman across the table from each other. Walking out: he holds the door; she says 'You're the German teacher'; he says 'Austrian, in fact'; she says 'Same road home'; they walk fifty yards and she forks left without farewell. Back in his room he opens the grade book with its Kurrent annotations, reads his own margin note about Hannah Pfeiffer ('Sie weiß mehr, als sie zeigt'), and thinks about the word unheimlich — the sensation of being exposed in a place because you have not grown native enough to disappear into it. He has not been heimisch anywhere since 1937. Thread 2 (The Man Still Moving) enters. Thread 3 (The Hearing) enters.

The harbor smell reaches the road at a ridge point approximately eight minutes from the Crow house, as the road crests the inland ridge before descending to the north end of town. This is the only point on the walk where the harbor is perceptible but not visible.

Marcus's grade book annotations are in Kurrent script. His notation shorthand: GU for gut (good); S. for Schwäche (weakness). Beside Hannah Pfeiffer's name, in the margin of her most recent examination, he has written in Kurrent: 'Sie weiß mehr, als sie zeigt' (She knows more than she shows).

Thibodeau is the mathematics teacher at Coldwater Reach High School. Miss Calloway is the home economics teacher; she has been at the school twenty-three years, one year longer than Fenn — approximately since 1920. These two are present in the teacher's lounge when Fenn mentions the body on the breakwater in late October 1943.

During the teacher's lounge conversation about the body (late October 1943), Thibodeau and Calloway each direct their gaze toward Marcus Klein for a fraction of a second before looking back at the table. Marcus has been calculating this reflex — the half-second look when a German-seeming subject arises near a German-seeming person — since at least 1936. He registers it with accuracy, not injury.

Marcus Klein thinks the word Duldung (German: toleration; being-permitted-to-remain; passive grammar — one is geduldet by someone) to describe his position in most of Coldwater Reach. He distinguishes this sharply from his position at the Crow household table, where he is not any particular category.

Marcus Klein has not been heimisch (at home in a way requiring no effort or calculation; fully absorbed into a place so that the place no longer exposes you from the outside) anywhere since 1937. He defines this with precision, not self-pity.

Marcus defines unheimlich as the reversal of heimlich's double meaning: heimlich means both 'familiar/belonging to the house' and 'hidden/secret.' Unheimlich is therefore both 'not at home' and 'not hidden' — the sensation of being exposed in a place because one has not grown familiar enough to disappear into it. Marcus applies it to his condition in Coldwater Reach as of autumn 1943.

The civil defense warden of Coldwater Reach is a man named Haverstock, formerly the hardware store owner before the county emergency acquisition program. He runs meetings with organizational precision.
The fire chief of Coldwater Reach is a man named Gould. Quiet; attends civil defense warden meetings regularly.
Marcus Klein holds the Tuesday fire watch rotation in the Coldwater Reach civil defense scheme.

The civil defense warden meetings are held in the school basement — the former mill boiler pit, made habitable by Henry Crow's nineteen years of custodial work. Low ceiling, old stone walls, voice-absorbing acoustics. Two long tables pushed together in the center.

Marcus Klein and Eira Bowman meet for the first time at the civil defense warden meeting in the first week of November 1943. They are seated across the table from each other. Leaving the meeting, they walk fifty yards together on the same road home; she turns left at the fork without a further word. Their exchange: she says 'You're the German teacher' (not a question); he says 'Austrian, in fact'; she says 'Same road home.'

Home economics teacher at Coldwater Reach High School. Has been at the school approximately twenty-three years — one year longer than Principal Fenn. The school's institutional memory. Present in the teacher's lounge reflex scene of ch03.

Civil defense warden of Coldwater Reach. Former hardware store owner before the county emergency acquisition program changed the nature of his business. Runs the monthly civil defense warden meetings from the school basement with organizational precision. First appears ch03.
Fire chief of Coldwater Reach. A quiet man who attends civil defense warden meetings with the patience of someone for whom the agenda holds no surprises. First appears ch03.
Student in Marcus Klein's German class at Coldwater Reach High School. Treats German grammar as a solvable puzzle; methodical and single-minded in his approach. First appears ch03.

Marcus Klein's better suit. Medium gray worsted, purchased in London (Cheapside) in autumn 1938 from a tailor who measured him in silence. Worn Monday, Wednesday, and Friday — the same days as the German class. As of autumn 1943 the elbows are beginning to go. The older suit goes on Tuesday and Thursday.

German word meaning uncanny, un-homely. In Marcus Klein's usage: the sensation of being exposed in a place because one has not grown familiar enough to disappear into it — the reversal of heimlich's double meaning (familiar/belonging-to-the-house; also: hidden/secret). Marcus thinks this word at the end of ch03 to describe his condition in Coldwater Reach. Introduced ch03.

German adjective: at home in a place in a way that requires no effort, no calculation, no attention. To be heimisch is to be absorbed into a place so that it no longer shows you from the outside. Marcus Klein has not been heimisch anywhere since 1937. Distinguished from merely residing somewhere or being tolerated (geduldet). Introduced ch03.

German noun: toleration; being-permitted-to-remain. Passive grammar — one is geduldet by someone who decides to permit. Marcus Klein uses this word to describe his position in most of Coldwater Reach, contrasting it with his unclassified position at the Crow household table. Introduced ch03.

Small room adjacent to Principal Fenn's office at Coldwater Reach High School. Site of the noon reflex scene in ch03 when Fenn mentions the breakwater body and Thibodeau and Calloway briefly redirect their gaze toward Marcus Klein.

The basement of Coldwater Reach High School, formerly the mill's boiler pit, made habitable by Henry Crow over nineteen years. Low ceiling, old stone walls, voice-absorbing acoustics. Two long tables pushed together serve as the civil defense warden meeting space. First appears ch03.

input.content:
# The Coast Guard

The northeast had been building since before light, and by seven the inner anchorage was throwing a chop that put white on the mooring lines. Eira was at the desk with the mooring register, working through the fall assignments against what she could see from the south window — the two lobster smacks at their positions, the anchorage otherwise empty now that Birch Macomber had taken the *Macomber* out. The register confirmed what the window showed. She noted the wind direction in the margin and did not write a number yet; the weather station at Machias would have a proper reading by eight, and she did not guess at what she could wait for.

She had made coffee at six, four measures of grounds in the tin percolator, and it was gone by the time she heard the engine.

Not the Machias pattern — bigger, and the rhythm was different, steadier, the sound of a boat running to a schedule rather than to fish. She went to the window. It came around the point past the breakwater — First District, the white hull carrying an unhurried pace, no running signal. Whatever its errand, it was not urgent.

She went back to the desk, straightened the mooring register, and did not put it away. She took the log from the drawer. She found October 9 and left the log open on the desk.

The surfboat tied up at the commercial float below the pier ramp. She watched through the south window as one man came up. Young — she placed his age before his rank, mid-twenties at the most, and then the rank after: a lieutenant's bar on the collar of the peacoat. He carried a folder under his arm and walked the way people walked when they had been taught to walk toward a purpose — not hurried, not looking around.

She turned from the window and sat down.

He came down the pier and knocked with one knuckle at the door, which was not quite a knock but the announcement of arrival.

"Come in," she said.

---

He was perhaps twenty-six. A straight fair face, the deliberate manner of a man who had decided early that deliberateness was an asset. He looked at the harbor office the way new people looked at it — quickly, ceiling to floor, taking inventory — then looked at her.

"Mrs. Bowman. Lieutenant Pruitt, First District." He produced the folder from under his arm and sat down across the desk before she indicated the chair, not rudely; he was accustomed to sitting across desks. "You spoke with Petty Officer Gervais on October thirteenth."

"I did."

He opened the folder on his knee. She could see the top sheet at an angle, upside down, typed at the top and handwritten below: her incident report on the left half, and on the right what appeared to be Gervais's field notes in the compact shorthand petty officers learned. Below these, further sheets she could not read clearly — but the top line of the third page resolved, at this angle, into a typewritten summary: *Adult male, drowning, approximate age 38–48, no identifying documents, no identifying marks.*

She had known the age. She had known the absence of documents; she had checked the pockets herself. The last phrase was new: no identifying marks. Nothing on the skin. Nothing that would carry back to a record, a service file, a tattoo registry. She made a note of this, mentally, and said nothing.

"I want to review the vessel traffic for the night of October ninth," Pruitt said. He had a small notebook out, pen uncapped. "Between twenty-two hundred and oh-four hundred. Your log covers that period."

"It does." She turned the log toward him slightly — enough that he could see the entries, not enough that he could read the whole page easily. "Lobster smacks *Thistle* and *Doris V.* at mooring. Those are Coldwater boats, Roy Blaine and Delmar Stannard. Coaster *Macomber*, Birch Macomber master, Machias — arrived October eighth, departure logged October eleventh. Lighthouse Service ice barge *Ploughman* at anchor, inner anchorage, during that period."

He wrote. His print was precise, the kind kept legible as a matter of policy.

"The *Macomber*." He looked up. "A coaster — what was her cargo?"

"Bagged lime. Machias to Lubec, calling here on the way."

"She was in harbor through the storm."

"She came in on the eighth with weather moving. Macomber is a careful man. He waited on it."

Pruitt wrote.

"Any vessel you observed and did not log?"

She had known this question was coming. Not because he had telegraphed it — he had not — but because it was the next question, the one the inquiry was building toward. Traffic on the water accounted for, and then: what about the water itself?

"No," she said.

He looked up from the notebook.

"Nothing at anchor or underway you didn't account for."

"No."

He wrote.

"During that period — the evening of the ninth through the morning of the tenth — did you observe anything unusual on the pier or dock?"

She looked at the open log on the desk. She did not need to look at it; she could have recited what was there from any room in the building. She looked at it because looking at something while you answered a question was not suspicious. It was what a careful person did.

"I was out at approximately oh-one-thirty," she said, "checking the *Macomber*'s mooring bridle. I'd noted a chafe point at the thimble two days before, and the wind was building. I wanted to verify the set of the bridle on the bollard before the blow came on full."

"And at that time?"

"Nothing unusual. The bridle was holding."

Pruitt wrote for several seconds. She waited.

"That's the full extent of your activity during that period."

"I was in and out during the storm, checking moorings. But nothing significant to report."

He wrote. She watched his pen complete the line, pause, and then move to underline something. Whatever it was, he did not share it.

He closed the folder on his knee. "I should tell you where we are with it," he said, which was a courtesy — he did not have to tell her anything. "Cause of death is drowning. The medical findings don't support foul play. No evidence of injury prior to submersion." He paused. "We haven't identified him."

"No."

"The case is open, but it's not — actively worked, at present." He stood, tucking the folder back under his arm. "If we develop anything further, you'll hear from us. If anything comes to your attention on your end, I'd ask you to contact the Machias station."

"I'll have the log available," she said.

He nodded — the nod of a man who had recorded what was available and found it in order — and went out.

---

She heard the surfboat's engine turn over.

Then she went to the window. The boat had backed off the float and swung its bow toward the harbor mouth. She watched it pass the end of the pier — Pruitt visible at the stern, not looking back at the dock — and round the breakwater. The fog sat low on the water two miles out to the southeast; she could see the horizon where the fog began, the grey wall of it, and the boat going into it. She watched until the shape of the hull was gone and then until the engine was gone.

Then she turned back to the log.

---

She had opened it to October 9 when she first heard the surfboat. She turned forward now, one page, to October 11.

The entry was in her hand. *Macomber — departure 0430.*

She knew her own handwriting in this log the way she knew the angle of the tide gauge in February, or the weight of the padlock on the pier shed door, or the particular resistance of the chart cabinet's second drawer. It was hers. The *M* of *Macomber* with its particular lean, the zero in *0430* closed at the bottom in the way she had always closed zeros — she had tried once to change this habit and not succeeded. No question of whose hand had made the entry.

She had no memory of making it.

October 11 was the second day of the blow. She had been in and out of the harbor office across the two days of the storm — checking moorings every two hours when the weather allowed, logging conditions when she came in, running on whatever the body would give her. On the storm's first night she had been out at midnight and again in the dark hours; in the morning of the eleventh she had come in from the second night soaked through Owen's oilskin, the cold in her hands clear to the wrist. She would have sat at this desk and made entries. She could account for that.

She could not account for 0430 specifically. 0430 on October 11 was before dawn, before she had come in from the second night, a time when she was in the weather and not at the desk.

The harbor had no formal overnight watchman. It had not had one since 1941, when the last man the selectmen employed for the position enlisted. But the pier shed was unlocked at night, and it was not unheard of for fishermen to leave a note — Blaine did it when his boat came in odd hours; Stannard occasionally. Word left on the hook beside the door: *Doris V. in at 0320, secured.* She would find it in the morning and enter it in the log at whatever hour she came in, logging the event with the fisherman's time rather than her own. This was common practice; the log did not require her to have witnessed every entry she made, only to have verified it.

She could have found a note at 0430. She could have come in before the dawn she remembered coming in, made the entry from a note, and gone back out. She had been on six hours in and two hours out for two days; there were portions of those days she could not now reconstruct cleanly.

She looked at the entry for a long time.

Then she closed the log and went to the chart cabinet.

---

The bolted drawer was the bottom one on the left side. The key on her ring was the small brass one, the only copy cut for that lock since Owen had it duplicated in Machias in 1932; she had used the original copy until it wore thin at the bow, then retired it to the pending drawer and switched to the duplicate. She unlocked the bottom drawer and found the *Macomber* file in its envelope, third from the front under *M*, where it belonged.

The documentation was standard: registration certificate, load line certificate, marine survey from Machias. Registered at Machias in 1937. She read it without needing to read it — she had pulled this file before, in the spring of 1940, when Macomber first put in at Coldwater Reach and she verified every vessel she did not already know. The registration confirmed what she already had: a ninety-foot coaster, lime and general cargo, operating out of Machias up and down the downeast coast. A working boat, in working order, making ordinary runs.

She knew Birch Macomber by sight, not by much more than that. She had seen him crossing the dock in rubber boots and a canvas work coat, the cap pulled low; she had spoken with him twice in four years, once about the set of his mooring and once when he asked about the harbor fee schedule. A man in his late fifties, spare frame, economical in movement. She had formed the impression — from the way he handled the mooring question, from the fee conversation — of a man who was careful with money in the way of men who had learned caution from close margins. Not a man you would describe as reckless. Not a man who would put out into a second day of a nor'easter without reason.

The *Macomber* had departed at 0430 on October 11. October 11 was the second day of the blow. The blow had run through the night and into the morning; by 0430 it was not finished.

A coaster does not put out on the second day of a nor'easter without reason.

She replaced the paper envelope in its position and pushed the drawer shut until the bolt engaged. She tested the lock once, which was habit, and put the key back on her ring.

---

She took Owen's oilskin from the wall peg and put it on. The oilskin had settled to her shoulders over eight years of use, the collar the right height, the sleeves the right length now that she had rolled them inside to take up what Owen's longer arms had required. She did not think about any of this; it was the coat she put on to go outside, and she put it on and went outside.

The wind on the pier was twenty knots, maybe a little over, out of the northeast and carrying spray off the anchorage. *Thistle* and *Doris V.* lay at their moorings in the positions she had noted in the register — pulling against their bridles with the indifference of things properly secured, the lines taut and the boats riding the chop without strain. The anchorage beyond was empty. She stood at the pier head for a moment and looked at the breakwater and then at the water south of the breakwater and then at the fog on the horizon.

There was nothing there that needed looking at.

She went back into the harbor office, rinsed the percolator in the handbasin, and refilled it with fresh grounds — four measures, the amount the pot took. She set it on the burner and sat back down at the desk, opened the mooring register to the page she had left it on, and took up the fall assignments where she had stopped.

Outside, the northeast held steady. She noted the time, noted the wind, and went on working.

input.summary: Early November 1943, Eira POV. A First District Coast Guard motor surfboat ties up at the commercial float and Lieutenant Pruitt — mid-twenties, deliberate manner, thin folder under his arm — comes to the harbor office. His file includes her incident report, Gervais's field notes, and a pathologist's preliminary she reads upside-down from across the desk: adult male, drowning, approximate age 38–48, no identifying documents, no identifying marks. He asks about vessel traffic on the night of October 9: she gives him the log — lobster smacks Thistle and Doris V. at mooring, coaster Macomber (Birch Macomber, Machias), ice barge Ploughman at anchor. Did she observe any vessel she did not log? No. Did she observe anything unusual on the pier or dock? She says she was out at approximately 0130 checking the Macomber's mooring bridle — first gusts running, concerned about the set on the bollard — nothing unusual. She does not say: there was a man at the end of the pier with a canvas bag. Pruitt delivers the official position — drowning, no foul play, open but not actively worked — and leaves. Eira watches the surfboat until the fog takes it. She turns to October 11 in the log: Macomber — departure 0430 — her own handwriting, which she cannot reconstruct. She accounts for the possibility (no overnight watchman; fishermen sometimes leave notes; two days of blow, portions unrecoverable). She closes the log and goes to the bolted drawer in the chart cabinet, checks the Macomber documentation: registered Machias, 1937. She knows Birch Macomber — late fifties, spare, careful with money, reliable. A coaster does not put out on the second day of a nor'easter without reason. She locks the drawer and goes back to work. Thread 1 (The Blank in the Log) advanced: first deliberate withholding complete.
newFacts: Lt. Pruitt is a Coast Guard First District (Portland) lieutenant, approximately mid-twenties, fair-faced, deliberate manner — went to school for the job. He is not a recurring major character but is named for consistency.

The pathologist's preliminary (visible to Eira from across the desk, upside-down) reads: Adult male, drowning, approximate age 38–48, no identifying documents, no identifying marks. The 'no identifying marks' is new information — nothing on the skin.

Vessels in the harbor log for the October 9 night: lobster smack Thistle (Roy Blaine, Coldwater Reach), lobster smack Doris V. (Delmar Stannard, Coldwater Reach), coaster Macomber (Birch Macomber, Machias, arrived October 8, departed October 11), Lighthouse Service ice barge Ploughman at anchor (inner anchorage).
Delmar Stannard: Coldwater Reach lobsterman, master of the smack Doris V.
Macomber (vessel): registered Machias, 1937. Marine survey from Machias.

Birch Macomber: late fifties, spare frame, economical in movement, careful with money — impression formed from two dock conversations over four years. Reliable; not a man to put out in bad weather without reason.

The October 11 departure entry — Macomber, 0430 — is in Eira's handwriting. She does not remember making it. Two possible explanations she can reconstruct: she made it herself in the exhaustion of the storm's second day at 0430, or she entered it from a note left by a fisherman (the harbor has no overnight watchman; Blaine and Stannard sometimes leave notes on the hook beside the pier shed door). She cannot now reconstruct which.

The bolted drawer is the bottom-left drawer of the chart cabinet. Vessel registration files inside, alphabetical by vessel name. The key is a small brass key on Eira's ring — the only copy, originally cut for that lock when Owen had it duplicated in Machias in 1932. Eira switched to the duplicate after the original wore thin at the bow.

Eira tells Pruitt she was out at approximately 0130 on the morning of October 10 checking the Macomber's mooring bridle — first gusts running, concerned about the set on the bollard. This refers to a second trip out (the blow coming on full) — not the first trip at 0015 when she saw the man. The first trip and the man are what she omits.
The Coast Guard official position on the case as of early November 1943: cause of death is drowning, no evidence of foul play, case open but not actively worked.
looseThreads: Thread 1 (The Blank in the Log): Eira has now completed her first deliberate withholding — she answered the precise question asked and not the question she should have asked herself. The omission is the man at the end of the pier, October 9–10, between 0015 and 0030. The departure entry (Macomber, October 11, 0430) is now a second irregularity in her attention.

The Macomber's departure on the second day of the blow: Eira has filed this as a fact that doesn't fit the pattern — a reliable careful man putting out in bad weather. She has not shared this with anyone and has not entered it in the official log. It will resurface in ch10.
The 'no identifying marks' on the pathologist's preliminary: this closes off one avenue of identification. The body has no tattoos, scars, or marks that would connect to a service record or registry.

Who left the 0430 note (if it was a note)? Or did Eira make the entry herself and lose the memory to exhaustion? This question is not answered and is not meant to be — its point is the gap in her reconstruction.
Delmar Stannard is a working lobsterman in Coldwater Reach; his vessel Doris V. is at mooring during the storm. He has not appeared in any scene yet and his name has only appeared in the harbor log.

## Chapter 04 — "The Coast Guard"

**Word count**: ~2,750 words  
**POV**: Eira Bowman  
**Thread advanced**: Thread 1 — The Blank in the Log (first deliberate withholding complete)

### What the chapter does

The chapter opens in the harbor office on a steel-grey early November morning, northeast wind putting white on the mooring lines. Lieutenant Pruitt arrives by First District motor surfboat and conducts a methodical follow-up inquiry. Eira gives him the vessel log precisely and correctly — *Thistle*, *Doris V.*, *Macomber*, *Ploughman* — then answers "no" to the unlisted-vessel question (accurate: she saw no unlisted vessel) and describes checking the mooring bridle at "approximately 0130" without mentioning the 0015 trip when she saw the man. Pruitt delivers the official verdict — drowning, no foul play, open but idle — and departs.

She watches the surfboat until the fog takes it. Then she reads the October 11 entry — *Macomber — departure 0430* — her own hand, no memory attached. She works through the possible explanations (exhaustion, a fisherman's note, the informal system of the pier shed hook) without resolving them. She goes to the bolted drawer, pulls the Macomber documentation, confirms what she already knew: Birch Macomber, Machias, 1937, reliable. And notes the thing she cannot explain away: a careful man doesn't put out in a second-day nor'easter without reason.

She locks the drawer, puts on Owen's oilskin, goes to the pier head, looks at the anchorage, comes back in. Refills the percolator. Returns to the mooring register. The record stays as it is.

input.content:
# Feeding Time

The cows knew her by her boots. The sound she made crossing the barn floor in the dark — the flat drag of rubber soles on frozen board — distinguished her from her father, who moved in wooden-soled clogs that knocked against the concrete lip of the gutter; distinguished her from the barn itself, settling in the cold. She didn't need a lamp for the five o'clock feeding. Her hands knew the latch, the hook, the weight of the pail before her eyes adjusted.

The smell in November was particular. In summer the barn aired through the big east doors; in November it concentrated: hay, the animal heat of twenty dairy cows packed close, the sharp undertone of manure from the previous evening's cleanup. Not unpleasant — she had grown up with it the way you grew up with the smell of your own house, not smelling it anymore except when she tried to smell it deliberately. Her mother still had a faint bewilderment on her face when someone from town wrinkled their nose at the dooryard. After twenty years you would think she would have stopped being surprised.

Five o'clock. She worked the alley from south to north. Else was always restless in the cold and needed the bucket first; she was liable to start in on the next cow's feed if left to wait, and the next cow was Hilde, who would not stand for it. Then down the line to Berta, who was patient the way some animals were patient, who turned her head to look at nothing in particular when Hannah came with the pail. She put her hand flat on Berta's flank as she went by. The cow breathed — a sound like a slow bellows, warm and even.

She was thinking: three weeks, almost four.

Three weeks since the truck came in the afternoon with the four men in the back. The mornings had a new shape to them now — not in the tasks themselves, which were the same tasks they had been since she was tall enough to carry a pail, but in the fact of something she was tracking at the edge of her attention. The sound of the icehouse door some mornings before light. The Army truck at seven when the sky had color but no light yet. She had not been sleeping as solidly as she usually slept, and she put that down to November.

She had been watching.

---

The truck came at seven, when the frost on the gatepost caught what light there was. The three older men went north to the pasture fence; she could see them go from the south side of the barn, three figures past the equipment shed with the guard walking wide of the group. They had been at the fence for three weeks now. The work was good, her father had said, once, at dinner — the only favorable thing she had heard him say about any of it.

Karl got the yard.

Manure hauling: the wheelbarrow, the pile at the far end of the working yard, back and forth. She had known from the first week that this was how it worked. The worst job went to the youngest. On farms and presumably everywhere else.

Between the third and fourth boards of the south barn wall there was a gap that she had found in those first days, narrow enough to keep out most of the cold and wide enough to see the working yard clearly. It was lower than the east wall gap, shoulder height when she stood, and it looked out onto the full south run of the yard: the gate in the fence that divided the barnyard from the working field, the icehouse at the near edge of her sight, Lonzo Reed in his folding chair. She had not told herself she was looking for a way to watch him. She had just found the gap and found herself using it.

He moved without complaint. This was the thing she had settled on early, as a fact she could rely on: he did not complain, or at least he gave nothing to the yard that looked like complaint. He navigated the frozen rut near the barn door at the same angle each time — she had counted it, five mornings and six and eight — the angle that kept the wheel from catching in the ice that had formed over the rut's lowest point. He had worked it out in the first two days and kept to it without varying. The approach, tested, arrived at, maintained.

What she noticed in the second week was that the seams of his jacket had been let out. The cloth ran smooth across his shoulders now — before it had pulled and bunched when he lifted anything — and the sleeves came to his wrists instead of riding up. She had not seen her mother take the jacket; she had not been present for any of it. But the stitch was Magda's. The same straight-line stitch on every garment in this house that had needed mending in the last twenty years: the trousers of Rolf's that she had worn through at the knee two winters ago, her father's work shirts, the curtain at the kitchen window that had torn at the rod pocket in the spring. Magda's stitch: regular, close, functional, no backstitch at the ends because she said backstitch wasted thread and made a ridge. Her mother had done the work without being asked and said nothing about it after. Hannah did not say anything about it either. These were the things her mother did when she had run out of other ways to do something.

Lonzo Reed sat in his folding chair beside the icehouse door. His bad knee kept him from walking circuits the way a younger man might, and so he sat and the yard was in his sight and most mornings he had something to read — a newspaper folded to a quarter of its size, or one of the small pamphlets the Coastal Patrol issued to its people. Some mornings he just sat. He did not seem like a man who minded sitting.

---

The gate in the south fence was for equipment: wide enough for the tractor when the tractor ran, which was less often since the gas ration. She used it every morning to carry the grain bucket to the calf pen on the other side. The bucket was metal, with a metal bail, and the bail was cold through her work gloves in November — the particular cold that came through metal in this weather, which felt almost deliberate, as if the temperature had a specific grievance with her palms.

On this morning it was cold enough that her breath sat in front of her face for a second before dispersing. The puddle beside the gate post had a serious quarter-inch of ice on it: not the overnight skim but a sheet that had been accumulating for several days, gray and bubbled in the center where the water had been deep, clear-edged where it had been shallow. She stood at the gate with the bucket in her right hand and looked at the ice.

She was tired from the milking. The bucket was heavy. The calf pen was thirty feet away, on the other side, and she stood at the gate and did not open it and looked at the ice on the puddle instead.

She set the bucket down on her side of the gate post.

Karl came around the corner of the barn with the wheelbarrow.

She had not planned this. He was just there, coming around the corner in his let-out jacket with his arms braced against the handles of the loaded barrow, navigating the frozen rut at the angle he had settled on in the second day. He saw her standing at the gate with the bucket at her feet and he stopped.

He set the wheelbarrow handles down. He came to the fence — to the post on his side — and reached over and picked up the bucket by the bail. He carried it to the gate post and set it at the top of the rail, where she could reach it from her side. He did not push it through the gate or hand it to her over the fence. He placed it where she could take it, at the post, and stepped back.

The right way to do it. She noticed that it was the right way to do it, and she noticed that she had noticed, and she did not know what to do with someone who understood that.

He said: *Da, bitte.*

She said: *Danke.*

The word came out before she had decided to say it, the way a word came out when you had owned it since before you were old enough to make decisions about words. She heard herself say it and then there was a silence in which Karl was looking at her with an expression she had not seen on him yet — something going still in his face, recalibrating. The German coming from this girl with the pails. He had not known she had it.

She reached over and took the bucket handle.

She said — because she had been carrying this sentence for three weeks, since the night she stood at Rolf's east window looking at the dark icehouse, since the night she lay in Rolf's bed with the geography book open to the map of Bavaria, her finger traveling the distance between two dots on a river —

*Mein Vater kommt aus Bayern.*

She said it haltingly. With the grammar her father used, which was the only grammar she had for German — the grammar of a language that had reached her secondhand, through someone else's mouth. She was not certain about the verb even as she said it. She heard the faults in it the way you heard the faults in something that had seemed correct in rehearsal.

Karl stood on his side of the fence with his hands back on the wheelbarrow handles. He looked at her.

He said: *Ich auch.*

Then he picked up the barrow and went back across the yard.

---

Lonzo Reed was ten yards away. He was bent over his left boot, working at the lacing with one finger, trying to get the tongue settled right beneath the top eyelet. He straightened and resettled the rifle across his knees and looked at the icehouse. He had not been watching.

Hannah carried the bucket to the calf pen and fed the calves and came back and did what came next, in order, thoroughly. She did not drop anything.

She was thinking about *auch*.

It was the most ordinary word — it appeared in sentences the way punctuation appeared, so commonly you stopped registering it separately. *Das auch*, this too. *Noch einmal, und auch das*, once more, and that as well. Her father used it constantly in the German he spoke at home, the auditing word, the word for checking things off a running total. But Karl had said it alone. *Ich auch.* Two words. A complete sentence. The most compressed possible way of saying: I am in that category also. Without naming the category. Without claiming anything about what the category contained.

She had named the category: *father from Bavaria*.

Her father was from Straubing. She had always known this the way you knew facts from before your own life — as a thing that had happened somewhere else, to someone you had not yet met. She had looked it up years ago in the geography book: Straubing on the Danube, a small dot in the curve of the river. She had run her finger south to Regensburg and north again. Not very far. Two cities on the same river, or near the same river — she was not precise about the river's exact route, only that her father had once said the Danube went past his door, or close enough to past his door, and she had filed this as a fact about a place she had never seen.

Karl was from the same river.

Her father had said it in the second week of the POWs' arrival, at dinner when the lights were low and he was finishing his beer: *Regensburg ist nicht weit.* Regensburg is not far. Said to the air, or to the beer, or to no one at all — not as a statement directed at anyone at the table, just a fact he appeared to be noting to himself. Hannah had heard it. Her mother had probably heard it. Neither of them had said anything. Her father had drunk the rest of the beer and gone to check the cows.

*Ich auch.* He had placed himself beside her father with two words, the gentlest possible grammar for it — not *I am from Bavaria* with the full declaration, just *also me*, the conjunction doing all the work.

She thought about her father's voice when he spoke German at home and the German was from somewhere specific, not the everyday German of instructions but the German that came from further back. There was a register to it she had been listening for since she was small — a loosening of something, a running-together of syllables that didn't run together in the everyday version. She couldn't have said what words. Only that the language her father used for the farm and the house was not entirely the same language he used when he was speaking from a place she hadn't been. Karl was fifty kilometers from that place. He had his own version of it, whatever it was.

She thought: and neither of them will tell me anything about it.

She thought: *auch*. Six words across a fence this morning. Which was six more than the house had managed in a month.

---

At noon her father came in from the north pasture. The heavy tread on the floorboard, the cold off his coat as he passed. He was up since four; she knew this not because she had seen him but because the creak of the bedroom floor at four was part of the morning the same way Else's impatience was part of the morning, the same way the cold bucket bail was.

He had mud on his knees and his face was the face of a man who had been outside for six hours in cold weather: closed down, used up in the specific way of outdoor work that left the body functional and quiet. He served himself from the pot without sitting, ate at the counter standing up, which he had been doing since Rolf left and which Hannah understood now had nothing to do with hurry, and rinsed the bowl in the washbasin, and went back out.

He did not say anything.

Her mother was at the stove. The kitchen smelled the way it had smelled every day since October: long-simmered broth with root vegetables, parsnip and carrot and something beneath it, the last ghost of the ham bone that had been gone for weeks. A patient smell. A smell that asked nothing.

Her mother stirred.

No one said anything.

Hannah sat at the table with her bowl and her cup of tea and the warmth coming back slowly into her hands from the morning's cold. She looked at her mother's back, the motion of her arm, the wooden spoon making its circles. She looked at the empty place at the counter where her father had been standing.

She thought: *Deutsch* and English both, in this house, twenty years of both, her father's Bavarian and her mother's Bavarian and the English they had built together word by word from neighbors and the radio and the town. All of that, all those words in two languages, and at noon the kitchen was as quiet as a room that had no one in it.

She thought: *auch.*

She washed the bowl and went back out to the barn. The ice on the puddle beside the gate post was still there. The sun was low and it had not reached the north side of the post, where the puddle sat in the shadow. It would be there at five o'clock too. It would be there tomorrow morning.

She went inside the barn. The cows knew she was back by her boots.

input.summary: November 1943, Hannah POV. Three to four weeks after the POWs' arrival. The chapter establishes Hannah's 5 a.m. barn routine in November (cold, dark, the cows knowing her by her boots: Else first, then down the line to Berta) and the new shape the mornings have taken since the truck came. At seven the three older POWs go north to the pasture fence; Karl is assigned manure hauling in the south working yard — the worst job, going to the youngest. Hannah watches from a gap in the south barn wall (between the third and fourth boards, shoulder height) and has been watching for weeks: cataloging Karl's efficiency, his navigation of the frozen rut at a fixed angle, and — in the second week — noticing that Magda has let out the seams of his jacket without announcement or explanation. The chapter's central scene: one cold morning Hannah sets her heavy grain bucket down at the south gate post instead of carrying it through, stands watching the ice on the puddle; Karl comes past with the wheelbarrow, sees the bucket, picks it up, and places it on the gate rail where she can reach it from her side — not through the gate, not handed over the fence, placed. He says: Da, bitte. She says: Danke. He looks up, recalibrating. She has been carrying the sentence for three weeks; she says it: Mein Vater kommt aus Bayern. He considers. He says: Ich auch. He goes back to work. Lonzo Reed is ten yards away adjusting his boot lacing and was not watching. For the rest of the working morning Hannah turns the word *auch* over in her thinking — the conjunction that places without explaining, that says 'also me' without naming the category — and thinks about Straubing and Regensburg on the same river, fifty kilometers, her father's second-week dinner remark (*Regensburg ist nicht weit*). Noon: Albrecht comes in, eats standing at the counter without speaking, goes back out. Magda stirs the pot. The kitchen is silent in two languages. Hannah thinks: *auch*. She washes the bowl and goes back to the barn. Threads 4 (The Fence) and 5 (The Pfeiffer Silence) advanced.

This is distinct from the east wall gap (between boards 4 and 5) which faces the farm lane and icehouse front.

The gate in the south fence is for equipment — wide enough for the tractor. It separates the barnyard from the working field. Hannah uses it daily to carry the grain bucket to the calf pen on the other side.
The calf pen is approximately thirty feet from the south gate, on the working-field side of the fence.

The three older POWs are assigned to the north pasture fence-repair detail daily; Karl is assigned to manure hauling in the south working yard. This has been the standing assignment since the first week of the work detail.
Karl's first words to Hannah: 'Da, bitte' — spoken while placing her feed bucket on the gate rail, November 1943. This is his first voluntary verbal contact with any member of the Pfeiffer household.

Hannah's first voluntary use of German with a stranger — she says 'Danke' reflexively, then after a pause, deliberately: 'Mein Vater kommt aus Bayern.' She has been rehearsing this sentence for three weeks.
Karl's first substantive verbal response to Hannah: 'Ich auch.' — two words, placing himself in the same Bavarian category without naming it. This is November 1943.
Lonzo Reed was adjusting the lacing on his left boot when the exchange occurred. He was ten yards away and was not watching.

Magda Pfeiffer let out the seams of Karl's Army-issue denim jacket in the second week of the POW work detail. The work was done in Magda's characteristic straight-line stitch, with no backstitch at the ends. She said nothing about it before or after.
Hilde: a named dairy cow on the Pfeiffer farm. Placed next to Else in the milking order. Established in ch05.

Hannah has understood since Albrecht's second-week dinner remark ('Regensburg ist nicht weit') that her father is aware of Karl's home city and that its proximity to Straubing registers for him, even if he will not discuss it.
looseThreads: What does Albrecht actually feel about Karl's presence — the fact that someone from fifty kilometers from Straubing is working his farm? He has acknowledged the geography once ('Regensburg ist nicht weit') and nothing more. This is sealed from Hannah's POV.
Karl's reaction to Hannah's German is established (he recalibrates) but his thinking is not accessible. What does 'Ich auch' cost him — what does it open? This is a loose thread for Thread 4.

Thread 4 (The Fence): the exchange has happened at its minimal level. The next advance is ch08, where Albrecht says seven words at dinner. The connection between Hannah's fence exchange and her father's acknowledgment is the thread's next beat.

Thread 5 (The Pfeiffer Silence): Hannah has now deployed her father's language outward, toward a stranger — the first use of it for contact rather than comprehension. The silence at noon is unchanged, but Hannah's relationship to it has shifted slightly. She is now in possession of something her father doesn't know she did.
The word *auch*: planted here as a structural word for Thread 4. It recurs as *Ich auch* in ch17 per the thread outline. Its meaning accumulates across the chapters.

Marcus Klein is Hannah's German teacher and has noted in his grade book that she knows more than she shows. He does not appear in this chapter but the fact that she has now spoken German to Karl — across a fence, not in a classroom — is something he will never know about directly.

Karl comes with the wheelbarrow, picks up the bucket, places it where she can reach it — the right way to do it. *Da, bitte.* *Danke.* He looks up. She says the sentence she has been rehearsing for three weeks: *Mein Vater kommt aus Bayern.* He says: *Ich auch.* Lonzo is adjusting his boot lacing and not watching. For the rest of the morning Hannah turns the word *auch* over — the gentlest possible grammar for placing yourself beside someone — and thinks about Straubing and Regensburg on the same river, fifty kilometers, her father's closed-down knowledge. input.content:
# What the Body Spoke

The message had been slipped under his classroom door, on school letterhead, in Mrs. Farwell's handwriting: *Mr. Fenn would appreciate five minutes at half-past eight, when convenient.* Marcus arrived at twenty-five past. It was a Tuesday, and he was wearing the older suit.

Fenn's office occupied the northeast corner of the building. Two windows looked north toward the ash trees, bare by now, and between their branches a band of sky the color of old pewter. Fenn stood when Marcus entered, which was his habit, and sat again once Marcus had taken the chair across the desk.

"Dr. Klein. I appreciate your time."

"Of course."

Fenn had a manila folder open in front of him, though his eyes were not on it. He had the manner of a man who had prepared this conversation in advance and was now delivering his prepared opening accurately, which Marcus respected. "You're aware of the body found on the breakwater last month."

Marcus said he was.

"There's been a development of a minor kind. The Coast Guard made a general inquiry — asked whether anyone in town had heard the man speak before the storm. A fisherman came forward. Ed Larrabee. He was patching a trap buoy at the commercial float that afternoon, the ninth, and he heard the man say something." Fenn placed a brief pause before his next words, as if to indicate that what followed was not his characterization but someone else's. "Something European, he said. Possibly German."

The word *European* was doing considerable work. Marcus allowed it to do that work in silence.

"The Coast Guard forwarded the report to the harbormaster's office, which mentioned it to the selectmen, which mentioned it to me." Fenn glanced once at the folder — some record of this chain, Marcus assumed. "I wonder whether you'd be willing to speak with the harbormaster. In case she has any materials — a phrase, something in writing — that might benefit from identification. Not as an investigator, in any sense. I would characterize it as a civic resource."

He had chosen the phrase *civic resource* with some care; Marcus felt the care in it. He was being used and also being asked, and Fenn was trying to accomplish both without the use becoming visible. This was decent of Fenn, whose decency on most matters was genuine if occasionally misaimed.

"I'd be glad to help," Marcus said. He meant this and was also aware that saying otherwise would have read as avoidance, and that avoidance in a particular direction was itself a kind of statement. But the genuine part was true. The body had been on the breakwater, and someone had heard what the man said, and what the man said may have been in a language that was Marcus's by birth and training. The connection was not exotic. It was simple, if a little cold in the feeling of it.

"Thank you." Fenn's relief was brief but visible, which told Marcus something about how Fenn understood his position in Coldwater Reach — that some real possibility of refusal had seemed available. He filed this without responding to it.

"I'll stop at the harbor office after my last class."

"Excellent." Fenn stood. Marcus stood, buttoned the older suit's coat, and went out.

---

He had Latin in the afternoon — the third declension, eight students, a standard Tuesday. He moved through it with adequate attention, and at half-past three collected his coat and hat from the faculty coatroom and went out the building's north door into the November afternoon.

The light was going. Not dark yet but committed to it: the sky a flat ash gray, the sun already behind the inland ridge, what remained of the day a kind of residual illumination without source or direction. He went south on Main Street. Burleigh's was closed, as it was on Tuesdays. The post office showed Ada Gile's handwritten notice about V-Mail hours. The general store had ration-book advisories taped to the inside of the glass. He passed all of it without entering any of it — the town familiar in the way a foreign country became familiar after sustained residence, which was to say: known without being owned.

He had been to the harbor office once before. He remembered the occasion exactly, as he tended to remember occasions in Coldwater Reach exactly, each one still distinct enough to locate precisely. He had gone in the spring of 1942, preparing a classroom exercise on maritime regulation — the Lighthouse Service's jurisdiction, the harbor log as a legal document, the intersection of admiralty law and local authority. An exercise that had ultimately not been used. He had not been sure, when it came to it, whether a visit to the harbormaster's office would be received as a legitimate pedagogical inquiry or as something less clearly categorized. He had decided the uncertainty was sufficient reason to use a different exercise, and he had not examined this decision afterward.

The pier lane smelled of salt and diesel even from the road. He heard the harbor before he saw it: the draw of water against pier pilings, the creak of mooring lines taking the tide's shift, a bell buoy at the anchorage's outer edge. The pier shed stood at the lane's end with a light burning over its door. At the near end of the shed the harbor office, where the structure changed from newer plank to older converted pilot house.

---

The office was twelve feet by fourteen, or close enough. A chart cabinet stood to the left of the desk, its drawers flush and latched. Above the south window: a VHF radio set on its shelf, dial unlit. On the east wall, at a height that would have suited a taller person, an aneroid barometer of the older enclosed type. Below the barometer: two hooks bent from a length of copper pipe, a coffee cup on each — one with a chip at the rim, one without. The room smelled of brine and the heating element and the particular cold that came in at the sill.

On the peg beside the door: an oilskin coat, too large for her frame, hanging with the particular weight of something used past the point of newness.

Eira Bowman was at her desk. She looked up when he came in. She did not stand. Her hands came to rest flat on either side of the open logbook — not a defensive posture but the posture of someone for whom this room was the full extent of the relevant territory, and who had not issued any invitation to renegotiate its dimensions.

"Dr. Klein."

"Mrs. Bowman." He stood inside the door. "I apologize for coming without notice. Principal Fenn asked me to stop by — regarding the inquiry about the man on the breakwater."

She waited. This was not impatience; it was the professional economy of someone who had found that waiting for the substance of a statement cost less than responding to its approach.

"A fisherman — Ed Larrabee, I understand — reported hearing the man say something that sounded European. Possibly German. The report went from the Coast Guard to the selectmen to Mr. Fenn, who asked whether I might be of use as —" Marcus applied Fenn's phrase with intention "— as a civic resource. In case there were any materials. A phrase, a written word. Something that might benefit from identification."

She considered him for a moment with the attention of someone gathering information rather than forming judgment. Then she said: "The Coast Guard has what I know."

He heard this carefully. The sentence was precise in the way of a sentence chosen for its precision: it said the Coast Guard possessed what she presently knew, which was true, and it said nothing about the conditions under which what she knew might later change, or expand, or become applicable in a different context. He registered the precision and did not indicate that he had.

"Of course," he said. "I won't take up your time."

He had his hand on the door latch when he stopped. He could not have said, after the fact, whether this was calculation or something less deliberate; he had turned back before he'd resolved the question.

"If it would be useful," he said, and heard his own formality and made an effort against it, and the effort produced the same formality at a slightly different angle. "Not to the Coast Guard, necessarily. To you. I know something about identification documents from that period — the late thirties, the early forties. Varieties of German. Regional and national variation in written forms. I'm at the school."

Eira Bowman looked at him. She was not deciding whether to trust him — that would have implied a current use, which she did not have — but the look had the quality of something being assessed for later applicability, the way one assessed a piece of equipment for a season not yet arrived. Two or three seconds. Then: "I'll note it."

Marcus thanked her, in a minor key, and went out.

---

The lane was darker than when he had come down it. The lamp above the pier shed door held the near ground but did not reach the road. He went north. Behind him the harbor worked against the pilings in its ordinary manner — water's commerce with structure, mechanical, without reference to what any particular tide had brought in.

He thought about the English phrase: *a man without papers*.

In English the phrase carried the inconvenience of a filing problem. A man who had not submitted the correct documentation, whose identity could not be properly processed because the supporting evidence was absent. Unfortunate. Correctable, in principle. Somewhere the proof existed; it had simply not been submitted.

In German the phrase was *ein Mensch ohne Papiere*. The word *Mensch* was not *Mann* — not man in the gendered or particular sense but person, human, the categorical term that the German philosophical tradition had spent considerable effort defining and that German legal tradition, in the decade just past, had spent considerable effort qualifying and subdividing and finally setting conditions on. To be *ein Mensch ohne Papiere* in Germany in 1936, or 1938, or 1941, was not to have neglected paperwork. It was to be without the documentation that confirmed your standing as a legal person in a system that had chosen to make personhood conditional on documentation. The papers were not evidence of identity; they were identity, in the only form the system would recognize. Remove the papers: remove the person. Not from the physical world — the body continued, as anyone looking at it could see — but from the world that the century had increasingly decided was the real one, the legal and administrative one, the world of records and proofs and the right to be processed.

The man on the breakwater had been thirty-eight to forty-eight years old. No identifying marks. No documents. Possibly speaking German, or something that a Maine fisherman found European and possibly German, which were not necessarily the same range. He had arrived from somewhere and come to rest at the foot of an iron ladder at the end of a granite breakwater. The question of who he was could not be answered from the body because the body's authorship had been removed — by the water, or by whoever had placed him in it, or by himself, if the removal had been intentional.

Had he known, Marcus wondered. Had the man understood what he was without — not just the practical inconvenience of it but the full weight of what the last decade had loaded into that absence. Had he understood himself to be *staatenlos*: without a state to claim him, without a floor of legal existence to stand on. The word in German carried both the administrative condition and a physical image: stateless, floorless, unsupported. The man who could not be proved.

It was possible the man had been an ordinary sailor with inadequate records, a document lost to water or carelessness. Marcus did not exclude this. He was also aware that he was carrying his own experience into the question, which was not sound methodology. But he carried it regardless, because this kind of knowledge did not wait for an invitation.

He had learned the weight of papers in a particular room, in a particular year, when a man in a uniform had looked at his documents and then looked at him and made a decision about what they proved. That was a kind of knowledge that did not translate cleanly across cases. It was, however, knowledge. He was not sure he could do anything useful with it here. He had made the offer anyway.

The ridge crested ahead. Below: the town's lights spread across the valley floor, the Congregational steeple white against the dark sky, chimney smoke running south on the harbor wind.

---

Lillian had left the kitchen lamp on. The low oven held supper — onion and potato, something preserved and darkened in the cooking, smelling of vinegar and thyme. He hung his coat and sat at the table. Henry came in from the back hallway a few minutes later, in his custodian's canvas, the key ring clipped at his belt. He washed his hands at the sink.

"You're late," Henry said. A notation, not a complaint.

"I stopped at the harbor office." Lillian set bowls on the table and went to slice the bread. "At Fenn's request. The fisherman who heard the man on the breakwater — what he heard, apparently, sounded German."

Henry sat. He took up his spoon and did not immediately use it. "How'd the harbormaster take it?"

"With appropriate reserve. She said the Coast Guard has what she knows."

Henry nodded once.

"I told her I could be of use with identification documents. Varieties of German. I said I was at the school, if she needed it."

"What'd she say to that?"

"She said she'd note it."

Henry ate. After a moment: "She'll use you when she needs you. Not before."

Marcus considered this. He found it correct. Henry's readings of these kinds of transactions were, as a general rule, more reliable than his own, which tended toward the philological and away from the practical.

"Yes," he said. "That is what I understood."

They ate. Lillian moved in the adjacent room, the sound of her work steady and unremarkable. Outside the kitchen window, the wind off the harbor worked the bare branches of the elm in the yard. Marcus ate his supper and said nothing further.

After supper he washed his bowl at the sink and dried it and went upstairs. He sat at the desk with the annotating pen in his hand. On the desk: the Latin papers, the open dictionary, the grade book in its brown cover. He did not open the grade book. He sat for a while with the pen, and then he capped it and put it down.
