input.oneLine: While freeing a grounded coaster from the inner-anchorage ice, Eira learns Birch Macomber held a Portland Navy district contract since fall 1943 and "had papers," adding the lead to her personal log.

input.summary: February 1944, Eira POV. The inner anchorage is frozen hard at the north end — ice out forty yards — and a Rockland coaster, the Wren, has grounded on the ice-ledge at the mooring field's north edge on a falling tide after coming in at half past eleven the night before. Eira is at the dock at first light, assesses the situation, speaks to the skipper (Estelle Hartley, about forty, has sailed the coast since 1927), and decides the tide won't lift the vessel in time and calls the Samoset for a tow. While they wait on the dock with coffee from Eira's thermos — the cold off ice noted as drier and stiffer than the cold off open water — Hartley and Eira talk the Maine coast trade in the way of two professionals with overlapping territory. In the flow of professional conversation, Eira asks after Birch Macomber. Hartley says he's been running Portland mostly, since fall, on some kind of Navy district contract that paid well enough to make him turn down a pine lath run to Bath in September 1943. He had papers, Hartley says. Eira says hm and offers the cover story of the annual fee schedule reconciliation. The Samoset arrives, the tow goes cleanly — one brief groan of wood finding buoyancy — no damage to the Wren's keel. Hartley goes south for Lubec. Eira spends the rest of the working day on administrative reports, goes home for supper, returns to the harbor office at half past five, and writes in her personal log: Macomber, Portland, Navy district contract, fall 1943, had papers per Hartley, district office Portland holds vessel contracts by master and registration, papers equals record somewhere. Thread T1 (The Blank in the Log) advanced: Eira now has the Macomber-Portland-Navy district connection in her private record and a next step (writing to the district).

The coaster Wren is approximately sixty feet, wooden, working trim, Rockland registry, wheelhouse painted light blue — a color uncommon this far downeast.

The inner anchorage of Coldwater Reach is frozen hard at its north end in February 1944 — solid ice from the granite shore out approximately forty yards, with a ragged fringe at the seaward edge where the channel current works.
The ice-ledge at the mooring field's north edge has a depth of approximately nine feet at the inner face at mean high water, running across the full width of the mooring field's north edge.
The cold off ice is drier and stiffer than the cold off open water; it radiates from a flat surface rather than rolling off moving water. The ice-cold has a faint mineral smell — Eira names it chalk.
Eira carries a thermos on harbor dock work when waiting is anticipated. She uses the enamel mug for herself and gives the thermos lid to whoever she shares coffee with.
The Samoset was on its regular coastal run out of Jonesport when Eira called it for the Wren tow on this February morning. It arrived at Coldwater Reach at approximately 0922.
Birch Macomber turned down a pine lath run from Hartley for Bath, Maine, in September 1943, saying he had a contract that paid better.
Birch Macomber told Hartley he had 'papers' — official documentation — for work out of Portland, possibly for the Navy district. He did not specify the nature of the contract.
The Portland district office (U.S. Navy First District) holds vessel contracts by master and registration — paper records exist for contracts of this type.
Eira's cover story for her Macomber inquiry to Hartley: annual fee schedule reconciliation, trying to account for his circuit stops.

Personal log entry written by Eira, February 1944, verbatim: 'Macomber — Portland, Navy district contract — fall 1943. Per Hartley (Wren / Rockland): turned down pine lath run for Bath, Sept. '43, said he had contract, it paid. Said he had papers. Distinct from ordinary cargo work. District office Portland holds vessel contracts by master and registration — file would exist. Inquiry: what was the contract? What dates?' Below which: 'Papers = record somewhere.'
The Wren came off the ice-ledge under tow with one low, dense groan of wood finding buoyancy — no damage to keel, no change in trim confirmed by Hartley's visual check of the waterline.

Eira went home for supper and returned to the harbor office at approximately half past five in the evening to write the personal log entry — consistent with ch07 where the personal log is written in the evening at the harbor office.
looseThreads: Thread T1 (The Blank in the Log): Eira now has a named connection — Macomber, Portland, Navy district contract, 'had papers' — logged in her personal notebook. Her next step, identified in the entry, is a written inquiry to the Portland district office. This will be developed in ch13 (Ada Gile's Window) and ch16 (The Inspector).

The question of what the Portland Navy district contract was — what cargo or route or service Macomber was providing — remains open. The outline notes this is not criminal and the connection to the body will be mundane. But the record that 'papers = record somewhere' sets up a future inquiry.

The timeline now confirmed: Macomber was on Portland contract at least since September 1943, possibly earlier; came through Coldwater Reach October 8–11; departed in storm conditions October 11 at 0430. The contract work and the Coldwater Reach stopover and the departure are now linked in Eira's private record.
Whether Eira will actually write to the Portland district, and how she will frame the inquiry without drawing attention to her investigation, is unresolved — handled in later chapters.

The Samoset is now established as the buoy tender Eira calls for the February tow. Its regular schedule (twice monthly, Jonesport run) is consistent with its October 1943 appearances. Its name should be used consistently in later Eira chapters that reference the buoy tender.
Thread T3 note: Marcus's board situation (from ch09) is not touched in this chapter — appropriate, as this is a single-thread T1 chapter. The parallel timeline continues.

input.content:
# The Word for Harbor

She was at the chopping block with the butter-box planks when she thought about making the new card.

The cedar dried fast behind the kitchen stove — three or four days, and what had been green-edged and faintly resinous came out pale and dry, holding a pencil mark the way good paper held it. Her father split the boards into kindling-lengths with his hatchet on the cold side of the woodpile, three strokes per board, Saturday mornings when the week's work let up enough for that kind of patience. She had been taking lengths before they reached the woodbox since January — three weeks now — slipping two or three boards out of the stack when her father was in the barn, then cutting them down on the cold side of the building with her pocket knife: six inches by two, roughly the size of a playing card, each face sanded clean with the fine side of the whetstone.

She had ten cards done. *Barn / Scheune. Gate / Tor. Bucket / Eimer. Rope / Seil.* And the more recent ones: *fence / Zaun. stone / Stein. field / Feld. morning / Morgen.* And two newer cards she'd put out in the second week of February and hadn't gotten back yet. The completed cards — given and returned — lived in the deep pocket of her apron alongside the pencil and whatever else a day accumulated. She turned them over without thinking sometimes, the way you turned a coin: English side, German side, English side. The English sides wore fastest. She didn't know why. Something about the letters, or the way they were handled.

The system: a card flat on the fence post nearest the icehouse, held down with the gray stone she'd moved from the field edge in October. By the following afternoon: gone. Two days later, sometimes one: back under the stone. He had been memorizing. She knew this from the wearing of the cards, and from the way he sometimes said an English word when he came to the fence — not working at it but using it, placing it where it belonged in a sentence.

Today she had the harbor card to bring out.

---

She'd made it the previous afternoon at the kitchen table, her mother out with the chickens, the house quiet around her. *Harbor / Hafen.* She drew the dividing line down the center with the ruler edge, pressed the letters on both faces, and held the card for a moment before tucking it into her apron.

The word had been in the edge of her thinking since October — not that she had details. The talk had reached her the way talk reached everyone in a town this size: in pieces, through the gaps. At school in the corridor, at the general store, after Sunday meeting in the road. A man. Unidentified. The breakwater. She didn't know more than that. The pieces hadn't arrived in any better form. But the word *harbor* had acquired something from those months of partial information — a quality of holding-in, a particular weight on both sides of the word. The harbor as the place that took you in from the sea. She turned the card over and thought about that, and then put it in her pocket.

She laid it under the gray stone before her morning work, face-down, and went about the day.

---

The three older POWs left for the north pasture with Lonzo at eight, which was when they always went. Karl stayed in the south yard. His standing assignment was the working-yard maintenance — manure hauling, the secondary clean, the daily accumulation that kept the south yard manageable. She could hear him before she saw him on most mornings: the barrow wheel on frozen ground, its particular stop-and-go rhythm telling her where he was in the task.

By two o'clock Lonzo was back from the pasture, settled in his chair beside the icehouse door with his coat collar turned up. He had his rifle across his knees and a small flask tucked in his coat's left breast pocket — she had begun noticing the flask in January, the way it had become part of the daily arrangement of him, as habitual as the chair. He was looking at the middle distance in the way that meant present, not watching. The expression of a man whose job was to be there rather than to attend.

Hannah came to the south gate with a bundle of baling wire she'd been meaning to move for a week. She set it against the fence post, let her eyes drop to the post's top.

The harbor card was gone.

She straightened without looking at the icehouse.

Karl came across the south yard with the slop bucket, moving at his ordinary pace. His coat was buttoned to the throat — the Army-issue denim her mother had let out at the seams without being asked, all buttons present, which she noticed the way she always noticed without meaning to. He set the bucket on the fence rail.

Then he reached into his coat pocket and took out the card.

He'd folded it once across its length to keep it flat in the pocket. He unfolded it carefully, with both hands, and held it with the English side facing him. She could see the letters from where she stood: the H of *harbor* worn nearly through, the rest still legible but going. He'd been working at it hard.

He said, in English: "Har-bor."

The word measuring itself in his mouth, testing whether it did what a word should do. Not a question, not a statement — something between the two, the sound of a word being claimed.

She said: yes.

He looked at the card, then at her. He said, in German: *Was ist das, ein Hafen, hier?* What is that, a harbor, here?

His German had the Bavarian roundness she recognized from her father, but set differently in the mouth — the vowels opened further forward, a younger voice in the same regional key. He asked questions as genuine questions. She had noticed this early and kept noticing: he was not asking in order to seem engaged. He wanted to know what a harbor was, here, in relation to where he was standing.

She said, in German: *Ja. Drei Meilen.* Three miles. And pointed south — past the barn, past the far field boundary, roughly toward the ridge line you couldn't see from the south yard.

He turned to look in the direction she was pointing. The bare birches at the field's edge, and beyond them the February sky, gray and flat and closed. He studied it the way you studied a map when the detail you needed wasn't printed — calculating from what was visible toward what wasn't.

He said, still facing south: *Ich habe das Meer nie gesehen.*

I have never seen the sea.

He said it with the particular tone he kept for things he meant completely — not louder or softer than his ordinary voice, but without hedge, the way you spoke when the saying mattered as much as the content. She had heard that tone twice before: in October when he'd said *da, bitte* setting her grain bucket on the rail, as if the act required its own precision; and in December when he'd noted, almost under his breath, that the east barn wall was separating from the foundation beam. Things he meant to be acknowledged, not just heard.

She looked at him.

Regensburg was ninety kilometers up the Danube from Munich, inland and river-anchored. She'd found it on the school atlas in October, placed her thumb beside Straubing to measure the distance — same river, same Bavarian valley. You could live your whole life in that valley with the Danube as your water and the sea as a mark on a map. And Tunisia: she'd traced the North African coast when the news came through about the surrender. A coast, yes. But Karl had come to that coast as a prisoner, at the end of something — she thought that was different from standing at the shore and looking at it, the way you looked at something that had been waiting for you and was now simply there.

She picked up the baling wire.

*Ich könnte es dir zeigen* — I could show you — is what she didn't say. The sentence arrived in both languages at once, the way certain thoughts did when she'd been thinking in German all morning, and she held it there while she gathered the wire, and did not say it. Because she didn't know yet whether she meant it. And she had learned — not from anywhere in particular, from the general running of a house where words were managed — that you didn't say a sentence until you knew what shape it would make afterward.

She took the bucket from the rail as his hands released it, the handoff wordless the way most things were, and went toward the barn.

Lonzo had not moved from his chair. He was watching the ice that had built along the near edge of the water trough at the icehouse corner, or watching nothing in particular; it was hard to say from across the yard.

---

She worked until four. Baling wire sorted and coiled on the barn's north hook. Second-stall trough drained and refilled. Hilde and Else brought in from the half-gate and settled in the milking alley. The tasks had their order and she followed it, and the thought moved with her through the order the way a thought did when it had gotten into the day's grain.

She had been nine years old when she walked to the harbor by herself. A clear October morning, the road dry, her shoes dry when she started. She had not asked permission because it had not occurred to her that permission was the relevant category — you walked south for three miles and the harbor was there, the same harbor it always was, and the sea beyond the harbor mouth was large and gray-green and smelled of brine and old rope and diesel from the lobster boats, and larger than she'd known any surface could be when you were standing at its edge. The tide had come in while she stood on the breakwater and soaked her shoes through to the wool socks. She had walked home in wet socks and told no one. There was nothing particular to say. She had gone and the sea had been there and she had come back.

She had never thought of it as something you could be denied. She had never thought of it the way she thought of meat ration or coffee — as something allocated, doled out, possible to be refused. The harbor was three miles south and it was simply there, whether or not you went to it. You could walk to it on any clear day without asking anyone's permission. That was the entire situation.

Rolf had grown up here, three miles from the coast his whole life. He'd been eighteen when he enlisted — Camp Lejeune, then North Africa, then Italy by last summer's letters, moving through theaters she could only trace on the school maps. She couldn't say whether he'd thought much about the harbor growing up. Boys went outward toward the fields; she had gone toward the water; they had never discussed it because there had never been a reason to discuss it. And then the war had taken him to the Pacific coast and the Mediterranean coast, and the sea was now something he crossed.

Karl Reinmar had lived nineteen years and never stood at the shore and looked at it.

She brought Hilde and Else in and settled them and cleaned the alley and went in for dinner.

---

Potato soup and bread. Her mother had the oil lamp on the table again — her eyes, in the February evenings. The lamp made a smaller circle than the ceiling fixture, and the three of them sat inside it with the kitchen in shadow around them. Outside the window the dark was already full, February dark that settled early and stayed.

Albrecht ate without speaking. Magda's spoon moved in its quiet way.

Hannah ate her soup with one hand and turned the harbor card over in her apron pocket with the other. English side, German side. The English side going smooth under her thumb, the letters almost gone by feel.

Then she said, in German: *Hast du das Meer gesehen, bevor du nach Amerika kamst?*

Had you seen the sea before you came to America?

Her father looked up from his bowl.

The question sat between them. She heard it arrive with more weight than she'd intended — not just a question about geography but about the years before she was born, about the man who had stood at a ship's rail in Hamburg in 1922 and watched the city go small behind him. She had not intended all of that. But intentions were not the unit the world measured in, she was finding.

Her father set his spoon down. He looked at her with the weighing expression — not warm, not cold, something in between deciding and knowing. At the far end of the table, Magda had gone still.

He said: *Erst in Hamburg.*

Not until Hamburg. When he'd boarded the ship, going out. The sea had been the threshold, and he had crossed it once, going, and here he was on the other side of it.

Hannah nodded.

She did not ask what it was like to stand at the rail and look at it for the first time, the water opening before you in every direction. She did not ask whether he had understood, in that moment, what the crossing meant.

Her father picked up his spoon. He finished his soup, folded his napkin with his two folds and the tucked corner, and went to the radio chair. He did not turn the radio on.

Magda rose and took the bowls to the sink. Hannah followed.

Through the window above the sink: the February dark, complete and flat. Three miles of it between here and the harbor, and the harbor there behind the dark, and the sea beyond the harbor mouth, and all of it there whether anyone looked at it or not.

Her mother handed her a bowl to dry.

She dried it and put it away.

The card was still in her apron pocket. She could feel it when she pressed her hand flat against the apron cloth: English side worn almost smooth, *Hafen* on the other side still legible, the letters still holding their shape.

Her mother handed her the last bowl.

She dried it and put it on the shelf.

input.oneLine: Hannah exchanges word-cards with Karl across the fence; he tells her he has never seen the sea, and she does not yet say she could show him.

input.summary: February 1944, Hannah POV. The word-card system is established: since January (three weeks prior), Hannah has been cutting small cedar rectangles from butter-box planks, writing one word per card in pencil (English left / German right), and leaving them on the fence post nearest the icehouse under a gray stone. Karl retrieves the cards, memorizes them, and returns them — the English sides worn from handling. This February afternoon Hannah makes a new card: harbor / Hafen, prompted partly by the fragments of town talk she has absorbed about the unidentified man and the breakwater in October (she has no details, only the shape of the event). She puts the card out in the morning. In the afternoon she comes to the south gate with baling wire as cover; Karl arrives from the south yard with the slop bucket; he takes the harbor card from his pocket, unfolds it, tests 'har-bor' in English, then asks in German: Was ist das, ein Hafen, hier? Hannah answers: Ja. Drei Meilen. And points south. Karl, still looking south toward the invisible ridge, says the chapter's hinge line: Ich habe das Meer nie gesehen. I have never seen the sea. He says it in the tone he reserves for things requiring acknowledgment. Hannah registers it, thinks through why she believes him (Regensburg, ninety kilometers inland, up the Danube; Tunisia a coast but not the same), and does not say: I could show you — because she doesn't know yet whether she means it. She takes the bucket and goes to the barn. Lonzo is present in his chair by the icehouse, flask in breast pocket, watching nothing in particular; he does not react to the German exchange. Interior: Hannah thinks about what it means to never have seen the sea — herself at nine walking to the breakwater without permission and soaking her shoes; Rolf growing up here but going to war across multiple coasts; the harbor as something she had never thought could be denied. At dinner she asks Albrecht in German: Hast du das Meer gesehen, bevor du nach Amerika kamst? He sets his spoon down, looks at her, and says: Erst in Hamburg. Not until Hamburg, when he boarded the emigrating ship in 1922. She nods; she does not ask more. The chapter ends on the card still in her apron pocket — English side worn smooth, the Hafen side still holding its letters. Threads T4 (The Fence) and T5 (The Pfeiffer Silence) advanced.

The English sides of returned cards wear fastest. The H in Hafen is gone to gray on the returned card by the day of the main scene. Karl has been memorizing the English side of each card.

Karl unfolds the harbor card with both hands, holding it with the English side toward him, close enough that Hannah can see the letters.
Karl tests 'har-bor' in English aloud before asking the German question — the word measured, not yet claimed.

Karl Reinmar's statement to Hannah Pfeiffer, February 1944, exact German: 'Ich habe das Meer nie gesehen.' (I have never seen the sea.) Said in his tone of precision, looking south toward the invisible ridge.
Hannah's response to Karl's position question: 'Ja. Drei Meilen.' She points south toward the ridge line she cannot see from the south yard.
Hannah does not say 'Ich könnte es dir zeigen' (I could show you) — she does not yet know if she means it. This is the suspended offer that will be realized in ch23.

Albrecht Pfeiffer's response to Hannah's dinner question about the sea: 'Erst in Hamburg.' (Not until Hamburg.) He set his spoon down before answering and picked it up again after. He looked at her directly during this exchange, unlike ch08 when he looked at his bowl.
Albrecht first saw the sea in Hamburg in 1922 when he boarded the emigrating ship to America. This is the first and, it is implied, only time he has crossed to open ocean.
Hannah thinks, in this chapter, that having access to the harbor has been so natural to her that she never framed it as something that could be denied — until Karl's line.

input.content:
# Two Suits

He checked the inside breast pocket of his overcoat before buttoning it. The letter was still there — twenty-two days in the same pocket, the envelope softened slightly by handling and body heat — and he had not expected otherwise, but the checking had become a habit now, the same way the harbor smell at the ridge had become a landmark: not surprising, simply expected, and therefore noted.

He was wearing the older suit. Saturday was outside the school week's schedule, which governed only working days, and on working days he held to it precisely — the good suit on Monday, Wednesday, Friday; the older suit on Tuesday and Thursday — but on weekends the jurisdiction lapsed, and without quite reasoning about it he had settled into reaching for the older suit on Saturday mornings. It asked less of him. This was, he acknowledged, a precise distinction between two suits that were both adequate and both gray, but in the small economies of a daily routine the precise distinction was the one that mattered.

Lillian was in the kitchen. He heard the stovetop lid being set aside and replaced.

"Post office," he said, from the entry.

"Leave the door," she said. "The latch is sticky again."

He left it open a hand's width and went down the step into the March morning.

---

The elm at the corner of the yard had begun to show buds — not open yet, but the branch-ends thickening, the shift from bare to almost-bare that was still the most reliable sign of spring on this coast, preceding by several weeks any warmth one could actually use. On school mornings he left at twenty past seven and the light was not yet what it would become; on Saturday mornings the light was different, and the walk was not purposeful in the same way, which meant he noticed the elm.

The road descended toward the town for eight minutes before rising to the ridge above the north end. The harbor smell reached him there, as it always did — salt and diesel residue, and beneath those the Atlantic's own colder exhalation, which still had no precise term in any of his four working languages. He had stopped registering it as foreign somewhere in the second autumn. It was now simply the smell of the road at the ridge, which was different from the smell of the road in other places, which was information and not otherwise.

He walked down into town. A Saturday had a different character than the working week — fewer men moving quickly, two boys outside the general store, a fisherman coming out of the hardware with a coil of line under his arm. The war was in the radio and the casualty lists and the rationing and the absence of certain young men, but on a Saturday morning in March it was also, factually, not on the main street of Coldwater Reach, and a person could notice this without sentiment.

He had told Lillian he was going to the post office for stamps. This was true. He was also, he had admitted to himself on the descent from the ridge, testing a proposition: whether going out on an errand changed the state of the question in his coat pocket. He had found in his experience — which was considerable, in the matter of decisions deferred — that movement sometimes performed a kind of argument on the body that the seated mind could not complete on its own. He would buy stamps. He would return. He would know more clearly what he intended to do, or he would not, and the walk would in either case have been a walk.

The post office stood between the hardware and the pharmacist's, which was where it had stood, visibly, for as long as anyone in Coldwater Reach appeared to have noticed. He went in.

---

Ada Gile was sorting V-Mail behind the counter.

The V-Mail forms were the thin paper of the reduced-and-reprinted system — the original letter photographed to microfilm, flown across, reprinted at a fraction of the original weight, the familiar censored rectangles that turned a letter into something between a communication and a diagram of what had been removed from it. He had observed, professionally and against his better inclinations, the typography of the printed forms, the specific font, the residue of the mechanical reduction in the letters' edges. Then he had made himself stop observing, because it was not his typography and the war was not a professional object.

Ada Gile had been postmistress since 1928, which meant she had the institutional memory of the town's correspondence in the way that he had the institutional memory of certain Viennese archives: not as information one volunteered, but as the ground on which more specific knowledge stood. She had treated him since his arrival in 1942 with the same direct efficiency she appeared to apply to everyone — not warmth, not suspicion, simply the recognition that he existed, had postal needs, and was entitled to have them addressed. He had found this, over time, a more reliable form of civility than its alternatives.

He said good morning. She said good morning and slid the stamp drawer open without being asked.

He asked for twelve three-cent stamps. She counted them onto the counter and gave him his change and did not entirely stop the V-Mail sorting during the transaction, which was the practiced courtesy of a counter kept moving.

He stayed at the counter a moment longer than the purchase required. He was aware of this; he suspected that Ada Gile was also aware of it. She was aware, he thought, of most things that occurred within the post office's dimensions, and of considerable things that did not.

He said: "I wonder whether there has been any further inquiry — about the business last autumn. The man on the breakwater." He had worked through several framings for this question on the walk down and had settled on the most indirect available: a general inquiry about the town's investigation, not about Ada's specific knowledge, so that she could answer as broadly as she chose. "From the Coast Guard, for instance."

She looked up from the V-Mail without the expression of someone who had been asked something unexpected. More the expression of someone for whom no counter question arrived entirely without precedent.

"The Coast Guard came to me in November," she said. "There was a piece of mail that came through in the first week of October — addressed to general delivery, no full name. I held it the usual time and then it was collected when they came and asked about it." She returned a bundled set of V-Mail to the tray. "They seemed to know what they were looking for."

He said: "What was the name on it?"

She said: "A. Schell."

She said it in the register she used for all postal information — the same tone as a box number or a forwarding address. A fact that belonged to the record and not to the present conversation. "No one of that name in Coldwater."

He said thank you.

She said the next window was available if he needed a money order, reflexive as a weather report.

He went out.

---

The name occupied him on the walk back the way a word did when he was establishing its origin — present before it was parsed, requiring accommodation before its full weight arrived. *Schell.* German, certainly. The family name distribution was Alemannic in origin — Swiss, Swabian, Rhenish — found also in the eastern valleys, common enough in the nineteenth century, associated in the current decade with territories that had been substantially reorganized in the matter of who remained to carry such names. He brought his attention off that road and back to the practical matter.

A. Schell. General delivery. The first week of October.

Three days, perhaps four, before the body came in on the breakwater.

Someone had known to send mail to this name in this town, or had expected this name to pass through, or had sent something ahead of an arrival that did not proceed as anticipated. These three possibilities had different implications; he filed them with the precision of a man accustomed to distinguishing between interpretations of incomplete evidence and knowing the difference between a hypothesis and a conclusion.

He had thought, last November, walking home from the harbor office — the harbormaster's careful deflection still in his ear, *the Coast Guard has what I know* — about what the German phrase *ein Mensch ohne Papiere* carried that its English equivalent did not. The German phrase sat inside a legal architecture in which papers were not merely documents but the mechanism of recognized personhood: to be without papers was to be without a floor, without the state's acknowledgment that one existed within the category of persons who had standing. He had thought the word *staatenlos* on that walk, and it had arrived with the particular weight it had accumulated over the previous decade, which was not an abstract weight.

The Coast Guard had known the name before they came to claim the mail. He turned this over as he climbed back to the ridge.

The harbor lay below and to the south, flat under the March light, the lobster boats at their moorings, the line of the breakwater along the outer edge of everything. He looked at it for a moment without thinking anything particular about it, and went down toward the house.

---

Lillian had put coffee on the table. She had not mentioned it; the cup was simply there when he sat down, and she had gone somewhere else in the house, which was her operating manner — hospitality delivered as a settled fact, not as a performance.

He took the Cambridge letter from his coat pocket and placed it on the table in front of him. He unfolded it and read it again.

The Emergency Committee's letter of February 4 offered a position at a Boston-area university: visiting instructor in German philology, one-year appointment, renewable upon review, with the possibility of conversion to an assistant professorship if a permanent opening emerged. His specialty was in active demand. The German department had lost two instructors to the services in 1942 and had been covering with graduate students and overloaded faculty since. The salary was given in precise figures.

*Philology.* His field, his training, the subject of three books published before 1938 — two in German, one in English, the English volume on the semantic history of German loan words in English military vocabulary, reviewed with modest interest in London in 1936. He knew what a career in philology looked like from the inside: the departmental meetings, the library, the colleagues who shared a vocabulary sufficient for the work, the particular satisfaction of a problem whose tools were exactly calibrated to it. He had had this life. He recognized its dimensions.

Boston. A city he had passed through twice and not yet understood. Which was not Vienna and not Paris and not London, but was not Coldwater Reach, and there was a meaningful distance between those two absences.

He folded the letter along its creases and held it in both hands.

---

The word *heimisch* arrived, as it usually did, without being summoned.

He had thought it first, in relation to Coldwater Reach, in the early autumn — leaving the civil defense warden meeting, walking fifty yards of the same road home as the harbormaster, and registering, as she turned left at the fork without a further word, that the registration of the whole transaction had been interior and not shared. *Heimisch* was the condition of being absorbed into a place with enough completeness that the place no longer exposed you from the outside — that you moved through it without the constant small effort of translation, the gap between your interior and what the street expected of you. He had been *heimisch* in Vienna before 1937. He had not been *heimisch* anywhere since: not in Paris, not in London, not in the rooming house on the Pentonville Road where he had lived eleven months between the fall of France and his passage to Canada — a room with a coal smell and the particular isolation of a foreign city absorbing a foreign emergency without fully registering it as human. Not in Sherbrooke. Not in Cambridge, which was the closest approximation, the Emergency Committee's milieu, tolerant by institutional habit — but even in Cambridge he had been a category of person usefully placed, which was a different condition from being *heimisch*. *Duldung.* The same grammar, a different address.

He could not say that he was *heimisch* in Coldwater Reach. He also could not say he would be *heimisch* in Boston. He did not know whether the condition could be recovered or whether it had simply been used up in Vienna and could not accumulate again elsewhere, the way certain things were used up rather than relocated when one moved.

He thought about Henry and Lillian. The coffee on the table, unannounced. The chess on Thursday evenings, the lamp over the board, Henry moving his rook with the economical gesture of a man for whom the game had long since stopped being about gestures. The compression of Henry's speech — *than I expected, as well* — in which a great deal that was not said arrived with additional weight in the space the saying left. Henry had been in Coldwater Reach for nineteen years and had not been asked whether he found it *heimisch*; he had made himself indispensable, which was a different and possibly more durable category. Marcus had found, over nineteen months at the Crow house table, that there were things he did not have to translate before speaking, and that this was rarer than it should have been.

He thought about Edna Locke in the empty faculty room in January. The brooch at her collar, the same small brooch every day. The three minutes she had taken, after staying when the others filed out, to say what she had come to say — what she owed a colleague who was also the category of person that made certain members of the school board uneasy, and the calculation she had run in those three minutes about whether the debt was worth paying. She had paid it. He had thanked her precisely and she had gone, and he had thought about the act with a specificity he did not usually apply to gestures from colleagues.

He thought about Hannah Pfeiffer in the second row on Friday morning, her pencil in her hand and her notebook open and her attention somewhere at the window's edge — the farm, probably, or the German, or the combination of the two that she was working with in ways he did not have the full picture of. He had written in the Kurrent in the margin of her examination, which none of the students could read: *sie weiß mehr, als sie zeigt.* She knows more than she shows. She was seventeen and in possession of a language absorbed rather than taught, and she was applying it to something; the shape of the application was recognizable to him as the shape of a person using a second language to think what the first language did not have the vocabulary for.

These were not arguments. He did not believe they needed to be treated as arguments.

---

He took a sheet of paper from the writing block Lillian kept beside the window for household accounts.

He wrote one page. He expressed gratitude for the offer, which he intended with precision — the Committee had done what it existed to do, and the position it had located was a real position in a real department, and ingratitude would be both inaccurate and imprecise. He stated that he intended to remain in Coldwater Reach for the present year. He stated that he would write again in the autumn.

He did not explain further. There was nothing that required explanation — the Committee understood his situation well enough to receive a decision without a narrative, and he did not have a narrative available in any case. He had a decision, legibly arrived at, and that was what a one-page letter to the Emergency Committee required.

He wrote *Dr. M. Klein* at the bottom. He folded the page, sealed the envelope, applied one stamp from the strip of twelve, and set the envelope beside the Cambridge letter on the table.

---

The Cambridge letter lay between the envelope and the salt cellar. Three pages, folded into thirds, the February 4 date at the top of the first page. The salary it described was still the salary. The open position was still open. The Boston department still lacked two instructors. All of it remained factually true and would go on being true and available for the autumn, when he had said he would write again.

He picked up his coffee cup.

He thought: *A. Schell.*

The name sat in him unresolved — a word not yet traced to its root, a question not yet turned over enough times to show what was on its other side. He had asked, and he had the name, and the name would remain there until some future context clarified it or did not. He had been in Coldwater Reach long enough to know that this town did not rush its answers. He had not rushed his own.

Outside the kitchen window the elm held its branches against the March sky, which was the gray of neither winter nor spring, positioned between two seasons with the flat patience of something that had learned not to anticipate. The buds were not open. They were simply there, thickening in the cold, accumulating toward something that could not yet be called a leaf.

input.oneLine: Marcus answers Cambridge with no, commits to staying in Coldwater Reach for the year, and learns the name "A. Schell" from Ada Gile at the post office.

input.summary: March 1944, Saturday. Marcus POV. He leaves the Crow house wearing the older suit — a Saturday loosening of the school week's schedule — with the Cambridge letter (received February 4, offering a visiting instructor position in German philology at a Boston-area university) in his coat pocket for twenty-two days. He walks to the post office ostensibly for stamps, internally testing whether the errand will resolve the decision he has been deferring. At the post office Ada Gile is sorting V-Mail; he buys twelve three-cent stamps. He lingers and asks, carefully framed, whether there has been further inquiry about the man on the breakwater. Ada tells him the Coast Guard came in November, that a piece of mail came through in the first week of October addressed to general delivery with no full name, that she held it the usual time and then the Coast Guard collected it in November — they seemed to know what they were looking for. He asks the name. She says: A. Schell. No one of that name in Coldwater. He thanks her and goes out. The name sits in him on the walk back — he notes its German/Alemannic origin, thinks again about ein Mensch ohne Papiere and staatenlos from his November walking meditation. He sees the harbor from the ridge. At home, Lillian has left coffee on the table without comment. He reads the Cambridge letter again — philology, colleagues, a salary in precise figures, a life he can recognize from the inside. He thinks about heimisch: cannot say he was heimisch in Cambridge, London (the Pentonville Road rooming house, eleven months), Paris, or Coldwater Reach; does not know whether the condition can be recovered. He thinks about Henry and Lillian, about Edna Locke's calculation in the empty faculty room, about Hannah Pfeiffer looking out the window in German class, knowing more than she shows. These are not arguments; he does not treat them as such. He writes one page to the Emergency Committee: grateful for the offer; intending to remain in Coldwater Reach for the present year; will write again in the autumn. No further explanation. Seals it, stamps it. He drinks his coffee and the chapter ends on the name A. Schell sitting unresolved, and the elm outside the window, buds thickening but not yet open. Threads T2 and T3 advanced.
newFacts: Marcus Klein's Cambridge letter is dated February 4, 1944, and offers a visiting instructor position in German philology at a Boston-area university — one year, renewable, possibility of conversion to assistant professorship. The German department there lost two instructors to the services in 1942.
Marcus has been carrying the Cambridge letter for twenty-two days as of this chapter (i.e., he received it approximately February 4, 1944, and the chapter is set in March 1944).

Ada Gile confirms: a piece of mail came through in the first week of October 1943, addressed to general delivery Coldwater Reach, no full name. She held it the usual time. The Coast Guard collected it in November 1943. They seemed to know what they were looking for.
The name on the general-delivery mail item: A. Schell. No person of that name lives in Coldwater Reach.
Marcus buys twelve three-cent stamps on this Saturday visit to the post office.

Marcus's reply to the Emergency Committee at Cambridge (March 1944): he is grateful for the offer; he intends to remain in Coldwater Reach for the present year; he will write again in the autumn. One page, signed Dr. M. Klein. He does not explain further.

Marcus's rooming house in London (between the fall of France and his passage to Canada) was on the Pentonville Road. He lived there eleven months (approximately August 1940 – June/July 1941, consistent with the fall of France in June 1940 and his departure for Canada).
Marcus uses the Crow house writing block (kept by the kitchen window for household accounts) to write his reply to Cambridge.
The elm in the Crow house yard has early buds visible in March 1944 — branch-ends thickening but not yet open.
looseThreads: Thread T2 (The Man Still Moving): Marcus has now committed to staying for the present year — the first genuine act of investment in Coldwater Reach, per the thread outline. The letter is sealed and stamped. He will write to Cambridge again in the autumn (i.e., before or around the November 1944 hearing in ch24); whether he did so is a detail later drafters can handle — the Emergency Committee does not reappear as an active plot element.

Thread T3 (The Hearing): By choosing to stay rather than pre-empting the board action by retreating to Boston, Marcus has committed to facing whatever the board does. The Cambridge letter could have been an exit; he sealed it into a delay instead.

A. Schell: Marcus now has the name. Per the outline, he does not rush to act on it — it sits in him, unresolved, until his Bangor trip in August 1944 (ch19) where he finds the name in the maritime record. No chapter between ch12 and ch19 should show Marcus actively investigating the name.

Thread T1 note: Ada Gile's account of the A. Schell mail will be cross-confirmed from Eira's POV in ch13 (Ada Gile's Window), where Eira learns the additional detail that it was a postcard. Marcus does not know it was a postcard — he only knows 'a piece of mail.'

The Cambridge letter and the Emergency Committee: the letter was dated February 4. Marcus's reply commits him to Coldwater Reach through the academic year. His promise to write again in the autumn must be consistent with whatever ch24 establishes about the November 1944 hearing timeline.

Continuity note: no contradictions with established facts. Good suit / older suit rotation honored (Saturday = older suit, loosely). The harbor smell at the ridge is consistent with ch03 and ch06. Ada Gile's character consistent with ch01 (she is the postmistress; the casualty telegrams pass through her). Edna Locke's January corridor scene referenced consistently with ch09.

facts: The Cambridge letter received by Marcus Klein is dated February 4, 1944. It offers a visiting instructor position in German philology at a Boston-area university — one-year appointment, renewable upon review, with the possibility of conversion to assistant professorship. The German department at this university lost two instructors to the services in 1942 and has been covering with graduate students since.

Marcus Klein has been carrying the Cambridge letter in his coat's inside breast pocket for twenty-two days as of the Saturday in March 1944 that constitutes the chapter's action. He received it approximately February 4, 1944.

Marcus Klein's reply to the Emergency Committee at Cambridge (written March 1944, one page, signed Dr. M. Klein): he is grateful for the offer; he intends to remain in Coldwater Reach for the present year; he will write again in the autumn. He does not explain further. He seals the envelope and applies one of twelve three-cent stamps purchased that morning. Later chapters must treat this commitment as active through at least the November 1944 board hearing (ch24).

Ada Gile confirms to Marcus Klein (March 1944): a piece of mail came through in the first week of October 1943, addressed to general delivery Coldwater Reach, with no full name. She held it the usual time. The Coast Guard came to her in November 1943 and collected it. They seemed to know what they were looking for. The name on the mail item: A. Schell. No person of that name lives in Coldwater Reach. Marcus does not know the item was a postcard — Ada says only 'a piece of mail.' Eira will learn the postcard detail separately in ch13.

The name A. Schell: Marcus Klein first learns this name from Ada Gile in March 1944. He does not act on it immediately. It sits in him unresolved until his Bangor trip in August 1944 (ch19), where he finds the name in the maritime record. No chapter between ch12 and ch19 should show Marcus actively investigating the name A. Schell.

Marcus Klein's London rooming house (the period between the fall of France and his departure to Canada) was on the Pentonville Road. He lived there for eleven months — approximately autumn 1940 through mid-1941, before his passage to Canada and the Sherbrooke internment. This is one of the waypoints in his displacement sequence: Vienna → Paris → London (Pentonville Road, 11 months) → Sherbrooke → Cambridge → Coldwater Reach.

Marcus Klein uses the Crow household writing block (kept beside the kitchen window for Lillian's household accounts) to write his reply to the Cambridge Emergency Committee. This is the same kitchen table at which he and Henry play Thursday chess.

The elm in the Crow house yard had early buds visible in March 1944 — branch-ends thickening but not yet open. In November 1943 (ch06) the elm was bare. The seasonal progression of the elm is a recurring background marker in Marcus's chapters at the Crow house.

entries.definition: The name on a piece of mail addressed to general delivery, Coldwater Reach, received in the first week of October 1943. No person of this name lives in Coldwater Reach. The Coast Guard collected the item in November 1943, apparently knowing the name before they asked for it. First revealed to Marcus Klein by Ada Gile in March 1944 (ch12). Eira Bowman will learn the additional detail that the item was a postcard in ch13. The name connects to the unidentified body on the breakwater; its full significance is developed in ch19 (Bangor) and later.

The address in London, England, where Marcus Klein lodged for eleven months between the fall of France (summer 1940) and his departure for Canada. One of the waypoints in his displacement sequence: Vienna → Paris → London (Pentonville Road) → Sherbrooke, Quebec → Cambridge, MA → Coldwater Reach, Maine.

A letter dated February 4, 1944, from the Emergency Committee in Aid of Displaced Foreign Scholars at Cambridge, Massachusetts, offering Marcus Klein a visiting instructor position in German philology at a Boston-area university. One-year appointment, renewable, with possibility of conversion to assistant professorship. Marcus carries it in his coat pocket for twenty-two days before replying (March 1944) that he intends to remain in Coldwater Reach for the present year and will write again in the autumn.

## Chapter 12 — Two Suits: what was written and why

**Draft** (`draft/12-two-suits.md`, ~2,600 words): Marcus's Saturday unfolds in four movements — dressing (the older suit, the letter checked in the pocket for the twenty-second morning), the walk down to the post office (the March elm, the harbor smell at the ridge, his admission to himself that the errand is also a test), the exchange with Ada Gile (stamps, the careful question, *A. Schell*), and the return to the kitchen table (Lillian's coffee, the Cambridge letter read for the eleventh time, the *heimisch* meditation, the one-page reply, the name sitting unresolved at the close).

**Voice**: Marcus's register held throughout — subordinate clauses doing the work of qualification, words weighed for origin and range, the slight syntactic strangeness of a man translating his own thinking. The Cambridge letter is rendered as genuinely attractive, not obviously inferior, so the choice to stay reads as a decision rather than a retreat.

**Threads advanced**:
- **T2 (The Man Still Moving)**: Marcus seals the reply — the first genuine act of investment in Coldwater Reach, made by process of elimination and something he cannot yet name.
- **T3 (The Hearing)**: By not taking the Boston exit, he has committed to facing whatever the board does.

**New canon logged**:
- Cambridge letter (dated February 4, 1944; Boston-area university; German philology; salary in precise figures)
- Marcus's reply: exact terms per outline, signed Dr. M. Klein, dated March 1944
- Ada Gile's disclosure: *A. Schell*, general delivery, first week October 1943, Coast Guard collected November 1943; Marcus does not learn it was a postcard (Eira gets that detail in ch13)
- Marcus's London rooming house: Pentonville Road, eleven months
- Elm in Crow house yard: early buds in March 1944

input.content:
# Ada Gile's Window

The east wind had come in overnight, and by the time Eira reached the far end of the pier shed the sleet was running sideways — small and sharp, the kind that found the gap between collar and neck without trying. She pulled the collar up and worked the inspection from the pier shed door outward, same as any other morning.

The bollards were holding. The float gangway had come through the winter without requiring attention to the hinge, which she had expected to replace before the new year; she added it to the inspection sheet anyway — *examine before spring season* — and moved on. Roy Blaine's smack was at its mooring, the fenders riding right, the mooring lines showing the correct tension for a northeast wind. The Doris V. was in; Stannard must have come back before the weather moved, which the tide chart had suggested was possible. The coaster berth on the outside face of the pier was empty. It had been empty since the last vessel cleared in February. The inner anchorage was open again — the ice had broken up in the mild spell ten days prior, and the water was back to its March color, which was the gray of old tin under overcast, the channel clear to the bar.

She checked the splice on one of the Doris V.'s mooring lines — showing chafe at the thimble, not urgent but worth noting — and came back into the pier shed out of the wind.

The harbor office mail had been accumulating at the post office for the better part of a week. She had sent one of the dock hands for it the two Thursdays previous, and the week before that, and this morning, without making a decision she could have articulated, she pulled her oilskin off the peg and went herself. The dock hands could manage the pier for an hour. The walk to Reach Road was twenty minutes each way in good weather and somewhat longer in sleet, and she had been meaning to go for a week, and she was done meaning to.

---

The road up from the harbor curved north past the trap storage lot and then east to Reach Road. The east wind was head-on for most of the second leg. She kept her hands in her pockets and walked at the pace the sleet allowed — the pier pace, the one she used on the breakwater in any weather that didn't preclude walking.

The town was quiet at this hour on a Wednesday morning. The hardware was open, a man she didn't look at coming out with a length of pipe under one arm. The pharmacist's light was on behind the glass. The diner on the corner — Burleigh's — was dark at this hour, though it opened Wednesdays; whatever time the proprietor considered reasonable for a Wednesday opening was not this one. A woman she recognized as one of the Harlow girls crossed the road ahead of her and went into the general store without looking up.

The post office stood between the hardware and the pharmacist's, where it had stood since before she was born, in the same shingled building with the same black lettering above the door. She had been coming here since she was small enough to see over the counter only if she stretched. The smell of the place — paper and ink and the particular dry warmth of old wood in an enclosed room — had been the same in every decade she could account for. She had been collecting the harbor office's mail from this counter since 1936, when Owen drowned and the work became hers, and before that she had sometimes accompanied Owen on his post office errands and waited while Ada sorted the official correspondence. The harbor's business passed through here. Lighthouse Service notifications, Bureau of Navigation circulars, V-Mail from vessel crews whose families were in town, letters from the district maritime office in Portland. Ada had been the harbor's institutional memory in that particular way for eight years: she did not volunteer what came through the counter, but what came through the counter she did not forget.

Ada Gile was at the counter with her reading glasses on, sorting V-Mail forms into the pigeonhole grid behind her. The grid held mail for most of the families in town; she worked it without apparent effort, the address on each envelope registered with a glance and the envelope placed in the correct pigeonhole without pause or correction. The glasses chain lay against the collar of her dress. She looked up when the door opened.

"Mrs. Bowman."

"Ada."

Ada set down the envelope she was holding and turned to the shelf behind her and came back with the harbor office's bundle: envelopes bound with a length of brown twine. She set it on the counter and untied the twine.

"Three Bureau of Navigation circulars — district quarterly distribution. The Lighthouse Service quarterly report, came in Tuesday. A V-Mail from the buoy tender schedule coordinator."

Eira checked the return addresses on the circulars. Standard district distribution, all three; she would read them at the desk. The V-Mail from the Samoset's schedule coordinator out of Jonesport was the March buoy-work calendar, or possibly an amendment to the February one, which had had a correction note attached. She tucked the bundle inside her coat and set the V-Mail on top of the counter where she could see it.

She stayed at the counter.

Ada had returned her attention to the pigeonhole grid. She placed three envelopes in three boxes without looking at the boxes — the motion of someone who knows a geography by its contents rather than its coordinates. She did not press Eira with a look or a question. The counter was the counter; people stood at it until their business was done, and Ada understood the difference between business concluded and business ongoing.

"Ada." Eira set the V-Mail aside. "That business last fall. The body."

Ada's hands stopped on the grid. Not the stop of a woman surprised, but the stop of a woman shifting registers with the same attention. She looked up.

"Yes."

"Did the Coast Guard speak with you."

"They did."

"About mail."

Ada set down the envelope she held. She faced Eira directly with the expression she had applied, in Eira's observation, to every postal matter from a certified letter to a change-of-address form: no warmth, no coldness, the accuracy of a woman who dealt in facts and gave them to the people entitled to receive them. "Yes," she said. "In November. A man came and asked, and I gave him a statement about what I had."

"Was there something," Eira said. "Before they came."

Ada considered. The consideration was not hesitation; it was the particular calibration Eira had recognized over eight years — the assessment of what fell within scope, which Ada conducted with the same precision she applied to everything else, and which had less to do with willingness than with the judgment of professional category. She was not a gossip. She answered questions asked correctly and she did not answer questions asked otherwise.

"A postcard," she said. "Came in the first week of October. Addressed to general delivery, Coldwater Reach. The name on it was A. Schell." She paused in the way of someone giving a listener time to be accurate. "No last name used as a first name or any other way I could place it. Coldwater Reach has no one by that name."

Eira said: "What did it say."

Another calibration. A shorter one.

"A date and a time," Ada said. "Nothing else. I took it as a meeting arrangement of some kind."

"The Coast Guard took it."

"In November, yes. Along with a statement from me about when it came in and what the inquiry process was." She reached for the envelope she had put down. "They knew what they were looking for when they came."

Eira said: "Thank you."

Ada nodded once — the acknowledgment that completed a transaction conducted correctly — and returned to the pigeonhole grid. Eira took the V-Mail from the counter and tucked it inside her coat with the rest of the bundle and went out.

---

The sleet had gone over to rain while she was inside. Not heavy — the same gray cold air but with the sharpness dissolved, the drops barely registering on her coat as individual impacts, more a presence than a fact. She walked back down Reach Road into the wind, which had softened along with the sleet. The mud at the road's verges had turned darker with the wet.

She turned onto the harbor road and began the descent. The harbor came back into view below — the moorings in their positions, the flat gray water, the pier shed running out from the near shore. The bar was visible at the harbor's mouth at this tide height, the granite just above the water, gray in the gray light. She went down and in.

---

The coal stove in the northeast corner was cold. She cleared the ash plate, put in kindling from the box beside it, added coal, and stood with her hands open over the first heat until her fingers had come back. The harbor office smelled of paper and the mineral cold of a closed room in March — different from the sleet outside, which was at least moving. She waited for the stove to take hold before sitting down.

She took the plain brown notebook from the second left-hand drawer. The Macomber entries ran across several pages. She turned to them: December — the departure entry at 0430, the question about source, the first *Inquiry?*. February — the conversation on the dock with Hartley, the contract, the papers, the district office in Portland. She had added to these pages when information came to her and not before; she had not gone looking, she had logged what arrived.

She turned to the blank space below the February entry and uncapped her pen.

*A. Schell — general delivery — postcard — Portland postmark — early October. Contents: date and time. No other text. Coast Guard has it.*

She read the line back. She set the pen down and sat back.

She looked at what she had.

A postcard, sent from Portland — the postmark was Portland, which she had not known before this morning — addressed to a name that had no established presence in Coldwater Reach. General delivery meant the sender expected the recipient to pass through the town, to check the post office counter, to know to look for mail under the name A. Schell. No boardinghouse registration, no rental on the town books, no family she had heard of. The name not in any directory she could consult. The Coast Guard had known the name before they arrived to collect the postcard, which was what it meant for them to have come knowing what they were looking for.

The postcard had contained a date and a time.

The Macomber's Navy district contract was out of Portland; this she had from Estelle Hartley, logged in February. The postcard's postmark was Portland. Birch Macomber's harbor fee receipt, dated October 8, was in the bolted drawer below her left hand. He had come in from Calais, lime and general cargo. The postcard had arrived the first week of October — before Macomber docked.

On the night of October ninth, past midnight, she had gone out to the pier to check the Macomber's mooring bridle. At the shore end of the pier, at the outer edge of the overhead lamp's reach, a man had been standing with a canvas bag at his feet, looking at the water. She had not seen his face. She had not spoken to him. She had not written him in the log.

The blow came in on the tenth. The body came in from the anchorage side on the thirteenth, three days later, on an eastern chop.

She did not write any of this down in the notebook.

What she had was a set of coincidences, and coincidences were what the record looked like when it was incomplete. A postcard was not a coaster, and a coaster was not a man on a pier, and a man on a pier was not a drowned man on the breakwater. The four points had positions. They formed a shape. The shape was specific. It was not evidence.

She held the theory the way she held a theory about the set of a mooring or the behavior of a tide at the inlet: as a working assessment from available data, held open to revision, not committed until confirmed. A postcard from Portland arranged a meeting in Coldwater Reach in early October — a date, a time, nothing else. A man who knew to look for it under the name A. Schell had come to this town without a local address and without, apparently, a boardinghouse or any other arrangement that left a mark in the town's record. The coaster running a Portland district contract had been at the outside face of the pier since the eighth. The man was at the pier after midnight on the ninth. Three days after the blow he was on the breakwater.

The theory explained the shape.

What she could not answer: what was the meeting? Who had sent the postcard from Portland?

What she could not ask without answering the first question: how to approach either inquiry without clarifying why she was making it, which would require clarifying what the existing record was missing, which was the man on the pier on the ninth of October, which was why she was sitting at this desk with the plain brown notebook and not the official incident log.

She had not committed a falsehood. The vessel-traffic column for the night of October ninth was marked with a dash, her convention for no anomalies noted, and there had been no anomaly recorded — what there had been was an observation she had not recorded, which was a distinction that remained technically accurate. She had been living inside this distinction for five months. She understood it precisely and she did not expect to resolve it today.

She capped the pen. She closed the notebook and put it back in the second left-hand drawer.

She got up and went to the percolator. She measured four scoops of grounds into the tin and filled it from the water jug and set it on the small burner beside the stove. While it heated she stood at the south window and looked at the harbor.

The rain had thinned to almost nothing. The anchorage was flat and dark, the moorings standing in their positions — Blaine's smack, the Doris V. — as they had stood since October, more or less, the water between them still and gray-green under the overcast. The bar at the harbor's mouth was covered again on the rising tide. The channel ran clear.

The percolator began to sound.

She took her enamel mug from the left hook below Owen's barometer and set it on the shelf beside the stove.

She did not pour the coffee.

input.oneLine: Eira visits Ada Gile and learns the general-delivery mail was a postcard from Portland — a date and a time — and sits alone with a theory she cannot put in the official log.

input.summary: March 1944, Eira POV. A Wednesday morning of east wind and sleet; the harbor is quiet, the inner anchorage recently opened after the February freeze. Eira does her morning inspection (bollards, float gangway, moorings — Blaine's smack and the Doris V. at moorings, coaster berth empty) and then, instead of sending a dock hand as usual, walks herself up to the post office on Reach Road. Ada Gile is sorting V-Mail behind the counter; she gives Eira the harbor mail bundle (three Bureau of Navigation circulars, Lighthouse Service quarterly, V-Mail from buoy tender schedule coordinator). Eira stays at the counter and asks, framed as an administrative inquiry, whether the Coast Guard spoke with Ada about the body last fall. Ada confirms: yes, in November. A postcard came in the first week of October, addressed to general delivery Coldwater Reach, name A. Schell — no person by that name in town. Ada held it the usual time; the Coast Guard collected it in November, knowing what they were looking for before they asked. Eira presses one question further than Marcus did in ch12: What did it say? Ada, having calibrated the question as within scope, answers: a date and a time, nothing else — a meeting arrangement. Eira thanks her and goes. The sleet has turned to rain on the walk back. At the harbor office she banks the coal stove, takes out the plain brown notebook from the second left-hand drawer, and writes the new entry below the February Macomber lines: 'A. Schell — general delivery — postcard — Portland postmark — early October. Contents: date and time. No other text. Coast Guard has it.' She sits back and works through the theory: a postcard from Portland (the same city as Macomber's Navy district contract) arranged a meeting in Coldwater Reach for a man with no local address; the man was at the pier past midnight on October 9 with a canvas bag; the body was found on the breakwater three days after the blow. The shape is specific. She does not write the connection down — it is coincidence, not evidence — but the shape is tighter now. She cannot investigate further without exposing what the official record is missing. She makes coffee and does not drink it. Thread T1 (The Blank in the Log) advanced.
newFacts: The mail item addressed to A. Schell was a postcard, not merely a 'piece of mail' — this detail Eira learns from Ada that Marcus did not learn in ch12 (Marcus did not ask what the item said or what format it was in).
The postcard was sent with a Portland postmark, first week of October 1943 — the postmark city is Portland, confirmed by Ada to Eira in March 1944.
The postcard's contents: a date and a time, nothing else. Ada took it as a meeting arrangement. This is the full content of the postcard.
Ada Gile stated that the Coast Guard arrived in November 1943 knowing what they were looking for before they asked for it — they came with knowledge of the name A. Schell and the mail item.

Eira's working theory (March 1944): A. Schell received a postcard from Portland arranging a meeting in Coldwater Reach in early October 1943; the man came; the man at the pier on the night of October 9 with a canvas bag was likely this man; three days after the blow he was on the breakwater.
Eira's March 1944 personal log entry (verbatim): 'A. Schell — general delivery — postcard — Portland postmark — early October. Contents: date and time. No other text. Coast Guard has it.'
On this March 1944 morning, the Doris V. (Stannard) is in, and Blaine's smack is at moorings. The coaster berth on the outside face of the pier is empty.
The inner anchorage ice broke up approximately ten days before this chapter's action (mid-March 1944), consistent with the world.md note that the anchorage is open water again by late March.
One of the Doris V.'s mooring lines shows chafe at the thimble as of mid-March 1944 — not urgent but noted in the inspection sheet.
Eira goes herself to the post office on Reach Road rather than sending a dock hand — the first time in several weeks she has done so.

The harbor office mail bundle for mid-March 1944: three Bureau of Navigation circulars (district quarterly distribution), Lighthouse Service quarterly report (arrived Tuesday), V-Mail from the Samoset buoy tender's schedule coordinator out of Jonesport.
looseThreads: Thread T1 (The Blank in the Log): The theory is formed and tighter. The postcard (Portland postmark, date and time, A. Schell, general delivery) connects to Macomber's Portland contract in Eira's private reckoning — but she cannot investigate further without first accounting for what the official record is missing, namely the man on the pier on October 9. This is the structural impasse that holds until ch16 (The Inspector) and ch20 (Addendum).
Who sent the postcard from Portland? This question is raised explicitly and left unanswered. It will remain in shadow — the spine question is deliberate in leaving this unresolved as a fact.

What was the meeting? The date and time on the postcard: Eira does not know which date or time was specified. This information does not surface in the narrative — the postcard is in Coast Guard custody and its specific date/time is never disclosed to Eira.

Ada's information that the Coast Guard 'knew what they were looking for' implies prior intelligence about the name A. Schell — this is consistent with the spine question's deliberate ambiguity about how the body's identity eventually becomes known to authorities.
The Doris V. mooring line chafe at the thimble: a minor maintenance note, not a narrative thread, but should be consistent if Eira references current harbor maintenance issues in later chapters.

Thread T1 continuity: ch16 (The Inspector) has Eira making a second deliberate withholding — of the name A. Schell when speaking to Commander Whitmore. The theory formed here provides the context for that choice.

Ada's calibration and scope: She answers what is asked correctly; she did not mention Marcus Klein's earlier inquiry (Saturday, ch12) to Eira. This is consistent with her character — she does not report one inquiry to another asker. Later drafters must not have Ada volunteering information about who else asked.

text: Now let me log all continuity facts and any glossary entries.

facts: The mail item addressed to 'A. Schell' at Coldwater Reach general delivery was a postcard — this specific format was confirmed by Ada Gile to Eira Bowman in March 1944. Marcus Klein (ch12) learned only that it was 'a piece of mail'; Eira is the first to know it was a postcard.
The A. Schell postcard bore a Portland postmark. This is confirmed by Ada Gile to Eira in March 1944. The postmark city (Portland) is now canon.

The A. Schell postcard's full contents: a date and a time, nothing else. Ada Gile read it and took it as a meeting arrangement. The specific date and time are not known to Eira or to the reader — they were on the postcard, which is in Coast Guard custody.

The Coast Guard arrived at Ada Gile's post office in November 1943 already knowing what they were looking for — per Ada's statement to Eira, 'They knew what they were looking for when they came.' This implies prior intelligence about the name A. Schell before the post office inquiry.

Eira's March 1944 personal log entry, verbatim (the third substantive entry in the Macomber series): 'A. Schell — general delivery — postcard — Portland postmark — early October. Contents: date and time. No other text. Coast Guard has it.'

Eira's working theory, formed March 1944: A postcard from Portland arranged a meeting in Coldwater Reach in early October 1943 (a date and a time, no other information) for a man using the name A. Schell who had no local address. The Macomber coaster, on a Portland Navy district contract, was at the outside face of the pier October 8–11. A man with a canvas bag was at the shore end of the pier past midnight October 9. The body came in from the anchorage side three days after the blow. Eira holds this as a working theory, not a conclusion, and does not commit it to the official log.

Eira does not write the postcard-Macomber-pier connection in any official log. She writes only the A. Schell postcard facts in her personal notebook. The official incident log remains unchanged through ch13. This remains the case until the addendum filed in ch20.

Ada Gile does not report Marcus Klein's Saturday inquiry (ch12) to Eira Bowman (Wednesday of the same week, ch13). She does not mention one asker to another. This is her established professional practice and must remain consistent.

The inner anchorage of Coldwater Reach was open water again by mid-March 1944 — the ice broke up approximately ten days before the main action of ch13. This is consistent with world.md ('open water again by late March').

As of mid-March 1944: Roy Blaine's smack is at mooring; the Doris V. (Delmar Stannard) is in. The coaster berth on the outside face of the main pier is empty. No vessel traffic since the previous Tuesday.

One of the Doris V.'s mooring lines shows chafe at the thimble as of mid-March 1944 — noted in Eira's inspection sheet as not urgent. This is the same type of defect (chafe at the thimble) she had noted on the Macomber's mooring bridle in early October 1943 (ch01/ch10).

The harbor office mail bundle for mid-March 1944 (a Wednesday): three Bureau of Navigation district quarterly circulars, Lighthouse Service quarterly report (arrived the previous Tuesday), V-Mail from the Samoset buoy tender schedule coordinator out of Jonesport. These are the standard categories of official mail the harbor office receives.

Eira Bowman goes herself to the post office on Reach Road in mid-March 1944, rather than sending a dock hand as she has been doing for the previous several weeks. This is the occasion for the Ada Gile conversation.

Burleigh's diner is dark before opening time on a Wednesday morning in March 1944 — consistent with world.md which states it closes on Tuesdays and presumably opens at some reasonable hour on other days. No new information; consistency confirmed.

The postmark on the A. Schell postcard (general delivery, Coldwater Reach, first week of October 1943).
