input.content:
# What Henry Notices

The piano lid in the music room was up again.

Henry set his bucket down and brought the lid closed, careful not to let it drop. Talmadge taught summer students on Wednesdays and Fridays — children whose parents wanted something to occupy the weeks before September — and she never remembered the lid. He always did. The instrument hadn't held its pitch reliably since the pipe-sweat situation above the ceiling in 1941, but that was no argument for leaving it open to the August humidity. He closed it and straightened the bench against the keys.

He started at the top of the building and worked down. Third floor to first: the mop bucket was easier on the stairs in that direction, and the third floor stayed cleaner than the floors below because people used those rooms and left. They didn't linger. The building at six in the evening was not quiet — it was never quite quiet; it lowered itself into a register of pipes and settling joists and the particular creak the third-floor crossbeam made when the temperature dropped toward dark — but it was as empty as it got. Three classrooms and a storage room for the band chairs and stands, plus the room labeled EXAMINATIONS since 1931 and still labeled that, though what was examined in it had changed twice since Henry arrived. He collected two music stands from the hall and racked them. He checked the east window latch, which had been intermittently failing since the winter of 1937. The requisition for a new mechanism had gone through three times without result. He was prepared to submit it a fourth.

Down one flight.

The second floor was the floor he knew most specifically. He'd been cleaning Edna Locke's history room for nineteen years, which meant he knew it the way she knew it and in different ways: where the chalk tray collected deepest, which desk wobbled on its back leg, and which corner of the canvas map on the east wall had begun to lift from its mount. The map had been there before Henry arrived and would be there after; Edna had been there longer still. The red pin in Normandy had gone in on the sixth of June; he had been mopping this same floor two days after when she drove it in. The pin in Florence was newer — the paper head still bright red against the faded coastline — put in last week when the news came over the radio. He moved the wastebasket from under the radiator, where it always drifted, back to its place below the chalk rail.

He went through the other second-floor rooms in order: the science room with its smell of sulfur that never entirely left, Marcus's room with the conjugation paradigms still written in neat columns on the right-hand half of the blackboard, the small room where Thibodeau taught mathematics to whoever still needed it at that hour.

The principal's office at the end of the hall: dark, door locked. Through the glass panel above the handle he could see the desk, and on the desk a letter on school committee stationery — the blue-grey kind with the raised seal in the upper corner. He could read the name in the address block; he recognized it. He could read the first sentence of the first paragraph, which said *the matter of Dr. Klein's continued appointment*, and he didn't read further.

He had not developed the habit of reading what sat on principals' desks. Nineteen years in this building had not given him that habit.

He mopped the second-floor hallway and the north bathroom and went down.

---

The teacher's lounge was off the main hall — originally a loading dock, still with the old iron rings along one wall and a floor that sloped slightly toward the former freight door. Three wastebaskets. A long table, six chairs. The coffee urn on the sideboard, there when Henry started and going to outlast him, the same as it had outlasted the urn before it. He knew this room's particular habits: the two cups always put in the basket under the sideboard rather than the sink, the chalk-dust footprints that gathered near the door in winter, the way the third basket — near the table, near the spot where people stood and talked in the mornings before the classrooms filled — collected things of a different kind than the others. Papers reconsidered. Notes started and set aside.

He went through them in order. Near the door: corrected papers, a wax paper bag, a pencil ground to nothing. Under the sideboard: two coffee cups in the basket instead of the sink, which was a practice teachers had and would keep. The third basket held one piece of paper that had been crumpled and then worked nearly flat again — the creases still in it, the corners not quite square — as if whoever crumpled it had paused and then decided to leave it anyway.

He didn't take it out.

From where he stood he could read the header in block capitals: *Petition to the Coldwater Reach School Committee*. Three names below it in a column. He recognized all three. Two he knew as parents: a woman whose daughter sat in the junior class, a man whose son had played cornet at last May's spring concert. The third was Seavey, who sat on the school committee. Henry had been doing evening work in the building when three board meetings ran past six o'clock; he'd seen Seavey in the corridor on two of those occasions, coming out.

He smoothed the paper enough to read the first sentence.

*We, the undersigned, respectfully request a formal review of the employment of Dr. Marcus Klein on the grounds of his foreign national status and the uncertain loyalties attendant thereto.*

He put the paper back. The same angle against the basket's side. Crumpled the same way it had been.

He emptied the other two baskets, swapped the liners, and mopped the floor from the windows back toward the door. He finished the building at half past seven, locked the front door, and walked home.

The August evening had some cool in it from the sea, not much. The street was quiet — Burleigh's dark, the post office dark, a light or two in the upstairs rooms of houses along the main road, and in one of them a radio playing something he couldn't make out from the street. The elms on the last block before the turn had gone darker than the sky behind them. He walked steadily and didn't think about the basket.

---

Lillian had made corned beef hash with the last of the week's red points. Marcus was at the kitchen table with his grade book open beside his plate, working through it in the tight Kurrent hand he used for his own notes — the letters pressed close and vertical, different from what went on the blackboard. Henry sat down across from him. Lillian brought the food.

They ate. Lillian asked Marcus whether the window in his room was still sticking; he said he'd used the block plane Henry had left; she said good. Nobody said more for a while. Outside, the late August light was still in the elm beyond the kitchen window.

When the plates were clear, Lillian took her tea to the front room. The kitchen settled.

Henry moved a piece of bread along the edge of his plate. He said: "You have at least one friend on the board. Edna Locke."

Marcus looked up from the grade book.

"And you have organized opposition. Not many people. But they have a draft petition."

A moment passed. Marcus said: "How organized?"

"Three names. One of them is on the committee."

Marcus was quiet. He set his pen inside the grade book and closed it. After a moment: "Do you want to tell me how you know?"

"Not particularly," Henry said.

Marcus nodded once. He said: "Thank you, Henry."

Henry moved the piece of bread. "You already knew something was coming. Now you know what it looks like."

"Yes," Marcus said.

"That's all I've got."

Marcus's hand rested on the cover of the grade book. He looked at the window. The elm outside still held a little of the last light.

Henry got up and carried his plate to the sink. Marcus brought his. They washed the dishes together — Henry washing, Marcus drying — the water running hot enough to raise a little steam in the cooling kitchen. Neither of them said anything. After a while the light in the elm was gone, and then it was just the sound of the water and the clatter of clean plates going into the rack, and after that the silence when the tap was off and the work was done.

input.summary: August 1944, Henry Crow's sole POV chapter. Henry works the school building top-to-bottom in his 90-minute evening routine. In the music room: piano lid up again (Talmadge). In Edna Locke's history room: the Normandy pin (June) and a fresh Florence pin (last week, August ~11). At the principal's office: through the glass panel he reads the first line — 'the matter of Dr. Klein's continued appointment' — on school committee blue-grey stationery; he does not linger. In the teacher's lounge: the third wastebasket holds a crumpled-then-partly-smoothed sheet — a Petition to the Coldwater Reach School Committee, three signatories (two parents: one with a daughter in the junior class, one whose son played cornet in May; the third is Seavey, a school committee member). Henry reads only the first sentence, puts the paper back exactly as found, finishes the building, and walks home. At the Crow house kitchen table: Lillian serves supper; Marcus annotates his grade book in Kurrent. After Lillian retires to the front room, Henry tells Marcus he has at least one board friend (Edna Locke) and organized opposition — three names, one on the committee. Marcus asks how Henry knows; Henry says not particularly. Marcus thanks him. Henry: 'You already knew something was coming. Now you know what it looks like.' 'That's all I've got.' They wash dishes together in silence; the chapter ends when the tap goes off.
newFacts: The petition against Marcus Klein is titled 'Petition to the Coldwater Reach School Committee.' Its opening sentence: 'We, the undersigned, respectfully request a formal review of the employment of Dr. Marcus Klein on the grounds of his foreign national status and the uncertain loyalties attendant thereto.'

The petition has three signatories as of August 1944: two parents of Coldwater Reach students (one with a daughter in the junior class, one whose son played cornet in the spring concert, May 1944) and a school committee member named Seavey.

The petition was found crumpled and partially re-smoothed in the third wastebasket of the teacher's lounge (the one near the long table, near the coffee urn). It was deposited and then reconsidered, then left.
Henry Crow works the school building in a fixed routine: top floor to first floor, approximately 90 minutes on weeknights, starting at 6 p.m., finishing around 7:30 p.m.

The principal's office has a glass panel above the door handle, through which Henry can read documents on the desk without entering. The school committee's formal stationery is blue-grey with a raised seal in the upper corner.

Edna Locke has been at Coldwater Reach High School since before Henry arrived (Henry started 1924-1925; Edna since 1929 per the glossary — but the glossary says 1919). Henry has cleaned her room for nineteen years.
Seavey: a school committee member seen by Henry in the school corridor on two occasions when board meetings ran late. One of three petition signatories against Marcus Klein.
Miss Talmadge: teaches piano to summer students at the school on Wednesdays and Fridays in August. Repeatedly leaves the piano lid up; Henry closes it.
The east window latch in the music room has been failing since winter 1937. Henry has filed the requisition for a replacement three times without result.

The Florence pin in Edna Locke's history room map was added approximately one week before this chapter, corresponding to the news of the liberation of Florence (August 11, 1944). This places the chapter at approximately August 18-20, 1944.

Henry's conversation with Marcus at the kitchen table takes place after Lillian retires to the front room with her tea. Henry and Marcus then wash dishes together without speaking — this is the chapter's closing image.
Thread T3 (The Hearing) advanced: Marcus now knows the petition exists, has approximately three names behind it, and has one identified friend on the board (Edna Locke).
looseThreads: Thread T3 (The Hearing): Who are the exact three signatories? The petition has three named people — but Henry only notes them in passing and does not relay the specific names to Marcus. The drafter of ch24 (The Hearing) should note: the petition originated from parents (one with a daughter in junior class, one whose son played cornet) and Seavey on the committee. At least three dissenting votes are consistent with the final 5-2 vote in ch24.

The petition was crumpled and partially smoothed — implying someone reconsidered discarding it and then discarded it anyway. The identity and significance of who prepared this draft is not resolved. It may be a failed first attempt that was superseded by a second version, or the final discarded draft after the formal petition was already filed.

The principal's letter on school committee stationery re: 'the matter of Dr. Klein's continued appointment' — this is separate from the petition and implies the board has already begun to act formally. Consistent with Thread T3 building toward the November 1944 hearing in ch24.

Henry tells Marcus he has 'at least one friend on the board' — Edna Locke. This is consistent with ch09 (Edna's private warning) and ch24 (Edna present as faculty representative). Subsequent chapters should not contradict that Edna is the faculty ally.
Henry does not reveal how he learned about the petition. This deliberate non-explanation is a permanent closure — no later chapter should have Henry reveal the source to Marcus.

text: Now log all the new continuity facts.

facts: The anti-Marcus petition's full title is 'Petition to the Coldwater Reach School Committee.' Its opening sentence (as read by Henry in August 1944): 'We, the undersigned, respectfully request a formal review of the employment of Dr. Marcus Klein on the grounds of his foreign national status and the uncertain loyalties attendant thereto.'

The petition has three signatories: two parents of Coldwater Reach High School students and a school committee member named Seavey. The two parents: one has a daughter in the junior class (as of August 1944), one has a son who played cornet in the May 1944 spring concert. Seavey's given name is not established.
Henry Crow has been seen by Seavey in the school building corridor on two occasions when board meetings ran past six o'clock. Henry recognizes Seavey's name from this exposure.

Henry Crow's after-hours school routine: starts at 6 p.m. on weeknights, works top floor (third) to first floor, finishes at approximately 7:30 p.m. (half past seven). Routine takes approximately 90 minutes.
The Coldwater Reach High School principal's office has a glass panel above the door handle. The school committee's formal stationery is blue-grey with a raised seal in the upper corner.

A formal letter concerning 'the matter of Dr. Klein's continued appointment' was visible on Principal Fenn's desk in August 1944, written on school committee blue-grey stationery, addressed to a committee member whose name Henry recognized.

The petition was found by Henry in the third wastebasket of the teacher's lounge (the one near the long table and coffee urn), crumpled and then partially re-smoothed, as if discarded after reconsideration.
Miss Talmadge teaches piano to summer students at Coldwater Reach High School on Wednesdays and Fridays in August. She consistently leaves the piano lid up; Henry closes it each time.
The music room's east window latch has been intermittently failing since winter 1937. Henry has submitted a requisition for a replacement three times without result.

The Florence liberation news (August 11, 1944) reached Coldwater Reach via radio, and Edna Locke added a pin to her wall map of Europe approximately one week afterward — consistent with the chapter date of approximately August 18-20, 1944.

Henry Crow did not tell Marcus Klein how he learned about the petition. This non-disclosure is deliberate and permanent — he has navigated the boundary between custodial access and investigative access for nineteen years and will not compromise it.
Henry Crow named Edna Locke to Marcus Klein as 'at least one friend on the board' in August 1944. This is consistent with Edna's role as the faculty representative at the ch24 board hearing.
Henry and Marcus wash dishes together after the August 1944 conversation — Henry washing, Marcus drying — without speaking further. This is the chapter's final image.

input.content:
# August

The bus out of Coldwater Reach left at seven-fifteen, before the heat was fully in the day. By the time it reached Harrington the trees had come down to both shoulders and the particular blue-grey of early August sat on every visible hill.

Marcus had taken the window seat on the shaded side — the right side, southbound. He had made this trip once before, in late 1942, to examine a document the Bangor collection held, and he had remembered the seat. The brown paper bag from Lillian was on the seat beside him. She had set it by the door without mentioning it when he left the house at six-thirty, as she did on days when he was going somewhere long. He recognized the wax-paper fold that meant ginger snaps.

The bus was half-empty. Ahead of him, a woman with two boys who were managing their August restlessness with varying success, the smaller one grinding a wooden toy against the window frame with methodical persistence. In the seat behind Marcus, a man in farm clothes who had fallen asleep before Machias and stayed that way, his hat brim down, chin on his chest, apparently having determined that this particular morning was best experienced unconscious. At the back of the bus, a soldier in summer service dress — a private, young enough that the uniform had not yet settled into its right creases — looking out at the roadside with the focused inattention Marcus recognized as trained rather than natural.

The road from Machias to Ellsworth ran through forty miles of spruce and fir. Marcus watched the trees for a while, and then stopped watching them, and thought about what he was doing.

He had been sitting with the name *A. Schell* since March.

Ada Gile had given it to him across the post office counter — in the manner she mentioned anything, which was factually and without inflection, as one item among the items in the ordinary mail that had turned strange. A postcard addressed to A. Schell at Coldwater Reach, general delivery, Portland postmark, received the first week of October 1943. No person of that name in the town; she had looked, as a matter of course. The Coast Guard had collected it the following month, appearing to know the name already before they asked about it.

That was all she had said. He had carried the name home, placed it in the back of his thinking, and left it there for five months.

He had considered, in those months, several explanations for why he was now on a bus to Bangor.

The most straightforward: the philologist's reflex. A name without a referent was an unfinished sentence. He had spent thirty years training himself to follow a reference until it pointed somewhere, and this habit was not something he switched off at the door of professional necessity — it went with him wherever he went, like the good suit, like the notebook. He had looked for A. Schell in every record that passed through the school, the harbor, the post office in his ordinary course. He had found nothing. A good library was the next place.

The second explanation was less comfortable: he had no right to pursue this. The body on the breakwater was Eira Bowman's professional question and had been since October 1943. He was a German and Latin teacher who had been peripherally consulted in November and not consulted since. Whatever had happened at the pier the night before the body came in was not his to investigate; it never had been.

He examined this objection on the road through Harrington, found it accurate, and could not make it stop him.

The third explanation he had not yet put into sentences, and he was not going to on the bus.

He ate the cheese sandwich at the Ellsworth stop, standing outside beside the bus, because the morning was still cool enough to stand in and the cheese was sharp the way Lillian made it — extra mustard, which she had apparently determined he preferred through some observational process he had never narrated to her. The Penobscot River appeared in pieces through the trees south of Bangor, catching the morning light in the gaps. The soldier at the back of the bus stood on the roadside during the stop and smoked, and looked at nothing in particular, and when the driver called out he got back on without finishing the cigarette.

Bangor at noon.

---

He knew how to work a library. He had worked the university reading room in Vienna for nine years; the Bibliothèque nationale for eight months under increasingly difficult conditions; the British Library Reading Room through the summer of 1940 in a state he did not review in detail. The Bangor Public Library was a good stone building, well-organized, its collection adequate for most working purposes. He went directly to the periodicals desk and asked for the *Bangor Daily News* from October through November 1943, in whatever form they held them.

Microfilm, the reference librarian said. She was a precise woman of perhaps forty-five who wore her glasses on a cord and handled the request with the manner of someone who has found all requests to be reasonable at the level of category, if not always at the level of execution.

He sat at the reader and moved through the October issues at a working pace — not hurried, not slow. The *Bangor Daily News* ran its shipping notices on the third page of each edition, in a narrow column of compressed type: vessel names, ports of origin, tonnage when significant, Portland arrivals and departures listed separately from the Bangor and Rockland columns. He had verified this format before making the trip. He had verified the library's holdings. He had done this kind of work many times in many languages and was not sentimental about the mechanics of it; the mechanics were the point.

October 8th issue. Portland column:

*SS Ångermanälven, Swedish, arr. Portland Oct. 7 from Halifax, Capt. Lindqvist, manifest filed.*

He read it twice. Then he moved forward.

October 13th issue:

*SS Ångermanälven, dep. Portland Oct. 12, bound Halifax.*

He paused on that. Arrived the seventh, departed the twelfth: five days in Portland. Ada Gile had said the postcard carried a Portland postmark and had been received the first week of October. The ship was in port through the twelfth. Someone had mailed a postcard from Portland to a general delivery address in a small coastal town, and five days later a man had gone into the water during a passage from Portland to Halifax.

He took out his notebook and wrote, in Kurrent: *Å. Ankunft Portland 7. Okt. Abfahrt 12. Okt.*

Then he moved to November.

The seamen's bureau notices appeared irregularly — when there was something to report, not on a daily cycle. He moved through three weeks of November issues, past harvest-market reports and the winter weather calendar and a notice about a lost lobster boat near Vinalhaven, before he found it in the November 17th edition. Three lines at the bottom of the maritime column, below a note about Jonesport vessel registrations:

*SS Ångermanälven reports crew member missing, August Schell, German national, Swedish citizen, age approx. 40, missing during Portland to Halifax passage, October 10–12, 1943. Contact Seamen's Bureau, Portland.*

He sat with this.

The name was complete now. Not *A. Schell*, with its administrative flatness, its reduction of a person to an initial and a surname — but *August Schell*, with the full first name that the seamen's bureau form required, which gave the man a different weight. August. A German first name. A month. Both, in 1943, carrying their own freight.

He took the notebook out again and wrote: *August Schell. Deutsch. Schwed. Staatsbürger. Geb. ca. 1903. Ångermanälven. Okt. '43.* Not a summary; a fixing of facts in the form he used for things that needed to hold their shape. He looked at the page for a moment. Then he put the notebook in his coat pocket, stood up from the reader, and returned the microfilm to the reference librarian.

She thanked him for coming in on such a warm day. He thanked her for the access. Both statements were courteous and accurate.

---

He had requested, two weeks earlier by letter, a copy of a journal article he had been meaning to read since January — a piece on medieval dialect transmission in the Passau corridor, from a 1943 volume of a philological journal the Bangor collection carried. It was waiting in a paper envelope at the reading room desk.

He sat at the table by the window, which had the right afternoon angle, and read it for an hour.

The argument was careful and, in one place where it addressed a competing claim Marcus had never found convincing, quietly devastating. The author had assembled fifteen years of material on high-vowel persistence in the sub-Alpine dialects and the assembly showed — in the way that scholarly work shows its quality not in the conclusion reached but in the intelligence of the path taken to it. Marcus made three marginal notes in Kurrent pencil on a notecard he had brought for the purpose. He knew he could not keep the journal. He read the article once through and then a second time for the structure of the argument, and on the second reading confirmed the first.

Then he returned the envelope to the desk, said goodbye to the librarian — who was occupied with a boy's schoolbook question and acknowledged him with a nod — and went outside and ate the apples and the ginger snaps on a bench in the August sun.

He had known the article would be exactly as good as the abstract had suggested. He had been correct.

---

He caught the three o'clock bus south.

For twenty minutes below Bangor the Penobscot River was at his window, widening where the road ran along the bank, the afternoon light on the water going from pewter in the direct sun to something darker, almost bronze, where the tree-shadows fell from the far shore. A heron stood very still at the muddy margin. The light was the kind of light that makes you believe a country is beautiful, which it is, alongside everything else that it is. Marcus watched it until the road curved inland and the spruce came back.

He thought about August Schell.

*German national, Swedish citizen.* Born, by the arithmetic of *age approximately 40*, somewhere around 1903. Which meant thirty-five when the Anschluss happened — when the annexation folded Austria into the Reich and made the taxonomy of what the state owned and what it would do a single category regardless of where you were born within its new perimeter. Thirty-seven when France fell. Thirty-nine when Marcus arrived in Coldwater Reach in March 1942.

A man who had — at some point in the decade between 1930 and 1940 — left Germany, or acquired Swedish citizenship, or both in some sequence, and was sailing neutral merchant vessels through Atlantic waters in wartime under a Swedish flag. This was a specific biography. Neutral ships were not safe. They ran the same water as military tonnage and took torpedoes from submarines that couldn't always read flags in the North Atlantic winter. Sailing that water on a German name with Swedish papers required a particular calculation — the calculation you made when the alternative was to become legible to a state that had stopped being interested in your continued existence as a free person.

Marcus recognized the arithmetic of that calculation. Not from having made it himself in that form — his route had run overland, through documents of a different kind, and he had arrived here by a different sequence of rooms. But the shape of the calculation was familiar. He knew what it looked like from a particular kind of inside.

He was not a spy.

Of this Marcus was nearly certain, with the certainty that belongs to a man who has considered a question carefully over five months, which is not the same as the certainty of evidence, and he did not confuse the two. He was a philologist; he was trained to build readings from incomplete evidence and to hold them open to revision. He built this one and held it open.

What the evidence said: a German-born man with Swedish citizenship sailed a neutral merchant vessel into Portland in the first week of October 1943. A postcard went out from Portland to a small Maine harbor town's general delivery — a date, a time — addressed to the name he sailed under. He came to the town. He died on the night of October ninth, or the night of October tenth, or sometime in the three days afterward, and three days later he was on the breakwater.

What Marcus had brought to the evidence: the recognition of a certain kind of displacement. A man who had made himself difficult to find on paper while continuing to move through the world — who had become, in the technical German sense, *staatenlos* in everything but the letter of the documents, because the state that might have claimed him was a state he had chosen not to belong to. This was not the profile of an agent. This was the profile of a man trying to remain alive and useful in a world where those conditions had become complicated.

He came for the meeting. He drowned in a storm. Nobody knew what had happened at the pier.

Marcus held the reading, and held the uncertainty inside it, and watched the last of the river light disappear behind the trees.

The Harrington marshes came in on the left. The smell of the coast arrived through the bus window before the road turned back toward it — salt, low-tide mud, the particular cool that the sea put into the air even in August.

He thought, without urgency, about whether to say anything to anyone. To Henry. To Eira Bowman, who was after all the person whose professional work made the question hers by right. To Commander Whitmore at the naval district, theoretically, although that would require an explanation of how a high school teacher came to be working the periodicals room in Bangor on a Wednesday in August, and the explanation would change the question from what had happened to what Marcus was doing asking.

He thought about it in the manner of a man who has already answered a question and is reviewing the answer.

He was not going to say anything.

The most honest account he could give of why: August Schell had made himself difficult to file, by intention and at some cost, and he had died in that condition. He had been, in the hours at the pier, the man with the canvas bag and the Portland postmark and the Swedish papers who had come to a meeting in a small town where no one knew his name — which was, in a specific and non-trivial sense, the condition of many men Marcus had known, before the war made the condition catastrophic rather than merely difficult. The official determination of such a man's identity and circumstances was never adequate to the man. Marcus had sat in enough rooms where such determinations were made to know this from a direction that was not theoretical.

He was not going to add August Schell to a file.

Whether this was wisdom or something that resembled wisdom without quite being it, he could not entirely say. He knew the note in his coat pocket, written in a script that no one in Coldwater Reach could read without being trained to read it. He was not going to transcribe it into anything else.

---

The bus came into Coldwater Reach at seven minutes past seven.

The harbor smell was there before the road turned toward the ridge — diesel and salt and the low-tide accumulation at the north end of the inner anchorage that was particular to August. Marcus stepped down with his coat over his arm and the empty paper bag folded into his pocket and walked the four blocks to the Crow house.

The kitchen light was on. Through the window he could see the table set for three, and Lillian at the stove, and beyond her the elm in the yard still holding the last of the evening light in its upper branches in the way it did this time of year — the leaves barely moving, the light going out of them slowly rather than all at once, the way light did in August when it meant to stay.

He stood on the sidewalk for a moment.

Then he went up the porch steps and in through the door.

Lillian said supper was in ten minutes.

Henry looked up from the newspaper and said nothing, which was his way of acknowledging that Marcus was back.

Marcus put his coat on the hook by the door. He could feel the notebook in the inside pocket — the slight weight of it, the hard cover against the cloth. He went to the kitchen sink and washed his hands and stayed at the water a moment longer than he needed to, not thinking about anything in particular, watching the water run. Then he dried his hands on the kitchen towel and turned to find his chair.

The elm outside the window had given up its light. The kitchen smelled of the corned beef hash Lillian made when she had the red points for it. Henry turned a page of the newspaper. Outside, somewhere, a car passed on Reach Road, and then the street was quiet, and the evening settled into the ordinary shape of an evening at the Crow house, which was the shape Marcus knew best in this town, and which held.

input.oneLine: Marcus takes the bus to Bangor, finds August Schell's name and biography in the Bangor Daily News seamen's bureau notice, and rides home holding the knowledge alone.

input.summary: August 1944, Marcus POV. Marcus takes the Machias bus to Bangor on a weekday, boards at 7:15 a.m. and arrives at noon. Lillian has packed a brown paper bag (cheese sandwich with extra mustard, two apples, ginger snaps) without being asked, as she does for his long-day trips. Bus passengers: woman with two boys, a sleeping farmer, a young soldier on leave at the back. On the road, Marcus thinks through why he's doing this: five months since Ada Gile gave him the name 'A. Schell' in March; he examines three explanations (philologist's reflex, lack of right to pursue it, a third he can't quite articulate) and finds none adequate but none stop him. At the Bangor Public Library, he requests the Bangor Daily News on microfilm, October–November 1943. In the Portland shipping column: SS Ångermanälven arrived Portland October 7, Captain Lindqvist, departed October 12, bound Halifax. In the November 17, 1943 issue: a seamen's bureau notice reporting August Schell, German national, Swedish citizen, age approximately 40, missing from the Portland-to-Halifax passage, October 10–12, 1943. Marcus sits with this, then writes a note in Kurrent: name, vessel, dates, 'German national, Swedish citizen' — not a summary, a fixing. He then reads, in the reading room for an hour, a journal article on medieval Bavarian dialect transmission in the Passau corridor that he has been meaning to read since January. It is exactly as good as expected. He eats the apples and ginger snaps on a bench. Bus home: the Penobscot River at the window, August light (pewter, bronze, heron on the bank). He reasons through August Schell's biography: born approximately 1903; 35 at the Anschluss, 37 when France fell, 39 when Marcus arrived in Coldwater Reach; a German who got out and became Swedish and sailed neutral ships through the wartime Atlantic. His conclusion: not a spy, not an agent — a man who got out before the war made leaving impossible, came to the Coldwater Reach pier for a meeting, drowned. Marcus acknowledges the conclusion is partly projection of his own displacement arithmetic. He considers whether to tell anyone (Henry, Eira Bowman, the naval district) and decides he will not. Reasoning: August Schell made himself difficult to file by intention; the official determination would be inadequate to the man. Marcus arrives back at the Crow house at 7:07 p.m. The chapter ends at the kitchen table — supper, the elm losing its light, the evening holding its ordinary shape.

Bus route: Coldwater Reach to Machias to Ellsworth to Bangor, approximately 5 hours each direction.

Lillian Crow's habit for Marcus's long-trip days: a brown paper bag packed without comment, set by the door. Contents on this trip: cheese sandwich with extra mustard, two apples, wax-paper fold of ginger snaps. She has determined his cheese preference (extra mustard) by observation without being told.
Marcus made a previous trip to the Bangor Public Library in late 1942 to consult a document the collection held.

Bangor Daily News Portland shipping column (October 13, 1943): SS Ångermanälven, departed Portland October 12, bound Halifax.

Bangor Daily News seamen's bureau notice, November 17, 1943: 'SS Ångermanälven reports crew member missing, August Schell, German national, Swedish citizen, age approx. 40, missing during Portland to Halifax passage, October 10–12, 1943. Contact Seamen's Bureau, Portland.'
Captain Lindqvist: captain of the SS Ångermanälven as named in the Bangor Daily News Portland shipping column, October 1943.

Marcus's Kurrent note, written in the Bangor library periodicals room: two entries. (1) 'Å. Ankunft Portland 7. Okt. Abfahrt 12. Okt.' (2) 'August Schell. Deutsch. Schwed. Staatsbürger. Geb. ca. 1903. Ångermanälven. Okt. '43.' Written in Kurrent script; not a summary, a fixing of facts. Kept in the inside pocket of his coat; not transcribed into any other document.

Marcus's private conclusion about August Schell: born approximately 1903; German national who acquired Swedish citizenship and sailed neutral merchant vessels; not a spy or agent; came to Coldwater Reach pier on October 9, 1943 for a meeting arranged by the Portland postcard; drowned. Marcus holds this as a careful reading from incomplete evidence, not as evidential certainty.

Marcus acknowledges to himself that his conclusion about Schell is partly projection — he recognizes the arithmetic of Schell's displacement because it resembles his own, in its broad outlines, and he knows this.

Marcus decides to tell no one what he found in Bangor. Reasoning: Schell made himself deliberately difficult to file; the official determination would be inadequate to the man; Marcus has sat in rooms where such determinations were made and found them accurate and inadequate. He will not add Schell to a file.

Marcus requested the Passau corridor journal article two weeks before the trip by letter to the Bangor Public Library. The article: on medieval Bavarian dialect transmission in the Passau region, from a 1943 philological journal. The argument addresses high-vowel persistence in the sub-Alpine dialect corridor. Marcus makes three marginal pencil notes in Kurrent on a notecard. He finds it exactly as good as the abstract suggested.

The Kurrent note is in Marcus's coat inside pocket as of August 1944, and is not transcribed into any other document at any point in this chapter or, per the outline's continuity note, at any later point.
looseThreads: Thread T2 (The Man Still Moving): advanced — Marcus makes a private personal investment in the town's question (researching Schell) with no instrumental purpose. He tells no one. The question of why he keeps it is deliberately left without a clean answer in this chapter.

Parallel knowledge: both Marcus and Eira now hold August Schell's probable identity (Eira from Whitmore in ch16, Marcus from the Bangor library in ch19). Neither knows the other holds it. This structural parallel is in place and should be maintained through later chapters.

The Kurrent note in Marcus's coat inside pocket is a physical object that persists. Later drafters: the note is in Kurrent, not readable by anyone in Coldwater Reach without special training; it is not transcribed; it should not be discovered or referenced as findable.

He returns on the three o'clock bus south and arrives back in Coldwater Reach at seven minutes past seven in the evening.
Marcus made a previous trip to the Bangor Public Library in late 1942 to examine a document the collection held. He remembered the right bus seat from that trip.

Lillian Crow's established habit for Marcus's long-trip days: she packs a brown paper bag without comment or announcement and sets it by the door before he leaves. Contents of the August 1944 bag: a cheese sandwich with extra mustard, two apples, a wax-paper fold of ginger snaps. She has determined Marcus prefers extra mustard by observation, without being told by him.
Bangor Daily News Portland shipping column, October 8, 1943: SS Ångermanälven, Swedish, arrived Portland October 7, Captain Lindqvist, from Halifax, manifest filed.
Bangor Daily News Portland shipping column, October 13, 1943: SS Ångermanälven, departed Portland October 12, bound Halifax.
Captain Lindqvist: named captain of the SS Ångermanälven in the Bangor Daily News shipping column, October 1943. He is not a character in the story — he appears only in the newspaper record.

Bangor Daily News seamen's bureau notice, November 17, 1943 edition: 'SS Ångermanälven reports crew member missing, August Schell, German national, Swedish citizen, age approx. 40, missing during Portland to Halifax passage, October 10–12, 1943. Contact Seamen's Bureau, Portland.' This is the published source Marcus finds independently in August 1944.

Marcus's Kurrent note, written in the Bangor library periodicals room in August 1944: two entries. First: 'Å. Ankunft Portland 7. Okt. Abfahrt 12. Okt.' Second: 'August Schell. Deutsch. Schwed. Staatsbürger. Geb. ca. 1903. Ångermanälven. Okt. '43.' Written in Kurrent script; not a summary, a fixing of facts to hold their shape. Kept folded in the inside pocket of Marcus's coat. Not transcribed into any other document. Not readable by anyone in Coldwater Reach without training in Kurrent.

Marcus's private conclusion about August Schell, reached August 1944: Schell was born approximately 1903 (making him 35 at the Anschluss, 37 when France fell, 39 when Marcus arrived in Coldwater Reach). He left Germany before the war made leaving impossible, acquired Swedish citizenship, and sailed neutral merchant vessels through wartime Atlantic waters. He came to Coldwater Reach for a meeting arranged by the Portland postcard and drowned. Marcus holds this as a careful reading from incomplete evidence, not as evidential certainty — he is explicit to himself that he does not confuse the two.

Marcus acknowledges that his reading of Schell's biography is partly projection of his own displacement arithmetic onto a man he does not know. The narration does not endorse the reading as fact; he holds it open to revision.

Marcus decides in August 1944 to tell no one what he found in Bangor — not Henry, not Eira Bowman, not anyone at the naval district. His stated reasoning: Schell made himself deliberately difficult to file and died in that condition; the official determination would be inadequate to the man. Marcus cannot fully justify this decision and acknowledges it may not be quite wisdom.

Marcus requested a journal article in advance of the Bangor trip by letter two weeks prior. Article: on medieval Bavarian dialect transmission in the Passau corridor, from a 1943 philological journal held by the Bangor Public Library. Argument: high-vowel persistence in the sub-Alpine dialect corridor. Marcus made three marginal pencil notes in Kurrent on a notecard; he did not keep the journal (it belonged to the library collection). He found the article exactly as good as the abstract suggested.

entries.definition: Captain of the SS Ångermanälven, as named in the Bangor Daily News Portland shipping column, October 1943. Appears only in the newspaper record; not a character in the story. His name is established for factual consistency if the Ångermanälven's command is referenced elsewhere.

Real institution in Bangor, Maine. Stone building, good periodicals collection, holds the Bangor Daily News on microfilm. Marcus visits in late 1942 (first trip, to consult a document) and again on a Wednesday in August 1944 to research the Ångermanälven shipping record. Periodicals room has two microfilm readers. Reading room has proper tables and lamp coverage.

Physical object: a small notebook page written by Marcus Klein in the Bangor library periodicals room, August 1944, in his natural Kurrent script. Contains two entries: arrival/departure dates for the SS Ångermanälven in Portland (October 7–12, 1943) and August Schell's name, nationality, approximate birth year, vessel, and date. Not a summary — a fixing of facts. Kept folded in the inside pocket of Marcus's coat. Not transcribed into any other document. Not readable without training in Kurrent script.

A 1943 journal article on medieval Bavarian dialect transmission in the Passau sub-Alpine corridor, held by the Bangor Public Library. Marcus requested it two weeks in advance by letter and read it in the library reading room in August 1944, making three pencil Kurrent notes on a notecard. The argument addresses high-vowel persistence in sub-Alpine dialects. Marcus found it exactly as good as its abstract suggested. Not a symbolic act — he read it because he wanted to and because it was good.

# Addendum

The fog had burned off by six but left the inner anchorage still and grey in the way September fog left it — the water flat, no texture, the lobster boats at mooring sitting in their own reflections. By seven o'clock the light was coming in at a low angle from the east and the water had taken some color back. Eira was at her desk.

The vessel traffic log was open to the previous day's entries. Three lobster boats in and out between five in the morning and two in the afternoon; the barge *Calvert* at anchor since Monday on its way to Lubec; the buoy tender due Friday. She had written the buoy tender into the incoming schedule in red pencil — the harbor's shorthand for expected, not arrived.

She closed the log.

The desk held the usual accumulation: correspondence from the Lighthouse Service regarding the fairway buoy on the south channel, which had been dragging its mooring since the August blow and was now approximately twelve feet out of position. A request from the selectmen for the harbor fee schedule, to be updated and resubmitted before the November town meeting. A carbon of the fuel oil delivery record for the month, which she had checked and initialed and which needed to be filed.

She moved these to the right side of the desk.

Then she opened the center drawer and took out a sheet of harbor office stationery.

The stationery had the harbor office heading printed at the top — HARBOR OFFICE, COLDWATER REACH, MAINE — in a dark blue-green that had been close to black when the stock was new and had come out slightly paler in the last order, the ink thin in a way she had noted without yet complaining about. She placed the sheet square on the blotter in front of her. She took up her pen.

She wrote the date: *September 14, 1944.*

Below it she wrote: *Addendum to Incident Report, October 13, 1943 (Unidentified Decedent, Breakwater).*

She looked at the heading. Then she wrote.

---

*On the night of October 9, 1943, at approximately 0130 hours, the undersigned Harbor Master departed the harbor office and proceeded to the main pier to inspect the mooring bridle of the motor vessel Macomber, which was berthed at slip 4 on the south side of the pier. The mooring had been rigged the previous afternoon and the undersigned had noted in the vessel traffic log that conditions over the following sixteen hours would include gusting to 20–25 knots from the northwest, with an incoming swell from the south expected after midnight. In the undersigned's judgment the bridle was at risk of chafing against the starboard bollard as the wind shifted. The inspection was conducted at 0130 to allow time for adjustment before the first gusts of the incoming blow.*

She wrote that in one pass without stopping. The handwriting was the same as it was in the harbor log: even, slightly compressed, legible at a working distance. This was the voice she used for official records. It was not a voice she had to think about.

*On proceeding to the pier, the undersigned observed a male individual at the far end of the public pier — the fixed structure south of the private vessel berths. The individual was standing still, facing the anchorage. He was carrying a canvas bag, single strap, worn over the right shoulder. He appeared to be in good physical condition. His clothing was consistent with maritime working dress. The undersigned did not recognize him as a resident of Coldwater Reach or as a known user of the harbor.*

She set down the pen. She read the last sentence. She picked up the pen.

*The undersigned did not approach the individual. The undersigned heard at that moment a sound from the direction of the Macomber's mooring — believed to be the bridle under strain — and turned to attend to it.*

*The inspection of the mooring required approximately five minutes. The undersigned checked the bridle at the bollard, found light chafe consistent with the angle of the wind against the current, adjusted the bridle by a half-turn of the starboard chock, and confirmed the adjustment satisfactory. On completing the inspection, the undersigned looked back toward the far end of the public pier. The individual was not visible. The undersigned assumed that he had taken shelter in the pier shed, which was standing open.*

*The undersigned returned to the harbor office at approximately 0140 hours. No entry was made in the vessel traffic log.*

She stopped.

She had been carrying the fact of that sentence for eleven months. It was a different thing to see it in her own handwriting on official stationery than it had been to carry it as a private knowledge she did not look at directly. She looked at it now. The sentence was six words. It stated a fact. It did not accuse, and it did not exonerate, and it was accurate.

She wrote the next sentence.

*No further investigation was conducted.*

Then:

*On October 13, 1943, the body of an unidentified male individual was recovered from the far end of the breakwater by the harbor patrol and subsequently transferred to the custody of the United States Coast Guard, First District, per standing protocol. See Incident Report, October 13, 1943, filed with the District office. The undersigned has reason to believe that the individual observed on the public pier on the night of October 9, 1943, was the same individual whose body was recovered on October 13, 1943.*

*This addendum is submitted as a correction to the record of the above-cited incident. No explanation is offered for the delay in its submission. The facts stated above are accurate to the undersigned's knowledge and recollection.*

*E.M. Bowman, Harbor Master*
*Coldwater Reach, Maine*
*September 14, 1944*

---

She read it back.

It took two minutes. The handwriting was even throughout — no place where she had pressed the pen harder or released it. She had written it in the same voice she used for every document the harbor produced: arrival logs, departure records, incident reports, the weekly summary for the Lighthouse Service, the semi-annual vessel inspection sheets. The same voice she had been using for eleven months of ordinary harbor work that had continued without interruption around the fact she was not writing down.

The addendum was accurate. She had not put in anything that was not true, and she had not left out anything that a complete record required. The phrase *no explanation is offered for the delay in its submission* was not a formula she had seen in other harbor documents. She had written it herself, because it was the accurate phrase.

She read the third paragraph again.

*The undersigned did not approach the individual.*

Five words. In the context of an official harbor record they were five words like any others — weight, draft, bearing, heading. They stated a fact in the voice of a person doing her job, without modifier and without editorial, which was what they were supposed to do and which was true and which did not change what the five words said.

She looked at this for a while.

Through the east window the harbor was fully lit now. One of the lobster boats had started its engine — the *Gertrude B.*, she knew the engine by sound. The diesel carried cleanly across the flat water the way sounds carried in September, when the air had thinned enough to let things through without distortion. In six weeks those boats would be pulling the last of the gear. By the first week of November the inner anchorage would be down to whatever Mayhew still chose to take out and the trap skiffs at the south mooring that nobody used after October.

She was not thinking about November. She was looking at the addendum.

She folded it in three.

She folded it the way she folded documents for the pending drawer: two crisp parallel folds, no overlap, the corners squared. Owen had shown her the fold the first winter of their marriage, when she had started helping him with the harbor correspondence. She had not had any reason to develop a different fold. She ran her thumb along the crease to set it.

She went to the chart cabinet.

The pending drawer was the bottom one on the right side — the drawer Owen had designated for pending items when he reorganized the filing system in 1929, and which she had been using in its same configuration ever since. She pulled it open. At the front her own working files: the Lighthouse Service notice, the selectmen's request, a pending fuel inspection she had not yet scheduled. She moved these to the side.

At the back of the drawer were two manila folders in Owen's handwriting. She had not moved them and had not thrown them away. His handwriting was still readable after nine years — still his. The particular way he formed the capital letters; the G in *Correspondence* slightly enlarged, as if the letter required the room; the way his words sat down squarely on the ruled line as if he had been taught not to leave them uncertain.

*Lighthouse Service correspondence, 1934.*

*Tidal survey, pending, 1935.*

He had been working on the tidal survey the autumn he drowned. She had not known what to do with an unfinished survey for nine years, and so it had stayed where he put it, in the place he put things he had not finished. She had put other things in front of it and taken them out again and put new things in front of it, and the survey had stayed at the back where he left it, waiting for a disposition she had not known how to give it.

She placed the folded addendum in front of his two folders.

She closed the drawer.

---

She went outside.

The morning had established itself fully by now. The fog was entirely gone, the ridge above town showing the first small turnings in the maples at the upper edge — not orange yet, not red, but the leaves beginning to consider it, a lightening in the green that was visible from the dock if you were looking for it. The air had the September quality: thin and clear but with the warmth not yet fully out of it, so that standing in direct sun on the dock felt almost like August, while the pier shed's shadow fell cool enough that she could feel the difference of five degrees when she walked through it.

She walked to the end of the dock.

The *Gertrude B.* had her engine running but was still at mooring, the old man doing his pre-departure check — she could see him moving on the afterdeck. Mayhew's skiff alongside at the north mooring, no name readable on the transom. The *Calvert* at anchor further out, her deck cargo covered in canvas, her anchor light still burning from the night, a pale smudge in the morning brightness. Beyond the barge the open water, and beyond that the breakwater: granite, running northeast, three hundred and forty feet of it in two sections with the gap for the passage channel, the far end just visible as a darker line against the lighter water outside.

She had walked the breakwater two hundred and some times since Owen drowned. She had walked it in November ice when the footing was wrong and in August fog when she could not see the far end from the near end, and she had not stopped walking it because it was the breakwater and it was the job and the job required it.

The far end was where the body had come in.

She stood at the end of the dock and looked at the end of the breakwater, which was empty, as it was on most mornings, as the far end of the public pier was empty now behind her, the boards bare in the September morning. She had been at this end of the dock eleven months ago on the night in question, and the man had been at the far end of the pier, and she had looked back and he had not been there.

She looked at the distance.

Then she went back inside.

---

The coffeepot was on the shelf above the duty log, where she kept it. She filled it from the water can, measured out the grounds from the tin she ordered monthly from the general store, and set it on the portable burner. While it heated she went back to her desk and read the Lighthouse Service letter regarding the fairway buoy, which was substantively unchanged from the previous letter and which required a reply she had been putting off. She drafted the reply on a notepad in abbreviated form — the buoy's position, the hazard assessment, the request for a correction tender in the next available service window — and set it aside to copy to stationery before the noon mail.

The coffee was done. She poured a cup.

She sat back down at her desk and drank it while it was hot.

The vessel traffic log was still open. She picked up her pen and wrote the date at the top of the new line: *September 14, 1944.* She noted the time: *0718.* Weather: *clear, light wind west-northwest, glass 30.14 and steady.* Anchorage: two lobster boats at mooring, one barge at anchor. Buoy tender expected Friday. Fairway buoy south channel continues approximately 12 feet out of position — see Lighthouse Service correspondence, pending.

She wrote the entry in the same hand she had been writing harbor entries in for eight years.

The *Gertrude B.* cleared the north mooring at 0723. She noted that too.

In Owen's pending drawer, behind her working files: a document on harbor office stationery, folded in three, with his two unfinished files behind it. She did not note this in the vessel traffic log. There was no category in the vessel traffic log for a thing that had been placed rather than sent. It was in the pending drawer where it was supposed to be, pending.

She picked up the Lighthouse Service letter and began to draft her reply in full.

In official harbor register voice, she sets down everything she saw on the night of October 9, 1943: the mooring inspection at approximately 0130; the man at the far end of the public pier — standing still, facing the anchorage, canvas bag over the right shoulder, maritime working clothes, in good physical condition; the sound from the mooring that drew her away; the five-minute inspection; looking back and the man not visible; her assumption he took shelter in the pier shed (standing open); her return to the harbor office; no entry made in the vessel traffic log; no further investigation conducted. She adds that the body recovered from the breakwater on October 13, 1943, was in her belief the same individual — referred to throughout as 'unidentified male individual,' not by name. She reads it back. She holds on the sentence 'The undersigned did not approach the individual.' She folds the addendum in three (the standard fold for the pending drawer), goes to the chart cabinet, opens Owen's pending drawer, and places the addendum in front of Owen's two remaining folders (Lighthouse Service correspondence, 1934; Tidal survey, pending, 1935). She closes the drawer without disturbing his files. She goes outside, walks to the end of the dock, looks at the harbor — Gertrude B. at mooring, Mayhew's skiff, the Calvert at anchor, the breakwater visible in the distance. She looks at the far end of the pier where she saw him. She goes back inside, makes coffee, and drinks it. The chapter closes on the Gertrude B. clearing the north mooring at 0723 and Eira at her desk writing the ordinary log entry and then drafting her Lighthouse Service reply.

input.content:
# V-Mail

The kitchen smelled of the previous night's potatoes — that particular root-cellar smell that came in with the first late-summer harvest and stayed until December, a smell like turned earth and wet burlap and something cooling that you couldn't name. She had been at the digging since Thursday with her father and the men, the days ending with her hands raw and the bags stacked in rows in the cellar. Good yield this year. Better than last.

The V-Mail form was on the table where she'd left it, under the salt dish.

Magda was not in the kitchen. Hannah could hear her somewhere in the back of the house — a door, the sound of movement in the room above — and then nothing. Her mother had been like this for weeks, appearing and disappearing through the rooms in a way that made the house feel larger than it was. It was not different from how she'd been since the news about Onkel Franz in 1942. It was the same. Hannah had stopped being frightened by it. She wasn't sure when that had happened.

From the barn, through the open kitchen window: her father's voice. The particular cadence of Bavarian German delivered to someone who understood it, which was different from the rhythm of a sentence you were meant to catch. He and the men were at the north pasture fence — four posts that had heaved over the summer and needed resetting before the ground hardened. She could hear Karl's voice too, shorter phrases, a question and then Vati's answer, and then silence, and then the sound of a post maul.

She moved the salt dish and pulled the form toward her.

The top block was already filled in: her name, the farm address, Rolf's APO number, and a date she'd filled in three weeks ago in a moment of optimism that had not produced a letter. She crossed out the old date and wrote September 18 in the space. She uncapped her pen.

*Dear Rolf,* she wrote. *I hope this finds you well.*

The form gave her five inches of lined space, which was never enough and always more than she managed to fill. She knew the shape of these letters by now — she'd written eight of them since November of last year, and before that the ordinary airmail. You chose. The censors saw it anyway, whatever you chose, and blacked out the things that put information where it shouldn't be. It was not only the censors. There were other reasons to choose.

*The potatoes came in good,* she wrote. *Better than last year. I think Vati is pleased.* He had asked about the potatoes in his April letter — *How do the fields look this spring?* — which was the kind of question you asked when you were somewhere that didn't have fields, or had different fields, or had fields you were not permitted to think of as fields. She thought about this when she wrote about the potatoes. She always sent him what he asked for.

She looked at the next line.

Martha.

Martha had died in July. A mastitis case — the big Holstein Rolf had named when they were seven and nine, for a reason Hannah couldn't remember anymore. He'd called her Martha with great ceremony, an announcement rather than a suggestion: *That one. She is Martha.* And Martha she had been for eleven years. The infection came on fast, the left quarter, and her father had called the veterinarian in from Machias, and they had done what they could for three days. She had been a good cow. Patient in the way that big cows were patient, which was an entirely different quality from what you'd call patience in a person.

She could write it. There was space.

She thought: Rolf reading the letter. Some room, some tent, some building where men read letters while the war was nearby. She thought of the sentence arriving on a reproduced form and Rolf holding it — *Martha died this summer; it was her left side; we couldn't save her* — and the word Martha, which had no military significance whatsoever, going through the censor's eyes and out the other side untouched, arriving whole. She thought: this is the thing you should be able to tell someone. The cow you both named.

She would tell him when he came home.

She wrote instead: *The cows are still out on the pasture but not for much longer.* She said *the cows* and meant all of them and did not mean Martha and moved on to the next line.

*The weather has turned cooler already. There is fog every morning off the ridge.* She looked at the fog sentence and let it stand. It was true. *Mutti sends her love.* Her mother had not said anything to send, but it didn't require asking. Magda's love for Rolf was not something you had to articulate; you could convey it on her behalf without lying about it. *Vati says the north pasture fence should hold through another winter.* Vati had said this two days ago, flat and declarative, to no one in particular, while she carried the fence brace past him. She had heard it and filed it and now it was in the letter. The north pasture fence would hold.

She looked at the lined space that remained.

She could not put Karl's name down. She had not thought it in terms of *not putting* — it was more that she looked at the space and Karl was simply not in it. But if she tried to locate the reason: she would have to begin with November of last year, when the Army truck came up the farm road with four men in the back. She would have to explain the icehouse and Lonzo Reed and the kind of fence that was both a boundary and a place where things happened. She would have to explain the word-cards, the cedar rectangles passing back and forth under the stone at the fence post. She would have to explain what it was like to have a conversation in two languages across a fence post with someone who was, technically, the enemy, who was also from a town fifty kilometers from where Vati grew up, and who had a father who ran a butcher shop in Regensburg, and who had never seen the sea before the troop ship brought him to this coast.

She would have to explain all of it without making it sound like a story, because it wasn't a story. It was the situation she was inside.

And she couldn't write *mündig* — the word Dr. Klein had put on the board in April, *having your own mouth*, the state of speaking for yourself — and what she'd been quietly doing with it, on her side of the fence, over these months. What it meant to choose what you said and to whom, and what that was making her.

The censors would have none of that anyway.

She wrote: *I am well.*

She wrote: *The weather is changing.*

She looked at what remained and wrote: *I think of you.*

She signed her name.

She read the letter over once, the way you read something you already knew was insufficient before you read it. The potatoes. The cows, unnamed. The fog on the ridge. Mutti's love by proxy. Vati's opinion of the north fence. I am well. The weather is changing. I think of you. It was true. All of it was true. It left out most of what was happening and it was still, she thought, the letter she had to offer him, at this distance, in this September.

She capped her pen, folded the form, and went to the hook by the door for Rolf's coat.

---

The coat was still two sizes too large across the shoulders, as it had been since the first winter she'd taken it off the hook. She had thought she might grow into it; she was as tall as she was going to be, which was two inches shorter than Rolf and narrower across the back, and the sleeves still came past her wrist cuffs by an inch when she let her arms fall. She wore it anyway. There was something about the extra room she had not wanted to give up — the way it felt like margin.

The road to town was the September version of itself: goldenrod going brown in the ditches, the sky the particular pale blue that arrived after the equinox and stayed until November. She passed no one on the road until she reached the edge of town.

Ada Gile took the form without comment, which was what Ada always did. She looked at the address block, looked at the date, and set the form on the scale without weighing it.

"Two cents," she said.

Hannah counted the pennies out of her coat pocket onto the counter. Ada handed her the stamp and waited while Hannah affixed it — square to the corner, pressed against the dampener Ada kept beside the window. She slid the form back across the counter.

Ada set it in the outgoing mail and returned to the letters she was sorting, rubber-banded in a stack.

On an impulse she hadn't planned, Hannah said: "Do you hear news from the boys?"

Ada did not look up from the letters. "Some. Fewer letters than there were."

She said it neutrally. She was stating an observable fact about the volume of mail that passed through her hands, the way she stated facts about mail. Hannah heard what lay beneath it: that some of the correspondents had stopped, and they had stopped for a reason that came first by telegram. The telegram came to Ada before anyone. Ada knew things by their shapes in the mail before anyone in town knew them by their names.

"Thank you," Hannah said.

"Mm," said Ada, still sorting.

---

She went home the long way.

The long way added a quarter mile and went past the school, which was the nicer road in September — more trees, the road slightly sheltered from the harbor wind. She was not in a hurry. She wanted to walk and the road through town was where her feet went.

The school building sat at the north end, the old mill converted, the upper windows tall and south-facing for the loom light that was now classroom light. She passed the front entrance, the sign she had walked past since she was six: COLDWATER REACH HIGH SCHOOL. The building was quiet. School had been out more than an hour.

She was nearly past when she noticed the light in the second window from the east end, upper floor. Room 6. The German and Latin room.

The afternoon sun came in from the west side, which meant the room's far side was in full light — she could see the chalkboard from the road, the map of Europe that always had pins in it, the rows of empty desks. At the front desk, visible through the glass, was Dr. Klein. He was sitting very straight. He was not writing. He was reading something he held in both hands, something flat, and he was not grading because he was not moving — no pen, no marking, no turning of pages every thirty seconds the way you turned pages when you were grading.

She stood on the road.

He was doing something private. She could not see what was in his hands. She could see only his posture — the same posture he maintained in class, very straight, very contained, the quality that made you think at first he wasn't breathing until you caught the small adjustment of his head as he worked down a page. She had been in his class for two years. She had looked at that posture across a classroom for two years and she had thought: this is what a person looks like when they are keeping themselves in order. She did not know why she thought that. She just thought it.

From the road, through the glass, he looked like a man doing what you'd expect him to be doing after four on a school day. He looked settled. He looked like someone whose stillness had a reason she couldn't see from where she was standing.

She thought: she had just put a letter in the outgoing mail that did not say the things that were actually happening on the farm. Her father was out at the north pasture fence right now, speaking Bavarian German with a man from Regensburg about posts and frozen ground. Her mother was upstairs moving through the house on tasks Hannah couldn't follow. Ada Gile stood at her counter knowing things by their envelopes that the families of those men did not yet know by name. And Marcus Klein was sitting in his classroom after four in the afternoon reading something by a lamp he did not need to keep lit for anyone who required it of him.

Everyone in this town was keeping something. She thought it the way she'd noted the fog that morning — as a fact about the weather, the same factual attention, no self-pity in it and no irony either, just the observation that this was the condition of things in Coldwater Reach in September 1944, and that she was one of the people in it.

She walked on.

The road curved south below the school and she could no longer see the window. The ridge above town was on her left, and from here she could see what she had told Rolf about in the letter: the leaves at the upper edge, not turned yet, but something had shifted in the green — a lightening at the margins of the topmost maples, a loosening — that said the turn was two weeks out, maybe three. She had been watching that ridge her whole life and she knew what it looked like before it changed.

She turned up the farm road.

The barn was visible from the bottom of the drive. The sound of the post maul was gone now — the fence work finished for the day or else the men gone inside. The cows in the south pasture were moving in the slow, indifferent way cows moved at the end of the afternoon, drifting toward the fence line, toward the barn door, toward the next thing required of them. She counted them by habit, the way she had been counting since she was small enough that it was something to do with her hands while she waited for her father.

Seven. Eight. Nine.

The herd complete, except for one.

She went inside.
