## Continuity Check — `draft/19-august.md`

### Against `canon/continuity.md` (30 hard facts)

| Canon fact | Chapter 19 detail | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| #14 Marcus born Vienna 1896, 47 at opening | Marcus uses "thirty-nine when Marcus arrived in Coldwater Reach in March 1942" — 1942 − 1903 (Schell's birth) = 39. Marcus's own birth year not invoked here; no conflict. | ✓ |
| #16 Marcus arrived Coldwater Reach March 1942 | "Thirty-nine when Marcus arrived in Coldwater Reach in March 1942" — date cited correctly. | ✓ |
| #17 Marcus boards with Henry and Lillian Crow | Chapter opens with Lillian packing his lunch; closes with Henry at the newspaper, Lillian at the stove. | ✓ |
| #29 Ada Gile is postmistress | "Ada Gile had given it to him across the post office counter" — consistent. | ✓ |
| #5 Body washes up after three-day blow, October 1943 | Newspaper notice: ship departed Portland Oct. 12, "missing during Portland to Halifax passage, October 10–12." Three-day blow bringing body to breakwater is fully consistent with these dates. | ✓ |
| #23 No spy/espionage elements | Marcus explicitly reasons Schell was not an agent; his conclusion is "the profile of a man trying to remain alive and useful." | ✓ |
| #25 Bangor is a real place, used accurately | Marcus works the Bangor Public Library periodicals room; Penobscot River described correctly on the southern approach. | ✓ |

### Against `logs/story-arc.md` — adjacency & prior arc entries

**Ch. 12 (two-suits):** Marcus learns the name "A. Schell" from Ada Gile. Chapter 19 says he has been sitting with this name "since March" — five months before August. Arc entry places ch. 12 in the late-winter/early-spring stretch before D-Day, consistent with March. ✓

**Ch. 13 (ada-giles-window):** Eira visits Ada and learns the postcard was from Portland, "a date and a time." Chapter 19's Marcus recalls Ada told him it was "a postcard… Portland postmark… received the first week of October 1943." The content description (date, time) matches. Both characters received information from Ada independently; the canon does not prohibit this. ✓

**Ch. 16 (the-inspector):** Arc says Whitmore names "August Schell — German-born Swedish national, crew of the Ångermanälven — as the probable body" to Eira. Chapter 19's seamen's bureau notice reads "German national, Swedish citizen." These are two different sources (a US naval inspector's verbal characterisation vs. a Norwegian/Swedish seamen's bureau filing) describing the same man. The slight variation in phrasing is realistic and not a contradiction; both descriptions are compatible with a German-born man holding Swedish citizenship. ✓

**Ch. 15 (the-sixth-of-june):** Places the D-Day chapter in June 1944. Chapter 19 is set in August 1944. The Bangor trip follows logically in the summer arc. ✓

**Ch. 18 (what-henry-notices):** Previous chapter, immediately adjacent.

### Against `draft/18-what-henry-notices.md` — adjacency

**Same characters, same setting:** Both chapters feature Marcus at the Crow house with Henry and Lillian. Chapter 18 ends with Marcus and Henry washing dishes after dinner; chapter 19 opens the following (or a later) morning with Marcus leaving on the 7:15 bus. Clearly different days. No conflict.

**The elm:** Ch. 18 ends: *"the late August light was still in the elm beyond the kitchen window."* Ch. 19 close: *"beyond her the elm in the yard still holding the last of the evening light in its upper branches."* Consistent fixture. ✓

**Kurrent script:** Ch. 18 notes Marcus's grade-book hand as "the tight Kurrent hand he used for his own notes." Ch. 19 has Marcus writing notebook entries in Kurrent and explicitly notes it as a script no one in Coldwater Reach could read without training. Fully consistent. ✓

**Corned beef hash:** Ch. 18 — "Lillian had made corned beef hash with the last of the week's red points." Ch. 19 — "The kitchen smelled of the corned beef hash Lillian made when she had the red points for it." These are different days (Marcus is away all of ch. 19's day); they can readily be different rationing weeks. The ch. 19 phrasing "when she had the red points for it" reinforces that this is a conditional, periodic occurrence. Not a contradiction.

**Henry's petition revelation:** Ch. 18 ends with Henry telling Marcus about the three-name petition. Ch. 19 contains no mention of the petition — Marcus's mind is entirely on the Schell question. This is consistent: he planned the Bangor trip weeks earlier (the journal article request is two weeks prior to the chapter), the trip proceeds on its own momentum. No conflict.

**Arithmetic on August Schell's age:** Seamen's notice says "age approx. 40" in October 1943 → born ~1903. Marcus's chain: 1938 (Anschluss) − 1903 = 35 ✓; 1940 (fall of France) − 1903 = 37 ✓; 1942 (Marcus's Coldwater Reach arrival) − 1903 = 39 ✓. All accurate.

---

### Verdict

**Chapter 19 is clean.** All facts are consistent with the 30 canonical hard facts, the accumulated story arc, and the immediately preceding chapter. No corrections required.

input.content:
# Addendum

The fog had burned off by six but left the inner anchorage still and grey in the way September fog left it — the water flat, no texture, the lobster boats at mooring sitting in their own reflections. By seven o'clock the light was coming in at a low angle from the east and the water had taken some color back. Eira was at her desk.

The vessel traffic log was open to the previous day's entries. Three lobster boats in and out between five in the morning and two in the afternoon; the barge *Calvert* at anchor since Monday on its way to Lubec; the buoy tender due Friday. She had written the buoy tender into the incoming schedule in red pencil — the harbor's shorthand for expected, not arrived.

She closed the log.

The desk held the usual accumulation: correspondence from the Lighthouse Service regarding the fairway buoy on the south channel, which had been dragging its mooring since the August blow and was now approximately twelve feet out of position. A request from the selectmen for the harbor fee schedule, to be updated and resubmitted before the November town meeting. A carbon of the fuel oil delivery record for the month, which she had checked and initialed and which needed to be filed.

She moved these to the right side of the desk.

Then she opened the center drawer and took out a sheet of harbor office stationery.

The stationery had the harbor office heading printed at the top — HARBOR OFFICE, COLDWATER REACH, MAINE — in a dark blue-green that had been close to black when the stock was new and had come out slightly paler in the last order, the ink thin in a way she had noted without yet complaining about. She placed the sheet square on the blotter in front of her. She took up her pen.

She wrote the date: *September 14, 1944.*

Below it she wrote: *Addendum to Incident Report, October 13, 1943 (Unidentified Decedent, Breakwater).*

She looked at the heading. Then she wrote.

---

*On the night of October 9, 1943, at approximately 0130 hours, the undersigned Harbor Master departed the harbor office and proceeded to the main pier to inspect the mooring bridle of the motor vessel Macomber, which was berthed at slip 4 on the south side of the pier. The mooring had been rigged the previous afternoon and the undersigned had noted in the vessel traffic log that conditions over the following sixteen hours would include gusting to 20–25 knots from the northwest, with an incoming swell from the south expected after midnight. In the undersigned's judgment the bridle was at risk of chafing against the starboard bollard as the wind shifted. The inspection was conducted at 0130 to allow time for adjustment before the first gusts of the incoming blow.*

She wrote that in one pass without stopping. The handwriting was the same as it was in the harbor log: even, slightly compressed, legible at a working distance. This was the voice she used for official records. It was not a voice she had to think about.

*On proceeding to the pier, the undersigned observed a male individual at the far end of the public pier — the fixed structure south of the private vessel berths. The individual was standing still, facing the anchorage. He was carrying a canvas bag, single strap, worn over the right shoulder. He appeared to be in good physical condition. His clothing was consistent with maritime working dress. The undersigned did not recognize him as a resident of Coldwater Reach or as a known user of the harbor.*

She set down the pen. She read the last sentence. She picked up the pen.

*The undersigned did not approach the individual. The undersigned heard at that moment a sound from the direction of the Macomber's mooring — believed to be the bridle under strain — and turned to attend to it.*

*The inspection of the mooring required approximately five minutes. The undersigned checked the bridle at the bollard, found light chafe consistent with the angle of the wind against the current, adjusted the bridle by a half-turn of the starboard chock, and confirmed the adjustment satisfactory. On completing the inspection, the undersigned looked back toward the far end of the public pier. The individual was not visible. The undersigned assumed that he had taken shelter in the pier shed, which was standing open.*

*The undersigned returned to the harbor office at approximately 0140 hours. No entry was made in the vessel traffic log.*

She stopped.

She had been carrying the fact of that sentence for eleven months. It was a different thing to see it in her own handwriting on official stationery than it had been to carry it as a private knowledge she did not look at directly. She looked at it now. The sentence was six words. It stated a fact. It did not accuse, and it did not exonerate, and it was accurate.

She wrote the next sentence.

*No further investigation was conducted.*

Then:

*On October 13, 1943, the body of an unidentified male individual was recovered from the far end of the breakwater by the harbor patrol and subsequently transferred to the custody of the United States Coast Guard, First District, per standing protocol. See Incident Report, October 13, 1943, filed with the District office. The undersigned has reason to believe that the individual observed on the public pier on the night of October 9, 1943, was the same individual whose body was recovered on October 13, 1943.*

*This addendum is submitted as a correction to the record of the above-cited incident. No explanation is offered for the delay in its submission. The facts stated above are accurate to the undersigned's knowledge and recollection.*

*E.M. Bowman, Harbor Master*
*Coldwater Reach, Maine*
*September 14, 1944*

---

She read it back.

It took two minutes. The handwriting was even throughout — no place where she had pressed the pen harder or released it. She had written it in the same voice she used for every document the harbor produced: arrival logs, departure records, incident reports, the weekly summary for the Lighthouse Service, the semi-annual vessel inspection sheets. The same voice she had been using for eleven months of ordinary harbor work that had continued without interruption around the fact she was not writing down.

The addendum was accurate. She had not put in anything that was not true, and she had not left out anything that a complete record required. The phrase *no explanation is offered for the delay in its submission* was not a formula she had seen in other harbor documents. She had written it herself, because it was the accurate phrase.

She read the third paragraph again.

*The undersigned did not approach the individual.*

Five words. In the context of an official harbor record they were five words like any others — weight, draft, bearing, heading. They stated a fact in the voice of a person doing her job, without modifier and without editorial, which was what they were supposed to do and which was true and which did not change what the five words said.

She looked at this for a while.

Through the east window the harbor was fully lit now. One of the lobster boats had started its engine — the *Gertrude B.*, she knew the engine by sound. The diesel carried cleanly across the flat water the way sounds carried in September, when the air had thinned enough to let things through without distortion. In six weeks those boats would be pulling the last of the gear. By the first week of November the inner anchorage would be down to whatever Mayhew still chose to take out and the trap skiffs at the south mooring that nobody used after October.

She was not thinking about November. She was looking at the addendum.

She folded it in three.

She folded it the way she folded documents for the pending drawer: two crisp parallel folds, no overlap, the corners squared. Owen had shown her the fold the first winter of their marriage, when she had started helping him with the harbor correspondence. She had not had any reason to develop a different fold. She ran her thumb along the crease to set it.

She went to the chart cabinet.

The pending drawer was the bottom one on the right side — the drawer Owen had designated for pending items when he reorganized the filing system in 1929, and which she had been using in its same configuration ever since. She pulled it open. At the front her own working files: the Lighthouse Service notice, the selectmen's request, a pending fuel inspection she had not yet scheduled. She moved these to the side.

At the back of the drawer were two manila folders in Owen's handwriting. She had not moved them and had not thrown them away. His handwriting was still readable after nine years — still his. The particular way he formed the capital letters; the G in *Correspondence* slightly enlarged, as if the letter required the room; the way his words sat down squarely on the ruled line as if he had been taught not to leave them uncertain.

*Lighthouse Service correspondence, 1934.*

*Tidal survey, pending, 1935.*

He had been working on the tidal survey the autumn he drowned. She had not known what to do with an unfinished survey for nine years, and so it had stayed where he put it, in the place he put things he had not finished. She had put other things in front of it and taken them out again and put new things in front of it, and the survey had stayed at the back where he left it, waiting for a disposition she had not known how to give it.

She placed the folded addendum in front of his two folders.

She closed the drawer.

---

She went outside.

The morning had established itself fully by now. The fog was entirely gone, the ridge above town showing the first small turnings in the maples at the upper edge — not orange yet, not red, but the leaves beginning to consider it, a lightening in the green that was visible from the dock if you were looking for it. The air had the September quality: thin and clear but with the warmth not yet fully out of it, so that standing in direct sun on the dock felt almost like August, while the pier shed's shadow fell cool enough that she could feel the difference of five degrees when she walked through it.

She walked to the end of the dock.

The *Gertrude B.* had her engine running but was still at mooring, the old man doing his pre-departure check — she could see him moving on the afterdeck. Mayhew's skiff alongside at the north mooring, no name readable on the transom. The *Calvert* at anchor further out, her deck cargo covered in canvas, her anchor light still burning from the night, a pale smudge in the morning brightness. Beyond the barge the open water, and beyond that the breakwater: granite, running northeast, three hundred and forty feet of it in two sections with the gap for the passage channel, the far end just visible as a darker line against the lighter water outside.

She had walked the breakwater two hundred and some times since Owen drowned. She had walked it in November ice when the footing was wrong and in August fog when she could not see the far end from the near end, and she had not stopped walking it because it was the breakwater and it was the job and the job required it.

The far end was where the body had come in.

She stood at the end of the dock and looked at the end of the breakwater, which was empty, as it was on most mornings, as the far end of the public pier was empty now behind her, the boards bare in the September morning. She had been at this end of the dock eleven months ago on the night in question, and the man had been at the far end of the pier, and she had looked back and he had not been there.

She looked at the distance.

Then she went back inside.

---

The coffeepot was on the shelf above the duty log, where she kept it. She filled it from the water can, measured out the grounds from the tin she ordered monthly from the general store, and set it on the portable burner. While it heated she went back to her desk and read the Lighthouse Service letter regarding the fairway buoy, which was substantively unchanged from the previous letter and which required a reply she had been putting off. She drafted the reply on a notepad in abbreviated form — the buoy's position, the hazard assessment, the request for a correction tender in the next available service window — and set it aside to copy to stationery before the noon mail.

The coffee was done. She poured a cup.

She sat back down at her desk and drank it while it was hot.

The vessel traffic log was still open. She picked up her pen and wrote the date at the top of the new line: *September 14, 1944.* She noted the time: *0718.* Weather: *clear, light wind west-northwest, glass 30.14 and steady.* Anchorage: two lobster boats at mooring, one barge at anchor. Buoy tender expected Friday. Fairway buoy south channel continues approximately 12 feet out of position — see Lighthouse Service correspondence, pending.

She wrote the entry in the same hand she had been writing harbor entries in for nine years.

The *Gertrude B.* cleared the north mooring at 0723. She noted that too.

In Owen's pending drawer, behind her working files: a document on harbor office stationery, folded in three, with his two unfinished files behind it. She did not note this in the vessel traffic log. There was no category in the vessel traffic log for a thing that had been placed rather than sent. It was in the pending drawer where it was supposed to be, pending.

She picked up the Lighthouse Service letter and began to draft her reply in full.

input.content:
# V-Mail

The kitchen smelled of the previous night's potatoes — that particular root-cellar smell that came in with the first late-summer harvest and stayed until December, a smell like turned earth and wet burlap and something cooling that you couldn't name. She had been at the digging since Thursday with her father and the men, the days ending with her hands raw and the bags stacked in rows in the cellar. Good yield this year. Better than last.

The V-Mail form was on the table where she'd left it, under the salt dish.

Magda was not in the kitchen. Hannah could hear her somewhere in the back of the house — a door, the sound of movement in the room above — and then nothing. Her mother had been like this for weeks, appearing and disappearing through the rooms in a way that made the house feel larger than it was. It was not different from how she'd been since the news about Onkel Franz in 1942. It was the same. Hannah had stopped being frightened by it. She wasn't sure when that had happened.

From the barn, through the open kitchen window: her father's voice. The particular cadence of Bavarian German delivered to someone who understood it, which was different from the rhythm of a sentence you were meant to catch. He and the men were at the north pasture fence — four posts that had heaved over the summer and needed resetting before the ground hardened. She could hear Karl's voice too, shorter phrases, a question and then Vati's answer, and then silence, and then the sound of a post maul.

She moved the salt dish and pulled the form toward her.

The top block was already filled in: her name, the farm address, Rolf's APO number, and a date she'd filled in three weeks ago in a moment of optimism that had not produced a letter. She crossed out the old date and wrote September 18 in the space. She uncapped her pen.

*Dear Rolf,* she wrote. *I hope this finds you well.*

The form gave her five inches of lined space, which was never enough and always more than she managed to fill. She knew the shape of these letters by now — she'd written eight of them since October of last year, and before that the ordinary airmail. You chose. The censors saw it anyway, whatever you chose, and blacked out the things that put information where it shouldn't be. It was not only the censors. There were other reasons to choose.

*The potatoes came in good,* she wrote. *Better than last year. I think Vati is pleased.* He had asked about the potatoes in his April letter — *How do the fields look this spring?* — which was the kind of question you asked when you were somewhere that didn't have fields, or had different fields, or had fields you were not permitted to think of as fields. She thought about this when she wrote about the potatoes. She always sent him what he asked for.

She looked at the next line.

Martha.

Martha had died in July. A mastitis case — the big Holstein Rolf had named when they were seven and nine, for a reason Hannah couldn't remember anymore. He'd called her Martha with great ceremony, an announcement rather than a suggestion: *That one. She is Martha.* And Martha she had been for eleven years. The infection came on fast, the left quarter, and her father had called the veterinarian in from Machias, and they had done what they could for three days. She had been a good cow. Patient in the way that big cows were patient, which was an entirely different quality from what you'd call patience in a person.

She could write it. There was space.

She thought: Rolf reading the letter. Some room, some tent, some building where men read letters while the war was nearby. She thought of the sentence arriving on a reproduced form and Rolf holding it — *Martha died this summer; it was her left side; we couldn't save her* — and the word Martha, which had no military significance whatsoever, going through the censor's eyes and out the other side untouched, arriving whole. She thought: this is the thing you should be able to tell someone. The cow you both named.

She would tell him when he came home.

She wrote instead: *The cows are still out on the pasture but not for much longer.* She said *the cows* and meant all of them and did not mean Martha and moved on to the next line.

*The weather has turned cooler already. There is fog every morning off the ridge.* She looked at the fog sentence and let it stand. It was true. *Mutti sends her love.* Her mother had not said anything to send, but it didn't require asking. Magda's love for Rolf was not something you had to articulate; you could convey it on her behalf without lying about it. *Vati says the north pasture fence should hold through another winter.* Vati had said this two days ago, flat and declarative, to no one in particular, while she carried the fence brace past him. She had heard it and filed it and now it was in the letter. The north pasture fence would hold.

She looked at the lined space that remained.

She could not put Karl's name down. She had not thought it in terms of *not putting* — it was more that she looked at the space and Karl was simply not in it. But if she tried to locate the reason: she would have to begin with October of last year, when the Army truck came up the farm road with four men in the back. She would have to explain the icehouse and Lonzo Reed and the kind of fence that was both a boundary and a place where things happened. She would have to explain the word-cards, the cedar rectangles passing back and forth under the stone at the fence post. She would have to explain what it was like to have a conversation in two languages across a fence post with someone who was, technically, the enemy, who was also from a town fifty kilometers from where Vati grew up, and who had a father who ran a butcher shop in Regensburg, and who had never seen the sea before the troop ship brought him to this coast.

She would have to explain all of it without making it sound like a story, because it wasn't a story. It was the situation she was inside.

And she couldn't write *mündig* — the word Dr. Klein had put on the board in April, *having your own mouth*, the state of speaking for yourself — and what she'd been quietly doing with it, on her side of the fence, over these months. What it meant to choose what you said and to whom, and what that was making her.

The censors would have none of that anyway.

She wrote: *I am well.*

She wrote: *The weather is changing.*

She looked at what remained and wrote: *I think of you.*

She signed her name.

She read the letter over once, the way you read something you already knew was insufficient before you read it. The potatoes. The cows, unnamed. The fog on the ridge. Mutti's love by proxy. Vati's opinion of the north fence. I am well. The weather is changing. I think of you. It was true. All of it was true. It left out most of what was happening and it was still, she thought, the letter she had to offer him, at this distance, in this September.

She capped her pen, folded the form, and went to the hook by the door for Rolf's coat.

---

The coat was still two sizes too large across the shoulders, as it had been since the first winter she'd taken it off the hook. She had thought she might grow into it; she was as tall as she was going to be, which was two inches shorter than Rolf and narrower across the back, and the sleeves still came past her wrist cuffs by an inch when she let her arms fall. She wore it anyway. There was something about the extra room she had not wanted to give up — the way it felt like margin.

The road to town was the September version of itself: goldenrod going brown in the ditches, the sky the particular pale blue that arrived after the equinox and stayed until November. She passed no one on the road until she reached the edge of town.

Ada Gile took the form without comment, which was what Ada always did. She looked at the address block, looked at the date, and set the form on the scale without weighing it.

"Two cents," she said.

Hannah counted the pennies out of her coat pocket onto the counter. Ada handed her the stamp and waited while Hannah affixed it — square to the corner, pressed against the dampener Ada kept beside the window. She slid the form back across the counter.

Ada set it in the outgoing mail and returned to the letters she was sorting, rubber-banded in a stack.

On an impulse she hadn't planned, Hannah said: "Do you hear news from the boys?"

Ada did not look up from the letters. "Some. Fewer letters than there were."

She said it neutrally. She was stating an observable fact about the volume of mail that passed through her hands, the way she stated facts about mail. Hannah heard what lay beneath it: that some of the correspondents had stopped, and they had stopped for a reason that came first by telegram. The telegram came to Ada before anyone. Ada knew things by their shapes in the mail before anyone in town knew them by their names.

"Thank you," Hannah said.

"Mm," said Ada, still sorting.

---

She went home the long way.

The long way added a quarter mile and went past the school, which was the nicer road in September — more trees, the road slightly sheltered from the harbor wind. She was not in a hurry. She wanted to walk and the road through town was where her feet went.

The school building sat at the north end, the old mill converted, the upper windows tall and south-facing for the loom light that was now classroom light. She passed the front entrance, the sign she had walked past since she was six: COLDWATER REACH HIGH SCHOOL. The building was quiet. School had been out more than an hour.

She was nearly past when she noticed the light in the second window from the east end, upper floor. Room 6. The German and Latin room.

The afternoon sun came in from the west side, which meant the room's far side was in full light — she could see the chalkboard from the road, the map of Europe that always had pins in it, the rows of empty desks. At the front desk, visible through the glass, was Dr. Klein. He was sitting very straight. He was not writing. He was reading something he held in both hands, something flat, and he was not grading because he was not moving — no pen, no marking, no turning of pages every thirty seconds the way you turned pages when you were grading.

She stood on the road.

He was doing something private. She could not see what was in his hands. She could see only his posture — the same posture he maintained in class, very straight, very contained, the quality that made you think at first he wasn't breathing until you caught the small adjustment of his head as he worked down a page. She had been in his class for two years. She had looked at that posture across a classroom for two years and she had thought: this is what a person looks like when they are keeping themselves in order. She did not know why she thought that. She just thought it.

From the road, through the glass, he looked like a man doing what you'd expect him to be doing after four on a school day. He looked settled. He looked like someone whose stillness had a reason she couldn't see from where she was standing.

She thought: she had just put a letter in the outgoing mail that did not say the things that were actually happening on the farm. Her father was out at the north pasture fence right now, speaking Bavarian German with a man from Regensburg about posts and frozen ground. Her mother was upstairs moving through the house on tasks Hannah couldn't follow. Ada Gile stood at her counter knowing things by their envelopes that the families of those men did not yet know by name. And Marcus Klein was sitting in his classroom after four in the afternoon reading something by a lamp he did not need to keep lit for anyone who required it of him.

Everyone in this town was keeping something. She thought it the way she'd noted the fog that morning — as a fact about the weather, the same factual attention, no self-pity in it and no irony either, just the observation that this was the condition of things in Coldwater Reach in September 1944, and that she was one of the people in it.

She walked on.

The road curved south below the school and she could no longer see the window. The ridge above town was on her left, and from here she could see what she had told Rolf about in the letter: the leaves at the upper edge, not turned yet, but something had shifted in the green — a lightening at the margins of the topmost maples, a loosening — that said the turn was two weeks out, maybe three. She had been watching that ridge her whole life and she knew what it looked like before it changed.

She turned up the farm road.

The barn was visible from the bottom of the drive. The sound of the post maul was gone now — the fence work finished for the day or else the men gone inside. The cows in the south pasture were moving in the slow, indifferent way cows moved at the end of the afternoon, drifting toward the fence line, toward the barn door, toward the next thing required of them. She counted them by habit, the way she had been counting since she was small enough that it was something to do with her hands while she waited for her father.

Seven. Eight. Nine.

The herd complete, except for one.

She went inside.

input.content:
# Reed's Chair

The first bunk: the gray-bearded one, flat on his back, arms at his sides, breathing like a man with opinions about sleep who has gone to the trouble of holding them. The second bunk: the big one, the one with the Bible — a German-language Bible they'd been issued by the Army, who knew where it thought they were going. He was reading it by lantern, which was more than Lonzo did with his Bible, which he hadn't opened since his mother's funeral in 1929. The third bunk: the one with the bad knees, on his side, his face to the wall.

The fourth bunk: empty. Blanket folded back, folded the way it was always folded, the way the youngest one made his bed every morning with the particular care of a person who had been taught to do it and had decided to keep doing it even here. On the crate that served as a shelf, a cedar piece with a word on it — HARBOR — turned English-side up in the lamplight.

He held the lantern up a little longer. As if looking again would change the arithmetic.

One. Two. Three.

He counted again. One. Two. Three.

He stood in the middle of the icehouse floor for what felt like a reasonable interval. The cold that the sawdust walls couldn't entirely stop came off the floor in the way it always did, working up through the soles of his boots. The gray-bearded one kept breathing. The big one turned a page.

---

Outside. The headcount book was in his coat pocket, same place it always was. He opened it against the icehouse door, found the October line, found the column. He kept his pencil sharpened — habit from twenty years of logging trap hauls, where a dull pencil in wet weather was nobody's friend. He looked at the column for the youngest one's name. He looked at it for what it would cost to put an accurate mark there.

He wrote: *Present.*

He wrote the date. He wrote *L. Reed, CP.*

He put the book back in his coat pocket and went to sit in his chair.

---

The chair was a folding camp model, canvas back and seat, that he'd been using since the arrangement started in November of 1943. Eleven months in this chair, give or take. He had a thorough knowledge of the chair by now — the left rear leg's tendency to stick in soft ground, the particular angle you got when you sat all the way back against the canvas versus when you stayed upright. He stayed upright tonight. His knee did not favor the other option.

October. Full dark at nine, which he still hadn't made his peace with, though he supposed he wasn't going to. The farm was quiet except for the cows in the barn, which were restless the way they always were in the first cold — shifting weight, knocking against the stall boards, that sound of large animals disagreeing with a change in weather. He didn't know if they felt the temperature or the smell of it or something he didn't have a name for. He'd asked the Pfeiffer man about it once, in the spring, in the brief period when they spoke English to each other. Pfeiffer hadn't known either, or hadn't known in a language they shared at that particular moment.

The knee told him the temperature. The winch accident had been 1937, on Murchie's boat, and the knee had been in the forecasting business ever since. Tonight it said October and meant it.

---

He had known since July. Not known the way you'd be required to put it in a report — known the way you knew a thing when you'd been sitting in the same chair watching the same yard for eleven months. You learned the yard when nothing was happening. You learned the particular shape of each man's way of moving through it and what each man looked like when the movement had a reason and when it was looking for one. The youngest one had started going to the north fence line more than the work required. The girl had started appearing on the far side of the fence at times that corresponded.

He had watched this by not watching it. There was a way to sit in a chair with your eyes open and your attention distributed across the whole yard that involved not directing your attention at the thing you weren't supposed to be seeing. He had not learned this method on this farm. He had learned it earlier, in other situations — on the boats, mostly, where you understood early that three men in a small space for fourteen hours needed a particular kind of privacy that wasn't available in any material sense and had to be manufactured from attention. You learned to see what you needed to see. The rest you saw and put somewhere that wasn't the front of your mind.

The word-cards had been obvious from the start. Cedar pieces, small enough to pass under a stone at the fence post — he'd seen the exchange more than once, English on one side, German on the other, a side going out and a different side coming back. He didn't read German. He didn't need to. He understood what the cards were for.

He had been watching the youngest one for eleven months. What he had seen:

A nineteen-year-old who had been drafted and would have preferred not to have been. From the sound of the German the Pfeiffer man used with him — *der Metzger*, once, the word for a butcher, and the boy nodding the particular nod of someone hearing the name of the thing he was — a butcher's apprentice, from the province the Pfeiffer family was from, which was not a coincidence in any ordinary sense of the word coincidence. Not a fanatic. Not a volunteer. A boy who fixed fences when told and did it right and asked the right questions about the right way to do it — the same way he'd ask about the right way to break down a cut, efficient and attentive. He wanted to go home. He probably couldn't yet imagine what going home would mean, how different the home would be from the one he left.

Lonzo took the flask out of his coat pocket and held it. He turned it in his hand and put it back.

What filing the count accurately would cost: the Bangor provost marshal's office would send someone within two days. The man from Bangor would review the arrangement — the farm, the icehouse, the work contract, Lonzo's logs going back to November 1943. Reviews of arrangements, in Lonzo's experience, tended to conclude that the arrangement was the problem. The Pfeiffers would lose the labor. The four men would go somewhere with more supervision per man — not Houlton, somewhere stricter, somewhere appropriate for men whose guard had taken his eye off. The youngest one would go there with the others. Lonzo could not say what happened after that, because what happened after that was not his to say.

He thought about what useful looked like, at sixty-one, with a bad knee, on a farm outside Coldwater Reach, with a folding camp chair and a rifle he hadn't fired at anything in two years. He thought about it in a practical, non-rhetorical way, the way you thought about whether a particular piece of line had another season in it. You assessed the condition. You made a judgment. The judgment was either that the line would hold or it wouldn't, and you made your call and lived with what the call cost you.

He sat.

---

At ten-fifteen he heard footsteps on the path from the direction of the ridge road. Light, careful on the gravel — the step of someone who knew how to move quietly and was doing so with a purpose. He heard the footsteps come around the barn corner and then the creak of the icehouse door opening and closing, and then nothing.

He did not turn around.

He noted the time by the watch on his wrist. He sat for another quarter hour. The temperature had dropped another degree; the knee confirmed this. The cows had settled. The yard was dark and empty and quiet.

At ten-thirty he took the lantern and went inside.

---

The gray-bearded one: asleep. The big one: Bible closed, face to the wall now. The one with the bad knees: asleep, same position as before.

The youngest one was in his bunk. Eyes shut. Breathing even.

Lonzo looked at him for the length of time it took to be certain of what he was looking at. Then he was certain. He did the rest of the lockup and went out.

---

His room was in the Pfeiffer barn — one stall partitioned off with board lumber, a cot from the Bangor provost's supply, a chair and a table that Pfeiffer had provided without discussion when Lonzo arrived in November of 1943. There was a small iron stove that took a piece of kindling and a split of hardwood, which was sufficient for the room's size. He had not lit the stove tonight. He had not yet decided whether the night required it.

He lay down on the cot.

The headcount book was still in his coat pocket. He did not take it out.

*What does it cost*, he thought. *What does it cost.*

He lay there with the question. He did not finish it. It was a question with the shape of something that wanted a number at the end — something you could put in a column and settle. He had looked at it long enough to know it was not that kind of question. Maybe it cost nothing. Maybe it cost something he didn't have a word for yet. He would find out or he wouldn't, and it would not be tonight, and there was nothing further to be done about it tonight.

Somewhere in the barn, one of the cows shifted in her stall with the sound of a large animal redistributing its weight — the sound of the world at night, the same sound it had been making since he was young enough that it was just the ordinary noise of things. He closed his eyes.

He went to sleep.

**Chapter 22 — "Reed's Chair"** is clean on all hard-canon facts. One internal/cross-chapter continuity issue was found and corrected in place:

|---|---|---|
| Word-card material: HARBOR card on Karl's shelf called **"a butter-box lid"** | Second sentence of the opening section | Changed to **"a cedar piece"** |

"Butter-box lid" conflated the container with the card and contradicted the material established in both ch. 21 and ch. 22's own generic description. The fix was a single phrase substitution that leaves all surrounding prose and rhythm untouched.

|---|---|---|

| "She had turned eighteen then" — in spring, when Marcus taught *mündig* | Canon #8: turns 18 in spring or summer 1944. Story arc Ch. 14 (Mud Season): turning 18 and *mündig* lesson are the same chapter. | ✅ |
| "They found a man there last year. He had drowned." (Oct 1944, body found Oct 1943) | Canon #5: body washes up in October 1943. "Last year" from Oct 1944 is accurate. | ✅ |
| Karl: "Dass ein Mann kam vom Wasser. Ohne Papiere." — he had heard about the body | Canon #23: no espionage angle. Karl's knowledge is framed as mundane camp gossip, not intelligence. | ✅ |
| Karl had never seen the sea | Story arc Ch. 11: "he tells her he has never seen the sea." | ✅ |
| Rolf's coat, Rolf in Italy | Story arc Chs. 14, 21: Rolf's V-Mail from Italy; coat established as hers across multiple chapters. | ✅ |
| Hannah out at 8:30, Karl through gate at ~9:10, back before 10:15 | Ch. 22: headcount at 9 p.m. (Karl absent), Karl returns 10:15. Hannah's timeline (gate at ~9:10, mile up, dwell, mile back faster, Karl goes "the long way around") is tight but plausible within the ~65-minute window. | ✅ |
| Hannah "heard Lonzo speak of it once" — the body | Canon #7: Coast Guard takes body; local knowledge minimal. Hearing Lonzo mention it once is consistent with minimal public information. | ✅ |
| Icehouse arrangement implied as ongoing in Oct 1944 | Ch. 22: arrangement started Nov 1943, eleven months prior = Oct 1944. | ✅ |
| *Mündig*: "having your own mouth, the state of speaking for oneself" | Etymologically accurate (from *Mund*, mouth); consistent with Marcus as German/Latin teacher. Story arc Ch. 14 confirms the lesson. | ✅ |

---

**Verdict:** Chapter 23 is clean. Every material fact — Hannah's age, the body's timeline, Karl's language arc, the headcount timing from Ch. 22, the icehouse arrangement dates, and the *mündig* lesson — checks out against canon and the adjacent chapter. input.content:
# The Hearing

The good suit had been pressed since Sunday. He had done it himself, in the kitchen, on the board Lillian kept behind the pantry door, working the iron in careful strokes along the trouser legs and then the jacket's sleeves and then the jacket's front, doing it correctly rather than quickly, the way he did anything that could not be undone if done poorly. He had hung the suit on the back of his bedroom door and left it there through Monday and into Tuesday. All day Tuesday — through the morning's Latin class, through lunch eaten at his desk, through the afternoon's German recitation — he had known it was hanging there. He had changed into it at half past five and walked to school through the November cold, the leather portfolio under his arm, arriving at the building's main door at twenty minutes before six.

The library windows were lit from the inside. He could see them from the street while he was still thirty yards from the door: the yellow warm rectangle of them, the shadow movement of people taking their seats at the long table. He stopped on the walk for a moment, not from hesitation, but from the minor curiosity of seeing a room he knew from a different angle. In daylight the library windows were unremarkable — they faced south and admitted the flat November light in two rectangles that fell across the card catalog and the periodical shelves without distinction. From the street at dusk, with the room lit from within, they were specific: he could see the top of the tall case of encyclopedias, the back of one board member's head, the ceiling's dropped plaster where the water had come in three winters ago and had been repaired but not replastered to match.

He went inside.

---

The library had been arranged. The long table from its usual center-wall position had been drawn toward the windows and seven chairs placed on the far side of it, leaving the near side open. A single chair was positioned on the near side, across from the board. At the table's far end, slightly separated, a school chair from the corridor — the kind with a paddle arm — had been placed against the wall. Edna Locke was sitting in it.

She was not a board member. Her presence had been listed in the formal notice as *faculty representative*, which meant that someone on the board had thought her presence was appropriate, or useful, or expedient — Marcus had not determined which, and she had not volunteered it. She had not told him she would be there; either she had not known until the last moment, or she had decided not to tell him. He was not sure which she would have thought was kinder.

At the library's side wall, turned at an angle to face neither the board nor Marcus but some space between them, Principal Fenn sat in an observer's chair with his hands folded in his lap. Fenn was not a man who revealed positions before they had been decided. He held his hands folded in his lap with the composure of a man who had practiced being present at decisions without being responsible for them.

The seven board members were ranged along the table's far side. Marcus did not know all their names; he recognized four faces. Aldrich, the chair, was at the center, a man of perhaps sixty with an administrator's careful face, the face of someone who had learned, over years of keeping proceedings orderly, that his own opinions were a disruption to the orderliness of proceedings. To Aldrich's left, three faces; to his right, three more. Among the seven: two whose families' names Marcus recognized from the petition Henry had described — he did not look at them directly.

He sat in the chair they had placed for him. He set the leather portfolio on the table before him, unopened. He put his hands on it.

Aldrich said that the hearing was called to order at six o'clock on the evening of November fourteenth, nineteen forty-four. He read the names of those present for the record. He read the petition's concerns. He read them in a measured voice — not hostile, not warm — the voice of a man reading aloud material he had not authored and for which he was taking no personal responsibility. Foreign national status. German origins — the word *German* stated twice in consecutive sentences, without the modification that would have made the distinction Marcus intended to make. The appropriateness of a German national in a wartime classroom. The question of undisclosed organizational membership.

Aldrich finished and looked up. "Dr. Klein. You've received the written petition."

"I have."

"Would you care to respond to the concerns as stated?"

"I would."

He had decided, between Sunday and today, that he would respond in the order the petition had presented the concerns, not in the order he might have preferred. Responding in the board's order was a form of cooperation he could extend without sacrifice; it also meant beginning with the error he was most able to correct, which was a logical efficiency.

"I am Austrian," he said. "I was born in Vienna, in 1896. I have not been a German citizen at any point in my life." He paused, not for effect, but because what followed needed to be said without appearing to follow automatically. "The distinction the petition has not made — I make it here, because accuracy is the appropriate standard for a proceeding of this kind."

He told them about the Anschluss. Not at length; the essential structure was simple and he presented its structure, not its texture. Austria absorbed in March 1938 into Germany, without the consent of the elected government, and in the view of most observers — he chose that phrase precisely, not wanting to claim more authority than the historical record provided — against the will of most of the population. He had left Vienna in March 1938, the same month. He had gone to Paris. After the fall of France in 1940 he had gone to London. In the autumn of 1940 he had arrived in Cambridge, Massachusetts, on the rolls of the Emergency Committee in Aid of Displaced Foreign Scholars.

He heard his own voice managing the room's acoustics. The library was smaller than a lecture hall; the sound returned to him more quickly, and the board members were closer than students, and there was no lectern. He was sitting, which was different. He spoke at the same pace he would have used for a lecture — measured, allowing time for the note-takers to keep up, neither rushing toward the difficult material nor lingering before it.

"I was interned at Sherbrooke, Quebec," he said. "Camp N, for civilian internees — enemy aliens, in the administrative language of the period. The internment lasted approximately nine months, in 1941. I was released and cleared by both the Canadian and American authorities before taking the position in Coldwater Reach in March of 1942."

He stopped. He had said what needed saying about Sherbrooke. He did not elaborate on the experience — the specific fact of it was relevant to the board's inquiry; the experience itself was not; and he had learned, over several years of being questioned by various administrative bodies in three countries, that elaboration was rarely requested in good faith, and that the absence of elaboration was not, in any case that mattered, mistaken for evasion.

"As to organizational membership," he continued, "I hold membership in the Modern Language Association and in the Linguistic Society of America. I am not a member of any political organization. My field of study is philology. I am a philologist."

The room was quiet in the way rooms went quiet when people were deciding what they were permitted to say next.

---

A board member to Aldrich's right — a woman named Ansel, whose family ran the feed store on the south end of Reach Road, and whose son Marcus had not taught because the boy had graduated in the spring of 1942 and gone directly to the Navy — said: "You mentioned Paris, Dr. Klein. What was your situation in France?"

He described the period: the Emergency Committee's Paris office, the academic network, the manner in which the fall of France had accelerated the Committee's effort to move its scholars to England and then America. He described it in the same flat register he had used for the rest, because the period in Paris had contained things that were not appropriate for this room, and because the things that were appropriate could be stated accurately in that register, and because the register was, in any case, his.

She nodded and wrote something.

Another board member — second from the left, a man with a gray mustache who had not spoken yet — said: "Do you have family in Austria, Dr. Klein?"

Marcus looked at him.

The question was, in structure, the simplest question that had been asked. A single subordinate clause. An answer that was, structurally, a single syllable or a brief phrase. He had not anticipated this particular question — or rather, he had anticipated the territory but not the exact topography, not this precise approach from this precise direction. He had been in this vicinity before. He knew the quality of the ground.

"I did," he said.

The board member looked at him. Marcus watched something change in the man's face — not sympathy, or not only that, but a kind of recognition that the footing had shifted, that the question he had asked had arrived somewhere he had not been planning to arrive. The man's pen was in his hand. He did not write.

He did not follow up.

Aldrich looked at the table. The room held itself for a moment longer than usual before someone's chair shifted slightly and the proceeding continued.

---

Near the end — after two board members had asked about his formal qualifications and he had answered them, after Aldrich had asked whether the Emergency Committee had documentation of his cleared status and Marcus had produced the provost marshal's release from the portfolio and placed it on the table — a man near the head of the table on Aldrich's left said: "I'll put it plainly. Do you consider yourself loyal to the United States of America?"

Marcus considered the word *loyal*.

He considered it for the length of time it took to consider a word seriously: two or three seconds, which was not long by a clock but was long enough in a formal proceeding that several board members looked up from their papers to see if he would answer.

*Loyal* came through French from the Latin *legalis* — lawful, of the law — from *lex*, the binding rule. In its feudal application it had named the bond between a person and the authority to whom that person was subject; one was loyal *to* a lord, a sovereign, an institution, by virtue of belonging under that authority's dispensation. The question it answered was not *what do you do* but *to whom do you belong.*

"I consider myself here," Marcus said. "Teaching your children German and Latin. And I expect to be here next term as well."

The man who had asked the question looked at him for a moment. He wrote something on his paper. He looked at Aldrich.

Aldrich looked at the board and said: "I think we've addressed the substance of the petition. Is there other business before we move to discussion?"

There was not. He thanked Marcus for appearing and asked him to wait in the corridor while the board deliberated.

---

The corridor was lit by three overhead fixtures. The third one, near the library door, had a loose contact; it had been flickering for two weeks, during which Marcus had noted it flickering on his way to the Latin class that met in the room opposite. It was not flickering tonight. He stood under it and looked down the corridor toward the main entrance and waited.

They deliberated for eleven minutes. He counted, because he was the kind of man who, in situations of waiting, found it useful to track what could be tracked. At eleven minutes Aldrich opened the library door and asked him to come back in.

Five in favor of maintaining his employment. Two opposed. The result was formal and final; Aldrich read it into the record. He said the review was concluded. He thanked Marcus again, using the word *forthright*, which Marcus received without comment.

He collected his documents from the table — the portfolio open now, the papers returned in their order — and put on his coat. He said good evening to the room. Several board members said good evening in return; one did not look up; Edna Locke, from her school chair at the far end, held his look for a moment and then looked at her folded hands. What her expression contained he read as the economy of a woman who had sat through a proceeding she had not been able to do much about, counting what it had cost and finding the total unsatisfying in a way that was nobody's fault and therefore nobody's to fix.

He went out.

---

Henry was in the corridor.

He was working from the far end, his mop in long even strokes, the wet head spreading ahead of him across the linoleum, the bucket on its wheeled base behind him at the corridor's far end. He was working steadily, without hurry — the methodical pace of a man moving through a task he had done so many times that the doing of it required only the fraction of attention that kept the strokes even. He had not looked up when the library door opened.

Marcus walked the length of the corridor. His footsteps were audible on the linoleum. The flickering overhead fixture was steady. He reached the main door.

"Five to two," Henry said. He did not pause the mop.

"Yes," Marcus said.

"Better than I expected."

"Than I expected as well."

Henry drew the mop back and sent it forward in the same even stroke, the head fanning the width of the corridor, spreading the clean water in a pale arc.

Marcus pushed open the main door and went out into the cold.

---

The November air had the density it acquired before snow — not the clean cold of a clear night but the held, flattened cold of a sky that had come down to think about what it was going to do. The clouds were not visible as clouds; they were visible as the absence of stars, the low ceiling that absorbed the town's light without giving any of it back. There would be snow by the weekend. He had been in New England long enough to know the quality of this particular dark.

He turned up Reach Road. The school was behind him. Ahead: the general store lit, everything else dark.

He thought about *loyal*.

He had given the board a substitution: *I am here* for *I belong to you.* It was not evasion — evasion would have been to give them the word they wanted attached to a sentiment he did not feel, which was not precision and was not honest and was not, in his estimation, what the occasion required. What he had done instead was to answer the question he could answer accurately, which was the question of his presence and his commitment and his intention to continue. These were facts. He had chosen to be here; he had declined to leave when leaving had been available to him; he had sat in a school board review at six o'clock on a Tuesday evening in November and answered questions in the board's order, with documents, and would continue to teach German and Latin to the children of Coldwater Reach in the spring.

That was not what the man at the table had meant by loyal. The man at the table had meant something closer to the feudal sense: to whom do you belong, where does your allegiance finally rest, who are you ultimately. Marcus had been asked versions of that question in Vienna, in Paris, in London, in an administrative building in Sherbrooke Quebec, and he had answered it differently each time, and none of the answers had been wrong but none of them had been complete, because the question assumed a kind of settled relationship to sovereignty and to belonging that he had not had since 1937 and perhaps had not fully had even then — had not had since he had understood, watching the early political agitation in Vienna, what the question was eventually going to be used for.

What he had given the board instead was the only honest answer he had: he was here, and he was here by decision, and he expected to remain.

He was not sure this was the same as *heimisch* — the condition of being at home in a place in the way a person was at home who had never needed to decide to be there. He did not think it was. He thought it was the thing before *heimisch*: the act of placing yourself in the vicinity of something that might, over time, become the condition. It was a practice, not a state. He was practicing it.

The Crow house on Birch Street had its kitchen light on, as it always did at this hour. He went up the porch steps and knocked once — his habit, though neither Henry nor Lillian had ever suggested it was necessary, and Lillian had said explicitly on two occasions that it was not — and opened the door.

The kitchen was warm. It smelled of something that had been on the stove in the afternoon and been put aside and not quite cleared, a good smell, the smell of a household that cooked in quantity and ate the surplus with satisfaction. Lillian was at the kitchen table with her mending basket, a cotton nightgown half-done in her lap. She looked up at him with the expression she had that contained patience and information at once — she knew; Edna Locke had told her, or Henry had sent word, or she had simply sat in the kitchen long enough for the news to find its way through the walls of the house, which was a thing she seemed able to do.

He set the portfolio on the chair inside the door. He hung his coat on the hook beside it.

"Five to two," he said.

She nodded once. The needle continued through the cloth. She said: "Sit down and I'll get you something."

He sat down at the table. She put the mending aside and went to the stove and he heard the lid of a pot come off and the sound of the ladle, and she set a bowl in front of him without ceremony — soup, dense with potato and whatever the week's ration had allowed — and went back to her chair and picked up the mending and did not look at him in a way that was not unconcern but rather the opposite: the careful not-looking of a person who understood that the best thing she could offer right now was to be in the room without requiring anything of him.

He ate. The kitchen was warm. Outside, the November sky was settling toward its decision — the clouds low and patient, the cold accumulating, the weekend's snow organizing itself in the dark above the town. The two votes that had gone against him were still two votes, and would remain two votes; the men who had cast them would still be on the school board when the veterans came back, and what happened at the next review, if there was a next review, was not a question for tonight. Tonight the soup was hot and Lillian's needle moved in and out of the cotton and the kitchen light was on and the house was warm and he was in it.

He thought, without quite deciding to, about the word *here*. Not the English word but the weight of what he had meant when he said it. *I consider myself here.* Here, in this room, in this house on Birch Street, in this town at the edge of the cold Atlantic, in the first winter of his third year in Coldwater Reach. Here, which was not Paris and not Vienna and not Sherbrooke and not Cambridge but was the place where he had decided to be, however many times now he had made the same decision, one year at a time.

He finished the soup. He said: "Thank you, Lillian."

She said: "There's more," and he shook his head and she said: "All right, then," and she went on with the mending, and outside the cold continued to assemble itself toward snow.

**One small fix applied. Summary of the review:**

---

**Issue found and corrected:** Winter-count arithmetic error in the final paragraph.

**Original:** *"in the second winter of his third year in Coldwater Reach"*  
**Corrected:** *"in the first winter of his third year in Coldwater Reach"*

**Reasoning:** Canon #16 establishes Marcus arrived in Coldwater Reach in March 1942. By anniversary year:
- Year 1: March 1942 – March 1943 → one winter (Nov '42–Mar '43)
- Year 2: March 1943 – March 1944 → one winter (Nov '43–Mar '44)
- Year 3: March 1944 – March 1945 → one winter, commencing November 1944 ← **the hearing date**

November 14, 1944 is therefore the *first* (and only possible) winter of his third year. "Second winter of his third year" is not achievable by any counting method. The fix is a single word change with zero effect on voice or rhythm.

**Everything else checks out cleanly:** birth year (Vienna, 1896 ✓), Camp N / Sherbrooke / ~nine months ✓, March 1942 arrival ✓, German and Latin instruction ✓, Edna Locke as faculty representative (consistent with ch09) ✓, Henry mopping as chief custodian ✓, Lillian at the Crow house ✓, five-to-two vote matching the story arc entry ✓, no espionage elements ✓, timeline within the story's October 1943–spring 1945 window ✓.
I'll read all the necessary files in parallel to begin the continuity review.

Now let me carefully cross-check the chapter against canon and the adjacency context. I have all I need — let me analyze.

**Findings from review:**

1. **Timing / "eight years" vs. nine:** Chapter 24 is explicitly dated **November 14, 1944** (in the hearing text). Chapter 25 follows immediately in November 1944. Within Ch. 25, Eira states she has been "making this argument since November 1935" — the month Owen died and she took over (consistent with canon facts 2 & 3). But two sentences later the chapter reads: *"She had told herself this for eight years."* November 1944 − November 1935 = **nine years**, not eight. The author likely carried forward the "eight years" figure from the canon opening (where it is correct for autumn 1943) and forgot that a full year has elapsed by this chapter. This is a small, precise fix.

2. **Owen's chart folders "from 1922 through 1935"** — Canon says Owen held the post since 1921. A filing system started one year into the post is plausible; not a contradiction.

3. **All other facts check out:** Eira's character, the harbor office layout, the civil defense meeting reference, Howie Beal as a new named character (no prior canon entry to contradict), Orcutt as selectman (no prior canon entry), the chandler in Machias, Owen's November 1935 death, the oilskin coat detail (consistent with Eira's character as established) — all clean.

**Action:** Fix the single instance of "eight years" → "nine years" in the chapter.

input.content:
# The Selectmen

The quarterly report wanted three more line entries and a fair copy of the mooring-field schedule. Eira had the raw figures spread on the desk: fourteen moorings active through October, two vacated in September — the Hadley boat hauled out for good, she suspected, and a trap-hauler whose owner had gone to work at the Bath iron works and not come back for the season. She had the maintenance ledger open beside the scrap sheet and the October tide gauge record clipped to it, and she was working through the figures in pencil before committing them to the report in ink.

The first snow of the season had come overnight. Two inches, still on the pier shed roof and the breakwater cap and the inner float line, pale and flat in the November light. It would be gone by Thursday if the temperature held above freezing in the afternoon, but its being there was the signal the harbor had been waiting on: the remaining lobster smacks to their winter moorings, the floats pulled and stacked, the mooring lines inspected for ice damage before the inner anchorage froze solid in January. She had the list. She always had the list.

She heard him before she saw him — the quality of footsteps on the pier planking that came from the direction of Reach Road. Two boots, unhurried, even weight. The door opened on the second knock, which she hadn't answered.

Dwight Orcutt came in.

He was a big man, well-fed in the particular way of men who had not found the war inconvenient in the usual ways. Good dark wool coat; the gray scarf he had been wearing since about 1940. Somewhere under forty, not called up. She had heard a murmur mentioned, though not confirmed. He was on the selectmen because his uncle was Briggs Orcutt, who ran the fish-buying operation from the south pier, and because Dwight had the kind of good intentions that made him comfortable holding authority he had never had to earn. He was not a malicious man. He was a man who had not had to think carefully about what his good intentions cost.

He surveyed the office — her desk, the ledgers, the windows overlooking the anchorage, the chart cabinet against the wall — and he looked at the chair across from the desk and sat in it. She had not offered the chair. He was the kind of man who would not have waited for the offer; not rudeness, exactly, but the practiced ease of a man who expected to be received without condition.

"Morning, Eira," he said.

"Dwight."

He put his elbows on his knees and looked out at the anchorage. Three boats on their winter moorings, the float line mostly pulled in, the breakwater cap white with yesterday's snow. The flat November sky. He was buying time; she let him.

"Good snow last night."

"Two inches." She returned to her figures. "Gone by Thursday."

He nodded at the anchorage and did not say anything for a moment. She moved the mooring count from the scrap sheet to the report. Fourteen active through October. Two vacated. The harbor's revenue was a modest thing and was not the point; the harbor existed because the harbor had to exist and her job was to manage it correctly.

"Howie Beal is coming home," Orcutt said.

She set down her pen and looked at him.

"December, they think. His family had a letter." He opened his hands toward the window, a gesture in the direction of civic warmth. "The town is glad. He was the marine engineer on the *Sentinel* before he signed up — knows the harbor as well as most men. He'll need something to come back to."

She had known Howie Beal by sight and little more. Marine engineer, competent by reputation. They had spoken perhaps six times over the years: on the pier, once at a civil defense wardens' meeting in the winter of 1943. A capable man; she had no reason to doubt it.

"Is Howie interested in harbor management?" she said.

The small shift of Orcutt's hands on his knees. "Well. He'll need work he knows."

"I imagine he will."

"The harbor's a natural fit. There'll be men coming home who'll need reintegration into the work they know. That's the situation we're all facing. The harbor's a natural fit for a man like Howie." He paused, the pause of a man approaching the sentence he had come to say. "He could learn the administrative side, with time."

"The administrative side is the job, Dwight."

He looked at the window. The harbor light at the end of the breakwater was dark at this hour, the stone and the caplight both white with snow in the flat morning.

He said, in the tone of a man not accustomed to being required to state things plainly: "We want to make sure our veterans have opportunities, Eira. I'm sure you understand the position."

She dated the top right corner of the quarterly report. "The quarterly report will be on your desk by the first of the month. Is there anything else?"

The pause lasted perhaps three seconds. He stood.

"I expect we'll have occasion to speak again," he said.

"My door's open," she said. It was not an invitation.

He went to the door. He was too careful a man for slamming; the door was too heavy in any case. But the click of the latch as it caught had a quality she registered the way she registered the barometer: a specific pressure, worth noting, filed.

---

She stood at the window.

The anchorage was quiet. Three boats, the float line, the white breakwater cap. Beyond the breakwater the water ran pewter-gray to a horizon she could not find today — sky and sea nearly the same color, the line between them somewhere out in the cold she could not see. The Lighthouse Service tender due in on Friday if the weather held.

She was going to fight this. Not by petition or letter. Not by appeal to the district or the state board, not by any mechanism that required her to step forward as a complainant and name what was being done, which required naming what she was. She was going to do the job. She had been doing it for nine years. The quarterly report on Orcutt's desk by the first. The mooring lines checked before the first freeze. The approach charts updated when the channel shifted. The harbor's record kept legibly, the maintenance log current, the tide gauge read and noted at the same hours it had been read for nine years. She had been making this argument since November 1935. She was naming it to herself now, clearly, as what it was — which was different from not having known.

When Howie Beal came home in December she would be at this desk.

She went back to her desk and finished the remaining columns of the report in ink. Then she crossed to the chart cabinet.

---

Five drawers, pine, painted gray. Owen had bought the cabinet in 1924 from a chandler in Machias who was closing his shop; she could still see, faintly, the price chalked on the back of the second drawer when you pulled it all the way out. She opened the fourth drawer from the top — the main chart drawer, the working charts for the harbor and its approaches — and took it out entirely and set it on the desk.

Twenty-three folders, Owen's hand on every tab: the dates he had first created each one, from 1922 through 1935. Not the chart dates but the folder dates — when he had opened that file and made it his. She knew his handwriting the way you knew a handwriting read over years: the slant, the flat crossbar on his sevens, the particular width of his capital letters. In the first months after Owen died she had thought she would reorganize the drawer. She had not. She had opened it daily, taken what she needed, replaced it when she was done. The system worked; there was no urgent reason to change it; the harbor's work got done. She had told herself this for nine years, and every word of it was true.

She pulled the first folder at random: June 1927. Harbor chart, inner anchorage. Owen's hand from that year: the mooring field as it had been, twenty-seven moorings in two rows, each one noted with his shorthand for the mooring type and the holding ground, his soundings in fathoms taken from a dinghy on a calm August day she could not have been present for. In the lower right corner, in her own hand, a penciled note from April 1940: the reconfiguration she had done after six outer-row moorings came out of service, the spacing adjusted, the coordinates updated. She had dated her addition. His folder date was June 1927; her notation was April 1940; the chart held fourteen years of the harbor's changes in both their hands.

She set it aside and pulled another: August 1929. The approach chart for the harbor mouth, the bar at the south side. Owen's original soundings in his notation — fathoms in a diamond symbol, every buoy position in his shorthand. Her addendum from 1938 after the first dredge run, and again from 1941 after the second — she had used a smaller symbol for her own soundings to keep them legible against his, a circled figure where he had used the diamond. The paper was the good linen stock the chandler in Machias had stocked before the war took the supply; it had held up well through the years of daily handling.

She went through all twenty-three folders. Each one she opened, checked the chart inside, set it face-up on the desk in its group. When she had seen them all she had the full picture.

She made seven new folders from the card stock on the supply shelf and labeled them in her hand: *Approach — outer*, *Approach — inner*, *Anchorage — main field*, *Anchorage — winter moorings*, *Buoy positions*, *Tide records*, *Maintenance*. Owen had filed by date of creation because that was how he had thought — by when things had occurred, what year the harbor had been in what condition. She thought by function: what a chart was for, what it answered, where you went in the drawer when you needed to know a specific kind of thing.

She moved each chart from his folders to hers. His original folder tabs she discarded — they were the filing mechanism, not the record — but his dates in the corners of the charts themselves she left exactly where they were. His hand in the corners, her hand in the margins and on the new labels. When she came to charts she had drawn entirely herself — the 1941 post-dredge approach charts, the mooring-field schedule she had redrawn from scratch in 1942 when the configuration changed again — she filed them alongside his in the same functional folders, in date order within the category. Her charts rested next to his in the same drawer.

It was past four when she finished. She checked each new folder in sequence, confirmed the contents, confirmed the label. She slid the drawer back into the cabinet. The wood moved cleanly on the runners and seated at the bottom with an even stop.

She stood with her hand on the drawer front for a moment. Seven labels in her hand. Owen's dates still in every corner of every chart back to 1922. She had not covered anything. She had not erased anything. She had made room.

She washed her hands at the basin — pencil dust and old paper, the brown edge of linen that had been handled for a long time — and put the kettle on and made coffee and stood at the window while the harbor went to its late-afternoon color. The snow on the breakwater cap had been worked off by what thin sun there was. The boats sat easy on their moorings; the water in the anchorage flattened toward evening, the light going off it.

She drank the coffee at the window. She washed the cup, dried it, set it on the shelf. She locked the harbor office and the pier shed, checked the padlock, and walked up to Reach Road.

---

The house was at the south end, a hundred yards past the general store. The light had gone while she was locking up; it was past five, and November closed down fast and definite at this latitude.

His oilskin coat was on the hook by the door.

She hung her coat on the hook beside it and went into the kitchen and put the kettle on.

The tea came up. She stood at the kitchen table and drank it without sitting, without adding anything to it. The kitchen window gave onto the back lot, dark now, the fence top a pale line where the snow still held. The temperature was dropping; she could feel it at the window glass. She would need to check the mooring lines in the morning before the day's work started. The Lighthouse Service tender on Friday. The quarterly report on the first.

She rinsed the cup and set it on the shelf.

She went to bed.
