**Assessment:**

The chapter is structurally sound: the body discovery, Gervais's efficient scene-work, the log entry, the flashback revelation, and the final coda with Owen's things all move at the right weight for an opening chapter. The closing line ("The dash where it was.") is clean and earned.

**One fixable redundancy in Section 5 (the coffee window):**

After the flashback, section 5 builds a composed atmospheric sequence: percolator, the south window, the fog lifting, the surfboat clearing the anchorage, the harbor-sounds paragraph, "She drank her coffee." — this lands perfectly. The section closes with a still, processed quality that earns the transition into the Owen-cups section.

Then Gervais returns on foot and makes a second goodbye. The problem is twofold:

1. **Repeated information**: At the scene he already said "She'll hear from the First District. When there's anything." The return adds only "Within the week, most likely" and a thin compliment ("Good documentation on the body. The position analysis."). Nothing advances T1 or any other thread. Ch04's follow-up comes from Lt. Pruitt, not Gervais — so this second exchange doesn't even set up the right person.

2. **Repeated atmospheric gesture**: His exit is closed with a harbor-sounds paragraph ("She heard nothing more from outside except the water and the idle of the smacks…") that mirrors the harbor-sounds paragraph immediately above ("The harbor was the harbor again: its sounds, its smells…"). Two nearly identical atmospheric beats in the same section.

The result: the chapter's close has four descending quiet beats (window/surfboat, Gervais redux, Owen's cups, breakwater inspection) where three would be more proportionate. Cutting the Gervais-return block — from "Gervais's boots on the pier boards" through "Roy Blaine's engine carrying south" — lets "She drank her coffee." serve as the section's natural close. Section 6 then arrives without a false restart.

This is a clean cut. Applying it now.

input.content:
# The Breakwater

Halfway down the breakwater she could see them: two yellow slickers at the far end, standing still, looking down. The fog was in close. The storm had moved offshore the day before, but the sea was still working off three days' worth of accumulation — she could hear it in the low sound running beneath the fog, a drag and suck at the base of the granite that wasn't the storm anymore but wasn't settled water either. Underfoot, the breakwater was wet and in two places glazed from a freeze that had let go by four in the morning, leaving the stone the color of wet slate.

She had walked this granite in every condition the Maine coast produced. She walked it now at the same pace she always walked, reading each course of stone by the memory of her feet.

She placed them as she got close. Roy Blaine's height and the way his hands were in his pockets. His nephew Eddie Dunst, Roy's sister's boy, standing with his weight on his right leg. They had not touched the body. They stood back from it six feet or so, which was the right instinct, and they said nothing when she came up because they understood she needed a moment and they had nothing to add to what was already visible.

"Morning," she said.

"Morning." Roy kept his hands in his pockets. "We come out to check my moorings. Found him before we got there."

"When?"

"Half past five. Before first light. Before we called."

She looked past him.

The man lay in the angle between the seaward face of the breakwater and the iron ladder bolted into the last course of granite at the far end. Facedown. Left arm outflung, right arm tucked under his body, feet pointing south. He was wearing oilskin over a wool sweater — dark wool, brown or black, the color indeterminate under fog and wet — and knee-length rubber boots in the standard pattern. Nothing on the oilskin indicated a vessel or a service. No name. No insignia. No painted mark.

She crouched at the edge and looked without touching. The iron ladder's lowest rung was eight inches above the waterline at this tide, the tide four hours past the ebb and still dropping. She traced the geometry of how he lay. The position was wrong for a fall from the structure: a man coming off the breakwater would have landed differently, closer in, at a different angle. He had come from the anchorage side. The eastern chop, wave action working off the storm, carrying him up against the granite when the tide had been higher and the water had been running higher along the ladder iron. He had not fallen. He had been deposited.

She checked his pockets. One hand in each side pocket of the oilskin, methodical, feeling without looking. Left pocket was empty. Right pocket had water in it and something that had been cloth before three days in the sound had taken whatever shape it had. She could not retrieve it without tearing it apart. She withdrew her hands.

His left hand — the one she could see, the outflung arm — was a working hand. Weather-roughened. The nails were close-cut and one was cracked at the quick. He had worked outside, and not recently become someone who did so.

She stood up.

"Go back to the shed," she told Roy. "The Coast Guard will need statements from both of you."

Eddie Dunst opened his mouth. Roy's hand came up and Eddie went quiet and they went.

---

She heard the surfboat before she saw it: a diesel note coming around the south point, careful in the fog, finding its way without rushing. Petty Officer Gervais stepped off the bow before the line was properly fast, which told her something about the way he operated. He was young — thirty, perhaps a year either side — and he had the face of a man who managed situations by managing his expression first. He came up the ladder and stopped when he saw her.

"Petty Officer Gervais, Machias station." He did not extend his hand. "Are you the harbormaster?"

"Eira Bowman. Yes."

"Did you disturb the body?"

"I checked the pockets. Nothing retrievable. I didn't move him."

He went past her to look, and she stepped back to give him the space for it. His crew came up the ladder with a canvas litter and a camera. Gervais crouched where she had crouched and looked at the same things she had looked at, and then looked at the ladder iron, and then stood.

"Your observations," he said.

She gave them to him in order of relevance: body position, angle inconsistent with a fall from the structure, placement consistent with arrival from the anchorage side on eastern chop. Clothing: oilskin, wool sweater, rubber boots, no insignia, no vessel markings. Apparent age thirty-five to forty-five. Working hands. Pockets empty or waterlogged, nothing retrievable.

He listened without writing anything down. She noted this: he was carrying it in his head, which was a particular skill, the kind that came from doing it often.

"Any unusual vessel traffic in the past seventy-two hours?" he said.

"Nothing cleared in or out during the blow. Standard, in a three-day nor'easter."

"Anything prior to the storm?"

She looked at the anchorage. The fog was thinning. She could see the *Macomber* at her mooring — Birch Macomber's coaster, in harbor since before the storm, waiting on weather — and the two lobster smacks on the south side, and the empty water between.

"Nothing unusual," she said.

He asked several more questions in the same efficient order, and she answered them the same way — precisely, within the scope of what she had noted in the official record. He thanked her. The crew moved to the body with the litter, and Eira stepped back farther and let them work. When they were done and the canvas was lashed, Gervais turned and looked at the anchorage the way she had looked at it, though he was looking for different things.

"She'll hear from the First District," he said, not quite to her. "When there's anything."

She walked back down the breakwater to the office.

---

The harbor office was twelve feet by fourteen, the converted pilot house at the near end of the pier shed. Chart cabinet to the left of her desk. VHF set on the shelf above the south window. Owen's barometer on the east wall at the height where Owen had hung it, which was not quite the height she would have chosen but was the height she had left it in, eight years ago, and now it was simply where the barometer was.

She had not reorganized the chart cabinet. She had not moved the barometer. She had added to the office over eight years — the new tide tables folder, the civil defense binder, the second mooring-line coil she kept under the desk in winter — and changed what the work required her to change, and left the rest where it was, and by now what had been Owen's arrangement and what was hers had grown together until she could no longer have said with certainty where one ended and the other began.

She opened the incident log to Wednesday, 13 October 1943.

She wrote the date and time: 0612. She wrote the entry in order. Male victim, apparent age 35–45, maritime dress — oilskin, wool sweater, rubber boots — no identifying documents or vessel markings recovered. Body position consistent with arrival from the anchorage side via eastern chop and inconsistent with a fall from the breakwater structure. Reported to the harbormaster at approximately 0545 by two fishermen who came out to check their moorings. Coast Guard Petty Officer L. Gervais, Machias station, attended the scene and took custody of the body at 0631.

She turned back two pages to Friday, 8 October, and read the last full entries before the blow: the coaster *Macomber*, in harbor since the third. The buoy tender *Samoset*, in and out on the fourth. Two lobster smacks, day trips, both back before dark.

She turned forward to Saturday, 9 October.

Her entry at 1400 on the following day — written Sunday afternoon, filling in from memory, the storm's second day — read: *Vessels in harbor: Macomber (coaster), moorings checked and secured, 0015. Weather deteriorating, bar seas building N to NE. Blow expected 1800–2000.*

Below the line: the column for vessel traffic, evening through midnight, which she had not completed when she wrote the entry and had not returned to.

She put her pen to the column now.

She thought about what it needed to say.

She made a dash.

---

It had been just after midnight when she went out. The mooring bridle on the *Macomber* had a chafe point she had noted two days before — wear at the thimble, the kind that would worsen fast in heavy weather — and with the barometer already well down from the afternoon reading and the first gusts beginning to make the pier lines snatch, she had gone out to check it before the main weather came. The walk from the office to the *Macomber*'s mooring pennant was three minutes in good conditions; in the first gusts from the north it was five.

She had been at the bow cleat, working the chafe point with a hand torch, when she looked up and saw him.

He was at the shore end of the pier. Standing at the edge of the overhead lamp's reach — in the light but only barely, which gave her the shape of a man and no more. Canvas bag on the ground beside his feet. He was looking at the water.

She had meant to call over to him. Ask where he was berthed. Ask what vessel. But the bridle was the immediate problem — one of the seizing wires was pulling and needed resetting — and she had attended to it, and when she straightened up and turned around, the end of the pier was empty.

She had assumed he had gone into the pier shed for shelter. Men did this: transient crew, waiting on weather, finding the shed door unlatched. She would find him in the morning and sort out the paperwork.

She had not found him in the morning. The blow came in at 0130, earlier than she had expected, and by 0200 there was no morning in any ordinary sense — only the harbor in storm, the *Macomber* pulling hard at her mooring, the smacks sheering, and the work of keeping it all from coming apart.

She looked at the dash in the column.

A dash in her log meant no anomalies noted. She had not written that; she had made a mark. The column had been blank before, and it was filled now — with a notation that meant one thing and described another. A man standing at the end of the pier, before a three-day blow, canvas bag at his feet, facing the water: this was not vessel traffic. There was no column in the vessel-traffic log for a man standing on the pier.

She had not entered him because she had not known where he was berthed, and without a vessel she could not make the entry complete, and then the storm had come. She could not enter him now.

The dash was not inaccurate to what the column was for. It was not accurate to what she had seen.

She closed the log and went to fill the kettle.

---

She made coffee in the tin percolator — four measures of grounds, which was right for the size of the pot — and stood at the south window while it came up. Outside, the fog was gone. The anchorage was clear in the October morning, the *Macomber* on her mooring with the settled motion of a vessel that has been through weather and is done with it. To the south, Roy Blaine's smack was heading out across the bar, the engine carrying that uneven idle she had been meaning to mention to him for three weeks.

The surfboat was still alongside the breakwater, finishing. She watched Gervais's crew, small at the far end, making their preparations. When they were done the surfboat's engine started and the boat swung away from the granite and came down the anchorage, giving wide berth to the *Macomber*, throttle opening as it cleared the pier end. Its wake spread across the flat water and reached the *Macomber* and lifted her briefly, then reached the near pilings and touched them and flattened out.

The harbor was the harbor again: its sounds, its smells, the diesel and the brine and the October cold that was coming off the water now that the fog was gone.

She drank her coffee.

---

She rinsed her cup at the utility sink and hung it on the hook below the barometer. She had put both hooks in herself, the week she moved into the office: a length of copper pipe from the bilge of a derelict smack, cut and bent and mounted below Owen's barometer on the east wall. She had put the second hook in the month after Owen died, when the office was hers and she had been deciding where things would go. His cup had been on its hook since 1935. She washed them both each morning and hung them back — hers on the left, Owen's on the right — and had done so for eight years without examining the practice.

She took Owen's oilskin from the wall peg. It was the right weight for the work. It had always been the right weight for the work, which was why she had taken it and had never found a reason to stop.

Outside, the breakwater lay in the October morning — granite and ordinary, the sea beyond it running a low swell, nothing that asked anything of anyone. She began the inspection at the pier shed door and worked seaward, the way she always did.

On the desk behind her: the incident report, signed. Beside it, the log. The dash where it was.

text:
**Fix applied.** Here is exactly what was cut and why:

---

**Cut:** The Gervais-returns block in Section 5 — from *"Gervais's boots on the pier boards…"* through *"…Roy Blaine's engine carrying south."* (7 paragraphs, ~150 words)

**Reason:** Double redundancy.
- *Information*: Gervais had already said "She'll hear from the First District. When there's anything." at the scene. The return added only "Within the week, most likely" and a thin compliment ("Good documentation on the body"). Neither advances T1 nor sets up Ch04's follow-up (which comes from Lt. Pruitt, not Gervais).
- *Atmosphere*: His exit was closed by a harbor-sounds coda that mirrored the harbor-sounds paragraph immediately above it — two nearly identical atmospheric gestures in the same section.

**Result:** Section 5 now ends cleanly at "She drank her coffee." — a still, self-contained beat that earns the transition into Section 6's Owen-cups passage. The chapter's close moves from four descending quiet beats to three, which is proportionate for an opening chapter that needs to leave pressure on the dash rather than dispersing it across a prolonged wind-down. The final line ("The dash where it was.") lands with the same force; the path to it is now tighter.

No larger issues warranting documentation. The chapter is otherwise well-paced: the body discovery is efficient, the Gervais scene-work is correctly brisk, the flashback is placed at the right structural moment (after the dash is made, before the log is closed), and the Owen-objects detail in the final section does exactly the character work it needs to do without overstaying.
I'll read all four files simultaneously before making any assessment.

Now I have everything I need. Let me assess the chapter carefully before deciding on action.

**Assessment:**

Chapter 2 is structurally sound. The arrival sequence is the chapter's best work — the cataloging of the four men is crisp and cinematic, the youngest-one observation earns T4's entrance, and the *fremd* close is correct. The kitchen scene does genuine T5 work (Magda's silence, Albrecht's declarative speech pattern).

One passage is redundant: the opening paragraph of the "She stayed until she was done" section restates information the reader has just witnessed — she finished milking, she watched through the gap, there was nothing more to see — without advancing either thread. It delays the chapter's pivot back to the yard (and Lonzo's small characterizing moment) with a summary the reader doesn't need. The section immediately reads better starting at "The light was different."

Everything else earns its length. This is a single-paragraph cut.

input.content:
# The Icehouse

She had her hands on Berta when she heard it.

Not a farm truck — the note was wrong, too low and too heavy, the kind of engine that didn't need to hurry because it weighed enough not to. The road from the county line came past the woodlot before the farm, and in October with the leaves thinned she could sometimes hear vehicles before they turned into the drive. She heard this one for a full half minute before it turned.

Berta shifted. Hannah kept her hands where they were.

The barn smelled the way it always smelled in the morning and in the late afternoon both: hide and manure and the particular vegetable sweetness of the hay in the upper mow, and underneath all of it the cold, which was October now, coming up through the concrete in the floor and pressing through the board-and-batten of the south wall whenever the wind had anything in it. By December the cold would be something else — something that worked on you instead of just sitting there — but for now it was only present, only a reminder of what was coming. She had left her coat on the hook by the stall partition. Her hands were warmed from the work.

The truck stopped somewhere past the barn. Then the engine cut.

She gave Berta another thirty seconds and then moved to the gap in the boards.

She had known about the gap since she was nine years old. It was between the fourth and fifth boards on the east wall, knee-high when she was nine and now at her waist, and it gave her a narrow rectangle of the yard and the lane and the icehouse front. She did not think of it as a secret. It was simply a gap that was there, and she used it the way she used any feature of a place she knew better than she knew most things.

The truck was Army. Olive drab, a canvas-covered bed, two soldiers in the cab. Another one standing in the truck bed with his back against the cab wall, rifle at sling arms. He was young — younger than she'd expected, though she hadn't known what she'd expected until she saw him and understood he wasn't it. The four men were already stepping down from the bed.

They did it without being told to. That was the first thing she noticed: no order, no gesture from the soldier, just the four of them stepping down in sequence with the practiced ease of men who had done this exact thing before, in other truck beds, in other farm lanes. They wore Army-issue denim — work clothes, the same cut on all four, none of them quite fitting. The denim was a pale blue-gray that in the October afternoon light looked almost colorless.

She cataloged them.

Three older men — not old but older, in the way that forty-something looked when the work had been physical for long enough. The first was heavyset, gray-bearded, with the settled quality of a man whose size had been a fact of his life long enough that he'd forgotten it. The second was very large, the kind of large that made him conspicuous in the denim as though the clothes had been sized for the idea of a man rather than this particular one. The third moved carefully when he stepped down from the truck, favoring his right leg, setting his weight with the deliberateness of someone who had learned to be deliberate because the alternative was pain.

The fourth was young.

She knew how old she was, which was seventeen, and she knew the arithmetic: he was maybe two years older than she was. Maybe three. He stepped down last, after the careful man, and stood in the lane with his hands at his sides and looked at the icehouse. Not at the barn. Not at the farmhouse. At the icehouse, which was what he would be living in, and which was a long low building of board-and-batten she had grown up next to and taken entirely for granted until three days ago when a man from the Army had come to talk to her father.

He looked at it the way you look at something you are trying to understand from the outside before you go into it.

She had seen that look before. It was the look of a person doing a calculation. She had seen it on Rolf's face the morning he left for Bangor on the bus, standing in the yard with his bag, looking at the farmhouse. He had been nineteen.

This one was no older than Rolf had been when Rolf left.

---

Lonzo Reed came down from the passenger cab last. He took the time to find his footing on the running board, his left hand on the door frame, his right holding the rifle by the action so the barrel cleared the truck frame as he stepped down. He put his weight on the right leg first. He stood still for a moment with one hand still on the door, letting whatever was happening in his knee finish happening, and then he straightened and walked around the front of the truck to where the four men were standing.

He was not a tall man. He was not, as far as Hannah could see, a frightening one. He had the rifle across the crook of his arm now, the way you carry a rifle when you're not pointing it at anyone but you haven't put it down. He looked at the four men and then he looked at the icehouse and then he turned and said something to the younger soldier, who reached into the truck bed and produced a folding canvas camp chair and carried it to the side of the icehouse door and opened it.

Lonzo sat down in it. He put the rifle across his knees.

That was all. That was apparently what he did.

---

Her father was in the farmhouse doorway.

She had not seen him come out, but he was there: in the door frame with one hand on the jamb, not coming down the porch steps, not crossing the yard. He watched the truck the way he watched most things that came onto the farm from outside — with the kind of attention that was absolute and gave nothing back. The younger soldier came up the porch steps with a clipboard. Her father looked at it. He took the pen the soldier held out and signed where the soldier indicated and handed the pen back and then he was looking at the yard again, at the four men in their denim, at Lonzo in his chair, and he had not said one word.

The soldier went back to the truck.

Hannah moved her hands back to Berta without meaning to. Berta made a sound that was impatience.

*Gut*, she thought, *gut, ich komme schon* — not thinking it in German on purpose, just thinking it the way she thought things when she was busy, which was in whatever language arrived first. She worked and watched and worked.

The older soldier gestured toward the icehouse door. The four men went. The careful man went first, the young one last. Before he went through the door the young one turned and looked at the farmhouse for a moment — not at anything specific, just at the building itself, the way he had looked at the icehouse — and then he went inside.

The door closed.

The two soldiers got back in the truck. The engine turned over, the olive-drab bulk swung around in the lane, and the truck went back down the road through the woodlot and the sound of it faded away.

Lonzo Reed sat in his chair. He did not appear to be doing anything in particular. He had his hands on the rifle across his knees and he was looking at the icehouse door, and his expression was the expression of a man who had made his arrangements for a long afternoon.

Her father went back inside the farmhouse.

The yard was the yard. The icehouse was the icehouse. The October light was coming down the way it did in the late afternoon, going amber in the lower quadrant, and Lonzo Reed sat in his chair in it and the icehouse door was closed.

Hannah went back to the milking.

---

The light was different when she carried the pails out to the cooler.

Lonzo Reed was still in his chair. He looked up when the barn door opened and then looked back at the icehouse when he saw it was only her. She did not look at him. She carried the pails across the yard with her eyes on the cooler building and went in and set the pails and came back out and the yard was quiet in the October afternoon, just the wind in the woodlot and the sound of the cows settling inside and Lonzo Reed in his chair with the rifle across his knees.

She went inside.

---

Her mother was at the stove when Hannah came into the kitchen. She had been at the stove for an hour, from the smell of it — root vegetables and the end of a ham bone, the smell of a soup that had been going long enough to have opinions. She did not look up when Hannah came in. She moved from the stove to the table and back with the precision of a person who had made the same circuit ten thousand times and required nothing to be in any position other than where it always was, and did not acknowledge the difference between a kitchen with another person in it and a kitchen without.

Hannah washed her hands at the sink and stood and listened to the sound of the soup.

Her father came in from the front of the house and sat down at the table. He had been somewhere — upstairs, the front room — but he brought nothing back with him, no book, no newspaper, nothing in his hands. He sat and put his hands flat on the table and looked at them. His hands were large and rough and he had worn them down over twenty years of farm work into something that no longer looked like the hands of a man who had come from a town to do this — they looked like they had always been farm hands, except she had seen a photograph once of him before, younger than she could imagine, with the hands of someone else.

*Das Essen wird fertig sein*, her mother said, without turning from the stove. The food will be ready.

Her father said nothing. He looked at his hands on the table.

They ate. Her mother had made the soup and also bread, dark bread from the rye flour, and there was butter for it because they made their own and the rationing only bit so deep into what you produced yourself. Her father ate with the systematic attention he brought to eating, which was the same attention he brought to most things: complete, and turned inward.

After a while he said: *Die Arbeit, die sie machen sollen, ist gut.*

The work they're supposed to do. It's good work.

He was not talking to either of them. He was stating a fact in the way he stated facts that he had been working out on his own and arrived at a conclusion about. He said: *Der Zaun auf der Nordweide muss repariert werden. Sie werden damit anfangen.* The fence on the north pasture needs to be repaired. They will start with that.

Her mother put down her spoon and picked it up again. That was all.

Hannah ate her soup. She had not told either of them what she had seen through the boards — the four men stepping down, the young one looking at the icehouse, the particular way Lonzo Reed had lowered himself out of the cab. Her father knew more than she did; he had signed the papers. Her mother knew whatever she knew, which she kept the way she kept everything.

*Wie viele Kilometer ist es von Straubing nach Regensburg?* she thought, which surprised her — she had not meant to think that, had not known she was going to, but there it was: how many kilometers from Straubing to Regensburg? She had looked at a map of Bavaria in her geography book once, years ago. The cities were close together. They were both on rivers that were the same river or near enough. She could not remember the number.

She thought about the young one's face when he had turned to look at the farmhouse before going in.

She could not have said what his expression was. Not frightened, she thought. Not angry. Something else — something that didn't yet have a name available to her, the way some things didn't, the way she sometimes felt something clearly and completely and could not find the word for it in either language.

*Schau nicht so*, her mother said — don't look like that — without looking at her. Hannah looked down at her soup. She had not known she was making a face.

Her father said: *Morgen früh.* Tomorrow morning.

He meant the fence. He meant they would begin tomorrow morning.

---

Rolf's room was hers now, which meant it was the room she slept in and the room that had Rolf's coat on the hook by the door. She had moved nothing when they gave it to her — his room was bigger, and it had the east window that caught the first light, and when her parents suggested the move she had agreed because it was the larger room and not because she wanted his things, and then she had moved in and not moved his things and now they were hers in the way everything becomes yours if you live with it long enough, which was to say they were still Rolf's.

The coat was on the hook. She changed into her nightclothes and stood at the east window in the dark. The yard below: the barn, the icehouse, the woodlot beyond. There was no light in the icehouse. There was no light in the yard except what came through the farmhouse windows. Lonzo Reed's chair was gone from beside the icehouse door — someone must have brought it inside, or taken it away at shift's end. She hadn't heard another truck. She didn't know how it worked. She didn't know any of the particulars of how this was going to work, which was a feeling she was becoming familiar with.

She thought: *fremd*.

It was one of her father's words, one he used when he meant something that was foreign or strange or not-from-here — a word that had three meanings that were almost the same meaning, and she had grown up hearing it applied to things that arrived from outside: a new variety of seed, a piece of machinery, a way of doing something they'd heard about from another farm. *Das ist fremd*. That is strange. That is not from here.

She did not apply it to anyone in particular. She just thought it, the way a word sometimes arrives and sits at the front of your mind without being aimed at anything.

The icehouse was dark. The woodlot moved in the wind.

She got into bed in Rolf's room, in the dark, and the word sat there for a while, and then she was asleep.

text:
**What was changed and why:**

One paragraph was cut — the opening of the transition section that formerly read:

> *She stayed until she was done. She didn't stay longer than that — there was no reason to, and the cows needed what the cows needed on schedule — but she didn't rush either. She worked Berta through and then Else and then the others and she did not go back to the gap in the boards. She didn't need to. What there was to see, she had seen.*

This paragraph summarized what the reader had just witnessed (Hannah finishing the milking, watching through the gap) without advancing T4 or T5 or providing any new information. Its closing sentence — "What there was to see, she had seen" — over-explains a reader who has been present for the scene. Removing it lets the section start on the concrete image of the changed light, which is a stronger re-entry into the yard and carries straight through to Lonzo's one characterizing moment (he looks up, registers it's only her, looks back at the icehouse).

Everything else in the chapter earns its length. The arrival sequence is the novel's first fully cinematic passage, the cataloging of the four men does essential T4 groundwork, and the *fremd* close is correctly spare. The dinner scene's apparent density is justified: it is doing simultaneous work for T4 (Albrecht authorizing the fence work that structures POW contact for the next several chapters) and T5 (Magda's silence, the mother catching Hannah's face, the household's two languages in operation). No further cuts were warranted.
I'll read all four files in parallel to get the full context before assessing.

Now I have everything I need. Let me analyze the chapter carefully before touching the file.

**Chapter 3 internal pacing assessment:**

The chapter's six sections form a clean shape: morning walk → classroom → teacher's lounge → Crow house dinner → civil defense meeting → night reflection. Each section advances T2 or T3 (or both). The open and close are both strong — the harbor-smell hook and the *unheimlich* coda are exactly right for an establishing chapter. The Rilke beat is well-placed and earned.

**One cuttable beat:** In the civil defense meeting section, Haverstock gets a two-clause embedded biography ("who had owned the hardware store before the county emergency acquisition program changed the nature of what he did, and who had found in his wartime administrative role a purpose commensurate with his organizational temperament") that the chapter doesn't need. He is a one-appearance walk-on; his psychology is irrelevant to either thread; the sentence delays the actual meeting by a full clause before it starts. Everything else in that section — the room, the attendee list with its "accumulate roles the way any surface accumulates what settles on it" metaphor (which earns its keep by echoing Marcus's own position) — is working.

**Everything else is clean.** The student-portrait paragraph is an efficient list that ends correctly on Hannah. The Henry dinner earns its length by introducing *Duldung* and the Crow house as the place where Marcus is "at the table" rather than merely *geduldet*. The corridor exchange with Eira is precise and well-paced. The lounge reflex meditation and the night's *unheimlich* meditation are complementary, not redundant.

Applying the one small fix now:

input.content:
# Monday, Wednesday, Friday

The harbor reached him before the harbor was visible.

He had learned this about the walk from his first week in Coldwater Reach: the road from the Crow house to the school ran along the inland side of the ridge, and then at a point roughly eight minutes from the house it crested, and for perhaps half a minute the smell came — brine and diesel exhaust and the cold particular to open water in October — before the road turned back down toward the north end of town and the harbor fell away, replaced by the back of the Congregational church and the bare maples behind it. He had been making this walk for nineteen months. He knew it in the way you know a repeated thing: without novelty, and without quite losing the fact of it.

He had left the house at twenty past seven, in the good suit. Monday, Wednesday, and Friday were the good suit; Tuesday and Thursday were the other one. This was not a rule he had stated; it had arrived as a consequence of his schedule and remained. The good suit was a medium gray worsted, bought in London in the autumn of 1938 — Cheapside, a tailor who measured him in silence and made no remark about his accent — and it was still good, with qualification. The elbows were going. He had been intending to address this for three months.

The Congregational church sat at the corner where the ridge road met the main street. It was never unlocked at the hour he passed it. He had been inside it twice in nineteen months, both times for community gatherings that his position on the faculty made appropriate to attend; on every other occasion he knew it only by its bell, which rang the quarter-hours and which had entered his internal chronometry without his permission. On the stretch of road between the ridge and the school he knew what time it was by the bell rather than the watch in his coat pocket, and this had been true for some time.

The high school occupied what had been a wool processing mill at the north end of the village — three stories of timber-frame and brick, the brick the dark orange-red of nineteenth-century New England industry, the smell inside a compound of chalk dust and old wood and the heat from the basement plant that Henry Crow had been managing for nineteen years. Henry's connection to the building and Marcus's were of entirely different kinds, and they arrived at different hours and by different doors; but they lived in the same house, and the school was the shared fact of their days in the way that the harbor and the farm were shared facts for others in the town.

He arrived before his students and wrote the paradigm on the board in chalk. *Ich habe. Du hast. Er hat.* He had trained himself to print for the blackboard, because his natural hand was the old Viennese Kurrent — letters joined in a form that a stranger would read as encryption — and there was no pedagogical advantage in writing what the students could not read. In his grade book, in the margins of examinations, he still wrote Kurrent: faster, more exact, and his, in the way that certain things remain yours even when you have given most of the rest away.

---

The German class met on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays. There were six students. He had expected this number; in a coastal town in wartime Maine, German was not a subject that recommended itself to practical parents. The six who were there were there for their own reasons, and he had learned to stop inquiring too directly into what those reasons were.

He had formed his opinion of each in the first weeks. There was Curtis Aiken, who had decided that German grammar was a solvable problem and was solving it with the single-mindedness of a man winning an argument with a machine. There were two girls in the front row, attending with consistent thoroughness, whose note-taking was immaculate; Marcus suspected that what interested them was the act of taking notes rather than the subject of the notes, but he did not consider this a defect. There was a boy who came irregularly and translated everything phonetically, with a sincerity that was touching and a method that was wrong. There was a girl in the back row whose last name Marcus had mispronounced twice — she had corrected him the second time with a precision he found professionally admirable — and had not mispronounced since.

And there was Hannah Pfeiffer, who sat in the second row on the left and already knew more German than any of them.

He had established this in the first week. She was not concealing it from him — she was deciding, with each exercise, how much to show, and that was her right. She knew the declensions. She knew the conjugations. When he asked for a translation and the room went quiet with effort, her pencil moved and stopped, and after the pause that indicated she had made a calculation, she gave the answer. Correctly, each time. He noted this in the margin of his grade book in Kurrent — *Sie weiß mehr, als sie zeigt*: she knows more than she shows — and said nothing to her about it directly. She would give him more when she decided to. Students usually did.

On a Friday in the third week of October he picked up the chalk to write the assigned passage from Goethe and wrote instead, without quite intending to:

*Wer, wenn ich schriee, hörte mich denn aus der Engel Ordnungen?*

The opening line of Rilke's first elegy — not on the curriculum, not what he had planned; it had come out of the chalk before the intention had caught up with the hand. He stood back and looked at it. Hannah Pfeiffer looked at the board for a longer time than the others did. He erased it before they had finished copying it and wrote the Goethe.

---

In the teacher's lounge at noon, the body came up.

The lounge was a small room adjacent to the principal's office. Aldous Fenn, the principal — fifty-five years old, twenty-two years in this particular post, a man of brisk administrative fluency who had reduced his job to something manageable without reducing it further — was refilling his coffee cup when he said it: "That body on the breakwater. Coast Guard still hasn't put a name to him." He replaced the pot and turned to the table. "No papers. No identification. Some kind of drifter, most likely."

There were four of them in the room: Fenn, Marcus, Thibodeau the mathematics teacher, and Calloway the home economics teacher, who had been at the school a year longer than Fenn and who Marcus understood to be its institutional memory. The body had been found two weeks before. Marcus had heard of it already, in the manner one heard of things in a town of nine hundred.

Thibodeau said: "Coast Guard say anything further?"

Fenn said: "Just that they were looking into it."

There was a pause. During the pause, Thibodeau and Calloway each looked at Marcus for a fraction of a second, and then looked at the table.

Marcus drank his coffee.

The pause lasted four seconds. Then Fenn said something about the coal delivery and the conversation reorganized itself around that.

He had been calculating this particular reflex — the half-second redirection of gaze — since at least 1936. He had been calculating it long enough that he no longer experienced it as injury. What it was, precisely, was a category error: when a German-seeming thing appeared in proximity to a German-seeming subject, the category operated. That he was Austrian rather than German, and that his relation to Germany's current behavior was rather different from what the category implied, was not information the reflex could accommodate. Reflexes were by definition prior to information.

He noted it, as he noted all such things, with accuracy rather than feeling. He was not offended. He had expended a certain portion of himself on being offended, between 1936 and 1940, and found the expenditure disproportionate to the result.

Walking back to his classroom, he thought: the reflex was a fact. Like the harbor smell at the ridge. You could know where the water was without seeing it.

---

The evenings at the Crow house ran on Lillian's schedule, which was a reasonable schedule. Dinner at six. The radio in the front room after, or reading, or chess with Henry on Thursday evenings if neither of them was needed elsewhere. He had adopted these rhythms by attrition — the steady pressure of nineteen months in the same house — and had stopped noticing that he was following one. Lillian had said once that he was the easiest person she had ever fed. He was not certain whether this was a compliment; it was, at minimum, accurate.

That week — the last of October, the body still recent enough to surface in conversation — he told Henry about the teacher's lounge.

Lillian had made salt fish with potatoes and there was bread, and they ate with the attention that a good plain meal rewards. After a while Henry said, without particular weight: "They find out who he was?" He meant the body.

"Not yet," Marcus said. "The Coast Guard is handling it."

Henry considered the bread. "They will or they won't," he said. "Doesn't change anything for us."

Marcus thought about this. It was a sentence that could have been resignation and was not; what it was instead was more precise — a declarative that came from a position Marcus recognized: the position of a man who has lived long enough inside systems that do not recalibrate for his presence to be surprised any longer by that fact. *They will or they won't.* An accurate forecast of probability. *Doesn't change anything for us* — and by *us*, the specificity was deliberate: this table, these two men, the house around them. Not offered with emphasis. Offered as a fact.

He thought about the word *Duldung*. Toleration: being-permitted-to-remain. One was *geduldet* by a town, by a board, by a room that looked at you for half a second. The grammar was passive; the condition was not chosen. In most of Coldwater Reach he was *geduldet*. In this kitchen he was not any particular category. He was at the table.

"No," he said. "It doesn't."

---

The civil defense warden meeting was on a Thursday evening in the first week of November, in the school basement. The basement had been the mill's boiler pit; Henry had made it habitable over nineteen years of custodial attention without overcoming its essential character as an underground room. The ceiling was low. The walls were old stone. Voices arrived already absorbed, as if the room had made its own prior arrangements with sound.

Two long tables had been pushed together at the center. A ring of men sat around them.

The civil defense warden was a man named Haverstock. He called the meeting to order at half-past seven. Around the table: Haverstock; two selectmen Marcus knew by sight; the fire chief, a quiet man named Gould, who attended these meetings with the patience of someone for whom the agenda held no surprises; two Coast Guardsmen in uniform; and six or seven others — the practical men of a small town who accumulate roles the way any surface accumulates what settles on it, simply by being present long enough.

He was there as the faculty representative for the building. It was his Tuesday fire watch rotation that would be confirmed tonight; he already knew this.

Across the table from him: Eira Bowman.

He knew who she was. He had seen her at the harbor — the figure in the oilskin coat at the pier shed door, the person who walked the breakwater at low water. He had known her name since his second month in Coldwater Reach and had encountered it since in the context of harbor business. She was the only woman at the table. She was not performing this fact; she had a folder of papers in front of her and she was listening to Haverstock with the quality of attention that assesses what is being said against what is already known, registering the difference without announcing it.

The meeting was procedural. Blackout compliance — the winter schedule, adjusted for the earlier nightfall. Fire watch rotation for the coming month. His Tuesday assignment was confirmed; he wrote it in his notebook. Forty-five minutes of the careful management of theoretical emergencies, conducted by men who understood that the theoretical and the practical were not entirely separated by geography. Then Haverstock said that was all, and the men pushed back from the table.

---

He and Eira Bowman reached the corridor door at the same moment.

He held it.

She stepped through without pausing and he followed and they were in the school hallway — chalk dust, old wood, residual warmth from the day's classes. She was moving toward the main entrance at the same pace he was, the pace of a person returning to something, direct, without social adjustment. He did not attempt conversation.

At the main door she stopped and looked at the sky. Clear, and cold; there would be frost before morning. He stopped beside her.

"You're the German teacher," she said. Not a question.

"Austrian, in fact," he said.

She turned slightly and looked at him — not with surprise, with the expression of a person indexing a correction — and then looked at the road. "Same road home," she said. Meaning the direction.

He fell in beside her. They walked without speaking. The silence had the quality of two people who had established, without discussion, that talk requires a reason. Fifty yards along, her road diverged from his and she turned left without a word — not abruptly, not with any social announcement; simply turned, because the road went that way, and she went with it.

He walked on alone.

---

At the desk under the north window of his room, he opened the grade book.

He kept things in order — the papers stacked on the right side of the desk, the pen in its place, the books on the shelf Henry had brought up from the basement storeroom. A man who has moved eight times in five years learns to keep things in order, because the order will need to survive the next move, and the one after.

He opened to the German class. His annotations in the margins were in Kurrent — *GU* for *gut*, *S.* for *Schwäche*, weakness, and occasional longer notes where the nature of the weakness rewarded recording. Beside Hannah Pfeiffer's name, in the margin of her last examination, he had written: *Sie weiß mehr, als sie zeigt.*

He had meant it as an observation of her method, which was a teacher's legitimate concern. Looking at it now in the script that no one else in Coldwater Reach could read, he thought that it might also be said of several people in this town — the harbormaster who had turned left without farewell; the men who had looked at him for a fraction of a second and looked at the table; the town itself, which had permitted him to be present for nineteen months without quite closing around him.

He thought about the word *unheimlich*.

It had no precise English equivalent — he had looked, professionally, and found only approximations. *Uncanny* came closest but carried a note of the supernatural that the German did not require. The literal structure of the word was plainer: *un-heimlich*, the negation of *heimlich*, which was itself double. *Heimlich* meant familiar, intimate, belonging to the house — and also, by extension, hidden, secret, kept from outside view. To be *heimlich* in a place was to be absorbed by it, to have grown so native to it that the place no longer showed you from the outside. *Unheimlich* was the reversal: the sensation of being in a place where you were exposed precisely because you had not grown familiar enough to disappear into it.

He had not been *heimisch* anywhere since 1937. He was not, in noting this, doing anything he would have called self-pity; he was being accurate, which was a different exercise, and one he had considerably more practice with.

Outside the north window, the night was clearing toward frost. The harbor smell came over the ridge in the wind — faint, cold, exact.

He put the cap on his pen and left the grade book open on the desk.

## What changed and what didn't

**One small cut applied** — in the civil defense meeting section, the Haverstock sentence:

> *before:* "The civil defense warden was a man named Haverstock, who had owned the hardware store before the county emergency acquisition program changed the nature of what he did, and who had found in his wartime administrative role a purpose commensurate with his organizational temperament. He called the meeting to order at half-past seven."

> *after:* "The civil defense warden was a man named Haverstock. He called the meeting to order at half-past seven."

**Why:** Haverstock is a one-appearance walk-on. His two embedded clauses (hardware store → acquisition program; organizational temperament) do no thread work, add no atmosphere the room description doesn't already supply, and slow the scene's entry by ~40 words before the meeting has even opened. The cut loses nothing.

---

**What was examined and left alone:**

- **Student-portrait paragraph.** Reads as a light checklist but it's an efficient five-sentence cascade that ends correctly on Hannah. Each sketch is doing minor character-mode work appropriate to an establishing chapter.
- **Henry dinner scene.** Earns its length by introducing *Duldung* and marking the Crow house as the one place Marcus is "at the table" rather than merely *geduldet* — genuine T2 advancement, not restatement.
- **Lounge reflex meditation vs. night *unheimlich* meditation.** These are complementary, not redundant: the lounge beat is about the category error *others* apply; *unheimlich* is Marcus's own interior condition of non-belonging. Different angles on the same situation.
- **The corridor / road departure.** Precise, well-paced, correctly short.
- **The *unheimlich* close.** Structurally sound coda for an establishing chapter. No issue.
I'll read all four files simultaneously before making any editorial judgment.

Now I have full context. Let me analyze the chapter before touching anything.

**Chapter 4 analysis:**

- **Thread**: T1 only (correct per threads.md). Two beats: (1) Pruitt interview — first deliberate withholding; (2) discovery of the Macomber log entry she can't remember making.
- **Hook**: Opens on weather/desk, boat arrives within ~250 words. Acceptable for an early chapter.
- **Interview scene**: Tight and well-paced. The "No" to Pruitt lands cleanly.
- **Log-discovery section**: Strong. The reasoning through possible explanations (fishermen's notes, storm exhaustion, gaps in memory) is exactly the right register and length.
- **The problem — Macomber file investigation section**: After the discovery beat, Eira goes to the chart cabinet and reads Macomber's registration file for ~350 words. The file tells her nothing she doesn't already know (standard coaster, ordinary runs). It delivers one genuinely useful insight — "a coaster does not put out on the second day of a nor'easter without reason" — but buries it after 300 words of procedural file-reading and a physical sketch of Birch Macomber that pays off nowhere in this chapter or, per the story arc, in any subsequent chapter. The file section delays the chapter's correct close (the pier, the empty anchorage, the resumed work) without raising pressure or advancing the thread.
- **Final section**: The pier scene and the percolator echo are right. The "Nothing there that needed looking at" line is excellent. The chapter closes at exactly the right register — but it arrives late because of the file section between it and the discovery.

**Fix**: Cut the file investigation block. Relocate its one load-bearing insight ("a coaster does not put out without reason") as a single sentence closing the log-discovery section. Cut is ~350 words; one sentence adapted and moved. No restructuring.

input.content:
# The Coast Guard

The northeast had been building since before light, and by seven the inner anchorage was throwing a chop that put white on the mooring lines. Eira was at the desk with the mooring register, working through the fall assignments against what she could see from the south window — the two lobster smacks at their positions, the anchorage otherwise empty now that Birch Macomber had taken the *Macomber* out. The register confirmed what the window showed. She noted the wind direction in the margin and did not write a number yet; the weather station at Machias would have a proper reading by eight, and she did not guess at what she could wait for.

She had made coffee at six, four measures of grounds in the tin percolator, and it was gone by the time she heard the engine.

Not the Machias pattern — bigger, and the rhythm was different, steadier, the sound of a boat running to a schedule rather than to fish. She went to the window. It came around the point past the breakwater — First District, the white hull carrying an unhurried pace, no running signal. Whatever its errand, it was not urgent.

She went back to the desk, straightened the mooring register, and did not put it away. She took the log from the drawer. She found October 9 and left the log open on the desk.

The surfboat tied up at the commercial float below the pier ramp. She watched through the south window as one man came up. Young — she placed his age before his rank, mid-twenties at the most, and then the rank after: a lieutenant's bar on the collar of the peacoat. He carried a folder under his arm and walked the way people walked when they had been taught to walk toward a purpose — not hurried, not looking around.

She turned from the window and sat down.

He came down the pier and knocked with one knuckle at the door, which was not quite a knock but the announcement of arrival.

"Come in," she said.

---

He was perhaps twenty-six. A straight fair face, the deliberate manner of a man who had decided early that deliberateness was an asset. He looked at the harbor office the way new people looked at it — quickly, ceiling to floor, taking inventory — then looked at her.

"Mrs. Bowman. Lieutenant Pruitt, First District." He produced the folder from under his arm and sat down across the desk before she indicated the chair, not rudely; he was accustomed to sitting across desks. "You spoke with Petty Officer Gervais on October thirteenth."

"I did."

He opened the folder on his knee. She could see the top sheet at an angle, upside down, typed at the top and handwritten below: her incident report on the left half, and on the right what appeared to be Gervais's field notes in the compact shorthand petty officers learned. Below these, further sheets she could not read clearly — but the top line of the third page resolved, at this angle, into a typewritten summary: *Adult male, drowning, approximate age 38–48, no identifying documents, no identifying marks.*

She had known the age. She had known the absence of documents; she had checked the pockets herself. The last phrase was new: no identifying marks. Nothing on the skin. Nothing that would carry back to a record, a service file, a tattoo registry. She made a note of this, mentally, and said nothing.

"I want to review the vessel traffic for the night of October ninth," Pruitt said. He had a small notebook out, pen uncapped. "Between twenty-two hundred and oh-four hundred. Your log covers that period."

"It does." She turned the log toward him slightly — enough that he could see the entries, not enough that he could read the whole page easily. "Lobster smacks *Thistle* and *Doris V.* at mooring. Those are Coldwater boats, Roy Blaine and Delmar Stannard. Coaster *Macomber*, Birch Macomber master, Machias — arrived October eighth, departure logged October eleventh. Lighthouse Service ice barge *Ploughman* at anchor, inner anchorage, during that period."

He wrote. His print was precise, the kind kept legible as a matter of policy.

"The *Macomber*." He looked up. "A coaster — what was her cargo?"

"Bagged lime. Machias to Lubec, calling here on the way."

"She was in harbor through the storm."

"She came in on the eighth with weather moving. Macomber is a careful man. He waited on it."

Pruitt wrote.

"Any vessel you observed and did not log?"

She had known this question was coming. Not because he had telegraphed it — he had not — but because it was the next question, the one the inquiry was building toward. Traffic on the water accounted for, and then: what about the water itself?

"No," she said.

He looked up from the notebook.

"Nothing at anchor or underway you didn't account for."

"No."

He wrote.

"During that period — the evening of the ninth through the morning of the tenth — did you observe anything unusual on the pier or dock?"

She looked at the open log on the desk. She did not need to look at it; she could have recited what was there from any room in the building. She looked at it because looking at something while you answered a question was not suspicious. It was what a careful person did.

"I was out at approximately oh-one-thirty," she said, "checking the *Macomber*'s mooring bridle. I'd noted a chafe point at the thimble two days before, and the wind was building. I wanted to verify the set of the bridle on the bollard before the blow came on full."

"And at that time?"

"Nothing unusual. The bridle was holding."

Pruitt wrote for several seconds. She waited.

"That's the full extent of your activity during that period."

"I was in and out during the storm, checking moorings. But nothing significant to report."

He wrote. She watched his pen complete the line, pause, and then move to underline something. Whatever it was, he did not share it.

He closed the folder on his knee. "I should tell you where we are with it," he said, which was a courtesy — he did not have to tell her anything. "Cause of death is drowning. The medical findings don't support foul play. No evidence of injury prior to submersion." He paused. "We haven't identified him."

"No."

"The case is open, but it's not — actively worked, at present." He stood, tucking the folder back under his arm. "If we develop anything further, you'll hear from us. If anything comes to your attention on your end, I'd ask you to contact the Machias station."

"I'll have the log available," she said.

He nodded — the nod of a man who had recorded what was available and found it in order — and went out.

---

She heard the surfboat's engine turn over.

Then she went to the window. The boat had backed off the float and swung its bow toward the harbor mouth. She watched it pass the end of the pier — Pruitt visible at the stern, not looking back at the dock — and round the breakwater. The fog sat low on the water two miles out to the southeast; she could see the horizon where the fog began, the grey wall of it, and the boat going into it. She watched until the shape of the hull was gone and then until the engine was gone.

Then she turned back to the log.

---

She had opened it to October 9 when she first heard the surfboat. She turned forward now, one page, to October 11.

The entry was in her hand. *Macomber — departure 0430.*

She knew her own handwriting in this log the way she knew the angle of the tide gauge in February, or the weight of the padlock on the pier shed door, or the particular resistance of the chart cabinet's second drawer. It was hers. The *M* of *Macomber* with its particular lean, the zero in *0430* closed at the bottom in the way she had always closed zeros — she had tried once to change this habit and not succeeded. No question of whose hand had made the entry.

She had no memory of making it.

October 11 was the second day of the blow. She had been in and out of the harbor office across the two days of the storm — checking moorings every two hours when the weather allowed, logging conditions when she came in, running on whatever the body would give her. On the storm's first night she had been out at midnight and again in the dark hours; in the morning of the eleventh she had come in from the second night soaked through Owen's oilskin, the cold in her hands clear to the wrist. She would have sat at this desk and made entries. She could account for that.

She could not account for 0430 specifically. 0430 on October 11 was before dawn, before she had come in from the second night, a time when she was in the weather and not at the desk.

The harbor had no formal overnight watchman. It had not had one since 1941, when the last man the selectmen employed for the position enlisted. But the pier shed was unlocked at night, and it was not unheard of for fishermen to leave a note — Blaine did it when his boat came in odd hours; Stannard occasionally. Word left on the hook beside the door: *Doris V. in at 0320, secured.* She would find it in the morning and enter it in the log at whatever hour she came in, logging the event with the fisherman's time rather than her own. This was common practice; the log did not require her to have witnessed every entry she made, only to have verified it.

She could have found a note at 0430. She could have come in before the dawn she remembered coming in, made the entry from a note, and gone back out. She had been on six hours in and two hours out for two days; there were portions of those days she could not now reconstruct cleanly.

She looked at the entry for a long time. The blow had run through October 11; a careful man did not put a coaster out on the second day of a nor'easter without reason.

---

She took Owen's oilskin from the wall peg and put it on. The oilskin had settled to her shoulders over eight years of use, the collar the right height, the sleeves the right length now that she had rolled them inside to take up what Owen's longer arms had required. She did not think about any of this; it was the coat she put on to go outside, and she put it on and went outside.

The wind on the pier was twenty knots, maybe a little over, out of the northeast and carrying spray off the anchorage. *Thistle* and *Doris V.* lay at their moorings in the positions she had noted in the register — pulling against their bridles with the indifference of things properly secured, the lines taut and the boats riding the chop without strain. The anchorage beyond was empty. She stood at the pier head for a moment and looked at the breakwater and then at the water south of the breakwater and then at the fog on the horizon.

There was nothing there that needed looking at.

She went back into the harbor office, rinsed the percolator in the handbasin, and refilled it with fresh grounds — four measures, the amount the pot took. She set it on the burner and sat back down at the desk, opened the mooring register to the page she had left it on, and took up the fall assignments where she had stopped.

Outside, the northeast held steady. She noted the time, noted the wind, and went on working.
