input.content:
# What the Body Spoke

The message had been slipped under his classroom door, on school letterhead, in Mrs. Farwell's handwriting: *Mr. Fenn would appreciate five minutes at half-past eight, when convenient.* Marcus arrived at twenty-five past. It was a Tuesday, and he was wearing the older suit.

Fenn's office occupied the northeast corner of the building. Two windows looked north toward the ash trees, bare by now, and between their branches a band of sky the color of old pewter. Fenn stood when Marcus entered, which was his habit, and sat again once Marcus had taken the chair across the desk.

"Dr. Klein. I appreciate your time."

"Of course."

Fenn had a manila folder open in front of him, though his eyes were not on it. He had the manner of a man who had prepared this conversation in advance and was now delivering his prepared opening accurately, which Marcus respected. "You're aware of the body found on the breakwater last month."

Marcus said he was.

"There's been a development of a minor kind. The Coast Guard made a general inquiry — asked whether anyone in town had heard the man speak before the storm. A fisherman came forward. Ed Larrabee. He was patching a trap buoy at the commercial float that afternoon, the ninth, and he heard the man say something." Fenn placed a brief pause before his next words, as if to indicate that what followed was not his characterization but someone else's. "Something European, he said. Possibly German."

The word *European* was doing considerable work. Marcus allowed it to do that work in silence.

"The Coast Guard forwarded the report to the harbormaster's office, which mentioned it to the selectmen, which mentioned it to me." Fenn glanced once at the folder — some record of this chain, Marcus assumed. "I wonder whether you'd be willing to speak with the harbormaster. In case she has any materials — a phrase, something in writing — that might benefit from identification. Not as an investigator, in any sense. I would characterize it as a civic resource."

He had chosen the phrase *civic resource* with some care; Marcus felt the care in it. He was being used and also being asked, and Fenn was trying to accomplish both without the use becoming visible. This was decent of Fenn, whose decency on most matters was genuine if occasionally misaimed.

"I'd be glad to help," Marcus said. He meant this and was also aware that saying otherwise would have read as avoidance, and that avoidance in a particular direction was itself a kind of statement. But the genuine part was true. The body had been on the breakwater, and someone had heard what the man said, and what the man said may have been in a language that was Marcus's by birth and training. The connection was not exotic. It was simple, if a little cold in the feeling of it.

"Thank you." Fenn's relief was brief but visible, which told Marcus something about how Fenn understood his position in Coldwater Reach — that some real possibility of refusal had seemed available. He filed this without responding to it.

"I'll stop at the harbor office after my last class."

"Excellent." Fenn stood. Marcus stood, buttoned the older suit's coat, and went out.

---

He had Latin in the afternoon — the third declension, eight students, a standard Tuesday. He moved through it with adequate attention, and at half-past three collected his coat and hat from the faculty coatroom and went out the building's north door into the November afternoon.

The light was going. Not dark yet but committed to it: the sky a flat ash gray, the sun already behind the inland ridge, what remained of the day a kind of residual illumination without source or direction. He went south on Main Street. Burleigh's was closed, as it was on Tuesdays. The post office showed Ada Gile's handwritten notice about V-Mail hours. The general store had ration-book advisories taped to the inside of the glass. He passed all of it without entering any of it — the town familiar in the way a foreign country became familiar after sustained residence, which was to say: known without being owned.

He had been to the harbor office once before. He remembered the occasion exactly, as he tended to remember occasions in Coldwater Reach exactly, each one still distinct enough to locate precisely. He had gone in the spring of 1942, preparing a classroom exercise on maritime regulation — the Lighthouse Service's jurisdiction, the harbor log as a legal document, the intersection of admiralty law and local authority. An exercise that had ultimately not been used. He had not been sure, when it came to it, whether a visit to the harbormaster's office would be received as a legitimate pedagogical inquiry or as something less clearly categorized. He had decided the uncertainty was sufficient reason to use a different exercise, and he had not examined this decision afterward.

The pier lane smelled of salt and diesel even from the road. He heard the harbor before he saw it: the draw of water against pier pilings, the creak of mooring lines taking the tide's shift, a bell buoy at the anchorage's outer edge. The pier shed stood at the lane's end with a light burning over its door. At the near end of the shed the harbor office, where the structure changed from newer plank to older converted pilot house.

---

The office was twelve feet by fourteen, or close enough. A chart cabinet stood to the left of the desk, its drawers flush and latched. Above the south window: a VHF radio set on its shelf, dial unlit. On the east wall, at a height that would have suited a taller person, an aneroid barometer of the older enclosed type. Below the barometer: two hooks bent from a length of copper pipe, a coffee cup on each — one with a chip at the rim, one without. The room smelled of brine and the heating element and the particular cold that came in at the sill.

On the peg beside the door: an oilskin coat, too large for her frame, hanging with the particular weight of something used past the point of newness.

Eira Bowman was at her desk. She looked up when he came in. She did not stand. Her hands came to rest flat on either side of the open logbook — not a defensive posture but the posture of someone for whom this room was the full extent of the relevant territory, and who had not issued any invitation to renegotiate its dimensions.

"Dr. Klein."

"Mrs. Bowman." He stood inside the door. "I apologize for coming without notice. Principal Fenn asked me to stop by — regarding the inquiry about the man on the breakwater."

She waited. This was not impatience; it was the professional economy of someone who had found that waiting for the substance of a statement cost less than responding to its approach.

"A fisherman — Ed Larrabee, I understand — reported hearing the man say something that sounded European. Possibly German. The report went from the Coast Guard to the selectmen to Mr. Fenn, who asked whether I might be of use as —" Marcus applied Fenn's phrase with intention "— as a civic resource. In case there were any materials. A phrase, a written word. Something that might benefit from identification."

She considered him for a moment with the attention of someone gathering information rather than forming judgment. Then she said: "The Coast Guard has what I know."

He heard this carefully. The sentence was precise in the way of a sentence chosen for its precision: it said the Coast Guard possessed what she presently knew, which was true, and it said nothing about the conditions under which what she knew might later change, or expand, or become applicable in a different context. He registered the precision and did not indicate that he had.

"Of course," he said. "I won't take up your time."

He had his hand on the door latch when he stopped. He could not have said, after the fact, whether this was calculation or something less deliberate; he had turned back before he'd resolved the question.

"If it would be useful," he said, and heard his own formality and made an effort against it, and the effort produced the same formality at a slightly different angle. "Not to the Coast Guard, necessarily. To you. I know something about identification documents from that period — the late thirties, the early forties. Varieties of German. Regional and national variation in written forms. I'm at the school."

Eira Bowman looked at him. She was not deciding whether to trust him — that would have implied a current use, which she did not have — but the look had the quality of something being assessed for later applicability, the way one assessed a piece of equipment for a season not yet arrived. Two or three seconds. Then: "I'll note it."

Marcus thanked her, in a minor key, and went out.

---

The lane was darker than when he had come down it. The lamp above the pier shed door held the near ground but did not reach the road. He went north. Behind him the harbor worked against the pilings in its ordinary manner — water's commerce with structure, mechanical, without reference to what any particular tide had brought in.

He thought about the English phrase: *a man without papers*.

In English the phrase carried the inconvenience of a filing problem. A man who had not submitted the correct documentation, whose identity could not be properly processed because the supporting evidence was absent. Unfortunate. Correctable, in principle. Somewhere the proof existed; it had simply not been submitted.

In German the phrase was *ein Mensch ohne Papiere*. The word *Mensch* was not *Mann* — not man in the gendered or particular sense but person, human, the categorical term that the German philosophical tradition had spent considerable effort defining and that German legal tradition, in the decade just past, had spent considerable effort qualifying and subdividing and finally setting conditions on. To be *ein Mensch ohne Papiere* in Germany in 1936, or 1938, or 1941, was not to have neglected paperwork. It was to be without the documentation that confirmed your standing as a legal person in a system that had chosen to make personhood conditional on documentation. The papers were not evidence of identity; they were identity, in the only form the system would recognize. Remove the papers: remove the person. Not from the physical world — the body continued, as anyone looking at it could see — but from the world that the century had increasingly decided was the real one, the legal and administrative one, the world of records and proofs and the right to be processed.

He had arrived from somewhere and come to rest at the foot of an iron ladder at the end of a granite breakwater. The question of who he was could not be answered from the body because the body's authorship had been removed — by the water, or by whoever had placed him in it, or by himself, if the removal had been intentional.

Had he known, Marcus wondered. Had the man understood what he was without — not just the practical inconvenience of it but the full weight of what the last decade had loaded into that absence. Had he understood himself to be *staatenlos*: without a state to claim him, without a floor of legal existence to stand on. The word in German carried both the administrative condition and a physical image: stateless, floorless, unsupported. The man who could not be proved.

It was possible the man had been an ordinary sailor with inadequate records, a document lost to water or carelessness. Marcus did not exclude this. He was also aware that he was carrying his own experience into the question, which was not sound methodology. But he carried it regardless, because this kind of knowledge did not wait for an invitation.

He had learned the weight of papers in a particular room, in a particular year, when a man in a uniform had looked at his documents and then looked at him and made a decision about what they proved. That was a kind of knowledge that did not translate cleanly across cases. It was, however, knowledge. He was not sure he could do anything useful with it here. He had made the offer anyway.

The ridge crested ahead. Below: the town's lights spread across the valley floor, the Congregational steeple white against the dark sky, chimney smoke running south on the harbor wind.

---

Lillian had left the kitchen lamp on. The low oven held supper — onion and potato, something preserved and darkened in the cooking, smelling of vinegar and thyme. He hung his coat and sat at the table. Henry came in from the back hallway a few minutes later, in his custodian's canvas, the key ring clipped at his belt. He washed his hands at the sink.

"You're late," Henry said. A notation, not a complaint.

"I stopped at the harbor office." Lillian set bowls on the table and went to slice the bread. "At Fenn's request. The fisherman who heard the man on the breakwater — what he heard, apparently, sounded German."

Henry sat. He took up his spoon and did not immediately use it. "How'd the harbormaster take it?"

"With appropriate reserve. She said the Coast Guard has what she knows."

Henry nodded once.

"I told her I could be of use with identification documents. Varieties of German. I said I was at the school, if she needed it."

"What'd she say to that?"

"She said she'd note it."

Henry ate. After a moment: "She'll use you when she needs you. Not before."

Marcus considered this. He found it correct. Henry's readings of these kinds of transactions were, as a general rule, more reliable than his own, which tended toward the philological and away from the practical.

"Yes," he said. "That is what I understood."

They ate. Lillian moved in the adjacent room, the sound of her work steady and unremarkable. Outside the kitchen window, the wind off the harbor worked the bare branches of the elm in the yard. Marcus ate his supper and said nothing further.

After supper he washed his bowl at the sink and dried it and went upstairs. He sat at the desk with the annotating pen in his hand. On the desk: the Latin papers, the open dictionary, the grade book in its brown cover. He did not open the grade book. He sat for a while with the pen, and then he capped it and put it down.

text:
**Done.** One targeted cut applied; no other changes made.

---

**What was cut and why:**

In the walk-home meditation, the paragraph that opened *"The man on the breakwater had been thirty-eight to forty-eight years old. No identifying marks. No documents. Possibly speaking German, or something that a Maine fisherman found European and possibly German, which were not necessarily the same range."* — those three sentences — were removed.

The reader already holds every one of those facts: the body's age from ch01, the paperlessness from ch01, the "possibly German" detail from the Fenn scene earlier in this same chapter. The restatement interrupted the meditation at its most philosophically charged moment — mid-build from the English phrase → German phrase → *staatenlos* — forcing the reader to process information they had already absorbed before the meditation could arrive at its real question.

The second half of that paragraph (*"He had arrived from somewhere and come to rest at the foot of an iron ladder…the body's authorship had been removed"*) was preserved and is now the paragraph's opening. It earns its place: *"the body's authorship had been removed — by the water, or by whoever had placed him in it, or by himself, if the removal had been intentional"* introduces deliberate self-erasure as a possibility and bridges directly to *staatenlos*. That is new and necessary.

Everything else in the chapter is clean. The 1942-visit backstory earns its place as the record of Marcus's prior self-censorship. The room inventory holds thematic weight (Owen's ghost, the oilskin coat). The Henry exchange is efficient. The capped pen at the close is correctly quiet for T2 at ch06.
I'll read all four files simultaneously to get the full picture before reviewing.

Now I have everything I need. Let me analyze before acting.

**Chapter 7 assessment:**

- **Thread served:** T1 only — correct per threads.md (the Macomber departure entry as companion irregularity to the blank).
- **Arc position:** ch07 is in the setup third (ch01–08), which the macro assessment says is sound. No special macro-level flag on this chapter.
- **Hook:** Strong — coal stove, anchorage darkness, ice working against the float. Sets the winter register immediately.
- **Core sequence (three possibilities):** Well-paced. The third possibility (unverified placeholder) correctly receives the most space and the most uncertainty. No issue.
- **Birch Macomber pivot:** The move from "how did the entry get there" to "why did a cautious man go out in those conditions" is structurally clean and advances the thread.
- **Close:** Cold tea as correlative for unresolved thinking — earned. The coda is brief and doesn't overstay.

**Pacing issue found — section 2, office inventory:**

Between pulling the logbook and beginning to read it, the chapter inserts a catalog of the harbor office that includes three strictly procedural sentences that create drag without doing character or thread work:

1. "Standard Lighthouse Service district format, the gray cover printed with the year and the month in the upper right corner, which had not changed since 1931 when the First District standardized the harbor records." — Administrative trivia. The reader gains nothing from knowing the cover format or when the First District standardized it.
2. "Changed monthly, first of each month, the old one folded and filed in the cabinet's upper left drawer where twelve years of prior charts accumulated in order." — Filing-procedure inventory. The twelve-years marker is the only value; the rest is noise.
3. "The VHF radio on the shelf above the south window had been dark since the twenty-hundred-hours check." — Status report with no narrative function.

The Owen-presence material in the same section (the barometer four inches too high, the two cups, "She had stopped wondering when this would change") is doing real character work and stays untouched. The fix is surgical: remove the three procedural sentences and consolidate the pending-drawer reference. Net cut: ~85 words. The logbook examination begins slightly faster, which is what the chapter needs.

Applying the small fix now:

# The Log

The coal stove in the northeast corner had been going since Eira came in at six, and the office held a working heat — not the kind that invited lingering, but enough to keep the logbook pages from sticking together in the cold. Through the south window the anchorage showed as a darkness differentiated by the riding lights of the two lobster smacks still at mooring, and at the inner anchorage's north end the ice margin had reached six inches past the wood float, close enough that she could hear it working against the float's outer face: a slow, patient knock, the harbor testing its own edges. Now and then a mooring line took the tidal draw and complained against a piling — a low groan, more felt than heard, the sound of the harbor settling into its winter register.

She had come out on a Thursday evening, after supper, and told no one she was going. There was nothing remarkable about the harbormaster working evenings. The harbor was not a nine-to-five enterprise and never had been, and she had worked most of the town's Christmas programs in some capacity or another for eight years. But tonight there was no scheduled work and she had come anyway.

The Congregational church stood a quarter mile up Reach Road, and its steeple lantern was burning for the program. She had seen it on the way down the pier lane — a lit white finger against the black December sky. Owen had gone every year they were married. He had a good voice and he liked the hymns, and he would tell her about the program afterward with the slight exaggeration of a man who had genuinely enjoyed himself. She had not gone in eleven years and she did not think about this very often and she was not thinking about it now. The town was occupied. The harbor road was empty. The lamp above the pier shed door held the near ground and nothing else.

---

The October logbooks were in the rolling cabinet beneath the east window: vessel traffic in the left-hand volume, harbor incidents in the right. She pulled October's vessel-traffic book and set it on the desk.

The office had been a pilot house before the town purchased it in 1921 — the same year Owen took the post, before Eira had any relation to it except as a building she passed daily. The conversion had kept the pilot house footprint: twelve feet by fourteen, a space designed for two or three people to work in proximity, not for meetings or long storage. The desk occupied the center. To its left, the chart cabinet with its four drawers latched by the brass tongue-and-groove fittings Owen had fitted after the original latches stripped, and at the bottom left the bolted drawer with its own lock. The tide chart hung pinned above the cabinet with its four brass tacks — the current one December's data, in the Lighthouse Service's standard column format.

Owen's barometer hung on the east wall at the height where Owen had hung it: four inches above what was comfortable for Eira to read without tilting her head. She had tilted her head at it for eight years of monthly calibrations and had not once considered moving it. Below the barometer, the two hooks she had bent from a length of copper pipe salvaged from the *Lettie Morse* the January after he died: her coffee cup on the left hook, the chip at its rim facing the wall, and Owen's cup on the right, washed that morning at the office sink as every morning and set back. The cups were there when she came in and would be there when she locked up and would be there in the morning. She had stopped wondering when this would change.

The coal stove settled in the corner with a sound like a long exhale when a piece of coal shifted. She had not opened the pending drawer since September, when she had put Owen's tide tables in it to get them off the working surface.

She had been through the October vessel-traffic log three times before tonight. On the morning of the thirteenth, before Gervais arrived, to prepare the harbor record for his file. On the fourteenth, for her own incident report. In early November, when Pruitt had sat across the desk and asked her to walk him through the vessel traffic for the night of October ninth. Each time she had read the pages with the attention of someone confirming what she already knew. That was not what she was here for tonight.

She turned to the week of October seventh. Went in order.

Lobster smack *Thistle*: Roy Blaine, Coldwater Reach registration, at mooring from October fifth. No movement noted through the period of the blow. Mooring bridle inspected October seventh — her own notation, good condition.

Lobster smack *Doris V.*: Delmar Stannard, Coldwater Reach. At mooring from October third. Same period, no movement.

Lighthouse Service ice barge *Ploughman*: arrived October eighth from Jonesport, anchor in the inner anchorage, single crewman aboard, standard tender call. Departed October fourteenth, after the bar had smoothed. She had logged that departure herself from the pier.

Coaster *Macomber*: Birch Macomber, master; Machias registry; arrived October eighth out of Calais with lime and general goods; berth at the outside face of the main pier. Harbor fee paid October eighth, Macomber's signature on the receipt. She had the receipt in the bolted drawer.

And the departure entry.

She read it again, though she had not forgotten it: *Macomber — departure 0430 — Oct. 11.* Her handwriting. The same hand that had made every other entry in the logbook, the same pen, the same deliberate spacing she had used for harbor records since she learned the format from the Lighthouse Service field manual in 1935.

She did not remember writing it.

---

October eleventh had been the storm's second full day. She had the harbor incident log for that morning: *Weather. NE 40. Arrive 0700. Harbor closed outbound. Inner anchorage holding.* She had made that notation on arrival, before she had done anything else — same as Owen had done, same as she had done every storm watch since she took the post. NE forty knots from the northeast. She had closed the harbor to outbound traffic at 0200 on the tenth, when the wind came on full and the bar began breaking. A coaster drawing eight feet under load could not make the entrance channel in those conditions without real risk. The regulation existed for reasons.

If the *Macomber* had put out at 0430 on the eleventh, it had gone two and a half hours before she arrived that morning. She did not remember stopping any departure or clearing one through on that date. Stopping a vessel, or passing one out in storm conditions, was logged as a separate event — not folded into the general storm notation. There was no such event log for October eleventh. Either she had been there at 0430 and cleared the *Macomber* out, and the memory had not survived the exhaustion and what came after. Or the *Macomber* had gone without her knowledge, and the departure entry had come from somewhere else.

She had worked through these possibilities in November. She did them again now, because that was how she worked.

The first: she had been there at 0430. She had come out in the dark on the storm's second night, checked the harbor in the way she did when she couldn't sleep and the wind was too loud to ignore, and had found the *Macomber* making ready to depart, and had cleared it through, and had logged the departure before going home to sleep. This was possible. She had not slept more than three hours combined on the nights of the tenth and the eleventh — the tenth because she had been out checking moorings, the eleventh because the wind had not settled enough to allow more. Exhaustion did things to memory. She knew this from the long storm watches before the war took the harbor's staff: the actions taken in the small hours of a blow sometimes went sideways in the recollection afterward, compressed or simply gone, as if the mind had chosen what to keep and discarded the rest on the basis of some accounting she was not party to. She had been out on the tenth at 0015 to check the *Macomber*'s bridle — she knew this — and again at 0130 when the blow came on. What happened between 0130 and 0700 she could account for only in broad strokes.

It was possible she had done this and did not remember it.

The second possibility was a note. The harbor had no formal overnight watchman since Harlow left in forty-one, and the informal arrangement was the hook beside the pier shed door, where fishermen left slips when they came in or went out at odd hours. Blaine did this regularly. Stannard less often. She had transferred departures and arrivals from these notes to the official record before, more times than she could count; the harbor's reduced wartime traffic made the informal system workable. The slips she did not keep once they were logged. If Blaine had come out before first light and seen the *Macomber* standing out through the entrance channel, and had left a note with the time he reckoned it had gone, and she had transferred the time to the log the next day without thinking — without thinking, that was the question — it was possible. She had done this before, with arrivals in the small hours.

The third possibility gave her the most trouble. Sometime on the fourteenth, going back through the week's logs in preparation for the incident report, she might have noticed that the *Macomber* departure was unconfirmed. She might have written *0430* as a placeholder — a time she estimated from the pattern of Macomber's habits, intending to verify it when the storm business was finished, intending to ask Blaine or Stannard or Macomber himself when the next supply run brought him back to Coldwater. She had not made that inquiry. The body came in on the morning of the thirteenth and everything that followed it was large enough to displace the placeholder from her attention, and the unverified time had stayed in the record as if she had confirmed it.

She did not believe she made unverified entries in the official log. It was her rule and Owen's before her and the Lighthouse Service district's before Owen. She did not put in what she had not witnessed or could not source to a witness. But she had been holding this belief about herself, and tonight she was less certain it excluded the possibility that she had once, in the middle of a storm and a body, done what she said she did not do.

She sat with all three and did not decide between them.

The coal stove settled. The ice at the north anchorage knocked against the float. The mooring lines held their cold tension against the pilings and said nothing useful.

What she could not set aside was Birch Macomber himself. She had spoken to him twice in four years — once about the set of his mooring, once about the harbor fee schedule — and in both conversations she had come away with the same impression: a spare man, careful with money, careful with his hull, not given to improvisation. Running lime and general goods along the downeast coast was not a trade that rewarded careless weather decisions; a coaster that put out in conditions the prudent men waited on came home shortened, or didn't come home. A man who had worked this coast for twenty years knew where the bar broke and how the entrance channel set in a nor'easter. He knew all of it and he had gone out anyway, at 0430, with the wind still running hard off the northeast.

A cautious man did not do that without a reason she did not know about.

She pulled the second left-hand drawer and took out the plain brown notebook — the personal log, not the official record, not submitted and not audited. She had kept it since the second year of the post, when she understood that there were observations the official record was not the correct place for: questions not yet resolved into reportable facts, leads she was not ready to formalize. She uncapped the pen and wrote:

*Macomber — departure Oct. 11, 0430. Machias reg. Reason for departure in storm conditions: unknown. Harbor closed outbound since 0200 Oct. 10. Log entry in my hand, source uncertain. Vessel clear of harbor before 0700. Did not observe departure or clear it through by record. Inquiry?*

She capped the pen. She read the entry once. Then she closed the notebook and put it back in the drawer.

---

The pier lane was cold outside the lamp's reach. She pulled the office door shut behind her and tried the latch once, which was habit even when she knew it had caught, and went up toward Reach Road.

The main street was quiet. The Congregational church at the far end showed its lit windows, and she could hear, at this distance, something that might have been singing — bass notes carrying further than the rest in the cold air. She went the other way.

Her house sat a quarter mile south on Reach Road, second back from the road, with the white porch railing Owen had painted in the last spring of his life. She repainted it every spring whether it needed it or not. Inside: the kitchen table set for one, the back bedroom's door closed, the hooks in the entry hall holding what she put there.

She took off her boots at the door. Owen's oilskin coat — the spare, the one she'd brought home rather than leaving at the office — she hung on the right hook, which was where it had hung for eight years, and she noticed this and went to the kitchen.

The water took its time coming to heat. She stood at the counter and listened to the harbor wind work in the eaves, the particular sound it made around the southeast corner of the house where Owen had never gotten around to repointing the mortar. Then she made the tea in the good china cup — the one that had been her mother's, not the enamel mug — and sat down at the kitchen table with both hands around it.

She did not drink it. She sat with her hands on the cup and felt the heat going out by degrees, and thought about Birch Macomber at the wheel of a loaded coaster in forty-knot weather, pushing out through the entrance channel in the dark before dawn on the second day of a blow, and she thought about what could make a cautious man do that, and she did not arrive at anything she did not already know.

When the tea was cold she took the cup to the sink and poured it out and went to bed.

## 08-vater.md

**Overall verdict:** Structurally sound. No restructuring needed. One pacing issue documented; fix is upstream in ch05, not here.

---

### Central beat deflated by retrospective disclosure (fourth section)

The chapter's earned beat is the dinner table exchange: Hannah asks the question she has been rehearsing through the whole feeding, Albrecht responds with *Regensburg ist nicht weit*, picks up his spoon, and the moment passes. The chapter has been building to that exchange as a **first naming** — both for Hannah and for the reader. The story-arc entry confirms this framing: "Albrecht names Karl's proximity for the first time in direct response to Hannah's question."

In the fourth section (Hannah upstairs, sitting on the bed in the dark), the following paragraph retroactively collapses that beat:

> *She had understood this since the evening in the second week when she had been carrying dishes from the table to the counter and heard him say it — Regensburg ist nicht weit — to no one in particular, or to her mother's back, or to the air above the table. She had heard it and understood what it meant and had not known what to do with it, and had been carrying it since then the way you carry something in a pocket that you keep reaching for without taking out.*

This reveals that Hannah has already heard *Regensburg ist nicht weit* — weeks earlier, at close range, at the same table. The dinner scene the reader just witnessed was not a first naming but a second. "Tonight was different" (the paragraph immediately following) is structurally dependent on this disclosure and cannot stand without it, but with it, the chapter's central beat arrives as a confirmation of something Hannah (and, prospectively, the reader via ch05) already held. The tension of the dinner scene — her rehearsing the German grammar, setting down her spoon, the formal deliberateness of the question — is built as if this answer will be new. The upstairs paragraph undoes that build retroactively.

**Connection to ch05 continuity finding:** This is the downstream pacing consequence of the issue already documented in the ch05 note (editor-pacing.md, *"One pacing issue — ch08 beat pre-empted"*). The source error is in ch05 (*auch* meditation, "Her father had said it in the second week of the POWs' arrival..."). The ch05 note correctly identifies the fix: remove the prospective reference there so that *Regensburg ist nicht weit* arrives in ch08 as new information to both Hannah and the reader.

**What changes in ch08 once ch05 is corrected:** If ch05's "second week" reference is cut or revised, the upstairs paragraph in ch08 must also be revised — it becomes internally inconsistent (there was no prior "evening in the second week" for Hannah to have overheard). The likely revision is to cut this paragraph entirely and reattach the logic of "Tonight was different" directly to the dinner table exchange just completed: the difference is not between hearing it aimed at the air versus aimed at her, but between knowing abstractly that her father knows and being told by him directly in response to her question. That reframe can be accomplished in one sentence without the backstory paragraph. The following paragraph ("She lay back on the bed...") is then unaffected.

**Specific location in ch08:** Fourth section (beginning *"She sat on the bed in the dark without lighting the lamp"*), third paragraph, beginning *"She had understood this since the evening in the second week..."* through *"...the way you carry something in a pocket that you keep reaching for without taking out."* This paragraph is the one to cut or revise after the ch05 correction.

---

### *Heimweh* paragraph: register note for voice pass

The paragraph beginning *"She thought about the word Heimweh"* (~130 words) analyzes the German word with an etymology gloss (Weh vs. sickness, "home-ache"), which is longer and more interpretive than the chapter's otherwise compressed method would suggest. It is not a pacing drag in itself — the upstairs section is appropriately interior — but the sustained word-meditation mode (defining, distinguishing, unpacking) is more characteristic of Marcus's chapter register than Hannah's usual concrete-behavioral mode. Flag for the voice/register pass.

input.content:
# February Ice

The north end of the inner anchorage had closed by the first week of January. By February it was hard out forty yards from the granite shore — ice opaque from the top and solid all the way down, nothing moving beneath it. At the seaward edge it thinned to a ragged fringe where the channel current kept the water turning, and this fringe advanced and retreated a few yards each day depending on what the overnight brought. The overnight had brought twelve degrees and no wind shift. The fringe had advanced.

Eira saw it before she reached the pier shed, in the thin light of a February morning that hadn't fully committed to itself yet: a coaster sitting wrong in the mooring field, its bow canted a few degrees to port, its riding light still burning. Not in distress. No whistle, no flare, no light signals. But sitting in a way no vessel sat by choice.

She unlocked the pier shed door, went through, and came out on the dock.

The coaster was about sixty feet, wooden, working trim, a Rockland registration from the wheelhouse color scheme — light blue, a color Eira didn't see often this far downeast. It had come in on the late tide, she thought, or past midnight, when the ice fringe would have been set back a few yards further and the mooring field would have looked clear. Then the overnight had done the rest. The tide had dropped away and left the keel on the ledge that formed at the mooring field's north edge, where the shallow water froze solid to the bottom and built upward. The vessel was sitting on this ledge, canted to port, the keel found.

Someone was standing in the bow. Not probing with the boat hook they held, just standing there in the way of a person who has already assessed the situation and is waiting for circumstances to change.

---

Eira went down the dock ladder to the float. The coaster's name was painted in dark blue on the bow: *Wren.*

She called up: who's master.

A woman in a wool watch cap came to the rail. She said: I am. Hartley. Estelle Hartley. Her expression was the expression Eira recognized — not from a mirror exactly, but from having lived inside a version of it for eight years: the look of a competent person confronting a situation that is inconvenient and non-catastrophic and requires patience rather than action. There were three or four women skippers working the Maine coast, the war having opened certain posts that would not otherwise have opened, and Hartley's manner made clear she had been sailing this water well before any of those openings. Eira noted it the way she noted a vessel's waterline — useful information for reading what she was dealing with — and did not remark on it.

She said: when did you come in.

Hartley said: half past eleven last night. Ice was back another five yards. She paused. It built overnight.

Eira said: it does that here in February. What's your draft.

Eight foot six.

She looked at the ledge. The tide mark on the nearest piling told her she had four hours to the next high, and even at high water she doubted the *Wren* would float free. The ledge ran the full width of the mooring field's north edge and had been building since early January; it was not going to release a keel on its own.

She said: you can try rocking her off with the engine. Your odds improve when the tide comes up, in about four hours.

Hartley said: and if the tide doesn't do it.

Eira said: I'll call the buoy tender. It can take you under tow from seaward and haul you over the ledge's edge. The ledge depth at the inner face runs about nine feet at this tide stage — your keel clears by half a foot.

She said it plainly, as a plan with a clear sequence. Hartley received it as the same. She said: what's the tender's ETA.

Eira said: the Samoset's on its coastal run out of Jonesport. I'd have it here by half past nine.

Hartley looked at the ice-ledge and then at the harbor's mouth, where the outer reach was open water. She looked at the sky, which was February-white and had nothing helpful to say about the next four hours. She said: call it. I have a consignment in Lubec tonight.

---

Eira went back to the harbor office and used the wireless. The Samoset's station answered in seven minutes and said it could make Coldwater Reach by half past nine, which was forty minutes from now. She called for a tow — non-distress, routine assistance, ice grounding — and gave the *Wren*'s particulars and the state of the mooring field. The station acknowledged. She signed off and wrote the time in the harbor incident log.

Then she made coffee. The percolator was on the coal stove's back plate. She emptied the morning's grounds and measured four into the basket, filled the pot at the sink, set it back on the plate. While it came up she opened the incident log and wrote in the *Wren* — arrival estimated 2330 the prior evening, position in the mooring field, keel grounded on ice-ledge at the field's north edge, no structural damage reported. She pulled the tide chart off its nail and confirmed the high at 0953. The Samoset would arrive before it, which was the right order.

She poured the finished coffee into her thermos and went back to the dock.

---

Hartley had sent her crew below. They were young men, both of them, and there was no work to be done on the deck while the vessel sat still. Hartley herself was at the rail when Eira came up from the float, and when she saw the thermos she climbed down to the dock.

They stood side by side at the float's edge. Eira poured into her enamel mug and gave Hartley the thermos lid, which held enough.

The cold was particular. Eira had stood on enough winter docks to know the difference between the cold that came off open water — weight and moisture to it, a cold that pressed — and the cold that came off ice. The ice-cold was drier. Stiffer. It radiated from the flat surface rather than rolling in off moving water, and the effect was not like being in cold air so much as being in cold that had already settled and had nowhere further to go. It met you at the wrists and cheekbones and stayed.

Hartley wrapped both hands around the thermos lid. She said: this is good.

Eira said: four measures.

They were quiet for a while. The harbor outside the mooring field was open water, gray-green and working, the chop from an easterly still running cross to the channel current and making the anchorage's surface irregular in the way that looked colder than it was. Above the harbor mouth the sky was February-white and flat.

Hartley knew the coast the way you knew it after the years on it — not the broad strokes but the specifics, the behavior of particular passages in particular conditions, which anchorages froze first and which didn't, where the ledges were that didn't appear on the charts until you were on top of them. She said she had been sailing it since 1927. She said this once, in passing, as a date that explained what she knew rather than as something requiring acknowledgment. Eira received it as the former.

They went through the tow plan. The Samoset would bring a line to the *Wren*'s bow cleat, take a strain, haul from seaward; Hartley would put the engine ahead slow on the pull to help. Fenders on the port side, though the ledge was smooth and the risk of hull damage was low if they came off cleanly. Four minutes to agree on this, another four to stop talking about it.

Then they were back to standing.

The cold off the ice had a smell to it as well — something dry and faintly mineral, different from the salt and diesel of the open harbor. Eira had registered it in past winters without naming it; standing here now she named it: chalk. The ice-cold smelled like chalk. She filed this without knowing what she would do with it.

After a while she said: you work far down this way often?

Hartley said: Rockland to Eastport, the full circuit. Lath and shingles and lime and general cargo, depending on what was moving. She named the stops: Machias, Lubec, Jonesport sometimes for the Lighthouse Service freight, occasionally further east. She had come into Coldwater Reach twice before, she said, but not often; the inner anchorage was tight.

Eira poured more coffee into her mug. She said: you see Birch Macomber down your way? He's Machias-registered, works the downeast trade.

Hartley considered. She said: Portland, mostly, these days. She paused. He's been running something regular down there since the fall.

Eira said: October-ish?

Hartley said: could be. Maybe September. He turned down a run from me in September — I had pine lath for Bath, decent money, the kind of thing Birch takes in any ordinary year without blinking. He said he had a contract. That it paid better than I was offering.

Eira said: what kind of contract.

Hartley gave the brief, total-shoulder shrug that meant the question was reasonable and the answer was absent. She said: he didn't say, exactly. He said he had papers — said it the way you say something when it explains the situation and you don't intend to say more. Something out of Portland, Navy district maybe. She paused. You know Birch. He doesn't explain what he doesn't see the need to explain.

Eira said: hm.

Hartley said: is there something with Birch?

Eira said: he was through here last fall. I've been reconciling his circuit for the annual fee schedule — trying to account for the stops.

Hartley accepted this. It was plausible; it was the kind of thing a harbormaster did. She poured what remained in the thermos lid and looked at the ice-ledge, which was doing what ice-ledges did in February, which was nothing.

Twenty-two minutes past nine, the Samoset came around the harbor's outer point. Its gray hull in the February light read as a continuation of the weather rather than a vessel moving through it — defined at its edges but reluctant, belonging to the same palette as the sky and the water and the ice.

---

The tow took forty minutes, unhurried. The Samoset's mate brought a line across to the *Wren*'s bow cleat; the tender came to a slow strain; Hartley put the engine ahead slow on the pull. The *Wren* moved off the ice-ledge with one low, dense sound — a brief groan of wood finding its way back to buoyancy — and then the keel was free and the vessel was in open water, moving seaward behind the tow line at walking pace.

Eira watched the waterline as it cleared the ledge. It was clean. The *Wren*'s trim was unchanged from its moored position; nothing had shifted.

Hartley slipped the tow line in the fairway and held station while her crew brought the fenders back inboard. She leaned over the rail and ran her eye along the hull's waterline on both sides, looking for any change in the vessel's sit that might speak to the keel below.

She said: looks right.

Eira said: have her hauled when you're next in port.

Hartley said: I'll do that. She looked down at Eira on the dock with the direct look of someone who has transacted business satisfactorily. She said: I appreciate the tow call.

Eira said: safe weather.

The *Wren*'s engine came up to running speed and it moved south through the harbor's mouth. The Samoset turned east, toward Jonesport and its regular circuit. The inner anchorage, without the *Wren* in it, was the mooring field and the ice and nothing else — the *Thistle* and the *Doris V.* at their moorings well clear of the ledge, both boats already back from the morning's haul, gear stowed.

Eira walked back to the harbor office.

---

She spent the rest of the working day on the quarterly maintenance report and a response to the district about the spring ice barge schedule. The percolator ran dry by midday. She refilled it, drank another cup standing at the south window while the harbor settled into its afternoon quiet, and went through the incident log entry once more to be sure it was complete. At four o'clock the light had gone flat and she banked the coal stove and locked the pier shed and went home.

She ate at the kitchen table. Owen's spare oilskin coat on the right hook in the entry hall, where it had been since 1935. She drank tea from her mother's cup and looked at the table surface, which had a water ring in the wood near the left edge that she had been meaning to deal with for several winters.

At half past five she went back to the harbor office.

She put a few coals back in the stove and stood at the south window. The riding lights of the *Thistle* and the *Doris V.* were on now, small and steady in the dark, their reflections working on the anchorage's chop. The ice was invisible in the dark but it was there, forty yards of it.

Birch Macomber. Papers. Navy district. Portland, since at least September — since before October, which meant since before he came through Coldwater Reach with lime out of Calais, waiting on weather at the outside face of the main pier, from the eighth through the eleventh. The departure she'd logged at 0430 on the storm's second day, in her own hand, source uncertain, harbor closed outbound since 0200 October 10.

Papers meant a record. The district office in Portland held vessel contracts by master and registration — she had processed the inquiry paperwork on two vessels out of Machias in the previous winter when the district had needed documentation for their records. If she wrote to the district asking about a vessel's fee status, routine administrative correspondence, they would reference whatever contract documentation existed.

She went to her desk. Second left-hand drawer. She opened the plain brown notebook past the December entry and found the next blank line.

She wrote: *Macomber — Portland, Navy district contract — fall 1943. Per Hartley (Wren / Rockland). District office Portland holds vessel contracts by master and registration — file would exist. Inquiry: what was the contract? What dates?*

She looked at the entry. She wrote below it: *Papers = record somewhere.*

She put the notebook back and closed the drawer.

The riding lights moved on the anchorage's surface. The ice out at the north end of the mooring field was the same ice it had been this morning — unchanged, unhelpful, doing what February ice did. She looked at it for a moment. Then she put on Owen's coat and walked home through the cold, which was the open-water kind again now that she was away from the dock, and had weight in it.

Distinct from ordinary cargo work. District office Portland holds vessel contracts by master and registration — file would exist. Inquiry: what was the contract? What dates?*

**After:**
> *Macomber — Portland, Navy district contract — fall 1943. Per Hartley (Wren / Rockland). District office Portland holds vessel contracts by master and registration — file would exist. Inquiry: what was the contract? What dates?*

---

**Rationale:**

Distinct from ordinary cargo work.") was a verbatim reconstruction of the dock conversation the reader completed two pages earlier. It added nothing beyond what the reader already held. The surrounding synthesis paragraph — "Birch Macomber. Papers. Navy district. Portland, since at least September — since before October..." — already does the deductive linking work (connecting the Macomber lead to the unexplained 0430 log entry from ch07), and does it without repetition. The notebook entry now retains only what's new: the source attribution (consistent with Eira's methodical record-keeping), and the forward-looking deduction that a district contract produces a queryable file. The chapter's closing line ("*Papers = record somewhere*") is unchanged.

**Everything else is clean:** the ice-grounding hook, the Hartley encounter and tow logistics, the dock-standing conversation, the home scene (earns its ~70 words by planting Owen's coat for the final line), the synthesis window-stare, and the "chalk" / open-water-cold sensory arc that opens and closes the chapter. No structural issues to document.

The macro assessment doesn't flag ch11 specifically, and the chapter's arc is sound: the word-card system is established, Karl's *Ich habe das Meer nie gesehen* is the right beat, Hannah's withheld sentence (*Ich könnte es dir zeigen*) is the chapter's emotional hinge, and the dinner scene with Albrecht's "Erst in Hamburg" is a strong T5 close.

input.content:
# The Word for Harbor

She was at the chopping block with the butter-box planks when she thought about making the new card.

The cedar dried fast behind the kitchen stove — three or four days, and what had been green-edged and faintly resinous came out pale and dry, holding a pencil mark the way good paper held it. Her father split the boards into kindling-lengths with his hatchet on the cold side of the woodpile, three strokes per board, Saturday mornings when the week's work let up enough for that kind of patience. She had been taking lengths before they reached the woodbox since January — three weeks now — slipping two or three boards out of the stack when her father was in the barn, then cutting them down on the cold side of the building with her pocket knife: six inches by two, roughly the size of a playing card, each face sanded clean with the fine side of the whetstone.

She had ten cards done. *Barn / Scheune. Gate / Tor. Bucket / Eimer. Rope / Seil.* And the more recent ones: *fence / Zaun. stone / Stein. field / Feld. morning / Morgen.* And two newer cards she'd put out in the second week of February and hadn't gotten back yet. The completed cards — given and returned — lived in the deep pocket of her apron alongside the pencil and whatever else a day accumulated. She turned them over without thinking sometimes, the way you turned a coin: English side, German side, English side. The English sides wore fastest. She didn't know why. Something about the letters, or the way they were handled.

The system: a card flat on the fence post nearest the icehouse, held down with the gray stone she'd moved from the field edge in October. By the following afternoon: gone. Two days later, sometimes one: back under the stone. He had been memorizing. She knew this from the wearing of the cards, and from the way he sometimes said an English word when he came to the fence — not working at it but using it, placing it where it belonged in a sentence.

Today she had the harbor card to bring out.

---

She'd made it the previous afternoon at the kitchen table, her mother out with the chickens, the house quiet around her. *Harbor / Hafen.* She drew the dividing line down the center with the ruler edge, pressed the letters on both faces, and held the card for a moment before tucking it into her apron.

The word had been in the edge of her thinking since October — not that she had details. The talk had reached her the way talk reached everyone in a town this size: in pieces, through the gaps. At school in the corridor, at the general store, after Sunday meeting in the road. A man. Unidentified. The breakwater. She didn't know more than that. The pieces hadn't arrived in any better form. But the word *harbor* had acquired something from those months of partial information — a quality of holding-in, a particular weight on both sides of the word. The harbor as the place that took you in from the sea. She turned the card over and thought about that, and then put it in her pocket.

She laid it under the gray stone before her morning work, face-down, and went about the day.

---

The three older POWs left for the north pasture with Lonzo at eight, which was when they always went. Karl stayed in the south yard. His standing assignment was the working-yard maintenance — manure hauling, the secondary clean, the daily accumulation that kept the south yard manageable. She could hear him before she saw him on most mornings: the barrow wheel on frozen ground, its particular stop-and-go rhythm telling her where he was in the task.

By two o'clock Lonzo was back from the pasture, settled in his chair beside the icehouse door with his coat collar turned up. He had his rifle across his knees and a small flask tucked in his coat's left breast pocket — she had begun noticing the flask in January, the way it had become part of the daily arrangement of him, as habitual as the chair. He was looking at the middle distance in the way that meant present, not watching. The expression of a man whose job was to be there rather than to attend.

Hannah came to the south gate with a bundle of baling wire she'd been meaning to move for a week. She set it against the fence post, let her eyes drop to the post's top.

The harbor card was gone.

She straightened without looking at the icehouse.

Karl came across the south yard with the slop bucket, moving at his ordinary pace. His coat was buttoned to the throat — the Army-issue denim her mother had let out at the seams without being asked, all buttons present, which she noticed the way she always noticed without meaning to. He set the bucket on the fence rail.

Then he reached into his coat pocket and took out the card.

He'd folded it once across its length to keep it flat in the pocket. He unfolded it carefully, with both hands, and held it with the English side facing him. She could see the letters from where she stood: the H of *harbor* worn nearly through, the rest still legible but going. He'd been working at it hard.

He said, in English: "Har-bor."

The word measuring itself in his mouth, testing whether it did what a word should do. Not a question, not a statement — something between the two, the sound of a word being claimed.

She said: yes.

He looked at the card, then at her. He said, in German: *Was ist das, ein Hafen, hier?* What is that, a harbor, here?

His German had the Bavarian roundness she recognized from her father, but set differently in the mouth — the vowels opened further forward, a younger voice in the same regional key. He asked questions as genuine questions. She had noticed this early and kept noticing: he was not asking in order to seem engaged. He wanted to know what a harbor was, here, in relation to where he was standing.

She said, in German: *Ja. Drei Meilen.* Three miles. And pointed south — past the barn, past the far field boundary, roughly toward the ridge line you couldn't see from the south yard.

He turned to look in the direction she was pointing. The bare birches at the field's edge, and beyond them the February sky, gray and flat and closed. He studied it the way you studied a map when the detail you needed wasn't printed — calculating from what was visible toward what wasn't.

He said, still facing south: *Ich habe das Meer nie gesehen.*

I have never seen the sea.

He said it with the particular tone he kept for things he meant completely — not louder or softer than his ordinary voice, but without hedge, the way you spoke when the saying mattered as much as the content. She had heard that tone twice before: in October when he'd said *da, bitte* setting her grain bucket on the rail, as if the act required its own precision; and in December when he'd noted, almost under his breath, that the east barn wall was separating from the foundation beam. Things he meant to be acknowledged, not just heard.

She looked at him.

Regensburg was ninety kilometers up the Danube from Munich, inland and river-anchored. She'd found it on the school atlas in October, placed her thumb beside Straubing to measure the distance — same river, same Bavarian valley. You could live your whole life in that valley with the Danube as your water and the sea as a mark on a map. And Tunisia: she'd traced the North African coast when the news came through about the surrender. A coast, yes. But Karl had come to that coast as a prisoner, at the end of something — she thought that was different from standing at the shore and looking at it, the way you looked at something that had been waiting for you and was now simply there.

She picked up the baling wire.

*Ich könnte es dir zeigen* — I could show you — is what she didn't say. The sentence arrived in both languages at once, the way certain thoughts did when she'd been thinking in German all morning, and she held it there while she gathered the wire, and did not say it. Because she didn't know yet whether she meant it. And she had learned — not from anywhere in particular, from the general running of a house where words were managed — that you didn't say a sentence until you knew what shape it would make afterward.

She took the bucket from the rail as his hands released it, the handoff wordless the way most things were, and went toward the barn.

Lonzo had not moved from his chair. He was watching the ice that had built along the near edge of the water trough at the icehouse corner, or watching nothing in particular; it was hard to say from across the yard.

---

She worked until four. Baling wire sorted and coiled on the barn's north hook. Second-stall trough drained and refilled. Hilde and Else brought in from the half-gate and settled in the milking alley. The tasks had their order and she followed it, and the thought moved with her through the order the way a thought did when it had gotten into the day's grain.

She had been nine years old when she walked to the harbor by herself. A clear October morning, the road dry, her shoes dry when she started. She had not asked permission because it had not occurred to her that permission was the relevant category — you walked south for three miles and the harbor was there, the same harbor it always was, and the sea beyond the harbor mouth was large and gray-green and smelled of brine and old rope and diesel from the lobster boats, and larger than she'd known any surface could be when you were standing at its edge. The tide had come in while she stood on the breakwater and soaked her shoes through to the wool socks. She had walked home in wet socks and told no one. There was nothing particular to say. She had gone and the sea had been there and she had come back.

She had never thought of it as something you could be denied. She had never thought of it the way she thought of meat ration or coffee — as something allocated, doled out, possible to be refused. The harbor was three miles south and it was simply there, whether or not you went to it. You could walk to it on any clear day without asking anyone's permission. That was the entire situation.

Rolf had grown up here, three miles from the coast his whole life. She couldn't say whether he'd thought much about it growing up. Boys went outward toward the fields; she had gone toward the water; they had never discussed it because there had never been a reason to discuss it. And then the war had taken him to sea, and the sea was now something he crossed.

Karl Reinmar had lived nineteen years and never stood at the shore and looked at it.

She brought Hilde and Else in and settled them and cleaned the alley and went in for dinner.

---

Potato soup and bread. Her mother had the oil lamp on the table again — her eyes, in the February evenings. The lamp made a smaller circle than the ceiling fixture, and the three of them sat inside it with the kitchen in shadow around them. Outside the window the dark was already full, February dark that settled early and stayed.

Albrecht ate without speaking. Magda's spoon moved in its quiet way.

Hannah ate her soup with one hand and turned the harbor card over in her apron pocket with the other. English side, German side. The English side going smooth under her thumb, the letters almost gone by feel.

Then she said, in German: *Hast du das Meer gesehen, bevor du nach Amerika kamst?*

Had you seen the sea before you came to America?

Her father looked up from his bowl.

The question sat between them. She heard it arrive with more weight than she'd intended — not just a question about geography but about the years before she was born, about the man who had stood at a ship's rail in Hamburg in 1922 and watched the city go small behind him. She had not intended all of that. But intentions were not the unit the world measured in, she was finding.

Her father set his spoon down. He looked at her with the weighing expression — not warm, not cold, something in between deciding and knowing. At the far end of the table, Magda had gone still.

He said: *Erst in Hamburg.*

Not until Hamburg. When he'd boarded the ship, going out. The sea had been the threshold, and he had crossed it once, going, and here he was on the other side of it.

Hannah nodded.

She did not ask what it was like to stand at the rail and look at it for the first time, the water opening before you in every direction. She did not ask whether he had understood, in that moment, what the crossing meant.

Her father picked up his spoon. He finished his soup, folded his napkin with his two folds and the tucked corner, and went to the radio chair. He did not turn the radio on.

Magda rose and took the bowls to the sink. Hannah followed.

Through the window above the sink: the February dark, complete and flat. Three miles of it between here and the harbor, and the harbor there behind the dark, and the sea beyond the harbor mouth, and all of it there whether anyone looked at it or not.

Her mother handed her a bowl to dry.

She dried it and put it away.

The card was still in her apron pocket. She could feel it when she pressed her hand flat against the apron cloth: English side worn almost smooth, *Hafen* on the other side still legible, the letters still holding their shape.

Her mother handed her the last bowl.

She dried it and put it on the shelf.

The harbor-word meditation in section two is brief enough not to drag. The close at the sink is exactly right. No larger issues to document.

Schell working in him, the decision sequence, and the elm-bud close — all of these are good. The prose does its job.
