**Analysis complete.** One voice-drift found. Everything else is clean and on-register.

**The problem:** In the addendum-reading scene, Eira reflects on her own sentence:

> *"It said so without saying so, in the same way that any sentence careful enough to state only what was provable left visible, in the space around it, what was not stated."*

The elaborating clause — *"in the same way that any sentence careful enough to state only what was provable left visible, in the space around it, what was not stated"* — is Marcus's register, not Eira's. Marcus is the character who notices the gap between language and meaning, who analyzes sentences for their construction and range. Eira is a proceduralist: she would notice that the record has a gap, not theorize about how careful sentences encode their own omissions. The opening clause "It said so without saying so" is fine — spare and observational. The elaboration after it is the drift.

**Fix:** Cut the elaborating clause. The implication stands; the literary-theoretical expansion goes.

All other voice elements are on-register:
- Dialogue is exemplary — "Yes." / "I'm listening." / "Understood." Eira at her clippedest.
- Emotion is entirely carried by action: filing the notebook, not touching Owen's folders, going out to the breakwater.
- No similes. No editorializing. No extended interior monologue elsewhere.
- The ending lands perfectly on action (coat on, walking down the dock) with no summarizing reflection.
- "spring line" for the telephone connection is a deliberate register choice — Eira's maritime vocabulary bleeding into perception — and is consistent with the style guide's directive that the narrator's language reflects the POV character's habitual attention.

input.content:
# April

She was copying arrival figures to the weekly report — seven vessels in the first two weeks of April, noted by type and date in the arrival log, to be totaled in the report — when she heard the first lobster boat of the morning come in from the near grounds and throttle back for the harbor mouth. She glanced at the clock above the duty log. Nearly half past eight. The boats had been going out before six this past week, which was the first full week of real spring work since the ice broke out of the inner anchorage in late March.

Through the east window the anchorage was the particular color it was in April — not the winter green-black and not the summer blue, but something between, a clarity the cold did its work to produce and left behind when it went. She had noted the breakout date in the harbor log on March 24, a Saturday, the ice going out in a northwest blow that sent the pans through the harbor mouth overnight. Three weeks now. Two lobster boats had been working the near grounds since the first of April; a third was laid up at its winter mooring with a stern cleat that needed replacing, which she had logged and which Mayhew had not yet addressed. The trap-haulers at the south mooring were active again, the gear stacked on their afterdecks in the mornings when they went out.

The buoy tender was due Thursday. She had its arrival in the incoming schedule in red pencil — first full spring run, coming down from Rockland and working the buoys east. She had the Lighthouse Service notice on the desk behind the report, listing the corrected positions for three buoys in the approach channel that had dragged their moorings through the winter.

She was finishing the arrival total when the telephone rang.

She set down her pen.

"Harbor office," she said.

"Miss Bowman." A man's voice, careful with its consonants, clear on the spring line. "Commander Whitmore. First District, Portland. I hope this is a convenient time."

"Yes," she said.

"I'm calling with the finalization on the October 1943 case. The unidentified decedent, Coldwater Reach."

She turned to the clear edge of the blotter and took up her pencil.

"I'm listening," she said.

"The cross-reference is complete. We've matched the October 1943 decedent against a missing crew report from the SS *Ångermanälven* — Swedish-flagged freighter, operating in North Atlantic coastal waters in the fall of that year. The manifest lists one August Schell, German national, Swedish citizen, as missing on or about the seventh of October 1943 in North Atlantic coastal waters." He paused, a pause of review rather than hesitation. "The district has examined the physical record, the timeline, and the geographic data. They are consistent with the October 1943 decedent from your breakwater."

She wrote the name. *August Schell.*

"The district's determination: August Schell, lost at sea, North Atlantic coastal waters, October 1943. Accidental drowning. Case closed." Another pause, this one transitional — the move from finding to administration. "You'll have the written notice and the formal determination for your permanent file within the week."

"Thank you, Commander," she said.

"The notice will include the First District case number and a copy of the cross-reference summary for your records. If you have questions when it arrives, the district office is the right contact."

"Understood."

The line had the quality of a clear spring day — steadier than the winter connections, the static quieter. She could hear, faintly through the receiver, the sound of papers on a desk.

"Thank you for your patience with the inquiry, Miss Bowman," he said. "These cross-referencing procedures take longer than anyone would prefer."

"Yes," she said. "Thank you, Commander."

He said good day. She said good day. The line was clear.

---

She hung the handset in its cradle.

She took the harbor log down from the shelf — the current volume, running from January 1943 — and turned it back to October. The incident from the thirteenth occupied the bottom third of the page: date, hour, nature of the event, response, disposition. The disposition column read *Transferred to First District USCG custody per standing protocol. Case referred to district for identification.* The vessel traffic column for the night of October 9 through 13 still carried the dash.

She set the volume flat and found the margin below the October 13 entry. She wrote:

*Cross-reference completed, April 1945. Decedent identified as August Schell, German national, Swedish citizen, crew of SS* Ångermanälven, *reported missing North Atlantic coastal waters on or about October 7, 1943. First District determination: lost at sea, accidental drowning. Case closed. — E.M. Bowman, HM*

She set the pen down. She read the entry back once. It said what it said.

She closed the harbor log and returned it to the shelf.

---

She crossed to the chart cabinet and opened the pending drawer — bottom, right side.

Her current working files were at the front: the Lighthouse Service notice on the spring buoy positions, the fuel oil delivery receipt for March she had not yet filed, the selectmen's fee schedule revision request. She moved these to the side.

Owen's two folders were at the back where they had always been, in his hand on the tabs: *Lighthouse Service correspondence, 1934.* *Tidal survey, pending, 1935.* She did not touch either of them.

In front of his folders, where she had placed it the fourteenth of September last year: the addendum. A folded document, three panels, the harbor office heading visible at the top edge of the upper fold.

She took it out and unfolded it on the desk.

*Addendum to Incident Report, October 13, 1943 (Unidentified Decedent, Breakwater).*
*September 14, 1944.*

She read through it. The handwriting was her September handwriting — even, compressed, slightly smaller than her correspondence hand because that was how she wrote in official documents. Six pages on harbor office stationery, the ink still dark. The bridle inspection that had put her on the pier at 0130. The individual at the far end of the public pier: canvas bag over the right shoulder, maritime working dress, good physical condition, not a known user of the harbor. The sound from the Macomber's mooring. The five minutes at the starboard chock. The bridle adjusted and confirmed. Looking back toward the far end and finding the pier empty. *The undersigned assumed that he had taken shelter in the pier shed, which was standing open.*

She had known, writing that sentence, that it was not an assumption she had actually made. It said so without saying so. *No entry was made in the vessel traffic log. No further investigation was conducted.*

She read those two sentences and moved on. Her signature at the bottom of the last page, and the date.

She folded the addendum back in three — the two parallel creases, corners squared — and set it on the desk.

She reached to the working shelf for the personal log: the small cloth-bound notebook, black cover, that she had started in November 1943 and finished the evening after Whitmore's July visit. Fourteen pages in her hand. She opened it to the first.

*November 6, 1943. The CG has the body. The dash in the October 13 log stands. Inquiry?*

She read through to the last entry: *Cross-reference to Ångermanälven pending. Schell, August, German national, Swedish citizen. Note: does not change what is in the pending drawer. That stays until the case closes one way.*

She closed the notebook.

---

She put on her coat and went out.

The April sun was full on the pier, the boards dry, the float at the ladder riding at mid-tide on its proper line. She walked past the pier shed without stopping and started down the breakwater.

Three hundred and forty feet, two sections, the gap between them showing the southeast swell running low and regular on the outside face. She walked it. The cap was dry except in two places in the middle section she already had on the maintenance list; the hand cable was sound on both sides; the capstones at the far end had not shifted. She reached the end and stopped.

The April sea. The small swell lifted against the breakwater's foot and let back with the soft sound of calm water on granite — a sound she had heard ten thousand times, this sound in particular, the particular contact of this weight of water with this angle of stone. Below the surface the weed moved in long slow shifts. On the outer face of the seaward section, the wrack line from the winter's high water ran brown and horizontal, eighteen inches above the current waterline, the storms' record.

She could see the approach channel from here — the two gear buoys strung out toward the mouth, the fairway buoy on the south side that the service tender had repositioned in March. The northeast point where the harbor's outer granite head turned the swell was visible clearly in the April light. Inland, the town ridge, the upper maples showing the first new green.

The tidal set from a position north of the harbor mouth over three days, with the swell running southeast: the body would have come in against the north face of the seaward section and settled into the angle between sections at this end. She had worked it through in November of 1943 and had not needed to work it again. The calculation was what it was. The body had come in at this end and she had been the first person from the harbor to find it.

She stood.

She knew the geography. She knew what the record contained and what it did not. She had been at this end of the dock in the early hours of October 10 looking toward the far end of the public pier, and she had walked back across the pier to the harbor office, and three days later there had been a body at the angle of the granite below where she was now standing, and she had filed what she had seen and what she had not done in a document that was now in her coat pocket.

The swell lifted and let back. Above the harbor the ridge was fully in the morning sun.

She turned and walked back.

---

Inside, she crossed to the permanent files cabinet against the north wall — the gray steel four-drawer that the Lighthouse Service had standardized across the Maine district stations, delivered to the Coldwater Reach harbor office in 1938. Three drawers held the district correspondence in order by year. She pulled the bottom drawer.

The tabs ran forward from the back: vessel inspection reports from 1937, the ice damage incident from February 1943, the *Calvert* grounding from that August. At the front of the drawer, a tab she had made from card stock the previous September: *Schell, August — Incident October 1943.* Behind the tab was the single Lighthouse Service cross-reference form she had completed in August at the district's request.

She filed the addendum behind the tab.

She filed the notebook behind the addendum — the fourteen pages of entries from November 6, 1943, to July 22, 1944, now in the same drawer as the addendum and the cross-reference form, behind the tab with the name. She settled both until they lay flat.

She closed the drawer.

---

She made coffee.

The coffeepot had been on the shelf since morning. She refilled it from the water can, measured the grounds from the tin — the general store order, which she placed monthly — and set it on the portable burner. While it heated she went back to the desk and the weekly report. The arrival log total was done. She moved to the mooring-field count.

The mooring ledger had the figures: fourteen active moorings, down from sixteen at the end of last season, the two vacancies still standing from the fall. She copied the count into the report.

The coffee came up. She poured a cup and set it beside the report.

The approach buoy notation was one line. She wrote it. She wrote the harbor master's signature at the bottom of the form and the date. The weekly report was done.

She drank the coffee.

The anchorage was quiet at mid-morning, the two boats in from the near grounds now at their moorings, the third still laid up with its unresolved stern cleat. The water had taken on the slight midday chop of an April southerly — nothing, a small agitation, the kind of day the harbor logged as calm. One of the trap-haulers was leaving the south mooring on a late start, its exhaust carrying across the flat water.

She set the empty cup on the draining board and went to the desk for the Lighthouse Service notice on the buoy positions, which she needed to review against the tender's work schedule before Thursday.

She was reading it when she heard the buoy tender.

The engine note: a slower, heavier frequency than the lobster boats, a frequency that belonged to a vessel of that tonnage running at service speed in a flat harbor approach. She was at the east window before she had consciously decided to cross the room.

The tender came around the northeast point — the granite head that was the harbor's outer protection on the northeast side — at twenty past ten. She was running her Lighthouse Service ensign and the service pennant, both clear in the April air, the flag staff at the bow picking up the morning light. Her hull was the standard service gray. The working deck forward of the wheelhouse was staged for the run: the davit rigged, locker hatches open, the foredeck crew in their working positions for a harbor approach. First full spring run.

She found the vessel arrival log on the desk and picked it up.

She put on her coat.

The buoy tender was through the harbor mouth and making for the pier when she came out of the harbor office and started down the dock to meet it.

**One fix applied. Chapter otherwise clean.**

**What changed and why:**

| Location | Original | Revised |
|---|---|---|
| Addendum-reading scene | *"It said so without saying so, in the same way that any sentence careful enough to state only what was provable left visible, in the space around it, what was not stated."* | *"It said so without saying so."* |

**Rationale:** The elaborating clause — *"in the same way that any sentence careful enough to state only what was provable left visible, in the space around it, what was not stated"* — is Marcus Klein's register, not Eira's. Marcus is the character whose narration notices the gap between language and meaning, who analyzes sentences for their construction and range. Eira is a proceduralist: she would observe that the record has a gap; she would not theorize about how carefully drafted sentences encode their own omissions. The opening clause *"It said so without saying so"* is sparse and observational — entirely on-register. The deleted elaboration was the drift. The meaning is preserved; the literary-theoretical expansion is gone.

**Everything else was clean:** dialogue (exemplary Eira — "Yes." / "I'm listening." / "Understood."), emotion carried entirely by action (filing the notebook, not touching Owen's folders, walking the breakwater), no similes, no editorializing, ending on action with no summarizing reflection.
I'll read all three files simultaneously to get started.
Now let me read the chapter carefully against the style guide and character voice notes.

Good. I have all three documents. Let me now analyze carefully before touching anything.

---

**My findings after reading against `canon/style.md` and Hannah's voice profile:**

**Five issues, ranging from a POV violation to local over-articulation:**

1. **Grammar-as-philosophy clause** — `"as if grammar were a structure that required respect rather than just retention"` — This is Marcus's register, not Hannah's. She observes his *behavior*; she doesn't theorize about the epistemology of grammar. The first "as if" clause already does the work.

2. **Adult-retrospective epigram** — `"only become more precisely himself"` — Formally constructed, too epigrammatic for a seventeen-year-old. The style guide explicitly warns against adult retrospective wisdom in Hannah's chapters.

3. **Omniscient breach (hardest violation)** — `"In two weeks it would. She did not know that — could not know it from inside it;"` — The narrator steps entirely outside Hannah's limited third-person to announce the war's end date. The style guide is categorical: *"No character should appear to understand the war's outcome before it happens. Write from inside the uncertainty."* The parenthetical walk-back (`"she did not know that"`) only worsens the breach by confirming the narrator is separate from the POV character.

4. **Reader-reassurance over-explanation** — `"This was not the same as not caring about the outcome — she had thought about it carefully enough to know the difference."` — Hannah is defending herself to an implied suspicious reader. The style guide: *"Trust the reader. Do not explain the emotional significance of what just happened."*

5. **Ending gloss** — `"in which those sets would do whatever onion sets did if you put them in correctly and gave them time"` — The subordinate clause teaches the metaphor. The conditional *if you put them in correctly and gave them time* signals to the reader how to read the image (patience, cultivation, uncertain future). The style guide: *"Do not explain what the ending means."*

All five are local fixes. Applying them now.

input.content:
# Before the Day

The barn smell was different now — warmer, animal, and underneath the animal smell something she could only call spring: not sweet, more like something tamped down all winter finally releasing. She hung the lantern on the hook by the near stall and went down the row.

The cows had come in from the pasture on their own at milking hour, which they had been doing for four days since they went out. The first morning, she had watched from the yard: the gate opened and they stood in it for a moment — Hilde first, looking at nothing in particular — and then Hilde went through and the others followed without hurrying, spreading out across the wet grass as if they had been told to take their time. The pasture was very green. Magda had stood at the kitchen window in her housedress watching them until the last one cleared the near fence. She had not said anything. Hannah had gone inside for coffee.

Now the cows were outside by day and in by night and would be until October. She went down the row with the hay and set up for milking.

It was quieter than winter. The thick cold was gone. Through the boards she could see gray-pink light beginning over the east ridge, and she could hear — or thought she could — the crows starting, earlier every morning now. She did the milking in the usual order: Bess first, then Maren, then the others. The pails filled. The barn had its own warmth now, the stored warmth of animals overnight, different from the kerosene warmth of winter mornings when you brought in a heater. She noticed this — the warmth that required nothing from her, just the bodies of fifteen cows in a closed space through the night.

By the time she finished the sky through the gaps in the boards was gray. She carried the pails to the cooler, hung the lantern back on its hook, and went out.

---

The kitchen garden was already occupied.

Her mother was kneeling at the near bed, working something with her hands. The garden fork was propped against the fence post at the corner. Hannah set the milk pails on the stone step by the cellar door and crossed the yard.

Magda said — to the soil, not to her: *Die Erde ist noch kalt.*

"Give it a week," Hannah said.

Her mother pressed her palm flat against the earth and held it there. Then she lifted it and looked at the bed she had been preparing for the better part of a week: smoothed and loosened and turned, the clods broken down, the surface raked. It was ready. The earth was still cold.

Magda nodded, not quite at anything, and went back to her hands.

Hannah went inside to put on her coat.

---

The lilac by the front gate had buds — tight and dark, purple-green, barely suggesting what they were going to be. Another week, maybe ten days. She went through the gate and down the road.

She heard them before she saw them: voices in German, direction and acknowledgment, the metal clank of the post driver. They were at the south fence line, all four, doing the spring maintenance. The big one — the schoolteacher from Hannover — was on the driver. Two others held the post. Karl was carrying a wire spool across the field toward them.

She went fifty yards down the road before she slowed. He had seen her; she knew by the small adjustment of his walk, nothing performed about it, just the noticing. He set the spool down and came to the fence line, the others not looking up from the driver. The old pasture wire between them was low — lower than the yard fence — and she could see him clearly. The denim jacket the Army had issued him was worn at the shoulder seams. He had been on this farm eighteen months.

He said: "Your German is better."

She said: "Your English is better."

He said: "Yes."

They stood. On her side the road bank, still damp at the root line. On his side the cows had already been across the grass that morning and marked it. She looked at the ridge. The trees there were at the edge of something — a haze across the branches, not leaves yet, just the willingness. From here you could not see the harbor. The ridge was in the way. She knew where the harbor was. She had stood at the top of that ridge in October with Karl three feet to her right on the outside of the fence and Lonzo's chair empty back by the icehouse, and they had looked down through the trees at the breakwater light and the gray water past it, very large in the dark.

She looked at the ridge. Karl looked at the ridge.

Neither of them said anything about October.

After a moment she said: "The wire at the north gate corner is going."

He said: "I know. We'll get to it this afternoon."

She nodded. He went back to the spool. She walked on to school.

---

She was wearing Rolf's coat.

It had been two sizes too large when she took it from his closet in November of 1943 — the week after the POWs arrived, and Rolf already at Camp Edwards, so there was no one to ask. She had worn it that way through a year and more: shoulders past her shoulders, sleeves folded up. By the second fall the sleeves fit. Now the seams sat where seams were supposed to sit. She had grown into it. She had not replaced it and she was not going to.

The three miles to school were the same road she had walked her whole life: the south fence, the Belanger woodlot, the fork where you always went right, the ridge road and down it into Reach Road proper and north along the main street to the old mill building. This time of year the road was dry and her footsteps made a sound. The crabapple trees near the ridge were not in blossom yet, but they were thinking about it, the buds swollen and reddish in the morning light. She passed the Congregational church, the post office, Burleigh's diner with its Tuesday-closed sign still in the window even though it was Wednesday. She kept walking.

---

German was second-to-last period.

There were six of them left in the class. There had been nine in September; Donna Parkhurst and one other had graduated mid-year, and the girl whose father got the Bangor paper mill position had gone with him. The six remaining sat in the front four rows at Klein's request. The room smelled of chalk and beneath it the building smell — old mill wood and cold damp, the particular odor of a structure that had been something else for too long to entirely stop being it.

Klein was reviewing the dative case. He had the declension chart on the board and was going through it with the methodical care he brought to everything, as if the procedure itself were worth attending to. She had been watching him do this for three years and he had not changed in any way she could point to.

*Dem Mann, der Frau, dem Kind.*

She had known this for years before she knew she knew it. Her father declined nouns at the dinner table when he was talking to himself or to Magda without thinking of it as teaching — *mit dem Brot, unter dem Tisch, nach der Schule* — just the way the words went. She had stored it without meaning to. This was how most of what she knew had come to her: through repeated presence, until a thing was part of her without the ceremony of learning.

She looked out the window.

The trees on the ridge were the same ones she had passed that morning.

She thought: *mündig.*

Klein had written the word on this board a year ago, last April, and she had barely been paying attention — Karl had said *Glückwunsch* at the fence that afternoon, without ceremony, just because it was her birthday, and her mind had already been partly there at the fence, partly at the afternoon. But the word had stayed. It was the kind that stays.

The state of having your own mouth. The age of speaking for yourself, legally, in full responsibility for what you said.

She thought about what she had said and not said in the year since.

The word-cards. Cedar rectangles she had cut with her father's knife and inked with his good pen, English on one side and German on the other, slid under the fence post stone through two winters. They had stopped making new ones in the fall — not because anything ended but because the words had moved off the paper into conversation, and conversation had moved into something she still did not have a name for: the understanding that builds out of enough accumulated mornings, the way a house fills with the sounds of the people in it until the sounds are just the house. She knew where the cards were. In the wooden box under Karl's bunk, with his papers, the photograph of his mother, and the Red Cross letter he had read twice and not taken out again.

She knew his father's name. *Friedrich.* Friedrich Reinmar. She had not said it to anyone — not in a letter to Rolf, not to her parents at the dinner table, not to Lonzo Reed, not to anyone in this room. She was holding the name the way she held a few other things: not pressing, not requiring anything, just present and hers. She had learned it in January, at the north fence, when Karl told her matter-of-factly — *mein Vater ist gestorben* — and she had said *das tut mir leid* and meant it, and walked back to the house and held the name through supper and through her mother's small careful sentences about the pipes and the cellar.

She had written Rolf in February. She had said: potatoes held well, weather has been hard, Mutti and Vati are fine. She had not said: Mutti is talking again, small sentences, the carrots in the cellar and what to do with the soft ones. She had not said anything about Karl's father, or the October ridge, or the harbor lights at night and the way the water had looked from up there, very large and going on.

These were not accidents. She had read back what she had written and known what was missing and folded the letter and sent it anyway.

Klein called on her. She came back to the room.

*Den Häusern,* she said. The dative plural.

He nodded and moved on.

---

Three weeks until graduation.

She had been thinking since January about nursing school in Bangor. Eastern Maine General took girls right out of high school — the pamphlet at the post office had been clear about that — and the program ran two years and you came out knowing how to do specific things under pressure and with precision. She was good at that. She had been practicing it her whole life without calling it by a name.

She had also been thinking about staying. Not because she couldn't imagine leaving. The farm was not a trap; she had never felt it as one. But she could imagine both equally and neither one pressed, and the farm needed what it needed: Albrecht's back had been bad since October, the cows were fifteen and not twelve, and Rolf would come home when the war ended and the farm would be four people again instead of three.

And then there was the question of what happened to Karl when repatriation came.

It would come. The radio made this clear enough for anyone paying attention: the Allies were across the Rhine, the German positions were losing the shape of positions, and when it ended — when it did — the eight thousand German POWs scattered across Maine on satellite details would need to go home, eventually, after the Army worked through the paperwork. It might be months. It might be longer. But Karl would go back to Regensburg, or to whatever Regensburg was by then. She did not know how much of it was standing. The radio said things about German cities but often not which ones specifically, or to what degree. His father's shop might be there or it might not. The tile floor, the east window. She had heard about these from Karl in October, at the ridge, in the way things come out when you are standing somewhere high and looking at something large and your language and his language are both almost sufficient to what you mean to say.

She did not know if she would write to him after.

She had thought about it enough to know she could not know in advance. There might not be an address, or not a reliable one. There might be reasons a letter went nowhere useful. She had decided not to try to work this out before it arrived. The future had its own business and she had hers. When repatriation came she would find out what it meant and decide then.

She picked up her books when the bell rang.

---

Her mother was still in the garden.

The spare trowel was in the bucket by the farm gate, the same bucket that held the stakes and string and the rest of the small tools that lived outside from April through October. Hannah picked it up on her way through, not deciding to, just picking it up. She went across the yard to the bed where her mother was kneeling.

Magda had the seed tray across her knees — the old one with the broken corner, the same one Hannah could remember using as a mudpie base when she was small, before she understood what it was for. In it: onion sets, small and papery, the beginnings of green at their tips. Her mother held the tray steady, then held it out.

*Hier,* she said. Here.

Hannah took the sets. She turned to the bed — dark and worked and cold, the cold that went but went slowly this time of year — and pressed the first one in with her thumb. Blunt end down. An inch and a half. The soil came under her fingernails. She moved along the row.

Her mother worked beside her. The light was going soft, the April light at this hour flattening as the sun dropped toward the ridge. The cows were somewhere out past the near fence in the green pasture. The lilac by the front gate had buds that in a week or ten days would open and smell like themselves, which was a thing she could never hold in her mind between years, only recognize when it came back.

The war had not ended yet. The radio gave you conclusions to draw, not dates. What was hers was this: the earth under her hands, still cold and giving slightly to her weight, and the onion sets going in one by one, and her mother's breathing beside her, and the whole summer ahead of them in which those sets would come up.

She pressed another set in and moved down the row.

text:
Five targeted changes applied. Here's an account of each:

---

**Changes made:**

1. **Grammar-as-philosophy clause deleted** *(German class section)*
   - Removed `", as if grammar were a structure that required respect rather than just retention"` from the double *as-if* construction.
   - *Why:* That clause belongs to Marcus's register — theorizing about the essence of grammar as a discipline. Hannah observes Klein's *behavior*, not his pedagogical philosophy.

2. **Adult-retrospective epigram deleted** *(German class section)*
   - Removed `", only become more precisely himself"` from the sentence about watching Klein for three years.
   - *Why:* The style guide explicitly warns against adult retrospective wisdom in Hannah's chapters. That clause is too formally epigrammatic for a seventeen-year-old's perception.

3. **Omniscient POV breach removed** *(penultimate paragraph)*
   - Deleted `"In two weeks it would. She did not know that — could not know it from inside it;"` and restructured the sentence.
   - *Why:* The hardest violation in the chapter. The narrator stepped entirely outside Hannah's limited third-person to announce the war's end date, then tried to walk it back with a parenthetical. The style guide is categorical: *"No character should appear to understand the war's outcome before it happens. Write from inside the uncertainty."* The surviving line — *"The radio gave you conclusions to draw, not dates"* — does the work correctly: the reader has been told (Allied advance, German positions collapsing) and can draw the conclusion themselves.

4. **Reader-reassurance sentence deleted** *(repatriation paragraph)*
   - Removed `"This was not the same as not caring about the outcome — she had thought about it carefully enough to know the difference."` The paragraph now ends on `"When repatriation came she would find out what it meant and decide then."`
   - *Why:* Hannah was defending herself to a skeptical implied reader. The style guide: *"Trust the reader. Do not explain the emotional significance of what just happened."*

5. **Ending gloss trimmed** *(final paragraph)*
   - Changed `"in which those sets would do whatever onion sets did if you put them in correctly and gave them time"` → `"in which those sets would come up."`
   - *Why:* The conditional *if you put them in correctly and gave them time* teaches the metaphor — patience, cultivation, uncertainty — rather than leaving it as image. The style guide: *"Do not explain what the ending means."* The image is strong enough without the gloss.

# Reader Act 1 Assessment — Chapters 01–10

## Overall Impression

Act 1 is doing the work it needs to do: all five threads are entered or advanced, three distinct voices are established, and the spine question is planted without being solved. The breakwater opening is genuinely excellent — one of the best chapter-ones the novel could have produced. The weaknesses are real but recoverable: a pattern of over-long interior monologue that runs longer than the style guide permits, a soft spot in Hannah's register in ch08, a recurrence of ending-mode passivity across the act, and one continuity error in the *Macomber* cargo route that needs to be resolved.

---

## What Is Working

**Eira's voice is the most fully realized of the three.** Ch01 is a model: spare, procedural, observant, with emotion surfacing only as the dash she makes in the vessel-traffic column. The final image — "the dash where it was" — closes the scene without explaining it and earns the style guide's directive completely. Ch07 sustains this: the coal stove, the ice knocking at the float, the three possibilities worked through for the *Macomber* departure, and then the personal log ending with a single word — *Inquiry?* Ch10 is equally strong, and the Hartley/Estelle encounter is the best secondary character work in the act. Two women in harbor authority exchanging professional competence without a line about it. The coffee-sharing scene over the ice ledge works because it trusts the situation.

**The *auch* passage in ch05 is the act's emotional high point.** Karl sets the grain bucket at the post rather than handing it over — "the right way to do it" — and Hannah registers both the action and her own registering of it. The six-word exchange then unpacks into a meditation on what the word *auch* does grammatically and personally. This passage earns every word and demonstrates that Hannah's voice, when it's working, is the act's most alive instrument.

**Thread management is structurally sound.** Per the weave table, every chapter in Act 1 advances at least one thread, and no chapter feels artificially loaded. T1 develops in ch01, 04, 07, 10 with clean escalation: the dash, the unexplained log entry, the Macomber departure question, the Portland contract lead. Each advance adds one fact and one new question. T4 enters through the gap in the boards (ch02) and moves to the first exchange (ch05), with Hannah's understanding of her father's knowledge arriving in ch08. The structural design of the act holds.

**Period texture is handled correctly throughout.** Rationing, V-Mail, the Navy district contract structure, the POW labor program, the Coast Guard inquiry chain — none of it is announced. It constrains action. The harbor-closed-outbound regulation in ch04, the Geneva Convention framework implied by Lonzo's chair, the district wireless procedure in ch10 — all absorbed into the scene without stopping to explain themselves.

**Marcus's etymological register is at its best in the *Mensch ohne Papiere* passage in ch06.** The distinction between the English "man without papers" as filing inconvenience versus the German *ein Mensch ohne Papiere* as the removal of legal personhood is one of the novel's most important insights, delivered in Marcus's characteristic mode: precise, qualified, personally implicated without announcing the personal implication.

---

## What Is Not Working

**Hannah's voice slips into Marcus's register in ch08.** The extended meditation on *Heimweh* — beginning "She thought about the word *Heimweh*" — runs for several hundred words of etymological and psychological analysis that the style guide assigns to Marcus, not Hannah. The style guide is explicit: Hannah "is not an intellectual" and her narration avoids "self-consciousness about her own narration." The distinction between *Weh* as ongoing ache versus English "sickness" as recoverable condition, and the extended analysis of whether her father's knowledge of Karl constitutes proof of the place's continued reality — this is careful adult retrospective wisdom applied to a seventeen-year-old. The passage undermines Hannah's register just when the chapter has established its strongest beat: the dinner scene, the seven words, Albrecht's "Regensburg ist nicht weit." The analysis should come from the reader, not the narrator.

**Four of the first nine chapters end with a character going to bed or lying down.** Ch02: "she was asleep." Ch07: Eira pours out the cold tea and goes to bed. Ch08: Hannah closes her eyes and lies still. Ch09: Marcus lies on the bed with his coat over him and "did not think further." The style guide states: "End on an image, an action, or a piece of dialogue — not on a summarizing reflection." A prone, passive character is not an action; it is the absence of one. This ending mode also concentrates in the quietest emotional register — resolution, rest, ceasing — at a point in the novel where the threads are still opening and the emotional register should be building. Ch01's ending ("the dash where it was") and ch05's ending (Hannah going back to the barn, the cows knowing her by her boots) are the models. The others need revision.

**Ch04 (The Coast Guard) runs longer than it earns.** The chapter's essential work is accomplished in two scenes: Eira withholds from Pruitt, and Eira discovers the unexplained departure entry. But the middle section — Eira working through three possibilities for the log entry — extends to roughly 400 words of deliberate interior analysis when two well-chosen possibilities would carry more impact by trusting the reader. The style guide warns against "two paragraphs of interior monologue" where a physical action communicates more. By the third possibility the accumulation has begun to explain what the narrative should allow to remain unresolved.

**A continuity error in the *Macomber*'s cargo route.** In ch04, Eira tells Pruitt: "Bagged lime. Machias to Lubec, calling here on the way." This establishes Machias as origin. In ch07, Eira reads her own log: "arrived October eighth out of Calais with lime and general goods." Calais, Maine is approximately sixty miles northeast of Machias. The two descriptions give incompatible ports of origin for the same voyage. A meticulous harbormaster with her log on the desk in front of her would not misremember where Macomber came from; Eira's entire characterization depends on her precision in exactly these matters. One of these must yield.

**Ch06 (What the Body Spoke) is padded at its structural joints.** The chapter's core — Marcus's visit to Eira, the exchange, the philological reflection on the walk home — is strong. But the surrounding scaffolding (the note from Farwell, Marcus's memory of his first and only prior visit to the harbor office eighteen months ago, the extended description of his Latin lesson and the ablative absolute, the supper scene) delays the chapter's actual business and adds length without proportional return. The ablative absolute lesson is the most elegant piece of double-coding in the chapter — *Caesar, Gallia victa, Romam rediit*, the subordinate world standing at the clause's edge — but it arrives forty minutes before Marcus reaches the harbor office and the chapter's real scene, making it feel like setup rather than simultaneity.

---

## Voice Slips

Three specific instances warrant flagging:

- Ch08: "She thought about the word *Heimweh*." Through the end of the paragraph on *Weh* as ache vs. English "sickness" — Hannah's narration here has become Marcus's analytical mode. The register slips from associative interiority to deliberate etymology.

- Ch09, final sentence: "He closed his eyes and lay still while the house settled around him in the cold... and did not think further." The phrase "did not think further" is a narrative gesture that names what the ending is doing. The style guide: "do not explain what the ending means."

- Ch05, final paragraph: "She thought: *auch*. Ten words across a fence this morning. Which was ten more than the house had managed in a month." The counting, the explicit arithmetic — "ten more than the house had managed" — is slightly too aware of its own significance. The *auch* meditation that precedes it is excellent; this coda over-explains what it has already earned.

---

## Thread Development in Act 1

- **T1 (The Blank in the Log):** Best developed thread in the act. Four beats, each adding one fact. The Hartley conversation in ch10 is well-staged — information arriving through professional courtesy, not investigation.
- **T2 (The Man Still Moving):** Correctly entered through *unheimlich* in ch03. The *Duldung* naming in ch09 is accurate to Marcus's habit of identifying the word for his condition before taking any other action. The thread is thinner in Act 1 than T1 — one fewer active beat — but appropriate for a thread whose arc is long and slow.
- **T3 (The Hearing):** Entered subtly in ch03 (the half-second look), formally in ch09. Edna Locke's scene is among the better secondary-character moments in the act. The thread is correctly subordinate to T2 at this stage.
- **T4 (The Fence):** *fremd* in ch02, *Ich auch* in ch05, the father's acknowledgment in ch08. Three strong beats. The fence as physical structure governing all contact is established without being announced.
- **T5 (The Pfeiffer Silence):** The Magda humming scene in ch08 — a few bars of something without a name, then stopping — is the thread's best moment in the act. The silence is briefly marked rather than broken, which is exactly right.

---

## Pacing

The act moves deliberately and correctly. The rotation (Eira, Hannah, Marcus, Eira, Hannah, Marcus, Eira, Hannah, Marcus, Eira) is clean and the weighting — Eira four times, the others three each — is well-justified by Eira's role as the act's structural anchor through T1. No chapter outstays its welcome fatally; ch04 and ch06 are the longest for what they accomplish. The act's best pacing is in the sequence ch07–ch08–ch09: three different characters in three different settings, each encountering a form of incomplete knowledge, none of them able to act on it yet. This is the act's emotional center.

text:
The Act 1 assessment has been written to `logs/reader-act-1.md`. Here's a summary of the key findings:

**Strengths identified:**
- Ch01 opening is exemplary — Eira's voice fully calibrated from the first page
- The *auch* passage in ch05 is the act's emotional high point
- Thread management is structurally sound across all five threads
- Period texture is handled as constraint, never set decoration
- Secondary characters Hartley and Edna Locke are well-drawn
- The ch07–ch08–ch09 sequence is the act's best sustained pacing

**Weaknesses flagged:**
1. **Hannah's register slips into Marcus's mode** in ch08 — the extended *Heimweh* meditation is etymological analysis, not adolescent interiority
2. **Ending-mode passivity** — four of nine chapters close with characters going to bed or lying down, against the style guide's explicit direction
3. **Ch04 over-explains** — the three-possibility interior monologue runs too long for its structural work
4. **Continuity error** — *Macomber*'s port of origin is "Machias" in ch04 and "Calais" in ch07; these can't both be right given Eira's precision
5. **Ch06 is padded** at its structural joints — the ablative absolute lesson and prior visit memory delay the chapter's actual scene
6. Three specific **voice slips** cited with verbatim text

# Developmental Reader — Act 2 Assessment
## Chapters 11–20 (The Word for Harbor through Addendum)

---

### Overall

Act 2 is the novel's most uneven act in terms of quality distribution — and that unevenness is the main story. Two chapters here (ch 15, ch 20) are among the best writing in the manuscript; two more (ch 17, ch 18) are nearly as strong. The remaining six are competent to good, with specific weaknesses in the Hannah chapters' tendency toward over-articulation, a redundancy problem in the spine-question reveals, and a formulaic repetition in the Marcus chapter endings. The act's structure is sound — the five-thread weave is maintained, no chapter is dead weight — but the craft ceiling is higher in some rooms than others, and the gap is wide enough to notice.

---

### What's Working

**Ch 15, "The Sixth of June"** is the act's standout, and not by a small margin. Marcus stands at his classroom window thinking about the Rue du Temple, the scholars at the kitchen table, the vendor at the Tuesday market "who would not be there in November of 1940." The prose carries the Holocaust's mass without naming it — "He had named this to himself once, precisely, and had then put the name down, because naming was a precision instrument and he had decided — carefully, with the deliberateness of someone making a decision that would govern him — that the instrument had a correct number of uses." This is exactly what the brief requires: the thing present in the room without being announced. The two-part structure of the gladness section — letting the gladness be full, then refusing to complete it — handles an enormous tonal challenge with precision. The chapter earns everything it tries.

**Ch 20, "Addendum"** is the act's best Eira chapter. The addendum-as-chapter-structure is a genuine formal invention: the official incident report language, with its compressed professional syntax, carries eleven months of withheld fact in a form that amplifies rather than explains the weight. "The undersigned did not approach the individual" — five words, and the chapter is right not to comment on them. Filing the document behind Owen's two unfinished folders, the fold she learned from him, the act of closing the drawer and going outside: this ending is the model for what Eira's chapters should always be doing and sometimes aren't.

**Ch 17, "Off Duty"** does Thread 4's heaviest lifting in this act. The perimeter-walk construction — her on the farm side, him on the outside, the four-foot fence between them — is rendered with the precision the setup requires. "She was on the farm side. He was on the outside, the perimeter, walking the edge of the property. They were about two feet apart with the fence between them, which was the way it was, and she did not name the way it was." The callback to *Ich auch* at the end earns its weight because nine months of cedar cards have deposited meaning into those two words. Lonzo's deliberate inattention ("the deliberate tilt of someone reading, the particular stillness of a man who had decided what he was attending to") is thread-perfect.

**Ch 18, "What Henry Notices"** — the exceptional POV chapter — justifies its existence. Henry's voice is dry, observant, and unsentimentalized; the detail of the piano lid, the east window latch requisition "through three times without result," the Edna Locke map with the fresh pin in Florence — all of this renders a man who has made himself indispensable through nineteen years of exactly noticing without once claiming he noticed. The petition scene is handled with exactly the right restraint: he reads the first sentence, puts the paper back in the same crumpled position, and mops the floor. The dish-washing ending lets two men finish a task together without explaining what the task costs.

**The mündig passage in Ch 14** is one of the act's smallest and best moments. Marcus writes the word on the board, derives it from *Mund*, delivers the lesson without editorial; Hannah writes "having your own mouth" in the margin of her notebook and underlines it. The word is planted without being displayed. Karl's *Glückwunsch* — "not *Herzlichen Glückwunsch* — not the full ceremony. Just *Glückwunsch.*" — is the act's most efficient single moment: two words doing nine months of accumulated relational weight.

---

### What Isn't Working

**Over-articulation in Hannah's chapters.** The brief specifies that Hannah avoids "adult retrospective wisdom" and the narration avoids explaining what just happened. Ch 11 violates this constraint in a specific passage: "She had learned — not from anywhere in particular, from the general running of a house — that you didn't say a thing until you knew what it would cost." This is a narrated moral, softened only by the parenthetical hedge ("not from anywhere in particular"), which itself reads as the narrator's awareness that the statement is too explicit and an attempt to blur it. Ch 14 has the same problem after the *mündig* lesson: "She had understood it before he finished saying it" — the understanding already shows in what she writes in her notebook margin; the sentence that follows tells us she understood. Both instances ask the reader to register less because the narration has registered for them.

**Ch 12 ("Two Suits") loses momentum in its middle third.** The *heimisch* meditation runs five paragraphs and recapitulates material the novel established in ch 3 (*unheimlich*, *Duldung*, the Crow household as the place where Marcus doesn't translate). The passage on Edna Locke's brooch — "the same small brooch every day. The three minutes she had taken, after staying when the others filed out, to say what she had come to say... and the calculation she had run in those three minutes about whether the debt was worth paying" — makes explicit a conclusion (he owes something to Coldwater Reach) that the act of writing to Cambridge already demonstrates. The telling comes before the showing, and the chapter would be tighter by a page.

**Ch 13 ("Ada Gile's Window") runs long in the harbor-office analysis.** After the post office visit, Eira returns to her desk and works through the postcard/Macomber/pier-night geometry in a passage beginning "A postcard, sent from Portland — the postmark was Portland, which she had not known before this morning." The problem is threefold: (1) the reader has been carrying these same pieces for three chapters and doesn't need the reconstruction; (2) the passage adds no new inference, only restates connections already implied; (3) the chapter's correct ending — the coffee she doesn't pour, the harbor through the window, the silence — is correct but arrives eight paragraphs too late. The harbor-office recap section should be cut by half.

**The August Schell reveal is duplicated too narrowly.** Ch 16 has Commander Whitmore name August Schell to Eira in July 1944. Ch 19 has Marcus independently discovering the same name in Bangor in August 1944. Three chapters apart, both gives the name; the structural logic (each POV gets their version of the spine-question's revelation) is sound in theory, but the proximity means the second revelation arrives before the first has had time to settle into the novel's field. Marcus's Bangor chapter has other work to do — the recognition scene ("the calculation you made when the alternative was to become legible to a state that had stopped being interested in your continued existence as a free person" is the act's most explicit thematic statement and one of its best) — but the discovery of Schell's name registers as delay rather than accumulation because Whitmore has already named him.

**The elm outside the Crow house window has become a formulaic chapter-ending.** Ch 12 (March buds in the elm), ch 15 (elm full-leafed in June dark as Marcus goes to sleep), ch 19 (elm in the yard "holding the last of the evening light" as Marcus comes home). Three Marcus chapters, three variations on the same closing image — Marcus inside the Crow house, elm outside, seasonal marker. The elm is an intentional continuity motif and the first two uses are defensible, but by ch 19 the construction reads as a default. The three Marcus chapters in this act share too much terminal structure.

---

### Continuity Flag

There is a discrepancy in the handling of the postcard's Portland postmark. In ch 12, Ada Gile tells Marcus about "a piece of mail that came through in the first week of October — addressed to general delivery, no full name"; she gives him the name "A. Schell" when asked, but does not mention a Portland postmark in the dialogue as written. In ch 13, Ada tells Eira about the same postcard; again, no postmark is mentioned in Ada's dialogue. But in ch 13, Eira's notebook entry reads "A. Schell — general delivery — postcard — Portland postmark — early October" — treating the Portland postmark as a fact she received from Ada. And in ch 19, Marcus's retrospective summary of the ch 12 conversation includes "Portland postmark, received the first week of October 1943" as part of what Ada told him. Both characters attribute a Portland postmark to their Ada conversations, but neither conversation as drafted includes Ada saying it. Either the Ada dialogue scenes need the postmark added, or the characters' recollections need to present it as inference rather than recalled fact. This is a continuity gap that affects both characters' subsequent reasoning.

---

### Thread Development in Act 2

**Thread 1 (Blank in Log)**: Three chapters (13, 16, 20), well-spaced. Ch 20 is the act's structural achievement. Ch 13 works but runs long.

**Thread 2 (Man Still Moving)**: Three chapters (12, 15, 19). The Cambridge decision in ch 12 is handled correctly. Ch 15 does far more than thread-work — it is the act's emotional center. Ch 19's reckoning section (his decision not to say anything, his recognition of Schell's kind of displacement) is the thread's best Act 2 moment.

**Thread 3 (The Hearing)**: One chapter (18), which earns its space and earns the exceptional POV.

**Thread 4 (The Fence)**: Three chapters (11, 14, 17). Ch 17 is the act's strongest fence chapter. The arc from word-cards to perimeter-walk to *Ich auch* is properly accumulated.

**Thread 5 (Pfeiffer Silence)**: Two chapters (11, 14). Albrecht's *Erst in Hamburg* in ch 11's dinner scene is one of the act's minor glories — five syllables for a man crossing an ocean. But the thread marks time in ch 17; Magda's return to speech, which the thread notes show beginning in ch 26, may need a slightly stronger planted beat here to avoid the later movement feeling abrupt.

---

### Voice Assessment

**Marcus**: Most consistent, most technically accomplished. The suit-governance system, the Kurrent notation nobody else can read, the German vocabulary carried as thought rather than display — all in register. Minor slippage in ch 15: "He carried them" is a redundant second sentence after "Their names he held the same way he held the name he had decided to use only once" has already performed the carrying.

**Eira**: Correct and often effective, but more static across chs 13 and 16 than the brief's model warrants. The procedural record-keeping of her chapters occasionally reads as the voice without the texture behind it. Ch 20 corrects this: the addendum makes the voice do its emotional work by structural means rather than modulation.

**Hannah**: Least consistent. The brief specifies strong opinions and uncertain foundations, observation before reflection, no adult retrospective wisdom. Chs 11 and 14 both contain passages where the narration editorializes about what Hannah understands in a way that exceeds what a seventeen-to-eighteen-year-old's narration would produce. The Rolf coat paragraph at the end of ch 14 ("or maybe that was the coat's own smell by now, her smell, after all the mornings she'd worn it") is the act's best Hannah observation — earned through sensory precision, not stated. More like that; fewer sentences that begin "She had learned."

---

### Pacing

The act's center of gravity is ch 15 — the correct structural choice for a D-Day chapter in a home-front novel. The five chapters before it build pressure; the five after carry its weight. The act does not sag in the middle so much as modulate unevenly: the outstanding chapters (15, 20, 17, 18) are distributed toward the act's second half, while the first five chapters are consistently good but not consistently excellent. Ch 12's middle section is the act's only true drag. The Schell reveal gap (ch 16 to ch 19) creates a local pacing hiccup but is not a structural failure.

---

**Strengths identified:**
- **Ch 15 ("The Sixth of June")** — the act's standout chapter; handles the Holocaust's weight through Marcus's Paris memories with precision and restraint that the brief demands
- **Ch 20 ("Addendum")** — the official-report-as-chapter structure is a formal invention; Eira's best chapter in the act

# Reader Act 3 Assessment — Chapters 21–30

## Overall

Act 3 is where the craft has most consistently found its footing. The five threads resolve in the order the canon specifies, the three exceptional-POV chapters earn their places, and several individual scenes reach the level the brief is aiming for. Where the act struggles, it struggles in a recognizable way: chapters that do their work and then keep going, explaining what they have already shown. These are not fatal problems — they are revision problems — but they recur often enough in this act to name clearly.

---

## What's Working

**Ch22, Reed's Chair.** The standout exceptional-POV chapter. Lonzo's moral calculus is rendered without sentimentality: the line "whether a particular piece of line had another season in it" is the book's idiom at its best — a man thinking about a moral problem in the only language he actually has. The decision to write *Present* in the headcount book and then wait in the dark is rendered through action and sequence rather than announced through interior reasoning. The ending ("*What does it cost. What does it cost.* … It was not that kind of question") is one of the act's finest closes.

**Ch23, October Night.** The harbor-from-the-ridge scene earns its long buildup across the whole T4 thread. Karl saying "*Ich habe davon gehört*" — I heard about that — about the drowned man is a perfectly placed moment of structural irony: Hannah has been thinking of the body as local news, contained within where the knowing can go, and Karl's response breaks that assumption without announcement. The ending lands exactly right: *Fest.* Solid. "She was not going to work through whether it was the right thing tonight." The chapter trusts the reader completely.

**Ch27, The Landlord's Game.** The best Marcus chapter. The chess structure is integrated, not decorative — Henry's patient queen-side build while Marcus watched the king side earns the scene's emotional revelation rather than merely illustrating it. "I think I have stopped moving" is six words, and the act around them is precisely right: Henry's response (*Then you should reorganize your defense*) stays inside the game's logic; Marcus thinks the word *Ansässigkeit* without staging it as an epiphany. The ending — washing the cup, going upstairs — is exactly what the style guide asks for: no summary, no explanation, just the next action.

**Ch26, The Last Winter.** The November woodpile scene — Hannah watching her father and Karl speak for twenty minutes in German, neither she nor the reader given the content — is the most disciplined restraint in the act. "He poured himself a glass of water at the sink and stood for a while with his hand on the edge of the counter, looking at something that was not in the room." *Das tut mir leid* at the fence is earned precisely because the novel has given that phrase three years to accumulate weight.

**Ch29, April.** Thread 1 resolves cleanly. The breakwater walk is right — Eira doing the tidal calculation she already did in 1943, because she still knows it by heart, not because she needs to. The buoy-tender ending is the best closing action in the book: "She found the vessel arrival log on the desk and picked it up. She put on her coat." The work continues. Nothing needs to be stated.

---

## What Isn't Working

**Ch24's extended reflective tail.** The hearing itself is excellent — "I did" about the family in Austria is the single most powerful moment in Marcus's arc, and the five-to-two corridor exchange with Henry is exactly the economy the book promises. But after Marcus leaves the building, the chapter continues for nearly two full pages of meditation on the etymology of *loyal* and the difference between *heimisch* and practicing presence. The style guide is explicit: end on an image or action, not a summarizing reflection. The chapter's image — snow assembling itself above the town — arrives after the reflection has already pre-explained what it should carry. Compare with ch27, which ends in three syllables and lets the kitchen do the work.

**Ch30's classroom reflection over-articulates the arc.** The German-class scene runs several pages in which Hannah retrospectively accounts for the year's choices: the word-cards, Karl's father's full name (*Friedrich Reinmar*, given here for the first time), the V-Mail she sent in February, the nursing-school question. Some of this introduces genuinely new material (the name, the nursing-school consideration) in ways that feel slightly ad hoc at this late stage. But the larger problem is that this section does work the preceding twenty-nine chapters have already done. "*Den Häusern,* she said" — Klein calling on her, she answers the dative plural and returns from her reverie — is a perfect small action. The long reflection surrounding it is not.

**Ch28's voice break.** Karl's chapter is the third exceptional POV, and most of it is calibrated correctly: the physical memories of Regensburg (the spire on the road from his grandfather's, the tile floor, the shop's smell of blood and sawdust), the flat practicality of the ending. But one passage breaks register: "He had been trying since October to find a word for this kind of exchange in either of his languages and the word had not come. There was *Freundschaft*, friendship, but that was a category. There was *Freundlichkeit*, kindness, but that was also a category, and also not exactly right." This is philological reasoning — Marcus's mode of attention, not Karl's. A butcher's apprentice from Regensburg does not systematically eliminate German nouns for their semantic range. The passage should give us the *absence* of the word — the feeling of reaching for a thing that isn't there — not the academic process of elimination.

**Continuity break: Karl's jacket.** Ch23 (Hannah POV): "He was wearing the jacket she always saw him wear — dark wool, too thin for October, the only coat he had." Ch30 (Hannah POV, eighteen months later): "The denim jacket the Army had issued him was worn at the shoulder seams." These are not the same garment; Hannah, who has observed Karl daily for eighteen months, describes contradictory fabrics in her own narration. One needs to be corrected.

---

## Voice

Act 3 generally holds register across the three POVs. Eira's chapters (21, 25, 29) stay procedural and spare; Hannah's chapters stay porous and associative; Marcus's chapters stay formally constructed. The exception noted above in ch24 — the *loyal* meditation running to essay length — is the act's most sustained voice-register drift: Marcus thinking in philological paragraphs after a plot crisis has been resolved reads less like Marcus translating his thinking in real time and more like Marcus writing a draft of the reflection. Ch25 has one uncharacteristic moment of explicit self-statement ("She had been making this argument since November 1935") that is more analytically declarative than Eira's usual compression of feeling into behavior, though it is brief and recoverable.

---

## Pacing and Thread Development

All five threads resolve in the chapters specified. The T2/T3 double resolution in ch24 is handled without either feeling perfunctory. The exceptional-POV chapters (22, 28) each advance exactly one thread and earn their existence. 

The act's pacing is strong. Ch28 (Karl) serves effectively as a meditative buffer between ch27's Marcus resolution and ch29's Eira resolution — it slows the act's final approach without losing momentum. The slight drag is in ch24, which is the act's longest chapter by feel and could give up 400–500 words from its reflective aftermath without losing anything.

Three chapters in this act end with a character going to sleep or going to bed (ch22: Lonzo, ch25: Eira, ch28: Karl). These endings serve different purposes and are distributed across different POVs, but their accumulation gives the act a slight closing-note uniformity that a revision pass might vary.
